- **Units Discipline**: Use $\text{J}$ for energy and $\text{J/K}$ for entropy; ensure temperature is in Kelvin (not Celsius) in all entropy calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thermodynamic Property?

A **Thermodynamic Property** is any **macroscopically observable, measurable characteristic of a system** whose value depends *only* on the **state** (i.e., the condition) of the system — and not on the *path* by which that state was reached.

Mathematically, a property $X$ is said to be a thermodynamic property if its differential $\mathrm{d}X$ is an **exact differential**, satisfying the Euler reciprocity condition:

$$\frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial y \, \partial z} = \frac{\partial^2 X}{\partial z \, \partial y}$$

Properties are also called **state functions** or **state variables**. They form the fundamental "language" of thermodynamics — every other concept (energy, work, heat, entropy) is *defined in terms of* properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):** A property is independent of the *history* of the system. If a gas is taken from State 1 to State 2 by any route (isothermal, adiabatic, or any combination), the change in any property $\Delta X = X_2 - X_1$ remains the **same**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bank Balance" Analogy:** Think of your **bank account balance** as a property. Whatever route you took to earn/spend money (job, business, lottery) does not matter. If your balance today is ₹10,000, *that is the state* — independent of how you got there. Similarly, the **temperature** of water at 100 °C is 100 °C, irrespective of whether it was heated slowly, fast, or mixed with hot water from elsewhere.

In contrast, **heat and work** are *not* properties — they are **path functions** (like the *journey* you took to reach that bank balance). The KTU examiner will frequently test this contrast.

---

## 1.3 Formal Classification of Thermodynamic Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification (Memorize for KTU 2-mark direct questions):**

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Examples** | **Dependency on System Size** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extensive Property** | Depends on the *size / extent* (mass) of the system | Mass $m$, Volume $V$, Total Energy $E$, Entropy $S$ | **Yes** — additive |
| **Intensive Property** | *Independent* of the size of the system | Pressure $P$, Temperature $T$, Density $\rho$, Specific volume $v$ | **No** |
| **Specific Property** | An *intensive* property obtained by dividing an extensive property by mass | $v = V/m$, $u = U/m$, $h = H/m$, $s = S/m$ | **No** (by definition) |
| **Derived / Mixed Property** | Combination of two or more properties | Specific gravity, Molar mass, Universal gas constant $R$ | Context-dependent |

> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Numericals:** SI units throughout — **Pressure in kPa or Pa**, **Temperature in K (Kelvin)**, **Volume in m³**, **Mass in kg**, **Energy in kJ**.

---

## 1.4 Pressure, Temperature, Density — The Three Primary Properties

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *primitive* measurable properties from which nearly all others (internal energy, enthalpy, entropy) are derived or tabulated.

### (a) Pressure ($P$)
Pressure is the **normal force exerted per unit area** by a fluid on a surface:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

- SI Unit: **N/m² = Pascal (Pa) = kg·m⁻¹·s⁻²**
- Commonly used: **kPa** (1 kPa = 1000 Pa) and **bar** (1 bar = 100 kPa).
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ **kPa** = 1.01325 bar.

### (b) Temperature ($T$)
Temperature is a property that determines the *direction* of **spontaneous heat transfer** between two bodies in thermal contact.

- **Absolute scales:** Kelvin (K) and Rankine (°R).
- Conversion relations:
  - $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$
  - $T(°\text{R}) = T(°\text{F}) + 459.67$

### (c) Density ($\rho$) and Specific Volume ($v$)

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}, \qquad v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

- $\rho$ has units **kg/m³**.
- $v$ has units **m³/kg**.
- These two are reciprocals of each other — *never confuse them* in numericals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Gravity (SG)** — a dimensionless intensive property:
> $$\text{SG} = \frac{\rho_{\text{substance}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4°C}}$$
> Numerically, $\rho_{\text{water at 4°C}} = 1000$ kg/m³, so $\text{SG} = \rho/1000$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The State and the State Postulate

A system is said to be in a **thermodynamic state** when *all* of its properties have fixed, definite values. Since properties are mathematically related to one another, **we do not need to specify every property** to fix a state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The State Postulate (also called the Two-Property Rule):**
> *The state of a simple compressible system (a system with negligible electrical, magnetic, gravitational, surface tension, and motion effects) is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.*

This postulate is the foundation of **property tables** (steam tables, R-134a tables, ideal-gas tables) — they tabulate properties as functions of two independent variables, typically $(T, v)$ or $(T, P)$ or $(P, v)$.

**Why "two" and not "one" or "three"?**
- One property is *insufficient*: e.g., $P = 100$ kPa could correspond to water at 99.6 °C *or* at 200 °C (compressed liquid vs. superheated vapor).
- Three or more is *redundant*: specifying $P$ and $T$ automatically determines $v$ for a pure substance — the third is calculable from an equation of state.

---

## 2.2 The Concept of Equilibrium

A system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** if *all four* sub-equilibria are satisfied simultaneously:

| **Sub-equilibrium** | **Condition Required** | **Property That Is Uniform** |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | No unbalanced forces / pressures | Pressure $P$ |
| **Thermal** | No temperature gradients | Temperature $T$ |
| **Phase** | No mass transfer between phases | Chemical potential $\mu$ |
| **Chemical** | No chemical reaction occurring | Composition |

> [!NOTE]
> A *non-equilibrium* state **cannot** be described by a single set of properties. This is why KTU problems always assume systems to be in equilibrium unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 2.3 Process, Path, and Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process:** A change of state from initial state (1) to final state (2).
> **Path:** The *specific sequence* of states through which the system passes during a process.
> **Cycle:** A process whose *initial and final states are identical* (a closed loop on a property diagram).

The change in **any property** between State 1 and State 2 is:

$$\Delta X = X_2 - X_1 \quad \text{(independent of path)}$$

But for **path functions** (heat $Q$ and work $W$):

$$\oint \mathrm{d}Q \neq 0, \qquad \oint \mathrm{d}W \neq 0 \quad \text{in a cycle}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the following table — it is the single most-tested set of relations in KTU Module 1.**

| **Formula / Relation** | **Symbol / Form** | **Units (SI)** | **Use in KTU Problems** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure definition | $P = F/A$ | Pa (= N/m²) | Manometer, barometer problems |
| Absolute pressure | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ | kPa | Gauge-to-absolute conversion |
| Vacuum pressure | $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$ | kPa | Below-atmospheric readings |
| Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ | Mass–volume problems |
| Specific volume | $v = 1/\rho$ | m³/kg | Reciprocal property of density |
| Specific gravity | $\text{SG} = \rho / \rho_{\text{water}}$ | dimensionless | Comparative density |
| State postulate | State fixed by 2 independent properties | — | Property table lookups |
| Mass conservation | $m = \rho V = V/v$ | kg | Closed system analysis |
| Temperature (C → K) | $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$ | K | Unit conversion |
| Temperature (F → R) | $T(°\text{R}) = T(°\text{F}) + 459.67$ | °R | Imperial-unit problems |
| Force = Pressure × Area | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | Hydraulic press, piston problems |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | Pa | Liquid column problems |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Property tables of water/steam drive the design of **thermal power plants, refrigeration cycles, heat exchangers, and process plants** (petroleum, chemical). The state postulate lets engineers fully characterize operating points with just two measurements.

---

## 2.5 Density of a Mixture and Partial Properties

For a mixture of $N$ components, the **average (mixture) density** is:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i}{V_{\text{total}}}$$

The **mass fraction** $w_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}}$ and **mole fraction** $y_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$ are intensive properties used to specify composition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** A mixture's *average* molecular weight $\bar{M}$ is **not** the arithmetic mean — it is the **mass-weighted harmonic mean**:
> $$\bar{M} = \frac{1}{\sum_i (y_i / M_i)}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Foundation:** The derivations below are the *exact* type expected in KTU 14-mark Module 1 questions. Practice rewriting each step in your exam copy.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Conversion Between Gauge, Absolute, and Vacuum Pressure

A measuring instrument (Bourdon gauge, manometer) measures pressure **relative to atmospheric pressure** (this is called *gauge pressure*). The true thermodynamic pressure, however, must be referenced to a perfect vacuum.

### Statement
For a system at gauge pressure $P_{\text{gauge}}$ (above atmospheric):

$$P_{\text{absolute}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atmospheric}}$$

For a system at vacuum (below atmospheric), the **vacuum pressure** is defined as:

$$P_{\text{vacuum}} = P_{\text{atmospheric}} - P_{\text{absolute}}$$

### Detailed Derivation

Consider a pressure gauge connected to a closed vessel. Let the local atmospheric pressure be $P_{\text{atm}}$. The gauge reading $P_{\text{gauge}}$ represents the *excess* of the internal pressure over the atmospheric pressure pushing on the gauge's external side:

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{inside}} - P_{\text{atm}}$$

Rearranging to isolate the *true* (absolute) pressure:

$$P_{\text{inside}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$$

Since the inside pressure is the absolute pressure, we identify:

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$$

> **Case 2 — Sub-atmospheric (Vacuum):** A vacuum gauge reads $P_{\text{vac}}$ equal to the *deficit* below atmospheric:

$$P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$$

> **Numerical Verification:** Suppose $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa. A gauge reads 250 kPa. Then $P_{\text{abs}} = 250 + 101.325 = 351.325$ kPa. A vacuum gauge reads 40 kPa, so $P_{\text{abs}} = 101.325 - 40 = 61.325$ kPa. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Hydrostatic Pressure of a Liquid Column

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Question (3 marks minimum, 7 marks in Part B):** "Derive the expression for pressure at the bottom of a liquid column."

Consider a vertical column of incompressible liquid of uniform density $\rho$, height $h$, and cross-sectional area $A$ (open to atmosphere on top).

### Derivation

The **weight** of the liquid column is:

$$W = m g = (\rho V) g = \rho (A h) g$$

Pressure is force per unit area, so the pressure *exerted by the liquid* at the base is:

$$P_{\text{liquid}} = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{\rho A h g}{A}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{liquid}} = \rho g h}$$

If the column is exposed to atmospheric pressure on the top surface, the **absolute pressure at depth $h$** is:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h}$$

> **SI Units Check:** $[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^3$, $[g] = \text{m/s}^2$, $[h] = \text{m}$ $\Rightarrow$ $[\rho g h] = \text{kg/(m·s}^2\text{)} = \text{N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}$ ✓

**Engineering application:** This is the principle behind **manometers, barometers, sphygmomanometers (blood pressure), and dam design**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Specific Volume from Density (Reciprocal Property)

Given an extensive property $X_{\text{extensive}}$ (e.g., volume $V$), the corresponding **specific property** is:

$$x = \frac{X_{\text{extensive}}}{m}$$

For volume, $V$ is the total volume of mass $m$:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho}$$

Dividing both sides by $m$:

$$\frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{\rho}}$$

This *reciprocal relationship* is fundamental — it is the reason why specific volume (m³/kg) is preferred over density in many steam-table and gas-equation contexts (e.g., the ideal gas law $Pv = RT$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Justification of the State Postulate (Two-Property Rule)

> [!TIP]
> **This derivation is often asked as a 7-mark sub-question: "Show that two independent properties are sufficient to fix the state of a simple compressible system."**

A pure substance in the absence of motion, gravity, electricity, magnetism, and capillarity is described by the **fundamental relation** (Gibbs phase rule for a single component):

$$F = C - P + 2$$

where $F$ = degrees of freedom, $C$ = number of components, $P$ = number of phases. For a single-component system ($C = 1$) with a single phase ($P = 1$):

$$F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$$

Hence, **two independent intensive properties are both necessary and sufficient** to fix the thermodynamic state.

> **Equivalent Derivation via Differentials:** Any property $Z$ can in principle be expressed as a function of *any* two other independent properties $X$ and $Y$:
> $$Z = Z(X, Y)$$
> The total differential is:
> $$\mathrm{d}Z = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial X}\right)_Y \mathrm{d}X + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial Y}\right)_X \mathrm{d}Y$$
> If only $X$ and $Y$ are *independent*, then knowing $(X, Y)$ everywhere determines $Z$ everywhere. Therefore, two independent properties suffice. ∎

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property Behaviour Across Processes

> The table below summarises how the major properties change (or do not) during a few standard processes — a frequent 3-mark question in KTU.

| **Process** | **Pressure $P$** | **Volume $V$** | **Temperature $T$** | **Internal Energy $U$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isobaric** (constant $P$) | **Constant** | Varies | Varies | Varies |
| **Isothermal** (constant $T$) | Varies | Varies | **Constant** | Varies (for ideal gas: constant) |
| **Isochoric** (constant $V$) | Varies | **Constant** | Varies | Varies |
| **Adiabatic** (no heat) | Varies | Varies | Varies | Varies |
| **Cyclic** (returns to start) | Net $\oint \mathrm{d}P = 0$ | Net $\oint \mathrm{d}V = 0$ | Net $\oint \mathrm{d}T = 0$ | Net $\oint \mathrm{d}U = 0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU trap: a **state function** always has $\oint \mathrm{d}X = 0$ around any closed cycle, but $Q$ and $W$ (path functions) generally do not.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps the complete classification hierarchy of thermodynamic properties as required by the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus. This is the most-examined organisational chart in the module.

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES] --> B[EXTENSIVE PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[INTENSIVE PROPERTIES]

    B --> B1[Mass m]
    B --> B2[Total Volume V]
    B --> B3[Total Internal Energy U]
    B --> B4[Total Enthalpy H]
    B --> B5[Total Entropy S]

    C --> C1[Pressure P]
    C --> C2[Temperature T]
    C --> C3[Density rho]
    C --> D[SPECIFIC PROPERTIES]
    C --> E[DERIVED PROPERTIES]

    D --> D1[Specific Volume v = V per m]
    D --> D2[Specific Internal Energy u = U per m]
    D --> D3[Specific Enthalpy h = H per m]
    D --> D4[Specific Entropy s = S per m]

    E --> E1[Specific Gravity SG]
    E --> E2[Molar Mass M]
    E --> E3[Universal Gas Constant R]
    E --> E4[Gas Constant R_specific]

    C1 --> P1[ABSOLUTE PRESSURE]
    C1 --> P2[GAUGE PRESSURE]
    C1 --> P3[VACUUM PRESSURE]

    P1 --> P1A[Referenced to perfect vacuum]
    P2 --> P2A[P_atm subtracted by instrument]
    P3 --> P3A[Below atmospheric reading]
```

## 4.2 State Postulate and Property Relationships Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1: P1, T1, v1, u1, h1, s1] --> PR[PROCESS OR TRANSITION]
    S2[State 2: P2, T2, v2, u2, h2, s2]
    PR --> S2
    SP[STATE POSTULATE] --> S1
    SP --> S2

    SP1[Two independent intensive properties] --> SP
    SP2[Examples: P and T, P and v, T and v] --> SP

    EQ[THERMODYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM] --> S1
    EQ --> S2

    MECH[MECHANICAL EQUILIBRIUM] --> EQ
    THERM[THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM] --> EQ
    PHASE[PHASE EQUILIBRIUM] --> EQ
    CHEM[CHEMICAL EQUILIBRIUM] --> EQ
```

## 4.3 Pressure Measurement Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[FLUID UNDER MEASUREMENT] --> GAUGE[PRESSURE MEASURING DEVICE]
    GAUGE --> R1[GAUGE READING]
    R1 --> CONV[CONVERSION LOGIC]
    CONV --> ABS[ABSOLUTE PRESSURE]
    CONV --> VAC[VACUUM PRESSURE]
    ATM[P_atmospheric equals 101.325 kPa] --> CONV

    CONV --> C1[If R is positive: P_abs = R + P_atm]
    CONV --> C2[If R is negative: P_vac = P_atm - P_abs]
    CONV --> C3[Reference state: Perfect Vacuum equals 0 kPa absolute]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in your exam answer:** The arrows in Mermaid show a *hierarchy* of concepts. Use these diagrams to structure long answers (7–14 marks) — examiners award marks for **logical flow**, not just for content.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1 — 14 marks ESE):**
> - Part A (3 marks × 2 questions = 6 marks): short definitions / direct recall.
> - Part B (14 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B): one full-length question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between intensive and extensive properties. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Intensive Properties** | **Extensive Properties** |
|---|---|
| Independent of system size (mass) | Directly proportional to system size (mass) |
| Two systems combined → *average* value | Two systems combined → *sum* of values |
| **Examples:** Pressure ($P$), Temperature ($T$), Density ($\rho$), Specific volume ($v$) | **Examples:** Mass ($m$), Total volume ($V$), Total internal energy ($U$), Total entropy ($S$) |

> **Key distinguishing test:** If you *double* the mass of the system, the **intensive property remains the same** while the **extensive property doubles**.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"A pressure gauge reads 80 kPa at a location where atmospheric pressure is 100 kPa. Determine the absolute pressure and vacuum pressure."**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $P_{\text{gauge}} = 80$ kPa, $P_{\text{atm}} = 100$ kPa.

Since the gauge reading is **less** than atmospheric pressure, the system is *below* atmospheric — i.e., a **vacuum** condition.

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{gauge}}$$

$$P_{\text{abs}} = 100 - 80 = 20 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}} = 100 - 20 = 80 \text{ kPa} \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students add 80 + 100 = 180 kPa. This is **wrong** because the *gauge* reads *less* than atmospheric. Always compare $P_{\text{gauge}}$ with $P_{\text{atm}}$ **first**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7 sub-parts).

---

### ❓ Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**"Thermodynamic properties form the foundation of all energy analysis. With suitable examples and derivations, explain the classification of properties and the conversion between pressure readings."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Derive the hydrostatic pressure relation $P = \rho g h$ and explain its use in a manometer."**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Definition:** Consider a vertical column of a static, incompressible liquid of uniform density $\rho$, height $h$, and cross-sectional area $A$, open to the atmosphere on top.

**Step 2 — Mass of the Liquid Column:**
$$m = \rho V = \rho (A \cdot h)$$

**Step 3 — Weight of the Column (force exerted on the base):**
$$F = m g = \rho A h g$$

**Step 4 — Pressure Definition (force per unit area):**
$$P_{\text{liquid}} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho A h g}{A} = \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{liquid}} = \rho g h}$$

**Step 5 — Absolute Pressure at Depth $h$:**
$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$$

**Step 6 — Manometer Application:** A U-tube manometer uses mercury (SG = 13.6) to measure pressure difference. The difference in mercury column heights $h_m$ gives $\Delta P = \rho_{\text{Hg}} g h_m$. For a closed manometer, the absolute pressure is:
$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{Hg column}} = \rho_{\text{Hg}} g h_m$$
where $h_m$ is the height difference of mercury columns.

**Engineering Use:** Blood pressure measurement, barometric pressure, hydraulic presses, dam design.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A Bourdon pressure gauge connected to a steam pipe reads 1.4 MPa. The local barometer reads 760 mm of Hg. Calculate (i) the absolute pressure in the pipe in kPa, and (ii) the absolute pressure if the gauge had read a vacuum of 40 kPa instead."**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{gauge}} = 1.4$ MPa = 1400 kPa
- $P_{\text{atm}} = 760$ mm Hg = $0.760 \times 13.6 \times 9.81$ kPa

**Step 1 — Calculate Atmospheric Pressure:**
$$P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{\text{Hg}} g h = (13600 \text{ kg/m}^3)(9.81 \text{ m/s}^2)(0.760 \text{ m})$$

$$P_{\text{atm}} = 101.396 \text{ kPa} \approx 101.4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Pressure (Case i):**
$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 1400 + 101.4$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{abs}} = 1501.4 \text{ kPa} \approx 1.501 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Pressure (Case ii — vacuum reading):**
$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} = 101.4 - 40$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{abs}} = 61.4 \text{ kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question A):** Marks are often *lost* when students:
> 1. Forget to convert **mm Hg to kPa** (you must multiply by $\rho_{\text{Hg}} g$, or use the conversion **1 atm = 760 mm Hg = 101.325 kPa**).
> 2. Confuse gauge and vacuum signs — *always draw a sketch of the gauge with atmospheric pressure pushing on the outside*.

---

### ❓ Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**"Properties characterise the state of a system. Discuss the state postulate, the concept of equilibrium, and the distinction between properties and path functions."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"State and explain the state postulate. Show by a phase-rule argument that two independent intensive properties are sufficient to fix the state of a pure substance."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement:**
> *The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**.*

**Step 2 — Definition of a Simple Compressible System:** A system whose state can be described by pressure, specific volume, and temperature, with negligible effects from gravity, motion, capillarity, electricity, and magnetism.

**Step 3 — Phase Rule Justification (Gibbs Phase Rule):**
$$F = C - P + 2$$
For a pure substance: number of components $C = 1$. For a single phase (e.g., compressed liquid only, or superheated vapor only): $P = 1$.

$$F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$$

Thus, **2 degrees of freedom** — two independent intensive properties uniquely fix the state.

**Step 4 — Alternative Mathematical Justification:**
Any other property $Z$ is a continuous function of two independent properties $X$ and $Y$:
$$Z = f(X, Y)$$
Once $(X, Y)$ are fixed, $Z$ is determined. Hence, the state is fixed by any two independent properties. Common pairs:
- $(P, T)$ — most common in tables of saturated water.
- $(P, v)$ — used in $P$–$v$ diagrams.
- $(T, v)$ — used in $T$–$s$ diagrams.

**Step 5 — Property Tables:** Steam tables, refrigerant tables, and ideal-gas tables are organised exactly on this principle — they tabulate properties as a function of *two* independent inputs.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"With two clear examples, explain why heat $Q$ and work $W$ are not thermodynamic properties. State three conditions of thermodynamic equilibrium."**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Why $Q$ and $W$ are not properties:**

| **Aspect** | **Property (e.g., $U$, $T$, $P$)** | **Heat $Q$ / Work $W$** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | State function — fixed by state | Path function — depends on *how* the process is carried out |
| Differential | Exact differential ($\mathrm{d}X$) | Inexact differential ($\delta Q$, $\delta W$) |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint \mathrm{d}X = 0$ | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$, $\oint \delta W \neq 0$ in general |

**Example 1 — Work:** To compress a gas from $V_1$ to $V_2$:
- Isothermal compression: $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$
- Polytropic compression $PV^n = $ const: $W$ takes a *different numerical value* for the same end states.

**Example 2 — Heat:** To heat 1 kg of water from 25 °C to 75 °C:
- Constant pressure: $Q = m c_p \Delta T$
- Constant volume: $Q = m c_v \Delta T$ (different value, same end states).

Since both $Q$ and $W$ change value with the *path* even when initial and final states are the same, they are **not properties**.

**Part 2 — Three Conditions of Thermodynamic Equilibrium:**

1. **Mechanical Equilibrium:** Pressure is uniform throughout the system (no pressure gradients causing unbalanced forces).
2. **Thermal Equilibrium:** Temperature is uniform (no heat flow, i.e., system is at the same temperature as surroundings or internally).
3. **Phase Equilibrium:** No mass transfer between phases (e.g., liquid and vapor coexist only at saturation conditions).
4. **Chemical Equilibrium:** No net chemical reactions occurring (composition remains constant).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. Do **not** confuse "state" with "process" — the state is a *snapshot*, the process is a *movie*.
> 2. Always state the **Gibbs phase rule** for the state postulate — it is a guaranteed 2–3 marks.
> 3. Avoid writing "heat is a property" — this is a **classic KTU mistake** worth 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Revision Checklist (last-night KTU prep):**

- **Property:** A measurable characteristic of a system depending *only* on the state, with an exact differential.
- **State Function vs Path Function:** State functions → $P$, $T$, $V$, $U$, $H$, $S$ (cyclic integral = 0). Path functions → $Q$, $W$ (cyclic integral ≠ 0 in general).
- **Intensive vs Extensive:** Intensive independent of mass; Extensive directly proportional to mass. **Specific properties = extensive/mass → intensive.**
- **Three Reciprocal Relations:** $v = 1/\rho$, $\text{SG} = \rho/1000$, $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$.
- **Pressure Conversions:** $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$; $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{abs}}$.
- **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mm Hg = 14.696 psi.
- **Hydrostatic Relation:** $P = \rho g h$ (SI: $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $h$ in m, $P$ in Pa).
- **State Postulate:** Two independent intensive properties fix the state of a simple compressible system. **Justified by Gibbs phase rule** $F = C - P + 2 = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$.
- **Four Sub-Equilibria:** Mechanical, Thermal, Phase, Chemical — all must be satisfied for full thermodynamic equilibrium.
- **Cycle:** A process returning the system to its initial state — all property changes = 0, but $Q_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{net}}$ (First Law consequence).
- **Density of Water:** 1000 kg/m³ at 4 °C (this is the *reference* for specific gravity).
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) — must be remembered, not derived.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermodynamic Process:** A *thermodynamic process* is the transformation of a system from one **equilibrium state** (State 1) to another **equilibrium state** (State 2), during which the system passes through a continuous sequence of intermediate states. Mathematically, it is the locus of successive states traced by the system, and is represented on a property diagram (P–V, T–S, P–T) by a **path** joining two end points.

A process is fully characterised by the **path function** integral:

$$\oint dX = 0 \quad \text{(cyclic, where X is a state function)}$$

$$\int_{1}^{2} dY = Y_{2}-Y_{1} \quad \text{(Y is a state function, e.g. U, H, S)}$$

$$\int_{1}^{2} dZ = \text{path dependent} \quad \text{(Z is a path function, e.g. W, Q)}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Hike"

Imagine you are hiking from **Base Camp (State 1)** to the **Summit (State 2)** of a mountain.

- The **altitude** at any point is a *state property* — it doesn't matter which trail you took, only where you are.
- The **distance walked** and the **calories burned** are *path functions* — they depend entirely on the route chosen.
- If you take a smooth, paved switchback trail at snail's pace, the hike is **reversible/quasi-static**. If you leap off cliffs, it's **irreversible**.

> [!NOTE]
> **In thermodynamics, properties like T, P, V, U, H, S are state functions** (path-independent). **Work (W) and Heat (Q) are path functions** (path-dependent). This single distinction is the single most-tested KTU concept in Module 1.

### 1.3 Process vs. State vs. Cycle — The Big Picture

| Concept | Definition | Mathematical Symbol | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State** | Condition described by independent properties | Fixed (P, V, T) | High |
| **Process** | Path of successive states | 1 → 2 | Very High |
| **Cycle** | Process returning to initial state | 1 → 1 (closed loop) | Very High |
| **Path** | Specific trajectory of the process | Curve on P–V diagram | High |

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **Ideal Gas** used in KTU numericals:
> - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Ratio of specific heats: **γ = Cp / Cv** (for air, γ = **1.4**; for monoatomic gas, γ = **1.667**; for diatomic gas, γ = **1.4**)
> - Mayer's relation: **Cp − Cv = R**
> - Gas constant in pressure–volume work: **R = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K)** for air
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Thermodynamic Processes

A KTU examiner will always expect you to know the **three primary classification axes**:

**A. Classification by Thermodynamic Coordinate Held Constant**

1. **Isobaric Process** — Pressure held constant (P = constant)
2. **Isochoric (Isometric) Process** — Volume held constant (V = constant)
3. **Isothermal Process** — Temperature held constant (T = constant)
4. **Adiabatic Process** — No heat transfer across boundary (Q = 0)
5. **Polytropic Process** — Generalised form: PV^n = constant, where *n* is the polytropic index.

**B. Classification by Reversibility**

1. **Reversible Process** — System and surroundings can be returned to original states; proceeds through a continuous series of equilibrium states.
2. **Irreversible Process** — Cannot be reversed without leaving permanent changes in the universe (most real processes are irreversible).
3. **Quasi-Static (Quasi-Equilibrium) Process** — Infinitely slow process where the system is in internal equilibrium at every instant — the *necessary condition* for a process to be reversible.

**C. Classification by Heat Transfer**

1. **Adiabatic** — Q = 0 (perfectly insulated boundary)
2. **Diabatic** — Q ≠ 0 (heat is exchanged with surroundings)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Ideal Gas)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your single revision sheet for the topic. All expressions are for a closed system of an ideal gas with **n** moles (or **m** kg). Use the relation **W = ∫P dV** for the general case.

| Process Type | Path Equation | Work Done (W) | Heat Transfer (Q) | ΔU (Internal Energy) | ΔH (Enthalpy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isobaric** | P = const | $P(V_2 - V_1) \;=\; nR(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isochoric** | V = const | $0$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isothermal** | PV = const ; T = const | $nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $W$ (since ΔU = 0) | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Adiabatic** | $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ ; $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$ | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{nR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$ | $0$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Polytropic** | $PV^{n} = \text{const}$ | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$ (for n ≠ 1) | $nC_n(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Cyclic** | Closed loop | $W_{net} = \oint P\,dV$ | $Q_{net} = W_{net}$ | $0$ | $0$ |

Where:
- $C_n = C_v \cdot \dfrac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$ is the **polytropic specific heat**.
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the **adiabatic index** (or isentropic exponent).

### 2.3 Quasi-Static Process — The Bridge to Reversibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **quasi-static process** is the *idealised limit* of an actual process. The system passes through a *continuous sequence of equilibrium states*. In KTU exams, this is the **most common source of conceptual confusion** — see Pitfall Callout in Section 5.

**Why does quasi-static matter?**
- It allows the system to be described by a single set of well-defined macroscopic properties at every instant.
- It is a **necessary (but not sufficient)** condition for reversibility.
- Reversibility additionally requires **no dissipation** (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, no mixing).

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Steam power plants** — Boiler processes approximate isobaric; Turbine expansion approximates isentropic (reversible adiabatic); Condenser processes approximate isobaric.
- **Internal Combustion Engines** — Otto cycle uses isochoric + adiabatic; Diesel cycle uses isobaric + adiabatic.
- **Refrigeration** — Vapour compression cycle uses isentropic compression, isobaric condensation, throttling, isobaric evaporation.
- **Compressors & Turbines** — Modelled as adiabatic (high-speed gas flow has negligible heat loss).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why Study Processes?

1. **Cycle Design**: Every thermodynamic cycle (Carnot, Rankine, Otto, Diesel, Brayton) is a *combination* of ideal processes.
2. **Performance Estimation**: Real machines are benchmarked against ideal reversible processes (e.g., isentropic efficiency of a turbine).
3. **System Modelling**: Refrigerants, combustion gases, and steam are modelled as undergoing a sequence of ideal processes to simplify otherwise intractable PDEs.
4. **Energy Audit**: Knowing ΔU, Q, W for each process lets engineers design energy-efficient systems in line with KTU's NEP 2020 sustainability outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Done in a Quasi-Static Process (Foundational)

Consider a gas in a piston-cylinder assembly expanding against a resisting pressure. The boundary work for a *quasi-static* change in volume is given by:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$$

**Step-by-step expansion of the integral:**

$$\text{Step 1:} \quad W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$$

Substitute the ideal gas equation $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{nRT}{V}\,dV$$

$$\text{Step 2:} \quad W = nRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$\text{Step 3:} \quad W = nRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

$$\text{Step 4:} \quad \boxed{W = nRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

This is the **work done during an isothermal process** of an ideal gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The general work integral $\int P\,dV$ becomes process-specific only when the **relationship between P and V is specified**. This is why every process derivation in KTU Module 1 starts by substituting a path equation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Work Done in a Polytropic Process (Most Important)

The polytropic process is defined by:

$$PV^{n} = \text{constant} = C$$

Hence:

$$P = \frac{C}{V^{n}}$$

Substitute into the boundary work integral:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C \cdot V^{-n}\,dV$$

$$W = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \quad \text{(for } n \neq 1 \text{)}$$

$$W = \frac{C}{1-n}\left(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\right)$$

Now use the identity $C = P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$:

$$V_2^{1-n} = \frac{P_2 V_2^{n}}{V_2^{n-1}} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_2^{1-n} = \frac{C}{V_2^{n-1}}\cdot \frac{1}{V_2^{0}}\text{ (simpler approach below)}$$

A cleaner way: note that $C = P_1 V_1^n$, so $C V_2^{1-n} = P_1 V_1^n V_2^{1-n} = P_1 V_1 \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}$... let's simplify directly using the identity:

$$C \cdot V^{1-n} = PV^n \cdot V^{1-n} = PV$$

Therefore:

$$W = \frac{1}{1-n}\left[ PV \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{1}{1-n}\left(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1\right)$$

Re-writing with the sign convention used in KTU textbooks:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}} \quad \text{(for } n \neq 1 \text{)}$$

For an ideal gas, using $P_1 V_1 = nRT_1$ and $P_2 V_2 = nRT_2$:

$$W = \frac{nR(T_1 - T_2)}{n - 1}$$

**Limit check** — if $n = \gamma$, we recover the **adiabatic work** formula (a frequently asked KTU question):

$$W_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{nR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature–Volume Relation in an Adiabatic Process

Starting from the first law for a closed system (adiabatic, Q = 0):

$$dU + \delta W = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad nC_v\,dT + P\,dV = 0$$

For an ideal gas, $P = \dfrac{nRT}{V}$. Substituting:

$$nC_v\,dT + \frac{nRT}{V}\,dV = 0$$

Divide both sides by $nT$:

$$C_v \frac{dT}{T} + R \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

Using Mayer's relation $R = C_p - C_v$:

$$C_v \frac{dT}{T} + (C_p - C_v)\frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

Divide throughout by $C_v$ and use $\gamma = C_p / C_v$:

$$\frac{dT}{T} + (\gamma - 1)\frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

Integrate from state 1 to state 2:

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T} + (\gamma - 1)\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + (\gamma - 1)\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 0$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = -(\gamma - 1)\ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}}$$

This is the **T–V adiabatic relation** and is a direct line of attack for KTU numericals.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sign Conventions Across Processes

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite KTU exam tabulation question**. Memorise it row-wise.

| Process | ΔU | ΔH | Q | W (Boundary) | Direction of T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Isobaric expansion ($V_2 > V_1$) | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | T rises if $C_p > 0$ (always) |
| Isochoric heating ($T_2 > T_1$) | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | $0$ | rises |
| Isothermal expansion | $0$ | $0$ | $+$ | $+$ | constant |
| Isothermal compression | $0$ | $0$ | $-$ | $-$ | constant |
| Adiabatic expansion | $-$ | $-$ | $0$ | $+$ (gas does work) | falls |
| Adiabatic compression | $+$ | $+$ | $0$ | $-$ (work done on gas) | rises |
| Polytropic (1 < n < γ) heating | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | $+$ | rises |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Classification Flow of Thermodynamic Processes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Thermodynamic Process 1 to 2]
    A --> B[By Held Property]
    A --> C[By Heat Transfer]
    A --> D[By Reversibility]
    A --> E[By Polytropic Index n]

    B --> B1[Isobaric P const]
    B --> B2[Isochoric V const]
    B --> B3[Isothermal T const]
    B --> B4[Adiabatic Q zero]

    C --> C1[Adiabatic Q zero insulated]
    C --> C2[Diabatic Q nonzero]

    D --> D1[Reversible]
    D --> D2[Irreversible]
    D1 --> D3[Quasi static infinite slow]
    D2 --> D4[Free expansion friction unrestrained]

    E --> E1[Isobaric n equals 0]
    E --> E2[Isothermal n equals 1]
    E --> E3[Adiabatic n equals gamma]
    E --> E4[Isochoric n equals infinity]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Process Analysis Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1[State 1: P1 V1 T1] --> proc[Process Definition]
    proc --> analysis[Compute Path]
    analysis --> state2[State 2: P2 V2 T2]

    state1 --> calcW[Work Calculator]
    proc --> calcW
    calcW --> integral[Integral of P dV from V1 to V2]

    state1 --> calcQ[Heat Calculator]
    proc --> calcQ
    calcQ --> firstlaw[Apply First Law]

    state1 --> calcU[Delta U Calculator]
    state2 --> calcU
    calcU --> res[Result: delta U and delta H]

    integral --> outbox[Output W Q deltaU deltaH]
    firstlaw --> outbox
    res --> outbox
```

### 4.3 P–V Diagram Schematic (Textual Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exams frequently ask you to **sketch and label** P–V diagrams. The mnemonic **"sAIr"** is useful:
>
> - **s**teepest = **A**diabatic (slope = $-\gamma P/V$)
> - **I**sothermal has slope = $-P/V$
> - **r**elation: since $\gamma > 1$, **adiabatic is always steeper than isothermal** on a P–V plot.

| Curve | Slope on P–V | Path Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Isothermal (T = const) | $-P/V$ | $PV = \text{const}$ |
| Adiabatic (Q = 0) | $-\gamma P/V$ | $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$ |
| Isobaric (P = const) | $0$ (horizontal) | $P = \text{const}$ |
| Isochoric (V = const) | $\infty$ (vertical) | $V = \text{const}$ |
| Polytropic | $-n P/V$ | $PV^{n} = \text{const}$ |

### 4.4 Quasi-Static vs. Non-Quasi-Static — System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    Q1[System at State 1] -->|driving force epsilon| Q2[Quasi Static Step 1 plus dV]
    Q2 -->|driving force epsilon| Q3[Quasi Static Step 2 plus dV]
    Q3 -->|driving force epsilon| Q4[Quasi Static Step 3 plus dV]
    Q4 --> Q5[Quasi Static Step n equals State 2]

    R1[Real Process Start] -->|finite pressure difference| R2[Rapid Expansion Step 1]
    R2 -->|turbulence and friction| R3[Non Equilibrium Step 2]
    R3 -->|wave propagation| R4[Non Equilibrium Step 3]
    R4 --> R5[Real Process End State 2]

    Q1 -. continuous equilibrium .-> Q5
    R1 -. discontinuous .-> R5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a thermodynamic process. Distinguish between a reversible and an irreversible process with one example each.** *[3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**

A thermodynamic process is the path of successive states by which a system moves from an initial equilibrium state (State 1) to a final equilibrium state (State 2).

- **Reversible process:** A process that can be retraced exactly in the opposite direction such that the system and surroundings pass through exactly the same intermediate states as in the direct process, leaving no net change in the universe. *Example:* Frictionless quasi-static expansion of an ideal gas.
- **Irreversible process:** A process that cannot be retraced without producing permanent changes in the surroundings. *Example:* Unrestrained (free) expansion of a gas into a vacuum, or any process with friction.

---

**Q2. State the conditions for a process to be quasi-static and reversible. Why is "quasi-static" only a necessary but not sufficient condition for reversibility?** *[3 Marks]*

**Model Answer:**

A process is **quasi-static** when the system is in thermodynamic equilibrium at every instant — it proceeds infinitely slowly so that the deviation from equilibrium is infinitesimal.

It is **reversible** when, in addition to being quasi-static, there is no dissipation (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, no heat transfer across a finite temperature difference).

> **Necessary but not sufficient:** A quasi-static process can still be irreversible if, for example, gas expands quasi-statically against a piston with friction. Hence quasi-static is required, but absence of dissipative effects is also needed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done by an ideal gas during a **polytropic process** $PV^{n} = \text{constant}$. Hence deduce the work done in an **adiabatic process** as a special case. *[7 Marks]*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Path equation:**

$$PV^{n} = C \quad\text{(constant)}$$

**Step 2 — Work integral:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C \cdot V^{-n}\,dV$$

**Step 3 — Integration:**

$$W = C \left[\frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{C}{1-n}\left(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use $C = PV^{n}$ to get $C \cdot V^{1-n} = PV$:**

$$W = \frac{1}{1-n}\left(P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1\right) = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

**Step 5 — Ideal gas substitution** $P_1 V_1 = nRT_1$ and $P_2 V_2 = nRT_2$:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{nR(T_1 - T_2)}{n-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Adiabatic case** ($n = \gamma$):

$$W_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{nR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$$

(For $n = 1$, the formula is indeterminate; the limit recovers the isothermal case $W = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ using L'Hôpital's rule.)

---

**(b)** Two kilograms of air initially at $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$ and $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$ are compressed **isentropically** (reversible adiabatically) to one-eighth of the original volume. Calculate the final temperature, final pressure, and work done. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. *[7 Marks]*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 2 \text{ kg}$, $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$, $V_2 = V_1/8$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 1 — Final temperature using $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = 300 \times (8)^{0.4}$$

Compute $8^{0.4}$: $\ln(8^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times 2.0794 = 0.8318 \Rightarrow 8^{0.4} = 2.2974$.

$$T_2 = 300 \times 2.2974 = 689.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Final pressure using $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = 100 \times (8)^{1.4}$$

$8^{1.4} = 8 \times 8^{0.4} = 8 \times 2.2974 = 18.379$.

$$P_2 = 100 \times 18.379 = 1837.9 \text{ kPa} \approx 18.38 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Work done (adiabatic):**

$$W = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{2 \times 0.287 \times (300 - 689.2)}{1.4 - 1}$$

$$W = \frac{0.574 \times (-389.2)}{0.4} = \frac{-223.4}{0.4} = -558.5 \text{ kJ}$$

**Result:** The negative sign (work done *on* the gas) confirms compression. $W_{\text{on gas}} = 558.5 \text{ kJ}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare **isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, and adiabatic** processes of an ideal gas in tabular form with respect to path equation, work done, heat transfer, change in internal energy, and P–V curve shape. *[7 Marks]*

**Model Solution (Tabular Comparison):**

| Feature | Isothermal | Isobaric | Isochoric | Adiabatic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Path Eq.** | $PV = C$ | $P = C$ | $V = C$ | $PV^{\gamma} = C$ |
| **Work W** | $nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ |
| **Heat Q** | $W$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $0$ |
| **ΔU** | $0$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_v(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **ΔH** | $0$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $nC_p(T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **P–V Shape** | Rectangular hyperbola | Horizontal line | Vertical line | Steeper hyperbola |

**Key observation:** Among the four, the **adiabatic curve is always steeper** than the isothermal curve on a P–V plot, because $\gamma > 1$.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **quasi-static process**. A gas in a piston-cylinder assembly expands from $0.01 \text{ m}^3$ to $0.05 \text{ m}^3$ at a constant pressure of $200 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate the work done and the change in internal energy if the gas absorbs $30 \text{ kJ}$ of heat. *[7 Marks]*

**Model Solution:**

**Conceptual explanation (3 marks):**

A **quasi-static process** is one in which the system departs from equilibrium by only an infinitesimal amount at any instant. The system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states, allowing the system pressure to be well-defined and uniform throughout. It is the **limiting case** of an actual process when driving forces (pressure or temperature differences) tend to zero. Quasi-static processes are idealisations that approximate well-engineered processes like slow piston movement or a throttled valve flow.

**Numerical solution (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Work done** (isobaric):

$$W = P(V_2 - V_1) = 200 \times (0.05 - 0.01) = 200 \times 0.04 = 8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law** (sign convention: W is work done *by* the system):

$$Q = \Delta U + W \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta U = Q - W$$

$$\Delta U = 30 - 8 = 22 \text{ kJ}$$

**Result:** $W = 8 \text{ kJ}$ (expansion work, positive); $\Delta U = 22 \text{ kJ}$ (internal energy increases because more heat is absorbed than work done).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Sign convention trap (5–7 marks lost routinely):** KTU textbooks follow the **engineering sign convention** where **W is work done BY the system** (positive for expansion). Some students mistakenly use the physics convention. Always state your sign convention at the start of the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Pressure in **kPa**, Volume in **m³** gives Work in **kJ**. Mixing **bar** with **m³** or **Pa** with **litres** is the #1 numerical error in KTU answer scripts.
> 3. **Using the wrong specific heat:** $C_p$ and $C_v$ must be consistent with the units of R. If $R$ is in kJ/(kg·K), then $C_p$ and $C_v$ must also be in kJ/(kg·K), **not** kJ/(kmol·K).
> 4. **Indeterminate form at n = 1:** The polytropic work formula $W = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(n-1)$ blows up at $n = 1$. You must **explicitly state** that the isothermal case is the limit, recoverable via L'Hôpital's rule.
> 5. **Confusing adiabatic with isentropic:** An **adiabatic** process has $Q = 0$. An **isentropic** process is a **reversible adiabatic**, i.e., $dS = 0$. All isentropic processes are adiabatic, but not vice versa. This is a **favourite 1-mark trap question** in KTU exams.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Topic: Process**
> 
> - [ ] A **process** is a path joining two equilibrium states on a property diagram.
> - [ ] **State functions** (U, H, S, T, P, V) are path-independent. **Path functions** (Q, W) are path-dependent.
> - [ ] **Isothermal:** $T = \text{const}$, $\Delta U = 0$, $\Delta H = 0$, $W = Q = nRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
> - [ ] **Adiabatic:** $Q = 0$, $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $W = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(\gamma - 1)$.
> - [ ] **Isobaric:** $P = \text{const}$, $W = P \Delta V = nR \Delta T$, $Q = nC_p \Delta T$.
> - [ ] **Isochoric:** $V = \text{const}$, $W = 0$, $Q = \Delta U = nC_v \Delta T$.
> - [ ] **Polytropic:** $PV^{n} = \text{const}$, $W = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(n-1)$ for $n \neq 1$.
> - [ ] **Cyclic:** $\oint dU = 0 \Rightarrow Q_{net} = W_{net} = \oint P\,dV$.
> - [ ] **Quasi-static** ≠ **Reversible** in general. Quasi-static + no dissipation = Reversible.
> - [ ] On a **P–V diagram**, adiabatic curves are **steeper** than isothermal curves.
> - [ ] **Mayer's relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$.
> - [ ] **Air values to memorise:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
> - [ ] **Engineering link:** Steam power → isobaric + isentropic; IC engines → isochoric + adiabatic; Refrigeration → isentropic + isobaric + throttling.
> - [ ] Always **declare your sign convention** for Q and W at the top of any numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermodynamic Cycle** is a defined series of thermodynamic processes in which a working fluid (system) returns to its initial state at the end of the sequence. Since the system returns to its initial state, all the **thermodynamic properties** (such as pressure, volume, temperature, internal energy, and entropy) undergo no net change over the complete cycle.

Mathematically, for any extensive property $X$ of the system:

$$\oint dX = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a thermodynamic cycle, the net change in internal energy $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$, and consequently, by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, the net heat transfer equals the net work transfer: $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$.

### 1.2 Classification of Thermodynamic Cycles

| Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Based on Execution** | Ideal (Reversible) Cycle | Actual (Irreversible) Cycle |
| **Based on Working Direction** | Power Cycle (Clockwise on P-V) | Refrigeration / Heat Pump Cycle (Anti-clockwise on P-V) |
| **Based on Heat Exchange** | Heat Engine Cycle | Reversed Heat Engine Cycle |
| **Based on Fluid Phase** | Vapor Cycle (e.g., Rankine) | Gas Cycle (e.g., Otto, Diesel, Brayton) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Cycle in Nature:**
> Think of the natural water cycle on Earth. Water evaporates from the ocean (absorbs heat), rises to form clouds, condenses into rain (releases heat), and falls back into the ocean. The water returns to its **exact same initial state** (same ocean, same chemical composition) but the surrounding environment has gained or lost energy in the process.
>
> Similarly, in a **thermodynamic cycle**, the working fluid comes back to its starting point, but **energy in the form of work and heat** has been transferred between the system and surroundings. The "purpose" of the cycle is to continuously convert heat into work (engine) or work into heat pumping (refrigerator).

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

A cycle is evaluated by two primary non-dimensional performance parameters:

* **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)** — For power-producing cycles (heat engines).
* **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — For refrigeration and heat pump cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Acceptance:** All thermal efficiencies are bounded by the **Carnot Efficiency** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$, where temperatures must be in **Kelvin (K)**. This is the maximum theoretical efficiency for any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: How a Cycle Works

A thermodynamic cycle operates through the following structured sequence:

1. **Process Initiation:** The working fluid starts at a defined initial state (State 1) with known properties $P_1, V_1, T_1$.
2. **Sequential Processes:** The fluid undergoes a series of processes (isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, isentropic, or polytropic) connecting State 1 → State 2 → State 3 → ... → State n.
3. **State Closure:** The final state is identical to the initial state. All property changes integrate to zero.
4. **Energy Accounting:** The First Law ensures the energy bookkeeping is closed: $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{net}$.
5. **Direction Determines Purpose:** Clockwise traversal on a P-V diagram produces net positive work (power cycle). Anti-clockwise traversal consumes net work (refrigeration cycle).

### 2.2 Classification Details

**A. Power Cycles (Heat Engines):**
* Convert heat input ($Q_{in}$) from a high-temperature source into net work output ($W_{net}$).
* Examples: Carnot cycle, Otto cycle (petrol engine), Diesel cycle (CI engine), Brayton cycle (gas turbine), Rankine cycle (steam power plant).
* Direction: **Clockwise** on P-V and T-s diagrams.

**B. Refrigeration and Heat Pump Cycles:**
* Use net work input ($W_{net}$) to transfer heat from a low-temperature body to a high-temperature body.
* Direction: **Counter-clockwise** (anti-clockwise) on P-V and T-s diagrams.
* Examples: Vapor compression cycle, vapor absorption cycle, air refrigeration cycle.

**C. Reversible vs. Irreversible Cycles:**
* **Reversible Cycle:** Composed entirely of quasi-static, frictionless processes with no heat transfer across finite temperature differences. Yields maximum possible efficiency.
* **Irreversible Cycle:** Involves friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, or finite temperature gradients. All real cycles are irreversible to some degree.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert instead of \vert in table cells to prevent markdown breakage.** All temperatures are in absolute units (**Kelvin**).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Applicable To | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Net Work of Cycle | $W_{net} = \oint \delta W = \oint \delta Q$ | All Cycles | J or kJ |
| 2 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Power Cycle | Dimensionless |
| 3 | COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Refrigeration Cycle | Dimensionless |
| 4 | COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Heat Pump Cycle | Dimensionless |
| 5 | COP Relationship | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Both Refrigeration/HP | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Carnot (Heat Engine) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Reversible Heat Engine | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Carnot Refrigerator | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible Refrigeration | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Carnot Heat Pump | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible Heat Pump | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Energy Closure | $\oint dU = 0 \Rightarrow Q_{net} = W_{net}$ | All Cycles | J or kJ |
| 10 | PMM-II Violation | $\eta_{th} > \eta_{Carnot}$ is impossible | Kelvin-Planck Statement | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Power Generation:** Steam power plants (Rankine) and nuclear plants use cycles to generate electricity.
* **Transportation:** Internal combustion engines (Otto and Diesel cycles) power automobiles, trucks, ships, and aircraft.
* **Aviation:** Gas turbines (Brayton cycle) power jet engines for commercial and military aircraft.
* **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning:** Vapor compression cycles enable domestic refrigerators, deep freezers, and central AC systems.
* **Cryogenics & Liquefaction:** Specialized reversed cycles (e.g., Linde-Hampson) liquefy gases like nitrogen, oxygen, and helium.
* **Heat Pumps for Heating:** Geothermal and air-source heat pumps use reversed cycles to heat buildings efficiently.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Work of a Thermodynamic Cycle from First Law

**Statement to Prove:** For any thermodynamic cycle, the net work done equals the net heat transfer.

**Starting Point — The First Law for a Closed System:**

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$

**Step 1:** Integrate both sides over the entire cycle (a closed loop integral):

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint dU + \oint \delta W$$

*Logic:* The integral $\oint$ denotes summation over the entire cycle from State 1 back to State 1.

**Step 2:** Evaluate the internal energy term. Since $U$ is a point function (state property), its cyclic integral must vanish:

$$\oint dU = 0$$

*Logic:* Internal energy depends only on the state, not the path. Going around a complete cycle means returning to the exact same state, so the net change is zero.

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:

$$\oint \delta Q = 0 + \oint \delta W$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ are path functions (their cyclic integrals are generally **non-zero**):

$$\boxed{W_{net} = Q_{net} = \oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W}$$

*Conclusion:* The net work output of a cycle equals the net heat input. This is the foundational energy balance of all cyclic devices.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between COP of Heat Pump and Refrigerator

**Given:**
* $Q_H$ = Heat rejected to high-temperature reservoir
* $Q_L$ = Heat absorbed from low-temperature reservoir
* $W_{net}$ = Net work input to the cycle

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law energy balance to the cycle (heat engine sign convention, magnitudes):

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

*Logic:* Energy leaving the high-temperature reservoir (input) must equal the sum of energy gained at the low-temperature reservoir (output) plus the work extracted, because energy is conserved.

**Step 2:** Rearrange to express net work:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3:** Write the definitions of the two COPs:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract $COP_R$ from $COP_{HP}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \\[6pt]
&= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude the relationship:

$$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$

*Conclusion:* A heat pump's COP always exceeds a refrigerator's COP by exactly **1** when both operate between the same two thermal reservoirs. This is a frequently tested 14-mark derivation in KTU ESE.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum (Carnot) Thermal Efficiency

**Premise:** A reversible cycle operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$ yields the maximum possible thermal efficiency.

**Step 1:** Write thermal efficiency in terms of heat:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2:** For a **reversible** cycle, apply the Clausius equality:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

*Logic:* This means the cyclic integral of heat divided by absolute temperature is zero for a completely reversible cycle.

**Step 3:** For an isothermal heat exchange with the reservoirs (Carnot assumption):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

*Logic:* During isothermal heat transfer from a reservoir at constant temperature $T$, $\int \delta Q_{rev}/T = Q/T$.

**Step 4:** Rearrange the equality:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the efficiency expression:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}}$$

*Conclusion:* The Carnot efficiency depends **only** on the reservoir temperatures and is the upper bound for any heat engine cycle. Increasing $T_H$ or decreasing $T_L$ improves efficiency, but $T_L$ cannot be reduced below the ambient (≈ 300 K) for practical terrestrial applications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A power cycle receives **2000 kJ** of heat from a high-temperature source and rejects **1200 kJ** to a low-temperature sink. Calculate: (a) the net work output, (b) the thermal efficiency, and (c) the COP if the cycle is reversed to act as a refrigerator.

**Solution:**

**(a) Net Work Output:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= Q_{in} - Q_{out} \\[4pt]
&= 2000 - 1200 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{800 \ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{800}{2000} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.40 \ or \ 40\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) COP as a Refrigerator (Reversed Cycle):**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{1200}{800} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{1.5}
\end{aligned}$$

*Verification using the relationship:* $COP_{HP} = 1.5 + 1 = 2.5$ (i.e., the same hardware delivers **2.5 kJ** of heating per **1 kJ** of work when used as a heat pump).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Thermodynamic Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermodynamic Cycle] --> B[Reversible Ideal Cycle]
    A --> C[Irreversible Actual Cycle]
    A --> D[By Direction of Execution]
    B --> B1[Carnot Cycle]
    B --> B2[Otto Cycle]
    B --> B3[Diesel Cycle]
    B --> B4[Rankine Cycle]
    B --> B5[Brayton Cycle]
    C --> C1[Actual Otto Engine]
    C --> C2[Actual Steam Plant]
    D --> D1[Power Cycle Clockwise on PV]
    D --> D2[Refrigeration Cycle Counter Clockwise on PV]
    D1 --> E1[Heat Engine]
    D2 --> E2[Refrigerator]
    D2 --> E3[Heat Pump]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Heat Engine (Power Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TH[High Temperature Reservoir TH] -- QIN Heat Input --> HE[Heat Engine Cycle]
    HE -- WNET Net Work Output --> OUT[Shaft Work to Load]
    HE -- QOUT Heat Rejected --> TL[Low Temperature Reservoir TL]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Refrigerator and Heat Pump (Reversed Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TL[Low Temperature Reservoir TL] -- QL Heat Extracted --> RC[Reversed Cycle Refrigerator]
    IN[Work Input WNET] --> RC
    RC -- QH Heat Delivered --> TH[High Temperature Reservoir TH]
    TH --- NOTE[Same hardware different purpose]
    TH --> HP[Heat Pump Mode Heating]
    IN --> HP
    TL -. QL Heat from outside cold air .-> HP
```

### 4.4 P-V and T-S Schematic Representation (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PVCYC[P-V Diagram Representation]
        P1[State 1 PV] --> P2[Process 1 to 2 Expansion]
        P2 --> P3[State 3]
        P3 --> P4[Process 3 to 4 Compression]
        P4 --> P1
    end
    subgraph ENOTE[Direction Note]
        EN1[Clockwise traversal on P-V means Power Cycle]
        EN2[Counter Clockwise traversal on P-V means Refrigeration Cycle]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** In any cycle diagram, the **enclosed area** represents the **net work transfer** of the cycle. The direction of traversal (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise) determines whether the cycle is producing work (engine) or consuming work (refrigeration).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**Define a thermodynamic cycle. Why is it said that the change in internal energy over a complete cycle is zero?**

**Model Answer:**

A thermodynamic cycle is a sequence of processes in which a system returns to its initial state, undergoing a series of state changes such that all properties (P, V, T, U, H, S) resume their original values.

Since internal energy $U$ is a **point function** (state property) and depends only on the state of the system, the change in $U$ between the same initial and final state must be zero:

$$\oint dU = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta U_{cycle} = 0$$

This is the foundation for applying the First Law to cycles: $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**State the relationship between the COP of a refrigerator and the COP of a heat pump operating between the same temperature limits.**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is:

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$

This arises from the First Law applied to the reversed cycle. Numerically, a heat pump's COP is always greater than a refrigerator's COP by exactly 1, because the heat pump delivers **both** the absorbed low-temperature heat **and** the work input as useful heating at the high-temperature side.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Carnot heat engine operates between a source at **800 K** and a sink at **300 K**. If the engine receives **1000 kJ** of heat, calculate: (i) thermal efficiency, (ii) net work output, and (iii) heat rejected to the sink. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Carnot Efficiency:**

*Assumptions:*
1. All processes are perfectly reversible.
2. Heat is transferred isothermally from constant-temperature reservoirs.
3. No friction, no heat loss, no leakage.

*Derivation (as shown in Section 3.3 above):*

Applying the Clausius equality for a reversible cycle:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

Substituting into the efficiency definition:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = Q_{in} = 1000$ kJ

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= 1 - \frac{300}{800} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.375 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.625 \ or \ 62.5\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Net Work Output:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_{in} \\[4pt]
&= 0.625 \times 1000 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{625 \ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Sink:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{out} &= Q_{in} - W_{net} \\[4pt]
&= 1000 - 625 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{375 \ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $Q_{out}/Q_{in} = 375/1000 = 0.375 = T_L/T_H$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a refrigerator and a heat pump. Derive the relationship between their COPs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reversed Carnot cycle is used as a refrigerator. It removes **500 kJ/min** of heat from a cold space at **-10 °C** and rejects heat to surroundings at **35 °C**. Calculate: (i) the COP of the refrigerator, (ii) the power required to drive the refrigerator in kW. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and COP Relationship:**

*Block Diagram:* (See Section 4.3 for Mermaid version.)

A refrigerator absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature space at $T_L$ when work $W_{net}$ is supplied, and rejects heat $Q_H$ to a high-temperature space at $T_H$.

*Energy Balance:*

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

*COP Definitions:*

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

*Derivation:*

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{HP} - COP_R &= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = 1 \\[4pt]
\therefore COP_{HP} &= COP_R + 1
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given (after unit conversion):**
* $Q_L = 500$ kJ/min $= 500/60 = 8.333$ kW
* $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263$ K
* $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308$ K

**(i) COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{263}{308 - 263} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{263}{45} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{5.844}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Power Required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8.333}{5.844} \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{1.426 \ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error:** Forgetting to convert °C to **Kelvin** before applying Carnot formulas. Marks deducted heavily (typically 2-3 marks lost per error).
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Mixing up $Q_H$ and $Q_L$. Always remember: in a refrigerator, $Q_L$ is the **desired** heat removal (numerator of COP).
> 3. **Missing the First Law Statement:** Students often write COP formulas without establishing the energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ first. Always lead with the energy balance.
> 4. **Confusing η with COP:** Thermal efficiency applies only to heat engines. COP applies only to refrigerators/heat pumps. Mixing them is a critical error.
> 5. **Incomplete Derivations:** Writing only the final formula in a 7-mark derivation sub-part without showing each step typically results in **3-4 marks deducted**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **thermodynamic cycle** returns the system to its initial state, so $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$ and $Q_{net} = W_{net}$.
- **Power cycles** run clockwise on P-V and T-s diagrams; **refrigeration/heat pump cycles** run counter-clockwise.
- **Thermal efficiency** $\eta_{th} = W_{net}/Q_{in}$ applies to heat engines only and is always less than 1.
- **COP of refrigerator** $COP_R = Q_L/W_{net}$ can be greater than 1 — it is not an efficiency.
- **COP of heat pump** $COP_{HP} = Q_H/W_{net} = COP_R + 1$ — this is a frequently asked 7-mark derivation.
- **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ is the **upper limit** of any heat engine between two given reservoirs.
- **Carnot refrigerator COP** $= T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ is the **maximum possible** COP for any refrigerator between those reservoirs.
- All temperatures in Carnot formulas **must be in Kelvin (K)** — this is a top-valuation pitfall.
- **Reversible cycles** are ideal benchmarks; **real cycles** are always irreversible and have lower performance.
- **PMM-II (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind)** violates the Second Law and is impossible — Carnot efficiency sets the natural ceiling.
- The **enclosed area** of a cycle on a P-V diagram equals the **net work** of the cycle.
- Always **state the assumptions** explicitly before any cycle derivation (reversibility, steady-state, no kinetic/potential energy changes).
- For KTU Module 1, focus on **conceptual clarity** and **derivation steps**, as derivation-based questions are heavily weighted (7-14 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic Equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamic Equilibrium ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thermodynamic Equilibrium** is the axiomatic state of a thermodynamic system in which every macroscopic property (state variable) is *spatially uniform, time-invariant, and uninfluenced by any net flow of mass, momentum, or energy* across its boundary. Formally, a system is in thermodynamic equilibrium if and only if it simultaneously satisfies **Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, and Phase Equilibrium** — and also remains free of unbalanced potentials. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **state of equilibrium** is the *reference condition* against which all thermodynamic processes (quasi-static, reversible, irreversible, cyclic) are defined. Any system that is not in this state will spontaneously tend toward it (governed by the **Second Law** and the principle of entropy maximization for isolated systems). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Still Pond" Model > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a perfectly still, deep pond.** > - **Mechanical Equilibrium** → the surface is level; there is no slope to cause water to rush from one point to another. > - **Thermal Equilibrium** → the water temperature is the same at the surface and the bottom; no heat "currents" exist. > - **Chemical Equilibrium** → the dissolved minerals are uniformly distributed; there is no tendency for any chemical reaction to proceed further. > - **Phase Equilibrium** → if there is floating ice, the rate of melting equals the rate of freezing — a *dynamic* balance where nothing *macroscopically* changes. > > Drop a stone (disturbance) → the pond responds, but eventually returns to **equilibrium** (the still-pond state). This *return tendency* is the essence of equilibrium in thermodynamics. --- ### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Thermodynamic Equilibrium A system is in **complete thermodynamic equilibrium** only when all of the following are simultaneously satisfied: 1. **Mechanical Equilibrium** — No unbalanced forces or pressures within the system. The pressure gradient $dP/dx = 0$ everywhere. 2. **Thermal Equilibrium** — No temperature gradients. $dT/dx = 0$ everywhere. Governed by the **Zeroth Law**. 3. **Chemical Equilibrium** — No chemical potential gradients. $\mu_i = \text{constant}$ for each species $i$. 4. **Phase Equilibrium** — For multi-phase systems, the mass of each phase remains constant (rate of phase change in one direction = rate in the opposite direction). ### 1.4 Key Empirical Constants & Benchmarks | Benchmark State | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Triple Point of Water** | **T = 273.16 K, P = 611.657 Pa** | Definitive thermodynamic fixed point | | **Standard Atmosphere** | **P = 101.325 kPa** | Reference pressure for gas tables | | **Universal Gas Constant** | **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in ideal gas law | > [!TIP] > The **triple point of water (273.16 K)** is the *single fixed point* used to define the **Kelvin thermodynamic temperature scale** (since 1954, by international convention). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Equilibrium is Established A thermodynamic system moves toward equilibrium through a hierarchy of relaxation processes, each operating on a different timescale: - **Step 1 — Mechanical Relaxation** (fastest, $\sim 10^{-9}\,$s): Pressure waves equalize any pressure differences. Governed by Newton's laws of motion applied to fluid elements. - **Step 2 — Thermal Relaxation** ($\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{0}\,$s): Heat conduction and convection equalize temperature differences. Governed by Fourier's law: $\dot{Q} = -kA \, dT/dx$. - **Step 3 — Chemical Relaxation** (slowest, $\sim 10^{0}$ to $10^{6}\,$s): Diffusion and reaction kinetics equalize chemical potentials. Governed by Fick's law and Arrhenius kinetics. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this hierarchy matter in KTU problems?** > In many exam questions, you are told a system is in *mechanical and thermal* equilibrium but not chemical equilibrium. Recognizing which type of equilibrium is **NOT** satisfied tells you which direction the spontaneous process will proceed. --- ### 2.2 The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics (Foundation of Equilibrium) > [!NOTE] > **Statement:** If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$T_A = T_C \quad \text{and} \quad T_B = T_C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_A = T_B$$ This is the *empirical justification* for the existence of a **temperature function** $T$ as a state property.
