Without the Zeroth Law, the very concept of "thermal equilibrium" would be undefined. --- ### 2.3 Equilibrium vs. Steady State vs. Quasi-Equilibrium | Concept | Definition | Distinguishing Feature | |---|---|---| | **Equilibrium State** | No change in any macroscopic property with time; *all* potentials balanced | $\partial \xi / \partial t = 0$ for *all* properties $\xi$ | | **Steady State** | No change in properties *at a given point* with time, but flow may occur | $\partial \xi / \partial t = 0$ locally, but $\nabla \xi \neq 0$ | | **Quasi-Equilibrium (Quasi-Static) Process** | A process that proceeds through a *continuous sequence* of equilibrium states | Infinitesimally slow; system remains "at equilibrium" at every instant | > [!WARNING] > **A steady state is NOT an equilibrium state.** A wire with constant temperature gradient (steady heat conduction) is in a steady state but not in equilibrium, because heat *is* flowing. This is a frequent KTU trick question! --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta U = 0$ for isolated system at equilibrium | Internal energy minimum | Stability analysis | | 2 | $\Delta S \ge 0$ (isolated system) | Entropy maximized at equilibrium | Direction of spontaneous change | | 3 | $T_A = T_B$ | Thermal equilibrium condition | Zeroth Law applications | | 4 | $P_A = P_B$ | Mechanical equilibrium condition | Hydrostatic problems | | 5 | $\mu_i^A = \mu_i^B$ | Chemical equilibrium condition | Phase change problems | | 6 | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ | Hydrostatic pressure variation | Mechanical equilibrium in fluids | | 7 | $PV = mRT$ | Ideal gas equilibrium equation of state | Closed-system problems | | 8 | $T \cdot ds = du + P \cdot dv$ | Tds equation (equilibrium form) | Property relations | | 9 | $A = U - TS$ | Helmholtz function; minimized at equilibrium (T, V constant) | Stability & spontaneity | | 10 | $G = H - TS$ | Gibbs function; minimized at equilibrium (T, P constant) | Phase & reaction equilibrium | > [!NOTE] > The **Helmholtz function $A$** and **Gibbs function $G$** are *fundamental equilibrium potentials* — they are at extremum (minimum) when a system is in equilibrium at constant $(T,V)$ or $(T,P)$ conditions respectively. This is a high-yield KTU concept. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Plant Boilers:** Water-steam equilibrium determines the saturation pressure at a given temperature — critical for boiler design and safety. - **Refrigeration Cycles:** Two-phase refrigerant equilibrium in the evaporator and condenser governs heat absorption/release. - **Chemical Reactors:** Equilibrium constants determine *maximum conversion* of reactants to products. - **Materials Science:** Phase equilibrium diagrams (Fe-C, Al-Si) predict microstructure and mechanical properties of alloys. - **Atmospheric Science:** Hydrostatic equilibrium of the atmosphere ($dP/dz = -\rho g$) governs pressure-altitude relations. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The State Postulate from Equilibrium **Starting Premise:** A system in thermodynamic equilibrium is fully described by a finite set of independent macroscopic properties. We wish to *count* how many are needed. --- **Step 1 — Identify the types of properties** A system is characterized by **extensive properties** (proportional to mass: $U, V, S, m$) and **intensive properties** (independent of mass: $T, P, \rho$). **Step 2 — Reduce using the "divide by mass" trick** Since any extensive property $\Phi$ can be written as $\Phi = m \cdot \phi$ where $\phi$ is its specific (intensive) form, *intensive properties alone suffice* to characterize the equilibrium state of a pure substance of given mass. **Step 3 — Apply the Gibbs Phase Rule** For a simple compressible system (pure substance, no chemical reactions, no surface or magnetic effects): $$\boxed{F = C - P + 2}$$ Where: - $F$ = degrees of freedom (independent intensive properties needed to fix the state) - $C$ = number of components - $P$ = number of phases **Step 4 — Specialize to a single-component, single-phase system** $$F = 1 - 1 + 2 = 2$$ **Interpretation:** Two independent intensive properties (e.g., $T$ and $P$, or $T$ and $v$, or $P$ and $v$) are sufficient to fully fix the equilibrium state of a pure, single-phase system. > [!NOTE] > This result is the **State Postulate** — the reason all standard thermodynamic tables (Steam Tables, Refrigerant Tables) use two-column entries (e.g., $T$ and $P$ → look up $v, u, h, s$). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Hydrostatic Mechanical Equilibrium **Step 1 — Free-Body Analysis of a Fluid Element** Consider a vertical fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ in a gravitational field $g$. Take a thin slice of thickness $dz$ at height $z$. **Step 2 — Force Balance** Pressure at the bottom of the slice pushes up: $+P(z) \cdot A$. Pressure at the top pushes down: $-P(z + dz) \cdot A$. Weight of the slice acts downward: $-\rho \cdot A \cdot dz \cdot g$. For **mechanical equilibrium** (no acceleration): $$P(z) \cdot A - P(z+dz) \cdot A - \rho g A \cdot dz = 0$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange** $$P(z+dz) - P(z) = -\rho g \, dz$$ **Step 4 — Take the limit as $dz \to 0$** $$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$ This is the **hydrostatic equation** — the differential statement of mechanical equilibrium in a gravity field. **Step 5 — Integrate for an incompressible fluid ($\rho = \text{const}$)** $$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz$$ $$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 6 — Express pressure difference as a height of fluid** $$\Delta P = \rho g \Delta z$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the prototype for many KTU barometer/manometer problems. For mercury ($\rho \approx 13{,}600 \,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$), a 1 mm column gives $\Delta P \approx 133.3\,\text{Pa}$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Equilibrium vs. Distinguishing Diagnostic | Observation in a System | Inferred State | Justification | |---|---|---| | $P$ constant everywhere; $T$ constant everywhere; $\rho$ constant; no mass flow | **Full thermodynamic equilibrium** | All four pillars satisfied | | $P$ uniform but $T$ varies with position | **Mechanical equilibrium only** | Pressure-balanced but heat conduction still occurring | | $T$ uniform but $P$ varies (e.g., hydrostatic fluid) | **Thermal equilibrium only** | Temperature equalized but pressure gradient sustains mechanical potential | | Mass of liquid decreases while vapor mass increases (at constant $T$, $P$) | **Phase equilibrium not yet established** | Net phase change still occurring; not at saturation | | Heat supplied at constant rate; temperature of source and sink constant with time | **Steady state** (not equilibrium) | Heat flow present, but temperatures unchanging | | Process so slow that system passes through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states | **Quasi-static (quasi-equilibrium) process** | Each intermediate step is an equilibrium state | | $\Delta S_{\text{isolated}} > 0$ | **Not at equilibrium** (still evolving) | Spontaneous change continues until $S$ is maximized | | $\Delta S_{\text{isolated}} = 0$ AND no net flows | **At equilibrium** | Maximum entropy; no driving force remains | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Identifying Equilibrium Type **Problem:** A sealed rigid container holds a mixture of liquid water and water vapor at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$. Heat is supplied slowly. State remains at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $101.325\,\text{kPa}$ while the liquid level slowly drops. Discuss the equilibrium. **Solution Walkthrough:** **Step 1 — Identify the state of each phase.** Liquid water at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ has saturation pressure $P_{\text{sat}} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$. The system pressure equals $P_{\text{sat}}$, so the vapor is **saturated**. **Step 2 — Check Mechanical Equilibrium.** The container is rigid and the pressure is uniform at $101.325\,\text{kPa}$. ✓ **Mechanical equilibrium holds.** **Step 3 — Check Thermal Equilibrium.** The temperature is uniform at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ throughout (assumed, since heat is supplied slowly and uniformly). ✓ **Thermal equilibrium holds.** **Step 4 — Check Phase Equilibrium.** Although the system satisfies $T = T_{\text{sat}}$ and $P = P_{\text{sat}}(T)$, the *masses of the two phases are changing* (liquid is vaporizing). Therefore, **phase equilibrium is NOT yet established** — the system is in a *transient* two-phase state. A true two-phase equilibrium requires the masses of both phases to be **constant in time**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** A two-phase, two-component system at saturation *temperature and pressure* is in *phase equilibrium* **only if** the mass of each phase is unchanging. The mere existence of a flat $P$–$T$ vapor-liquid interface does not, by itself, guarantee phase equilibrium if mass is still transferring between phases. --- ### 3.5 Stability Condition — The Second Law Statement For a system in *isolated* equilibrium, the entropy is at a *maximum*. Expanding a small perturbation $\delta S$: $$\delta S = 0 \quad \text{(first-order condition)}$$ $$\delta^2 S < 0 \quad \text{(stability condition — incorrect sign for maximum!)}$$ **Corrected Stability Form:** For a maximum, the second variation must be **negative**: $$\delta^2 S < 0 \quad \text{at equilibrium (isolated system)}$$ This implies that the heat capacity $C_v > 0$ and the compressibility $\kappa_T > 0$ — these are the classical **stability criteria** for thermal and mechanical equilibrium respectively. > [!WARNING] > A negative $C_v$ or $\kappa_T$ would imply that the system is *unstable* — any infinitesimal perturbation grows. The fact that all real substances have $C_v > 0$ and $\kappa_T > 0$ at equilibrium is the empirical foundation of thermodynamic stability. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Four Pillars of Thermodynamic Equilibrium ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([Thermodynamic System]) --> Q1{Is pressure uniform
throughout?} Q1 -- Yes --> Mech[Mechanical Equilibrium] Q1 -- No --> MechNo[Pressure gradient exists
System NOT in mech. eq.] Start --> Q2{Is temperature uniform
throughout?} Q2 -- Yes --> Therm[Thermal Equilibrium] Q2 -- No --> ThermNo[Temperature gradient exists
System NOT in thermal eq.] Start --> Q3{Is chemical potential
constant for all species?} Q3 -- Yes --> Chem[Chemical Equilibrium] Q3 -- No --> ChemNo[Reaction or diffusion
still occurring] Start --> Q4{Is mass of each phase
constant in time?} Q4 -- Yes --> Phase[Phase Equilibrium] Q4 -- No --> PhaseNo[Net phase change
still occurring] Mech --> AND1{AND} Therm --> AND1 Chem --> AND1 Phase --> AND1 MechNo --> AND1 ThermNo --> AND1 ChemNo --> AND1 PhaseNo --> AND1 AND1 -- All four YES --> Final([COMPLETE THERMODYNAMIC
EQUILIBRIUM]) AND1 -- Any one NO --> Partial([PARTIAL EQUILIBRIUM
Spontaneous change continues]) ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Equilibrium Hierarchy in Real Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Fast["Fastest Relaxation ~10⁻⁹ s"] A[Mechanical Equilibrium
Pressure equalization
Sound waves] end subgraph Medium["Medium Relaxation ~10⁻³ s"] B[Thermal Equilibrium
Heat conduction
Fourier's law] end subgraph Slow["Slowest Relaxation ~10⁰ s"] C[Chemical Equilibrium
Diffusion and reactions
Arrhenius kinetics] end Disturb[External Disturbance] --> A A --> B B --> C C --> Eq([Full Thermodynamic
Equilibrium State]) ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for KTU Equilibrium Problems ```mermaid flowchart TD Input([Problem Statement]) --> Check1{Process mentioned
or implied?} Check1 -- Quasi-static --> Q1[Identify intermediate states
all must be equilibrium] Check1 -- Cyclic --> Q2[Net change in properties
over cycle is zero] Check1 -- Isolated --> Q3[Entropy maximized
dS greater or equal 0] Check1 -- Open system --> Q4[Mass and energy cross
boundary in steady state] Q1 --> Solve([Apply State Postulate
and property tables]) Q2 --> Solve Q3 --> Solve Q4 --> Solve ``` --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Equilibrium Verification Module ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["INPUT DATA BLOCK"] S1[System Boundary Definition] S2[Property Measurements: T, P, V, m, n] S3[Time Duration of Observation] end Input --> Proc1{Gradient Check} Proc1 --> dP[Compute dP/dx, dP/dy, dP/dz] Proc1 --> dT[Compute dT/dx, dT/y, dT/z] Proc1 --> dMu[Compute chemical potential gradient] dP --> MechTest{P gradient
equals zero?} dT --> ThermTest{T gradient
equals zero?} dMu --> ChemTest{Mu gradient
equals zero?} MechTest -- Yes --> M1[Mechanical Eq. SATISFIED] MechTest -- No --> M2[Mechanical Eq. VIOLATED] ThermTest -- Yes --> T1[Thermal Eq. SATISFIED] ThermTest -- No --> T2[Thermal Eq. VIOLATED] ChemTest -- Yes --> C1[Chemical Eq. SATISFIED] ChemTest -- No --> C2[Chemical Eq. VIOLATED] M1 --> Final1{Final Verdict} T1 --> Final1 C1 --> Final1 M2 --> Final1 T2 --> Final1 C2 --> Final1 Final1 -- All three satisfied --> Out1([EQUILIBRIUM CONFIRMED]) Final1 -- Any violated --> Out2([EQUILIBRIUM NOT ESTABLISHED]) ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **"Define thermodynamic equilibrium. List the different types of equilibrium that a system must satisfy to be in complete thermodynamic equilibrium."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thermodynamic equilibrium is the state of a system in which all macroscopic properties are spatially uniform and time-invariant, with no net flow of mass, momentum, or energy across its boundary. A system is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium only when it simultaneously satisfies: 1. **Mechanical Equilibrium** — uniform pressure throughout the system. 2. **Thermal Equilibrium** — uniform temperature throughout the system. 3. **Chemical Equilibrium** — uniform chemical potential for each species, with no net reaction. 4. **Phase Equilibrium** — in multi-phase systems, mass of each phase remains constant. > **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1 mark) + 4 types listed (½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **"State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it called the 'Zeroth' law?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. **Why "Zeroth":** It was formulated *after* the First and Second Laws were already established, but it was realized to be a more *fundamental* prerequisite (it logically precedes the First Law). Rather than renumber the existing laws, it was designated the **Zeroth Law** (1 mark for naming significance). **Significance (1 mark):** The Zeroth Law justifies the existence of the **temperature function** $T$ as a state property. It allows construction of thermometers and definition of temperature scales (e.g., Kelvin scale based on the triple point of water). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Adaptation]** **a) Distinguish clearly between a 'steady state' and an 'equilibrium state' in thermodynamics. Provide one example of each. (7 marks)** **b) A sealed rigid container of volume $0.05\,\text{m}^3$ contains a mixture of saturated liquid water and saturated vapor at $100\,^\circ\text{C}$. The mass of liquid is $5\,\text{kg}$ and the mass of vapor is $0.5\,\text{kg}$. The container is now heated slowly and uniformly to $150\,^\circ\text{C}$. Discuss the equilibrium at the initial and final states. Given: At $150\,^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{\text{sat}} = 475.8\,\text{kPa}$, $v_f = 0.001091\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $v_g = 0.3924\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) Solution:** | Aspect | Equilibrium State | Steady State | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | No change in any macroscopic property with time; *no flows* across boundaries | Properties at a given location constant with time, but flows may exist | | **Mathematical form** | $\partial \xi / \partial t = 0$ for *all* $\xi$ AND $\nabla \xi = 0$ | $\partial \xi / \partial t = 0$ at fixed points, but $\nabla \xi$ may be nonzero | | **Flows** | None | Steady flows may exist | | **Example 1** | A sealed can of gas at uniform $T$ and $P$, left undisturbed for a long time | A hot metal rod with one end in boiling water, the other in ice water — after initial transient, the temperature distribution along the rod becomes time-invariant | | **Example 2** | A piston-cylinder with gas at rest, pressure uniform | A pipe with constant mass flow rate; velocity profile is time-invariant | > **Key distinguishing line:** A steady state *allows* gradients; an equilibrium state *requires* their absence. (1 mark for this clarity.) **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1 — Verify mechanical and thermal equilibrium at the initial state.** At $T = 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = P_{\text{sat}} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$, both liquid and vapor are at saturation. Pressure is uniform (mechanical equilibrium) and temperature is uniform (thermal equilibrium). **Step 2 — Verify phase equilibrium at the initial state.** For phase equilibrium, the *masses* of liquid and vapor must be constant in time. Since the system is sealed and isolated from external heat during the observation, the masses are constant → **phase equilibrium holds at the initial state.** ✓ (1 mark) **Step 3 — Compute specific volume of the mixture.** $$v = \frac{V}{m_{\text{total}}} = \frac{0.05}{5 + 0.5} = \frac{0.05}{5.5} = 0.00909\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Step 4 — Determine phase state at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$.** The specific volume of saturated liquid and vapor at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$: $$v_f = 0.001091\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.3924\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Since $v_f < v < v_g$ (i.e., $0.001091 < 0.00909 < 0.3924$), the system is a **saturated liquid-vapor mixture** at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$. (2 marks) **Step 5 — Compute the quality $x$ at the final state.** $$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_g - v_f} = \frac{0.00909 - 0.001091}{0.3924 - 0.001091}$$ $$x = \frac{0.00800}{0.3913} = 0.02045 \quad \text{or} \quad 2.045\%$$ **Step 6 — Discuss equilibrium at the final state.** The system reaches a new equilibrium at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $P = 475.8\,\text{kPa}$ once heating stops and the masses of both phases stabilize. All four pillars of equilibrium are satisfied: - Mechanical: $P$ uniform at $475.8\,\text{kPa}$ ✓ - Thermal: $T$ uniform at $150\,^\circ\text{C}$ ✓ - Phase: masses of liquid and vapor are now constant (quality = 2.045%) ✓ - Chemical: pure substance, no reactions ✓ (1 mark) **Conclusion:** The system is in a **two-phase saturated equilibrium** at both the initial and final states, but with the vapor mass fraction having increased (more liquid vaporized) due to the heating. (1 mark) > **Mark Distribution (Part b):** Initial equilibrium check (1) + Specific volume (1) + Phase state identification (2) + Quality calculation (2) + Final equilibrium discussion (1). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Adaptation]** **a) State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. With a neat schematic, explain how it leads to the concept of temperature. (7 marks)** **b) Derive the hydrostatic equation $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ from first principles. A U-tube manometer uses mercury (specific gravity = 13.6) to measure the pressure of a gas in a tank. If the mercury column shows a difference of $250\,\text{mm}$ and atmospheric pressure is $101.325\,\text{kPa}$, find the absolute pressure of the gas in $\text{kPa}$. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Statement of the Zeroth Law (2 marks):** If two systems $A$ and $B$ are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system $C$, then $A$ and $B$ are in thermal equilibrium with each other. **Step 2 — Schematic (describe or sketch):** ``` [System A] [System B] [System C] (T = T_A) ---heat?--- (T = T_C) ---heat?--- (T = T_B) no net no net flow flow Implication: T_A = T_C and T_B = T_C ⟹ T_A = T_B ``` **Step 3 — Concept of temperature (3 marks):** The Zeroth Law guarantees the existence of a *universal property* called **temperature** that is the same for all systems in mutual thermal equilibrium. This allows: - Construction of thermometers (system $C$ is a thermometer). - Definition of a temperature scale. - Thermodynamic temperature in Kelvins via the **ideal gas law** ($T = PV/nR$ in the limit of zero pressure). **Part (b) Solution:** **Step 1 — Derivation of hydrostatic equation (4 marks):** Consider a fluid element of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dz$ at height $z$ in a gravitational field. Force balance (no acceleration for equilibrium): $$P(z) \cdot A - P(z+dz) \cdot A - \rho \cdot A \cdot dz \cdot g = 0$$ Dividing by $A$ and rearranging: $$P(z+dz) - P(z) = -\rho g \, dz$$ In the limit $dz \to 0$: $$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g}$$ **Step 2 — Manometer problem (3 marks):** **Given:** $\Delta h = 250\,\text{mm} = 0.25\,\text{m}$, $\rho_{\text{Hg}} = 13{,}600\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325\,\text{kPa}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. **Pressure exerted by mercury column:** $$\Delta P = \rho_{\text{Hg}} \cdot g \cdot \Delta h = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$ $$\Delta P = 33{,}354\,\text{Pa} = 33.354\,\text{kPa}$$ **Absolute pressure of the gas** (assuming the gas pressure is *above* atmospheric — a closed-tank gauge manometer): $$P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \Delta P = 101.325 + 33.354 = 134.68\,\text{kPa}$$ > **Mark Distribution (Part b):** Derivation (4 marks: free-body 1, force balance 1, algebra 1, limit 1) + Mercury pressure (2 marks) + Final absolute pressure (1 mark). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Thermodynamic Equilibrium Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **Confusing steady state with equilibrium (–2 marks).** If a system has constant temperature *at a point* but heat is flowing, it is in steady state, not equilibrium. Always check for *net flows* before declaring equilibrium. > > 2. **Forgetting one of the four pillars (–1 mark).** Students often list only mechanical and thermal equilibrium, omitting chemical and phase equilibrium. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the *complete* four-pillar definition. > > 3. **Sign error in hydrostatic equation (–1 to –2 marks).** Pressure *decreases* with height: $dP/dz = -\rho g$. A common mistake is writing $+g$ instead of $-g$. Use physical reasoning: higher altitude → lower pressure. > > 4. **Using gauge pressure when absolute is required (–1 mark).** Manometer readings give *gauge* pressure (above atmospheric). For thermodynamic state calculations (using steam tables, ideal gas law), always convert to **absolute** pressure first. > > 5. **Ignoring the mass conservation in phase-equilibrium problems (–2 marks).** When a sealed rigid container is heated, the *specific volume* of the mixture is invariant. Always use $v = V/m_{\text{total}}$ first, *then* locate the state on the $T$–$v$ diagram. > > 6. **Mis-stating the Zeroth Law (–1 mark).** The law says *thermal* equilibrium specifically, not "equilibrium in general." Avoid vague statements like "if two systems are equal in temperature…" — use the precise wording. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Thermodynamic Equilibrium:** - ✅ **Definition:** State of no net flows, uniform properties, time-invariant — the *reference* for all processes. - ✅ **Four Pillars (must list all):** Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Phase. - ✅ **Zeroth Law:** Transitivity of thermal equilibrium → justifies the *existence* of temperature. - ✅ **State Postulate:** $F = C - P + 2$; for pure single-phase system, $F = 2$ (two independent intensive properties fix the state). - ✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$ — pressure decreases upward. - ✅ **Triple Point of Water:** $T = 273.16\,\text{K}$, $P = 611.657\,\text{Pa}$ — defines the Kelvin scale. - ✅ **Equilibrium ≠ Steady State** — steady state allows gradients and flows; equilibrium forbids them. - ✅ **Quasi-static process** = continuous sequence of *equilibrium* states (infinitesimally slow). - ✅ **Second-Law Statement:** Entropy is *maximized* at equilibrium in an isolated system. - ✅ **Stability criteria:** $C_v > 0$ (thermal), $\kappa_T > 0$ (mechanical). - ✅ **Helmholtz function $A = U - TS$:** minimized at constant $(T, V)$ equilibrium. - ✅ **Gibbs function $G = H - TS$:** minimized at constant $(T, P)$ equilibrium. - ✅ **Phase equilibrium condition:** $T_{\text{liq}} = T_{\text{vap}}$, $P_{\text{liq}} = P_{\text{vap}}$, $\mu_{\text{liq}} = \mu_{\text{vap}}$, AND masses of both phases *constant in time*. - ✅ **Chemical equilibrium condition:** $\mu_i = \text{constant}$ for each species $i$ throughout the system. - ✅ **Specific volume invariance in sealed rigid container:** $v = V/m_{\text{total}}$ is constant; use this as your *first calculation* in any sealed-rigid problem. - ✅ **Always use absolute pressure** in thermodynamic state calculations, never gauge.

topics.title: Quasi-Static Process and State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quasi-Static Process and State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Concept of a **State**

In thermodynamics, the **state** of a system is defined as the complete condition of the system at a specific instant, described entirely by its macroscopic **properties** (such as pressure $P$, temperature $T$, volume $V$, internal energy $U$, enthalpy $H$, and entropy $S$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"A thermodynamic state is the condition of a system characterized by a set of independent properties. For a state to be uniquely defined, the system must exist in **thermodynamic equilibrium**."*

A system passes through a **continuum of equilibrium states** during a process. A property at a state has a single, definite value — it does not depend on the path taken to reach that state. Such properties are called **state functions** or **point functions** (e.g., $U, H, S, T, P, V$).

### 1.2 The **State Postulate**

For a **simple compressible system** (a system whose state is completely specified by two independent intensive properties), the **State Postulate** states:

> *"The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**."*

This is foundational because, once two independent properties are fixed, all other properties are automatically determined. For example, specifying $P$ and $T$ uniquely determines $V$, $U$, $H$, and $S$ for a given mass of a pure substance.

### 1.3 The Concept of a **Quasi-Static Process**

A **quasi-static process** (also called a **quasi-equilibrium process**) is an idealized process in which the system deviates from thermodynamic equilibrium by only an **infinitesimally small amount** at every instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Slow Deflation" Picture:**
> Imagine a bicycle pump piston pushed **infinitely slowly**. At every instant, the gas inside has time to equalize pressure and temperature throughout its volume. There are no shock waves, no turbulence, no temperature gradients — the gas is essentially in equilibrium at every point. This is what a quasi-static process *looks like* in real life.
>
> In contrast, if you slam the piston down rapidly, the gas near the piston is compressed before the gas at the far end even "knows" — a pressure gradient exists, and the system is *not* in equilibrium. This is a **non-quasi-static** (irreversible) process.

### 1.4 Why Quasi-Static Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) emphasizes the quasi-static process because:

1. It allows the system to be described by properties ($P, V, T, U, H, S$) at every intermediate point.
2. The process can be **plotted as a continuous curve** on a $P\text{-}V$, $T\text{-}S$, or $P\text{-}T$ diagram.
3. It is a **prerequisite** for a **reversible process** (a quasi-static process + no friction/unrestricted expansion).
4. The work done, $W = \int P\, dV$, can only be evaluated for quasi-static processes using the system's own pressure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Quasi-Static Process (Structured Logic)

A process is quasi-static **if and only if** the following hold:

- **Step 1 — Infinitesimal Deviation:** The external forces (pressure, temperature reservoir, etc.) change the system state by an *infinitesimally small* amount $dP$, $dT$, etc.
- **Step 2 — Internal Re-equilibration:** The system has enough time to relax back to a uniform equilibrium state before the next infinitesimal change occurs.
- **Step 3 — Uniformity of Properties:** At every instant, the system pressure $P_{\text{sys}}$ equals the boundary/environment pressure $P_{\text{ext}}$, i.e., $P_{\text{sys}} = P_{\text{ext}}$ (mechanical equilibrium).
- **Step 4 — Path Representability:** The process can be drawn as a continuous, smooth line on any property diagram — every point on the line is a valid equilibrium state.
- **Step 5 — Reversibility Subset:** A quasi-static process is a *necessary but not sufficient* condition for reversibility. Reversibility additionally requires the **absence of friction, hysteresis, mixing, and unrestricted expansion**.

### 2.2 Quasi-Static Process vs. Reversible Process

| Aspect | Quasi-Static | Reversible |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Infinitely slow; system always in internal equilibrium | Quasi-static + no dissipative effects |
| **Equilibrium** | Internal equilibrium only | Both internal and external equilibrium |
| **Direction** | Can occur in one direction only | Can be reversed without leaving any net change in surroundings |
| **Practical Realization** | Approximated by very slow processes | An idealization — never perfectly achieved |
| **Work Done** | $W = \int P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$ | $W = \int P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$ (maximum useful work) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Quasi-static gets you the **curve**; Reversible gets you the **curve + the ability to retrace it** without penalty."*

### 2.3 Distinction Between **State** and **Process**

| Property Class | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **State (Point) Function** | Depends only on state, not path | $P, V, T, U, H, S$ |
| **Path Function** | Depends on the path taken between two states | $W$ (work), $Q$ (heat) |

A **process** is the *path* or *transformation* that connects two equilibrium states. The change in any state function between two states is fixed, regardless of the path:

$$\Delta U = U_2 - U_1 = \int_1^2 dU$$

In contrast, for a path function:

$$W = \int_1^2 P_{\text{ext}}\, dV \quad \text{(depends on path)}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Postulate (simple compressible) | Fix any 2 independent intensive properties | $P, T$ or $P, v$ or $T, v$ |
| 2 | Work in Quasi-Static Process | $W = \displaystyle\int_1^2 P\, dV$ | $W$ in **J** or **kJ** |
| 3 | Work in Polytropic Process | $W = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$ | $P_1V_1^n = P_2V_2^n = C$ |
| 4 | Work in Isothermal Process (ideal gas) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | $T = \text{const}$, $n=1$ |
| 5 | Work in Isobaric Process | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $P = \text{const}$, $n=0$ |
| 6 | Work in Isochoric Process | $W = 0$ | $V = \text{const}$, $n = \infty$ |
| 7 | Work in Adiabatic (Ideal Gas) | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | $n = \gamma = C_p / C_v$ |
| 8 | Polytropic Specific Heat | $C_n = C_v \cdot \dfrac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$ | Used in heat calculation |
| 9 | Heat Transfer (First Law, closed system) | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | $Q, W$ in **kJ** |
| 10 | Internal Energy Change (ideal gas) | $\Delta U = m\, C_v\, \Delta T$ | State function — path independent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The formula $W = \int P\, dV$ is **valid ONLY** for quasi-static processes where the system pressure $P$ is well-defined and uniform. For non-quasi-static processes (e.g., free expansion in a vacuum), this integral has *no meaning* because $P_{\text{sys}}$ is not defined.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quasi-static analysis is the **backbone of all thermodynamic cycle design**:

- **Otto Cycle** (spark-ignition engines): Approximated as a sequence of quasi-static isochoric and adiabatic processes.
- **Diesel Cycle**: Uses quasi-static isobaric (heat addition) and adiabatic (expansion) processes.
- **Rankine Cycle** (steam power plants): Pumps, boilers, turbines, and condensers are modeled as quasi-static devices.
- **Refrigeration Cycles** (Vapor Compression): All four processes (compression, condensation, expansion, evaporation) are analyzed as quasi-static.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use real (irreversible) processes in design?** Because the *quasi-static reversible work* represents the **maximum possible work** output (or minimum work input) for a given pair of end states. Actual devices fall short by the amount of irreversibility, which engineers quantify as **exergy destruction**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Done in a General Quasi-Static Process

**Starting Premise:**
Consider a closed system (a gas in a piston-cylinder assembly) undergoing a quasi-static expansion. The system pressure $P_{\text{sys}}$ is uniform and equals the external resisting pressure $P_{\text{ext}}$ at the boundary at every instant.

**Step 1 — Differential Work Element:**
When the piston moves by an infinitesimal displacement $dx$, the volume changes by:

$$dV = A\, dx$$

where $A$ is the piston cross-sectional area. The differential force exerted on the piston by the gas is:

$$F = P_{\text{sys}} \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Differential Work:**
The infinitesimal work done **by** the system on the surroundings is:

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = P_{\text{sys}} \cdot A \cdot dx = P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$$

**Step 3 — Integration Over a Finite Process:**
For a finite process from state 1 to state 2, the total work is the path integral:

$$W_{1\to 2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation:**
The integral $\int P\, dV$ is **the area under the process curve** on a $P\text{-}V$ diagram. This is the most direct way to evaluate $W$ in a quasi-static process.

> **Convention Used in KTU:** $W$ is taken as **work done BY the system** (positive when the system expands). Some textbooks use the opposite sign; always check the convention in the problem statement.

### 3.2 Derivation: Work in a Polytropic Process

**Polytropic Process Definition:**
A process following the relationship:

$$P\, V^n = C = \text{constant}$$

where $n$ is the **polytropic index**. Special cases:

- $n = 0 \Rightarrow P = C$ (isobaric)
- $n = 1 \Rightarrow PV = C$ (isothermal, ideal gas)
- $n = \gamma \Rightarrow PV^\gamma = C$ (adiabatic, ideal gas)
- $n = \infty \Rightarrow V = C$ (isochoric)

**Step 1 — Express $P$ in terms of $V$:**

$$P = C \cdot V^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the work integral:**

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C\, V^{-n}\, dV$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= C \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n}\, dV \\
  &= C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \quad \text{(for } n \neq 1 \text{)} \\
  &= \frac{C}{1-n} \left( V_2^{\,1-n} - V_1^{\,1-n} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back $C = P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{1}{1-n} \left( P_2 V_2^{\,n} \cdot V_2^{\,1-n} - P_1 V_1^{\,n} \cdot V_1^{\,1-n} \right) \\
  &= \frac{1}{1-n} \left( P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1 \right) \\
  &= \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result (Polytropic Work Formula):**

$$\boxed{\,W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}, \quad n \neq 1\,}$$

### 3.3 Limiting Case: Isothermal Process ($n = 1$)

**Step 1 — L'Hôpital's Rule Application:**
As $n \to 1$, the formula above becomes indeterminate ($0/0$). Applying L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\lim_{n \to 1} \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

**Step 2 — Use $PV = mRT = \text{const}$ for an ideal gas isothermal process:**

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 = mRT$$

**Step 3 — Direct Integration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V}\, dV \\
  &= mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} \\
  &= mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative form using pressures:**

$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Work in an Adiabatic (Isentropic) Process for an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Use the adiabatic relation:**

$$P V^{\gamma} = C, \quad \gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = \gamma$ into the polytropic formula:**

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - \gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange (note the sign convention):**

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Use $PV = mRT$ to write in terms of temperature:**

$$W = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$$

This form is particularly useful when the temperatures are given or easily computed.

### 3.5 Derivation: Polytropic Specific Heat

**Premise:** The First Law for a closed system undergoing a quasi-static process:

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W = m C_v\, dT + P\, dV$$

**Step 1 — Use $P V^n = C$ and $PV = mRT$ for an ideal gas:**

$$d(PV^n) = 0 \Rightarrow nP V^{n-1}\, dV + V^n\, dP = 0$$

**Step 2 — Divide the polytropic equation by $PV$ to derive a relation between $dP, dV, dT$:**

$$\frac{dP}{P} + n \frac{dV}{V} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Combine with the ideal gas relation $\frac{dP}{P} + \frac{dV}{V} = \frac{dT}{T}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{P} + n \frac{dV}{V} &= 0 \\
\frac{dP}{P} + \frac{dV}{V} &= \frac{dT}{T}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solving yields $\frac{dV}{V} = \frac{-1}{n-1} \cdot \frac{dT}{T}$. Substituting into $\delta Q$:**

$$\delta Q = m C_v\, dT + P \cdot \frac{V}{1-n} \cdot \frac{dT}{T} \cdot (-1) = m C_v\, dT - \frac{mRT}{1-n} \cdot \frac{dT}{T}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\delta Q = m \left( C_v - \frac{R}{1-n} \right) dT = m \cdot \frac{C_v(1-n) - R}{1-n}\, dT$$

**Step 6 — Use $R = C_p - C_v = C_v(\gamma - 1)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta Q &= m \cdot \frac{C_v(1-n) - C_v(\gamma - 1)}{1-n}\, dT \\
         &= m C_v \cdot \frac{1 - n - \gamma + 1}{1 - n}\, dT \\
         &= m C_v \cdot \frac{n - \gamma}{1 - n}\, dT
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result — Polytropic Specific Heat:**

$$\boxed{\,C_n = C_v \cdot \frac{n - \gamma}{1 - n} = C_v \cdot \frac{n - \gamma}{n - 1} \cdot (-1)\,}$$

With the sign convention (positive $Q$ into system):

$$C_n = C_v \left( \frac{\gamma - n}{n - 1} \right)$$

**Verification of Special Cases:**

- $n = 0$ (isobaric): $C_n = C_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{1} = \gamma C_v = C_p$ ✓
- $n = 1$ (isothermal): $C_n = \infty$ (heat exchange without temperature change) ✓
- $n = \gamma$ (adiabatic): $C_n = 0$ (no heat transfer) ✓
- $n = \infty$ (isochoric): $C_n = C_v$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture: How a Quasi-Static Process is Realized

The following block diagram maps the logical and physical steps required to *engineer* a quasi-static process. Real devices (pistons, throttles, heat exchangers) are never perfectly quasi-static, but the diagram shows the *theoretical* structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Equilibrium State 1] --> B[Infinitesimal Change in Boundary Condition]
    B --> C[System Deviates by Infinitesimal Amount dP dT dV]
    C --> D[Internal Re-equilibration Occurs]
    D --> E[New Equilibrium State 1a]
    E --> F{More Change Required?}
    F -- Yes --> B
    F -- No --> G[Final Equilibrium State 2]
    G --> H[Process Represented as Continuous Path on P-V Diagram]
    H --> I[Work equals Area under P-V Curve]
    I --> J[Apply First Law Q equals Delta U plus W]
```

### 4.2 State Point and Path on a P-V Diagram

The diagram below represents the **abstract structure** of a state on a property diagram. Each *point* corresponds to an equilibrium state; each *curve* corresponds to a quasi-static process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STATES ["Equilibrium States on P-V Diagram"]
        S1["State 1 : P1 V1 T1 U1 H1 S1"]
        S2["State 2 : P2 V2 T2 U2 H2 S2"]
        S3["State 3 : P3 V3 T3 U3 H3 S3"]
    end
    subgraph PATHS ["Quasi-Static Process Paths"]
        P1["Path A : Isothermal PV equal C"]
        P2["Path B : Adiabatic PV gamma equal C"]
        P3["Path C : Polytropic PVn equal C"]
    end
    S1 --- P1
    S1 --- P2
    S1 --- P3
    P1 --> S2
    P2 --> S3
    P3 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Quasi-Static vs. Non-Quasi-Static Comparison (Logical View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Initiated] --> Check{System remains in equilibrium at every instant?}
    Check -- Yes --> QS[Quasi-Static Process]
    Check -- No --> NQS[Non-Quasi-Static Process]
    QS --> RevCheck{No friction, mixing, or hysteresis?}
    RevCheck -- Yes --> Rev[Reversible Process]
    RevCheck -- No --> IrrevQS[Irreversible but Quasi-Static]
    NQS --> Irr[Always Irreversible]
    Rev --> WorkMax[Work equals Integral P dV maximum work]
    IrrevQS --> WorkRed[Work less than maximum possible]
    Irr --> NoPath[No well-defined P-V path; W cannot be computed from system P]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: When is $W = \int P\, dV$ Valid?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given a Process] --> Q2{Is the process quasi-static?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use W equals Integral P dV]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Use W equals Integral P_ext dV]
    Q4 --> Q5{P_ext known during process?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Integrate with given P_ext profile]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use energy balance to find W indirectly]
    Q3 --> Q8[For ideal gas use P V equals m R T to simplify]
    Q3 --> Q9[For polytropic use W equals P2V2 minus P1V1 over 1 minus n]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In board exams, you may be asked to "sketch the process on a $P\text{-}V$ diagram and shade the work area." Always label the axes with units (e.g., $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³), mark the initial and final states with filled circles, and shade the area **under the process curve** (not above) to indicate the work done *by* the system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define a quasi-static process. Why is it an idealization?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A quasi-static process is one in which the system departs from thermodynamic equilibrium by only an **infinitesimally small** amount at every stage, and the system has sufficient time to re-establish internal equilibrium before the next change occurs.

It is an **idealization** because:

- **(1 mark)** True equilibrium can only be approached in the limit of infinitely slow changes — in reality, *all* real processes occur at a finite rate.
- **(1 mark)** Such a process would require an infinite amount of time to complete, which is impractical in engineering applications.
- **(1 mark)** Despite being unattainable, it serves as a useful *theoretical benchmark* (the maximum work output of a device corresponds to the quasi-static case).

**Allocation:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three reasons (any 2 well-explained).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the state postulate for a simple compressible system and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**State Postulate (2 marks):** *"The state of a simple compressible system is completely specified by **two independent intensive properties**."*

**Significance (1 mark):** Once two independent intensive properties (e.g., $P$ and $T$, or $P$ and $v$, or $T$ and $v$) are fixed, all other properties ($U, H, S$, etc.) are uniquely determined by the system. This dramatically reduces the experimental/computational effort required to characterize a system — only two measurements are needed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**A closed system containing 0.5 kg of an ideal gas undergoes a quasi-static polytropic process from an initial state of $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $V_1 = 0.1$ m³ to a final volume $V_2 = 0.3$ m³. The polytropic index is $n = 1.3$. Assume $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.**

### **(a)** Determine the work done and the final pressure. (7 marks)

### **(b)** Determine the heat transfer, change in internal energy, and change in enthalpy. State whether the process is heating or cooling. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the polytropic relation $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^n \\
    &= 200 \cdot \left(\frac{0.1}{0.3}\right)^{1.3} \\
    &= 200 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{1.3} \\
    &= 200 \cdot (0.3333)^{1.3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $(0.3333)^{1.3}$:**

$$(0.3333)^{1.3} = e^{1.3 \cdot \ln(0.3333)} = e^{1.3 \cdot (-1.0986)} = e^{-1.4282} \approx 0.2397$$

$$\boxed{P_2 \approx 200 \cdot 0.2397 \approx 47.94 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate work done using the polytropic formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \\
  &= \frac{(47.94)(0.3) - (200)(0.1)}{1 - 1.3} \\
  &= \frac{14.382 - 20}{-0.3} \\
  &= \frac{-5.618}{-0.3} \\
  &\approx +18.73 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Positive $W$ indicates the system **does work on the surroundings** (expansion). ✓

$$\boxed{W \approx +18.73 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Mark Split:** 2 marks for $P_2$ calculation, 3 marks for setting up the work formula, 2 marks for substitution and final answer.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Find the gas constant $R$:**

$$R = C_p - C_v = 1.005 - 0.718 = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Find initial and final temperatures using $PV = mRT$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{P_1 V_1}{m R} = \frac{200 \cdot 0.1}{0.5 \cdot 0.287} = \frac{20}{0.1435} \approx 139.37 \text{ K} \\
T_2 &= \frac{P_2 V_2}{m R} = \frac{47.94 \cdot 0.3}{0.5 \cdot 0.287} = \frac{14.382}{0.1435} \approx 100.22 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Change in internal energy (state function):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta U &= m C_v (T_2 - T_1) \\
         &= 0.5 \cdot 0.718 \cdot (100.22 - 139.37) \\
         &= 0.5 \cdot 0.718 \cdot (-39.15) \\
         &\approx -14.05 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Change in enthalpy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= m C_p (T_2 - T_1) \\
         &= 0.5 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (-39.15) \\
         &\approx -19.67 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Heat transfer (First Law):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta U + W \\
  &= -14.05 + 18.73 \\
  &\approx +4.68 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

Since $Q = +4.68$ kJ (positive), **heat is transferred INTO the system** (heating process).

**Mark Split:** 2 marks each for $\Delta U, \Delta H, Q$; 1 mark for final statement.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Define (i) state, (ii) state function, and (iii) quasi-static process. Differentiate clearly between a quasi-static process and a reversible process with a suitable example for each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fluid in a piston-cylinder assembly expands quasi-statically from $V_1 = 0.05$ m³ to $V_2 = 0.15$ m³, following the path $P = 5V + 100$ (with $P$ in kPa and $V$ in m³). Determine: (i) the work done by the system, (ii) the initial and final pressures, and (iii) the work done if the same process were isobaric at the average pressure. Compare both work values. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**(i) State:** The condition of a system completely described by its macroscopic properties. A state is well-defined only when the system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium**. (1 mark)

**(ii) State Function:** A property whose value depends only on the *current state* of the system, not on how that state was reached. The differential $dZ$ of a state function is **exact** (i.e., $\oint dZ = 0$). Examples: $P, V, T, U, H, S$. (1 mark)

**(iii) Quasi-Static Process:** A process that proceeds infinitely slowly such that the system remains in (or infinitesimally close to) internal equilibrium at every instant. Every intermediate state is an equilibrium state and can be plotted on a property diagram. (1 mark)

**Quasi-Static vs Reversible Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Quasi-Static | Reversible |
|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium** | Internal equilibrium only | Internal *and* external equilibrium |
| **Friction/Mixing** | May or may not be present | Must be absent |
| **Directionality** | One direction only | Can be reversed without net change in surroundings |
| **Work** | $W = \int P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$ | $W = \int P_{\text{sys}}\, dV$ (maximum) |

**Examples:**

- **Quasi-static but irreversible:** Slow compression of a gas with significant friction between the piston and cylinder walls. The system is in internal equilibrium at every instant (quasi-static), but the friction dissipates energy as heat (irreversible).
- **Reversible:** Frictionless, infinitely slow isothermal expansion of an ideal gas against a piston loaded with infinitesimally small sand grains (which are removed one at a time). Both the system and surroundings can be returned to their initial states.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Initial and Final Pressures:**

Using $P = 5V + 100$ (with $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 5(0.05) + 100 = 0.25 + 100 = 100.25 \text{ kPa} \\
P_2 &= 5(0.15) + 100 = 0.75 + 100 = 100.75 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Work Done (Linear Process):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV = \int_{0.05}^{0.15} (5V + 100)\, dV \\
  &= \left[ \frac{5V^2}{2} + 100V \right]_{0.05}^{0.15} \\
  &= \left( \frac{5(0.15)^2}{2} + 100(0.15) \right) - \left( \frac{5(0.05)^2}{2} + 100(0.05) \right) \\
  &= (0.05625 + 15) - (0.00625 + 5) \\
  &= 15.05625 - 5.00625 \\
  &= 10.05 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Average Pressure and Isobaric Work:**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \frac{100.25 + 100.75}{2} = 100.5 \text{ kPa}$$

For an isobaric process at the average pressure:

$$W_{\text{iso}} = P_{\text{avg}} (V_2 - V_1) = 100.5 \cdot (0.15 - 0.05) = 100.5 \cdot 0.1 = 10.05 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

Both work values are **identical** ($10.05$ kJ). This is a special property of *linear* $P\text{-}V$ relationships: the work done over a linear path equals the work done at the average pressure, because the integral of a linear function is the rectangle under the midpoint (trapezoidal area = average height × base).

**Mark Split:** 2 marks for initial/final pressure, 3 marks for integration of $W$, 1 mark for average pressure calculation, 1 mark for comparison statement.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** The KTU 2024 scheme uses $W$ as **work done BY the system** (positive for expansion). Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks silently across multiple sub-parts.
> 2. **Using $W = \int P\, dV$ for Non-Quasi-Static Processes (–3 marks):** This formula is valid **only** for quasi-static processes. For free expansion, the integral is meaningless because the system pressure is not defined.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Units (–1 to –2 marks):** Pressure in **kPa**, Volume in **m³** → Work in **kJ**. Mixing kPa with litres or bar with m³ is a common error.
> 4. **Confusing $C_p, C_v, R, \gamma$ in the Polytropic Formula (–2 marks):** Always verify the value of $n$ relative to $\gamma$ before deciding the formula. For an adiabatic process, $n = \gamma$ specifically.
> 5. **Marking the Wrong Area on the $P\text{-}V$ Diagram (–1 mark):** For expansion, shade the area *under* the curve. For compression, the area is still under the curve, but the work is *negative* — shade it with a different pattern or label it explicitly.
> 6. **Path vs. State Function Error in $\Delta U, \Delta H$ (–2 marks):** Internal energy and enthalpy are state functions — they depend only on the initial and final temperatures (for an ideal gas), not on the path. Do not compute them using the path integral; use $\Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$ directly.
> 7. **Omitting the "quasi-static" qualifier in the answer (–1 mark):** Every time you write $\int P\, dV$, explicitly state that the process is quasi-static. KTU examiners award this 1 mark for terminology precision.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **state** is defined only for an equilibrium system; it is fully described by its macroscopic properties.
- [x] The **State Postulate** requires **two independent intensive properties** to fix the state of a simple compressible system.
- [x] A **quasi-static process** is an idealized, infinitely slow process where the system is in internal equilibrium at every instant.
- [x] **State functions** ($P, V, T, U, H, S$): depend only on the state. **Path functions** ($W, Q$): depend on the process path.
- [x] **Work** in a quasi-static process: $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\, dV$ (area under the $P\text{-}V$ curve).
- [x] **Polytropic process:** $P V^n = C$ with $W = \dfrac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$.
- [x] **Limiting cases of $n$:** $n = 0$ (isobaric), $n = 1$ (isothermal), $n = \gamma$ (adiabatic), $n = \infty$ (isochoric).
- [x] **Isothermal work** (ideal gas): $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2 / V_1)$.
- [x] **Adiabatic work** (ideal gas): $W = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2) / (\gamma - 1)$.
- [x] **Quasi-static ≠ Reversible.** Quasi-static is a *necessary* condition for reversibility, but friction/mixing/hysteresis must also be absent.
- [x] **$\Delta U$ and $\Delta H$ for an ideal gas** depend only on $\Delta T$, regardless of the process path.
- [x] **First Law (closed system):** $Q = \Delta U + W$ (with KTU sign convention: $W$ = work done *by* the system).
- [x] **Units check:** kPa · m³ = kJ. Never mix bar/litre/kPa/m³ inconsistently.
- [x] **Drawing tip:** Always label axes, mark state points, and shade the work area on the $P\text{-}V$ diagram in board-exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point and Path Functions; Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Point and Path Functions; Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Point Functions (State Functions) A **Point Function** (also called a **State Function** or **State Property**) is a thermodynamic property whose value depends *only* on the **state** (i.e., the instantaneous condition) of the system at a given instant, and **NOT** on the path or process by which that state was reached. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > A property is a point function if a change in its value between two states is independent of the path connecting those two states. Mathematically, $dZ$ is an **exact differential**. **Examples:** Pressure ($P$), Volume ($V$), Temperature ($T$), Internal Energy ($U$), Enthalpy ($H$), Entropy ($S$), Specific Volume ($v$), Density ($\rho$). #### Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are standing on top of a hill. Your **altitude** depends *only* on your current GPS coordinates (state). It does not matter whether you walked up the gentle slope or climbed the steep rocky face — if you end up at the same point, your altitude reading is identical. Altitude behaves like a **point function**. --- ### 1.2 Path Functions A **Path Function** is a thermodynamic quantity whose value depends on the **specific path/process** followed by the system as it moves from an initial state to a final state, in addition to the end states themselves. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A property is a path function if its change depends on the path taken between the two states. Mathematically, $\delta Z$ is an **inexact differential**, and it can only be expressed as a line integral along a specific path. **Examples:** Heat transfer ($Q$), Work transfer ($W$), Heat transferred in a reversible process ($\int T\,dS$), and Work done in a reversible process ($\int P\,dV$). #### Conceptual Analogy Now consider the **fuel consumed** by your car driving from the base of the hill to the top. If you take the steep, rocky path, your engine works harder and burns more fuel. If you take the long, winding road, you may burn *more* fuel because of the greater distance. The fuel used depends on the *route*, not just the start and end points. Fuel consumed behaves like a **path function**. --- ### 1.3 The Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics > [!NOTE] > **Statement of the Zeroth Law:** > *"If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other."* This law is the foundation of the concept of **Temperature**. It establishes that temperature is a measurable, transitive property of thermodynamic systems. #### Conceptual Analogy Think of three people — A, B, and C — speaking different languages. A and C cannot directly communicate that they are "comfortable" at the same temperature. But if both A and C can independently tell B (a translator) that they are "comfortable," and B confirms both say the same thing, then A and C would, in fact, agree on temperature if they met. B acts as the **thermometer** that provides a common reference for temperature. --- ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - The **Zeroth Law** assigns a numerical value to the property "temperature" only *after* a reference scale is chosen. - Standard reference points include the **triple point of water at 273.16 K** (basis of the Kelvin scale). - The SI unit of temperature is the **Kelvin (K)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Zeroth"?** This law was formulated *after* the First and Second Laws but is logically more fundamental. Rather than renumbering all three laws, it was placed *before* the First Law and given the index "zero." ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Test: Exact vs. Inexact Differentials A point function $Z(x, y)$ has the property that its total differential is **exact**: $$dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$$ For $dZ$ to be an exact differential, the **Euler reciprocity condition** must hold: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$ **Conversely**, for a path function, this condition fails, and we write the differential as $\delta Z$ (not $dZ$) to indicate that the differential is **inexact** and cannot be integrated without specifying a path. --- ### 2.2 The Cyclic Integral Principle One of the most powerful consequences of being a point function is the **cyclic integral**: $$\oint dZ = 0 \quad \text{(Point Function)}$$ $$\oint \delta Z \neq 0 \quad \text{(Path Function — generally)}$$ If a system undergoes a **cycle** (returns to its initial state), every point function regains its original value. Path functions, however, do not necessarily vanish over a cycle. --- ### 2.3 Reasoning Behind the Zeroth Law 1. **Step 1 — Existence of Equilibrium:** Two bodies in isolated contact will, after a sufficiently long time, reach a state where no further change occurs in their macroscopic properties. This state is **thermal equilibrium**. 2. **Step 2 — The "Zeroth" Insight:** If body A is in equilibrium with body C, and body B is also in equilibrium with body C, then A and B are in equilibrium with each other. This **transitivity** allows us to define a property (temperature) such that thermal equilibrium is equivalent to equal temperatures. 3. **Step 3 — Operational Definition:** A **thermometer** (body C) is calibrated using a known reference (e.g., triple point of water at **273.16 K**). Any body in thermal equilibrium with the calibrated thermometer shares the same temperature reading. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize This!** | Property / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Type | Cyclic Integral | |---|---|---|---| | State change of $Z$ | $\Delta Z = Z_2 - Z_1$ | Point Function | $\oint dZ = 0$ | | Path-dependent $Q$ | $\delta Q$ (no exact differential) | Path Function | $\oint \delta Q \neq 0$ | | Path-dependent $W$ | $\delta W$ (no exact differential) | Path Function | $\oint \delta W \neq 0$ | | Exactness Condition | $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ | Point Function test | — | | Reversible Heat | $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$ | Path-dependent (along path) | $\oint T\,dS = 0$ only for cyclic reversible | | Reversible Work | $\delta W_{rev} = P\,dV$ | Path-dependent (along path) | — | | Zeroth Law (Transitivity) | $T_A = T_C$ and $T_B = T_C \implies T_A = T_B$ | Property Equality | — | | Ideal Gas Relation | $PV = mRT$ | Point Function (state equation) | — | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** In KTU textbooks, exact differentials are written as $dQ$, $dW$ when treated as *properties* (e.g., $Q$ as a function of state in reversible processes), but in irreversible or unspecified processes, they are written as $\delta Q$, $\delta W$ to denote path dependence. Be alert to this notational convention. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Point functions** enable the construction of **property tables** (steam tables, refrigerant tables) — engineers only need to look up the two independent properties (e.g., $P$ and $T$) and the rest follows. This would be **impossible** if properties were path-dependent. - **Path functions** govern **device efficiency**: the work output of an engine, the heat input to a refrigerator, and the energy cost of a compression process all depend on the *route* taken through state space. - The **Zeroth Law** underpins **all temperature measurement** — from mercury thermometers to RTDs, thermocouples, and infrared pyrometers. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Exactness Condition for Point Functions Let $Z = Z(x, y)$ be a single-valued, continuous, and differentiable function of two independent state variables $x$ and $y$. The total differential is: $$dZ = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y dx + \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x dy$$ We define: $$M = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial x}\right)_y, \qquad N = \left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x$$ Now, compute the second partial derivative of $Z$ with respect to $x$ and $y$ in two different orders. If $Z$ has continuous second partial derivatives (a property of well-behaved thermodynamic potentials), **Clairaut's theorem** (equality of mixed partials) applies: $$\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial y\,\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\,\partial y}$$ This gives us the **Euler reciprocity relation**: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$ **Conclusion:** The mixed partial derivatives of a point function are always equal. This is the **mathematical fingerprint** that distinguishes a point function from a path function. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: The Cyclic Integral of a Point Function Consider a closed path (cycle) connecting states 1 → 2 → 1 in state space. For a point function $Z$: $$\oint dZ = \int_{1}^{2} dZ + \int_{2}^{1} dZ = (Z_2 - Z_1) + (Z_1 - Z_2) = 0$$ Since $Z$ is single-valued, going from 1 to 2 and back to 1 must return the same value. The integral vanishes for **every** closed path, regardless of shape. **Contrast with a path function** $W$ (e.g., work done around a cycle in a heat engine): although the system returns to its original state, the work output over the cycle is **not zero** — it equals the enclosed area on a $P$–$V$ diagram: $$\oint \delta W = \text{Area enclosed by the cycle} \neq 0 \text{ (in general)}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Point vs. Path Functions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU Exams** | Criterion | Point Function | Path Function | |---|---|---| | Differential symbol | $dZ$ (exact) | $\delta Z$ (inexact) | | Depends on | State only | Path taken | | Cyclic integral $\oint dZ$ | Zero | Not zero (in general) | | Example properties | $P, V, T, U, H, S$ | $Q, W$ | | Test | $\left(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$ | Above equality fails | | Can be plotted on $P$–$V$ diagram? | Yes (as a coordinate) | No (only as a curve integral) | | Tabulated in steam tables? | Yes | No | | Direction matters? | No (only magnitude) | Yes (sign of integration matters) | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Identifying Exactness **Problem:** For a differential form $\delta Z = (2xy)\,dx + (x^2 + 3y^2)\,dy$, determine whether $Z$ is a point function or a path function. **Solution:** We identify $M = 2xy$ and $N = x^2 + 3y^2$. Compute the mixed partial derivatives: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(2xy) = 2x$$ $$\left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^2 + 3y^2) = 2x$$ Since both are equal ($2x = 2x$), the **Euler reciprocity condition is satisfied**. Therefore, $\delta Z$ is an **exact differential** and $Z$ is a **point function**. We can recover the function $Z$ by integration: $$Z = \int M\,dx = \int 2xy\,dx = x^2 y + f(y)$$ Differentiate with respect to $y$ and equate to $N$: $$\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x = x^2 + f'(y) = x^2 + 3y^2$$ Thus, $f'(y) = 3y^2$, giving $f(y) = y^3 + C$. $$\boxed{Z(x, y) = x^2 y + y^3 + C}$$ This result is **path-independent**, confirming $Z$ is a point function. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Concept Map: Hierarchy of Thermodynamic Properties ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Thermodynamic Properties"] --> nodeB["Point Functions"] nodeA["Thermodynamic Properties"] --> nodeC["Path Functions"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB1["Pressure P"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB2["Volume V"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB3["Temperature T"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB4["Internal Energy U"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB5["Enthalpy H"] nodeB["Point Functions"] --> nodeB6["Entropy S"] nodeC["Path Functions"] --> nodeC1["Heat Q"] nodeC["Path Functions"] --> nodeC2["Work W"] nodeB1["Pressure P"] --> nodeTest["Exactness Test"] nodeB2["Volume V"] --> nodeTest["Exactness Test"] nodeC1["Heat Q"] --> nodePath["Inequality Test"] nodeC2["Work W"] --> nodePath["Inequality Test"] nodeTest["Exactness Test"] --> nodeRes1["Cyclic Integral = 0"] nodePath["Inequality Test"] --> nodeRes2["Cyclic Integral not 0"] ``` ### 4.2 Process Diagram: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics (Thermal Equilibrium Transitivity) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSysA["System A
Temperature TA"] nodeSysB["System B
Thermometer"] nodeSysC["System C
Temperature TC"] nodeEq1["Thermal Equilibrium
TA equals TB"] nodeEq2["Thermal Equilibrium
TB equals TC"] nodeEq3["Concluded Equilibrium
TA equals TC"] nodeSysA["System A
Temperature TA"] -->|Indirect contact| nodeEq1["Thermal Equilibrium
TA equals TB"] nodeSysB["System B
Thermometer"] --> nodeEq1["Thermal Equilibrium
TA equals TB"] nodeSysB["System B
Thermometer"] --> nodeEq2["Thermal Equilibrium
TB equals TC"] nodeSysC["System C
Temperature TC"] -->|Indirect contact| nodeEq2["Thermal Equilibrium
TB equals TC"] nodeEq1["Thermal Equilibrium
TA equals TB"] --> nodeEq3["Concluded Equilibrium
TA equals TC"] nodeEq2["Thermal Equilibrium
TB equals TC"] --> nodeEq3["Concluded Equilibrium
TA equals TC"] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Identifying a Point Function ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Given Property Z of x and y"] nodeStart["Given Property Z of x and y"] --> nodeCheck1["Compute partial M over partial y"] nodeStart["Given Property Z of x and y"] --> nodeCheck2["Compute partial N over partial x"] nodeCheck1["Compute partial M over partial y"] --> nodeCompare{"Are both equal?"} nodeCheck2["Compute partial N over partial x"] --> nodeCompare{"Are both equal?"} nodeCompare{"Are both equal?"} -->|Yes| nodeYes["Z is a Point Function"] nodeCompare{"Are both equal?"} -->|No| nodeNo["Z is a Path Function"] nodeYes["Z is a Point Function"] --> nodeUse1["Use in cyclic integral equal 0"] nodeNo["Z is a Path Function"] --> nodeUse2["Must specify integration path"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1.** Distinguish between a point function and a path function. Give two examples of each. **Model Answer:** A **point function** is a property whose value depends only on the state of the system and is independent of the path followed. Its cyclic integral is zero: $\oint dZ = 0$. *Examples:* Pressure ($P$), Temperature ($T$), Internal Energy ($U$). A **path function** is a property whose value depends on the path or process by which the system changes from one state to another. Its cyclic integral is generally not zero: $\oint \delta Z \neq 0$. *Examples:* Heat ($Q$), Work ($W$). (3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples) --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2.** State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics. Why is it called the "Zeroth" law? **Model Answer:** **Statement:** *If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.* This law establishes the **transitivity of temperature** and provides the foundation for the concept and measurement of temperature using a thermometer. **Why "Zeroth":** It was formulated after the First and Second Laws had already been numbered. Since it is logically more *fundamental* than the First Law (the First Law depends on the concept of temperature, which in turn depends on the Zeroth Law), it was placed *before* the First Law and numbered as the "Zeroth" rather than renumbering the existing laws. (3 marks: 1.5 marks for statement + 1.5 marks for explanation of nomenclature) --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the exactness condition that distinguishes a point function from a path function. State and prove the cyclic integral property for a point function. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the differential form $\delta Z = (3x^2 y + 2y^2)\,dx + (x^3 + 4xy)\,dy$, determine whether $Z$ is a point function. If it is, find $Z(x, y)$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Let $Z = Z(x, y)$ be a single-valued, differentiable function of two independent state variables. The total differential is: $$dZ = M\,dx + N\,dy$$ where $M = (\partial Z / \partial x)_y$ and $N = (\partial Z / \partial y)_x$. Taking mixed second partial derivatives, and assuming $Z$ has continuous second-order partial derivatives, **Clairaut's theorem** (equality of mixed partials) gives: $$\frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial y\,\partial x} = \frac{\partial^2 Z}{\partial x\,\partial y}$$ Expanding: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = \left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y$$ This is the **Euler reciprocity condition** — the test for exactness. **Cyclic Integral Property:** For a closed path returning the system to its initial state 1: $$\oint dZ = \int_{1}^{2} dZ + \int_{2}^{1} dZ = (Z_2 - Z_1) + (Z_1 - Z_2) = 0$$ Since the value of a point function is single-valued and path-independent, the cyclic integral always vanishes. (7 marks: 3 marks for derivation of exactness condition, 2 marks for Clairaut's theorem, 2 marks for cyclic integral proof) **Model Solution (b):** Identify $M = 3x^2 y + 2y^2$ and $N = x^3 + 4xy$. Compute mixed partial derivatives: $$\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial y}\right)_x = 3x^2 + 4y$$ $$\left(\frac{\partial N}{\partial x}\right)_y = 3x^2 + 4y$$ Since both are equal, the **Euler condition is satisfied**; $Z$ is a point function. To find $Z$, integrate $M$ with respect to $x$: $$Z = \int (3x^2 y + 2y^2)\,dx = x^3 y + 2xy^2 + f(y)$$ Differentiate with respect to $y$ and equate to $N$: $$\left(\frac{\partial Z}{\partial y}\right)_x = x^3 + 4xy + f'(y) = x^3 + 4xy$$ So $f'(y) = 0 \implies f(y) = C$. $$\boxed{Z(x, y) = x^3 y + 2xy^2 + C}$$ (7 marks: 3 marks for test + verification, 4 marks for finding the function) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** State the Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics and explain how it leads to the concept of temperature. Discuss the role of a thermometer in this context. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two systems A and B are separated by a diathermic wall, and system B is in thermal equilibrium with system C. If A and C are also brought into contact through a diathermic wall and no change occurs in their properties, what conclusion do you draw? Justify your answer using the Zeroth Law. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Statement of the Zeroth Law:** *If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.* **Concept of Temperature:** When two bodies in thermal contact show no net exchange of energy as heat, they are said to be at the same **temperature**. The Zeroth Law guarantees that "being at the same temperature" is a **transitive, consistent** property across all systems — it does not depend on the materials involved. This consistency is what allows temperature to be a universally meaningful, measurable quantity. **Role of a Thermometer:** A thermometer (system C) is a calibrated body used as a common reference. By bringing the thermometer into contact with any system, we read off the temperature from the thermometer's equilibrium state. The Zeroth Law ensures that any two systems showing the same thermometer reading are themselves in mutual thermal equilibrium. To obtain numerical values, a temperature scale (e.g., Kelvin, Celsius) is defined using reproducible reference points, such as the **triple point of water at 273.16 K**. (7 marks: 2 marks for statement, 3 marks for temperature concept, 2 marks for thermometer role) **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** - A and B are in thermal equilibrium (separated by a diathermic wall, no net heat flow). - B and C are in thermal equilibrium. - A and C are brought into contact through a diathermic wall; **no change occurs** in their properties. **Conclusion:** Systems A and C are in **thermal equilibrium** with each other. **Justification:** By the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, if system A is in thermal equilibrium with system B, and system B is in thermal equilibrium with system C, then system A must be in thermal equilibrium with system C. This is consistent with the experimental observation that bringing A and C into diathermic contact produces no change in their macroscopic properties — they are already at the same temperature. This transitivity underpins the validity of any common temperature measurement scheme. (7 marks: 3 marks for statement of conclusion, 2 marks for invoking Zeroth Law, 2 marks for explaining thermal equilibrium observation) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Notation Mistake:** Writing $dQ$ or $dW$ instead of $\delta Q$ or $\delta W$ for heat/work. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this. Use $\delta$ to emphasize path dependence. > 2. **Confusing "Path Function" with "Process":** A path function is a *quantity*, not a process. A process (like isothermal or adiabatic) is a *route*; path functions (Q, W) are evaluated along that route. > 3. **Zeroth Law Trivia:** Many students incorrectly state that the Zeroth Law *defines* temperature. It does NOT define temperature — it only establishes that temperature is a meaningful property. Temperature is *defined* by the Second Law (in classical thermodynamics) or by a chosen scale. > 4. **Cyclic Integral Confusion:** Some students write $\oint dQ = 0$ or $\oint dW = 0$. This is **false** in general — it is true only for ideal reversible cycles with appropriate sign conventions. The cyclic integral being zero is a property of **point functions**, not path functions. > 5. **Forgetting Constants:** In the exactness integration (Part B above), students often forget the arbitrary function of the second variable $f(y)$ and the constant of integration $C$. Always include them. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Point function** = state property; depends only on the state. *Examples:* $P, V, T, U, H, S$. *Differential symbol:* $dZ$ (exact). - **Path function** = process-dependent quantity. *Examples:* $Q, W$. *Differential symbol:* $\delta Z$ (inexact). - **Exactness test:** $(\partial M / \partial y)_x = (\partial N / \partial x)_y$. If satisfied → point function. - **Cyclic integral of a point function is always zero:** $\oint dZ = 0$. - **Cyclic integral of a path function is generally not zero:** $\oint \delta Z \neq 0$. - **Zeroth Law** states transitivity of thermal equilibrium → foundation of temperature measurement. - The Zeroth Law is "zeroth" because it was formulated after the First and Second Laws but is logically more fundamental. - Use $\delta Q$ and $\delta W$ notation in all KTU answers for path functions. - Steam tables and refrigerant tables list **point functions only** — this itself is proof that these are state properties. - The mixed second partial derivative of a point function is always equal (Clairaut's theorem).

topics.title: Measurement of Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**Temperature** is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantitatively represents the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. According to the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, temperature is the property that determines whether a body is in thermal equilibrium with another body. Two bodies in thermal equilibrium with a third body are in thermal equilibrium with each other, and they possess the same temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Temperature is an *intensive property* — it does not depend on the mass or size of the system. It is a *scalar* quantity measured in **Kelvin (K)** under the SI system.

### Empirical vs. Thermodynamic Temperature

| Type | Definition | Reference |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| **Empirical Temperature** | Defined using a particular thermometric substance and property (e.g., mercury expansion) | Calibrated against fixed points like ice point and steam point |
| **Absolute (Thermodynamic) Temperature** | Defined through the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Carnot cycle efficiency) | Independent of any substance; SI unit is **Kelvin (K)** |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of temperature as "thermal level":**
