> Just as water flows from a higher elevation to a lower one, heat flows from a body at a higher temperature to a body at a lower temperature. Temperature is the *driving potential* for heat transfer — it is not heat itself, but rather the level of thermal energy density.

A simple analogy: If you pour hot coffee into a cup and place a metal spoon in it, the spoon handle gradually warms up. The spoon was at a lower temperature, and heat flowed into it until both reached the same temperature (thermal equilibrium). The **measurement of temperature** is therefore the process of quantifying this thermal level using a calibrated instrument (thermometer).

### Thermometric Properties

A **thermometric property** is any physical property that varies predictably and continuously with temperature. Common thermometric properties include:

- **Volume expansion** of liquids (used in mercury/ alcohol thermometers)
- **Pressure variation** of gases at constant volume (constant-volume gas thermometer)
- **Electrical resistance** of metals (RTD — Resistance Temperature Detector)
- **Thermoelectric EMF** generated at a junction of dissimilar metals (Thermocouple)
- **Radiative intensity** of a hot body (Pyrometers, for high temperatures)
- **Color** of emitted radiation (Optical pyrometers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$ — used extensively in thermodynamic temperature relations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Temperature Measurement

The process of temperature measurement involves three fundamental steps:

1. **Selection of a thermometric substance** (mercury, alcohol, hydrogen, nitrogen, platinum, etc.).
2. **Selection of a thermometric property** (volume, pressure, resistance, EMF, radiation).
3. **Establishment of a temperature scale** by assigning numerical values to reproducible physical states called **fixed points**.

### 2.2 Fixed Points

**Fixed points** are standard, reproducible temperatures used to calibrate thermometers. The two primary fixed points are:

- **Ice Point (Lower Fixed Point):** Temperature at which pure ice coexists in equilibrium with air-saturated water at standard atmospheric pressure. Defined as **0 °C (273.15 K)**.
- **Steam Point (Upper Fixed Point):** Temperature at which pure water boils at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa). Defined as **100 °C (373.15 K)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triple Point of Water (273.16 K / 0.01 °C):** The single temperature at which ice, liquid water, and water vapor coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. This is the *fundamental fixed point* of the International Temperature Scale (ITS-90).

### 2.3 Temperature Scales and Conversion Formulas

The relationship between any two linear temperature scales is:

$$t = a \cdot X + b$$

where $X$ is the value in the chosen scale, and $a$, $b$ are constants determined by the fixed points.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Conversion / Formula | Equation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Celsius to Kelvin | $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$ | K |
| 2 | Fahrenheit to Celsius | $t(°C) = \dfrac{5}{9}\left[t(°F) - 32\right]$ | °C |
| 3 | Celsius to Fahrenheit | $t(°F) = \dfrac{9}{5}\,t(°C) + 32$ | °F |
| 4 | Fahrenheit to Rankine | $T(°R) = t(°F) + 459.67$ | °R |
| 5 | General linear scale relation | $\dfrac{t_1 - t_{1,\text{lower}}}{t_{1,\text{upper}} - t_{1,\text{lower}}} = \dfrac{t_2 - t_{2,\text{lower}}}{t_{2,\text{upper}} - t_{2,\text{lower}}}$ | — |
| 6 | Ideal gas thermometric relation | $T = 273.16 \cdot \lim_{P_{tr} \to 0}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{tr}}\right)$ | K |
| 7 | Constant-volume gas thermometer | $T = 273.16 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{tr}}$ (at constant $V$) | K |
| 8 | Platinum RTD (linear approx.) | $R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha\, T\right)$ | Ω |
| 9 | Thermocouple EMF | $\mathcal{E} = a\,T + \dfrac{b\,T^2}{2}$ (approx., $T$ in °C) | mV |
| 10 | Planck radiation law | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$ | W/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Note:** $\vert K \vert = \vert °C \vert$ in terms of magnitude (size of degree). Only the reference point differs. This is why the *difference* in temperature is the same in K and °C: $\Delta T(K) = \Delta t(°C)$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Systems:** RTDs and thermocouples monitor air and fluid temperatures in chillers, boilers, and air-handling units.
- **Power Plants:** Thermocouples measure steam temperatures up to 1000 °C; radiation pyrometers measure flame temperatures up to 3500 °C.
- **Cryogenics:** Specialized gas thermometers and germanium RTDs measure temperatures as low as 0.001 K.
- **IC Engines:** Thermocouples in exhaust manifolds feed data to ECU for air–fuel ratio optimization.
- **Metallurgy:** Optical pyrometers measure molten metal temperatures without contact.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: General Linear Interpolation Between Two Scales

Let two temperature scales be defined by the same two fixed points (ice point and steam point). Let:

- Scale 1: ice point $= t_{1,L}$, steam point $= t_{1,U}$
- Scale 2: ice point $= t_{2,L}$, steam point $= t_{2,U}$

Since the scales are linear, any intermediate reading must satisfy the proportion:

$$\dfrac{t_2 - t_{2,L}}{t_{2,U} - t_{2,L}} = \dfrac{t_1 - t_{1,L}}{t_{1,U} - t_{1,L}}$$

Solving for $t_2$:

$$t_2 = t_{2,L} + (t_{2,U} - t_{2,L}) \cdot \dfrac{t_1 - t_{1,L}}{t_{1,U} - t_{1,L}}$$

Substituting Celsius (0–100) and Fahrenheit (32–212):

$$t(°F) = 32 + (212 - 32) \cdot \dfrac{t(°C) - 0}{100 - 0} = 32 + \dfrac{180}{100}\,t(°C)$$

$$\boxed{\,t(°F) = \dfrac{9}{5}\,t(°C) + 32\,}$$

Substituting Celsius (0–100) and Kelvin (273.15–373.15):

$$T(K) = 273.15 + (373.15 - 273.15) \cdot \dfrac{t(°C)}{100} = 273.15 + t(°C)$$

$$\boxed{\,T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Scale Conversion

**Problem:** A patient has a body temperature of $98.6\,°F$. Convert this to (a) °C and (b) K.

**Solution (a):** Using $t(°C) = \dfrac{5}{9}\left[t(°F) - 32\right]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t(°C) &= \dfrac{5}{9}\left[98.6 - 32\right] \\
       &= \dfrac{5}{9} \times 66.6 \\
       &= 37.0\,°C
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution (b):** Using $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$:

$$T(K) = 37.0 + 273.15 = 310.15\,K$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Constant-Volume Gas Thermometer Reading

**Problem:** A constant-volume gas thermometer has a pressure of $P_{tr} = 80\,\text{kPa}$ at the triple point of water. When placed in a furnace, the pressure becomes $P = 152\,\text{kPa}$. Determine the furnace temperature.

**Solution:** Using $T = 273.16 \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{tr}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 273.16 \times \dfrac{152}{80} \\
  &= 273.16 \times 1.9 \\
  &= 518.996\,K \\
  &\approx 519.0\,K \\
  &= 245.85\,°C
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Thermometers

| Property | Mercury Thermometer | Gas Thermometer | RTD (Platinum) | Thermocouple | Pyrometer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermometric property** | Volume expansion | Pressure variation | Electrical resistance | Thermoelectric EMF | Radiant intensity |
| **Range** | $-39\,°C$ to $357\,°C$ | $-270\,°C$ to $1500\,°C$ | $-200\,°C$ to $850\,°C$ | $-200\,°C$ to $2300\,°C$ | $700\,°C$ to $3500\,°C$ |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.1 °C | ±0.001 °C (primary standard) | ±0.1 °C | ±1–2 °C | ±5–10 °C |
| **Response time** | Slow (seconds) | Moderate | Fast | Very fast | Instantaneous |
| **Contact type** | Contact | Contact | Contact | Contact | Non-contact |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Best suited for** | Lab use | Standards lab | Industrial process | Industrial / engines | Furnaces, stars |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Temperature Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Measurement Methods] --> B[Contact Type]
    A --> C[Non-Contact Type]
    B --> D[Liquid in Glass]
    B --> E[Gas Thermometer]
    B --> F[Resistance Type RTD]
    B --> G[Thermocouple]
    B --> H[Bimetallic Strip]
    C --> I[Radiation Pyrometer]
    C --> J[Optical Pyrometer]
    C --> K[Infrared Pyrometer]
    D --> D1[Mercury]
    D --> D2[Alcohol]
    E --> E1[Constant Volume]
    E --> E2[Constant Pressure]
    F --> F1[Platinum]
    F --> F2[Nickel]
    F --> F3[Copper]
    G --> G1[Type K Chromel Alumel]
    G --> G2[Type J Iron Constantan]
    G --> G3[Type T Copper Constantan]
    G --> G4[Type R Platinum Rhodium]
    I --> I1[Total Radiation]
    I --> I2[Selective Band Pyrometer]
    K --> K1[Blackbody Emissivity Based]
    K --> K2[Thermal Imaging Camera]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Temperature Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Heat Source or Process] --> node2[Sensor Probe Sensing Element]
    node2 --> node3[Signal Conditioner Amplifier Filter]
    node3 --> node4[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    node4 --> node5[Microprocessor Signal Processing]
    node5 --> node6[Display Unit LCD LED]
    node5 --> node7[Output Interface 4 to 20 mA or Modbus]
    node8[Reference Standard ITS 90 Fixed Points] --> node5
    node9[Power Supply] --> node3
    node9 --> node4
    node9 --> node5
```

### 4.3 Thermocouple Measurement Loop (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hot Junction T Hot] --> nodeB[Conductor A e.g. Chromel]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reference Junction T Ref Ice Bath]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Conductor B e.g. Alumel]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Measuring Instrument Millivolt Meter]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Temperature Reading T Hot Computed]
    nodeG[Ice Bath 0 Degree Celsius Reference] --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics:**
> The block diagrams above depict the standard signal flow in any modern digital temperature measurement system. The reference standard (ITS-90 fixed points) is the calibration backbone ensuring traceability of every measurement to international standards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** *Distinguish between **empirical temperature** and **thermodynamic (absolute) temperature**. Why is the thermodynamic scale preferred in engineering applications?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Empirical temperature** is defined using a particular thermometric substance and its chosen thermometric property (e.g., volume of mercury). It depends on the substance used, so different thermometers can give slightly different readings for the same hot body. (1 mark)
- **Thermodynamic (absolute) temperature** is defined independently of any substance, using the Second Law of Thermodynamics and the efficiency of a Carnot cycle: $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$. It is the fundamental scientific scale. (1 mark)
- **Engineering Preference:** The thermodynamic scale (Kelvin) is preferred because it is *universal*, *reproducible*, and forms the basis of all thermodynamic relations such as the ideal gas law ($PV = nRT$) and the entropy relation ($dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$). (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *Define the **triple point of water**. Why is it chosen as the single fundamental fixed point of the International Temperature Scale (ITS-90)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **triple point of water** is the unique state at which pure ice, liquid water, and water vapor coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. It occurs at a temperature of **273.16 K (0.01 °C)** and a pressure of **611.657 Pa**. (1 mark)
- It is chosen as the fundamental fixed point because it is **uniquely reproducible** — a single combination of temperature and pressure simultaneously fixes all three phases, eliminating any dependence on barometric pressure or external atmospheric conditions. (1 mark)
- All other fixed points on the ITS-90 are referenced to this triple point, making it the cornerstone of modern thermometry. (1 mark)

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 – Internal Choice Pattern [KTU University Exam Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **mercury-in-glass thermometer**. Discuss its limitations and sources of error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A mercury-in-glass thermometer consists of a glass stem with a fine, uniform capillary bore, a bulb at one end containing mercury, and a sealed evacuated space above the mercury column.

**Construction:**
- **Bulb:** Thin-walled glass reservoir holding pure mercury. Acts as the sensing element. (1 mark)
- **Stem:** Thick-walled glass capillary with a fine uniform bore to ensure linear expansion with temperature. (1 mark)
- **Scale:** Engraved on the stem, calibrated against two fixed points (ice point and steam point). (1 mark)
- **Inert gas / vacuum:** Inert gas (nitrogen) filled above mercury to prevent evaporation at high temperatures. (0.5 marks)

**Working Principle:** Mercury expands uniformly with temperature rise. The expansion of mercury in the bulb is magnified and displayed in the narrow capillary, allowing precise reading. The relationship is:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta t$$

where $\beta$ is the volumetric expansion coefficient of mercury. (1 mark)

**Sources of Error / Limitations:**
1. **Inversion of mercury column** at high temperatures due to gas pressure issues.
2. **Stem exposure error** — if part of the stem is exposed to surroundings not at the measured temperature.
3. **Latent heat lag** — slow response time.
4. **Parallax error** while reading the scale.
5. **Limited range** — mercury freezes at $-38.83\,°C$ and boils at $356.7\,°C$. (2.5 marks)

**(b)** A platinum resistance thermometer has a resistance of $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at $0\,°C$ and $R_{100} = 139.16\,\Omega$ at $100\,°C$. If the resistance-temperature relationship is given by:

$$R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha\,T + \beta\,T^2\right)$$

where $T$ is in °C, calculate $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Also find the temperature when $R_T = 200\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

At $T = 100\,°C$:

$$139.16 = 100\left(1 + 100\alpha + 10000\beta\right)$$

$$1.3916 = 1 + 100\alpha + 10000\beta$$

$$100\alpha + 10000\beta = 0.3916 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

We need another equation. Using the standard value of $\alpha$ from the Callendar–Van Dusen equation, at $T=0$: $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ which gives $1 = 1$, a trivial equation.

Standard practice: Use $R_{100}/R_0 = 1.3916$ and the Callendar coefficient. For pure platinum, the standard mean coefficient is known. But here, using $T=0$ condition alone gives us only one equation. Apply the standard Callendar relation with the **ice-point value**:

For a platinum RTD, the linear approximation gives $\alpha \approx (R_{100} - R_0)/(100 \cdot R_0)$:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{139.16 - 100}{100 \times 100} = \dfrac{39.16}{10000} = 3.916 \times 10^{-3}\,°C^{-1}$$

Substituting into equation (i):

$$100 \times 3.916 \times 10^{-3} + 10000\beta = 0.3916$$

$$0.3916 + 10000\beta = 0.3916$$

$$\beta = 0$$

This indicates that *within the linear approximation*, the quadratic coefficient vanishes. Therefore, the platinum RTD behaves linearly:

$$R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T) = 100(1 + 3.916 \times 10^{-3}\,T)$$

To find $T$ when $R_T = 200\,\Omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
200 &= 100 \left(1 + 3.916 \times 10^{-3}\,T\right) \\
2 &= 1 + 3.916 \times 10^{-3}\,T \\
3.916 \times 10^{-3}\,T &= 1 \\
T &= \dfrac{1}{3.916 \times 10^{-3}} \\
T &= 255.36\,°C \quad \text{...(Answer)}
\end{aligned}$$

(7 marks — full marks distributed across derivation and substitution)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **Celsius** and **Fahrenheit** scales. A temperature of $-40\,°C$ corresponds to what value on the Fahrenheit scale? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $t(°C)$ and $t(°F)$ be the temperatures on the two scales. Using the linear relation $t(°F) = a \cdot t(°C) + b$:

**Condition 1:** At ice point, $t(°C) = 0$ and $t(°F) = 32$:
$$32 = a \cdot 0 + b \Rightarrow b = 32$$

**Condition 2:** At steam point, $t(°C) = 100$ and $t(°F) = 212$:
$$212 = 100a + 32 \Rightarrow 100a = 180 \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{9}{5}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,t(°F) = \dfrac{9}{5}\,t(°C) + 32\,}$$

(3 marks for derivation)

Now substituting $t(°C) = -40$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t(°F) &= \dfrac{9}{5} \times (-40) + 32 \\
      &= -72 + 32 \\
      &= -40\,°F
\end{aligned}$$

**Remarkable fact:** $-40\,°C = -40\,°F$ — this is the only temperature at which the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales coincide. (1 mark)

(7 marks — derivation, substitution, result)

**(b)** Explain the working of a **thermoelectric (thermocouple) thermometer**. State **Seebeck**, **Peltier**, and **Thomson effects**. List the laws governing thermocouple circuits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Thermocouple Construction:**
A thermocouple consists of two dissimilar metal wires joined at one end (the hot / measuring junction). The other ends form the cold / reference junction. A millivolt meter or ADC measures the EMF generated. (1 mark)

**Working Principle (Seebeck Effect):**
When two dissimilar metals are joined and the junction is heated, an EMF is generated that depends on the temperature difference between the hot junction and the cold (reference) junction. The magnitude of this EMF is given by:

$$\mathcal{E} = a\,(T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{ref}}) + \dfrac{b}{2}\,(T_{\text{hot}}^2 - T_{\text{ref}}^2)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are Seebeck coefficients of the materials. (1 mark)

**Three Thermoelectric Effects:**

- **Seebeck Effect:** EMF generated due to a temperature gradient at the junction of two dissimilar metals. (0.5 marks)
- **Peltier Effect:** Heat absorbed or evolved at a junction when an electric current flows through it. (0.5 marks)
- **Thomson Effect:** Heat absorbed or evolved in a single conductor when an electric current flows through it in the presence of a temperature gradient. (0.5 marks)

**Laws of Thermocouple Circuits:**

1. **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into the circuit does not alter the EMF, provided both junctions with the third metal are at the same temperature.
2. **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF generated with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs generated with junctions at $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_3)$.
3. **Law of Homogeneous Circuit:** A current flowing in a homogeneous conductor does not produce an EMF effect; only junctions of dissimilar metals generate EMF. (2.5 marks)

(7 marks — full coverage of construction, EMF relation, three effects, three laws)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing temperature with heat:** Marks are lost when students write "temperature is the amount of heat" — *temperature is the degree of hotness, not the quantity of heat.*
> 2. **Skipping the reference junction concept:** In thermocouple problems, students often forget to account for the cold-junction compensation, leading to wrong EMF readings.
> 3. **Sign errors in scale conversion:** Use the algebraic form carefully — for example, $-40\,°F$ conversion yields $-40\,°C$, which is a frequent verification check.
> 4. **Mixing up fixed points:** The *ice point* (0 °C) and *triple point* (0.01 °C) are different — KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** All final answers must clearly state units (K, °C, °F, °R). Marks are deducted for missing units.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivations, students should explicitly state that the scales are linear and that the thermometric property varies linearly with temperature.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Temperature** is an **intensive, scalar thermodynamic property** indicating the degree of hotness. It is *not* heat.
- The **Zeroth Law** defines temperature as the property governing thermal equilibrium.
- **Four primary scales:** Celsius (°C), Kelvin (K), Fahrenheit (°F), Rankine (°R).
- **Conversion formulas (memorize):**
  - $K = °C + 273.15$
  - $°F = (9/5)\,°C + 32$
  - $°R = °F + 459.67$
  - $°R = (9/5)\,K$
- **Two primary fixed points:** Ice point (0 °C / 273.15 K) and Steam point (100 °C / 373.15 K).
- **Triple point of water:** 273.16 K (0.01 °C) — the fundamental fixed point of ITS-90.
- **Five major classes of thermometers:** Liquid-in-glass, Gas, RTD, Thermocouple, Pyrometer.
- **Three thermoelectric effects:** Seebeck, Peltier, Thomson — KTU loves to ask these in Part A.
- **Three laws of thermocouple circuits:** Law of Intermediate Metals, Law of Intermediate Temperatures, Law of Homogeneous Circuit.
- **Magnitude identity:** $\Delta T(\text{K}) = \Delta t(°C)$ — only the *reference* differs, not the *degree size*.
- **$-40\,°C = -40\,°F$** — the only intersection point of Celsius and Fahrenheit scales.
- **Selection criteria** for a thermometer: range, accuracy, response time, cost, contact vs. non-contact.
- **Mercury** is preferred over alcohol in liquid-in-glass thermometers because of its *uniform expansion*, *non-wetting of glass*, *high boiling point*, and *visibility*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reference Points
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reference Points

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In engineering thermodynamics, **Reference Points** are precisely defined, universally agreed-upon states of a substance (or system) that serve as a benchmark from which the magnitude of thermodynamic properties — particularly **specific enthalpy ($h$)**, **specific internal energy ($u$)**, and **specific entropy ($s$)** — are measured, tabulated, and calculated.

Unlike temperature and pressure, which can be measured absolutely, properties such as internal energy, enthalpy, and entropy are **relative quantities** — only their *changes* are physically measurable. Therefore, an arbitrary but internationally standardized datum (reference point) is fixed, and property values are assigned relative to this datum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A reference point is a fixed state assigned to a substance at which the values of certain thermodynamic properties (usually $u$, $h$, $s$) are arbitrarily set to zero, and all other values are measured relative to it.

The two most important international reference points in thermodynamics are:

| Reference Point | Symbol | Temperature | Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triple Point of Water** | TPW | **273.16 K** (exact, by definition) | **611.657 Pa** (0.006112 bar) |
| **Normal Boiling Point / Steam Point** | NBP | 373.15 K | 101.325 kPa |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Sea Level and Altitude**
>
> Imagine telling a friend, "I climbed 1,500 metres today." The friend immediately understands your effort because altitude is universally measured from **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** — a globally agreed reference. If each country used a different starting point (say, the nearest hill), elevation maps would be utterly meaningless.
>
> Reference points in thermodynamics serve the **exact same purpose**: they give engineers a *common language* to communicate property values. Without a reference, no two engineers could ever compare their enthalpy or entropy readings.

Another helpful analogy is a **bank account balance**:

- Your actual wealth (absolute value) is unknown without knowing your starting balance.
- Banks always show *"Balance: ₹X"* — this is the amount relative to the moment your account was opened at ₹0.
- The opening balance is your **reference point**.

Similarly, when a steam table says "enthalpy of saturated liquid at 100 °C is 419.04 kJ/kg", that value is **measured from the reference state of saturated liquid water at 0 °C** where $h_f$ is conventionally taken as zero.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The most important datum values used universally:

- **Triple Point of Water Temperature:** $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K (this is the **exact, fixed** value used to define the Kelvin scale)
- **Ice Point:** $T_{ice} = 273.15$ K = 0 °C at 1 atm
- **Steam Point:** $T_{steam} = 373.15$ K = 100 °C at 1 atm
- **Standard Ambient Temperature and Pressure (SATP):** 298.15 K, 100 kPa
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** 273.15 K, 101.325 kPa
- **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP):** 293.15 K, 101.325 kPa

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board Note:** The triple point of water is *not* 273.15 K — it is exactly **273.16 K**. Many students lose marks by confusing this with the ice point. The difference of 0.01 K arises because the ice point (water-ice equilibrium at 1 atm) is slightly lower than the true triple point (water-ice-vapour equilibrium at 611.657 Pa).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Reference Points

Reference points can be classified into three broad categories:

### (a) Reference Points for Temperature Scales
Used to calibrate temperature measuring instruments.

- **Celsius Scale:** Defined by two fixed points — ice point (0 °C) and steam point (100 °C) at standard atmospheric pressure.
- **Kelvin Scale (Absolute):** Uses the triple point of water as the single defining fixed point. $T = 273.16$ K at the triple point.
- **Fahrenheit Scale:** Ice point = 32 °F, Steam point = 212 °F.

### (b) Reference States for Property Tables
Used in steam tables, refrigerant tables, ideal-gas tables.

- **Steam Tables (Cengel & Boles convention):** Saturated liquid water at **0.01 °C (273.16 K)** is the reference state. Here:
  - $h_f = 0$ kJ/kg
  - $u_f = 0$ kJ/kg
  - $s_f = 0$ kJ/kg·K
- **Refrigerant Tables:** Saturated liquid refrigerant at **−40 °C** is typically chosen as the reference state.
- **Ideal Gas Tables (e.g., air tables):** Reference state is taken at **0 K**, where $u = 0$, $h = 0$, and $s^\circ = 0$. Property values are then computed using $\Delta u = c_v \Delta T$ and $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$.

### (c) Reference Points for Process Calculations

- **Dead State:** The state at which a system is in complete thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings ($T = T_0$, $P = P_0$). All exergy is referenced to the dead state.
- **Restricted Dead State:** Useful when chemical equilibrium is not fully attained with the environment.

---

## 2.2 Why Reference Points are Necessary (The "Why")

The second law of thermodynamics, through the Clausius inequality, allows us to **only define entropy in terms of a definite integral**, which requires a baseline:

$$s_2 - s_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$

Similarly, internal energy and enthalpy are path-independent state functions whose absolute values are unmeasurable. Only differences $\Delta u$ and $\Delta h$ are physically meaningful.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When a question asks "Why can't we assign an absolute value to internal energy?", the examiner wants you to mention:
> 1. Internal energy includes microscopic kinetic + potential energies of all molecules, which is impractical to count.
> 2. Only changes $\Delta u$ are measurable (via the First Law: $\Delta u = q - w$).
> 3. Hence, we must pick an arbitrary reference state and measure everything from it.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relation between Celsius and Kelvin | $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$ | General conversion |
| 2 | Relation between Kelvin and Celsius | $t(°C) = T(K) - 273.15$ | General conversion |
| 3 | Reference state enthalpy (steam) | $h_{f@273.16K} = 0$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Reference state entropy (steam) | $s_{f@273.16K} = 0$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | Reference state internal energy | $u_{f@273.16K} = 0$ | kJ/kg |
| 6 | Change in enthalpy (ideal gas) | $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$ | kJ/kg |
| 7 | Change in internal energy (ideal gas) | $\Delta u = c_v \Delta T$ | kJ/kg |
| 8 | Change in entropy (ideal gas, constant $c_p$) | $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | kJ/kg·K |
| 9 | Triple Point Temperature (exact) | $T_{tp} = 273.16$ K | Kelvin scale defined here |
| 10 | Absolute zero (lower reference) | $T = 0$ K = −273.15 °C | Theoretical limit |
| 11 | Relation between Fahrenheit-Celsius | $°F = (9/5) \times °C + 32$ | Empirical |
| 12 | Relation between Rankine-Kelvin | $°R = 1.8 \times K$ | Absolute scale conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:**
> Always use **uppercase $T$** for absolute temperatures (Kelvin, Rankine) and **lowercase $t$** for relative temperatures (Celsius, Fahrenheit). Examiners deduct marks for this confusion. The same applies to $P$ (absolute) vs $p$ (gauge/relative).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Power Plants:** Engineers use reference-state enthalpies from steam tables to calculate turbine work ($w_T = h_1 - h_2$), heat added ($q_{in} = h_2 - h_1$), and Rankine cycle efficiency.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Refrigerant property tables (R-134a, R-410A) reference saturated liquid at −40 °C; without this, no refrigeration cycle could be designed.
- **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines:** Ideal-gas tables for air (referenced to 0 K) are used to compute $\Delta h$ and $\Delta s$ across compressors and turbines.
- **Cryogenics:** Reference at absolute zero is essential when dealing with liquefied gases (LNG, liquid nitrogen).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Standard reference state for enthalpy of formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$) is the **most stable form of an element at 25 °C and 1 atm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Reference-Based and Absolute Temperatures

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Experimentally, the volume of an ideal gas at constant pressure varies linearly with temperature:

$$V = V_0 (1 + \alpha \, t)$$

where $\alpha = 1/273.15$ is the volumetric coefficient of expansion, $V_0$ is the volume at 0 °C, and $t$ is the Celsius temperature.

### Step 2 — Predicting Absolute Zero
Setting $V = 0$ to find the temperature where gas volume would vanish:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= V_0 (1 + \alpha \, t_{abs}) \\
1 + \alpha \, t_{abs} &= 0 \\
t_{abs} &= -\frac{1}{\alpha} \\
t_{abs} &= -\frac{1}{1/273.15} \\
t_{abs} &= -273.15 \; °C
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Defining the Kelvin Scale
To eliminate negative values, Lord Kelvin proposed shifting the zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(K) &= t(°C) + 273.15 \\
T(K) &= t(°C) + \frac{1}{\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the **ice point** corresponds to $T = 273.15$ K and the **steam point** to $T = 373.15$ K.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reference-State Entropy Definition (Planck's Formulation)

### Step 1 — Clausius Definition
For any reversible process:

$$ds = \frac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}$$

### Step 2 — Integrating from Reference State
Choosing a reference state $(T_0, P_0)$ and integrating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(T, P) - s(T_0, P_0) &= \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{c_p(T)}{T} dT - R \ln\frac{P}{P_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Assigning Zero at Reference
By convention, we set $s(T_0, P_0) = 0$, which gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(T, P) &= \int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{c_p(T)}{T} dT - R \ln\frac{P}{P_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Ideal-Gas Standard-Entropy Form
For constant $c_p$ (a common textbook simplification):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(T, P) &= c_p \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely the formulation used in the **standard air tables** (referenced at $T_0 = 0$ K, $P_0 = 1$ atm, with $s^\circ = 0$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Exam Numerical Problems

### Problem Type 1 — Temperature Conversion
A gas cylinder is stored at $t = -45 \; °C$. Express the temperature in:
(a) Kelvin
(b) Rankine

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(K) &= t(°C) + 273.15 \\
T(K) &= -45 + 273.15 \\
T(K) &= 228.15 \; K
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(°R) &= T(K) \times 1.8 \\
T(°R) &= 228.15 \times 1.8 \\
T(°R) &= 410.67 \; °R
\end{aligned}
$$

### Problem Type 2 — Finding Reference-State Enthalpy
Saturated liquid water at 0.01 °C has a sensible heat reference. If the specific enthalpy at 100 °C (saturated liquid) is $h_f = 419.04$ kJ/kg, calculate the change in enthalpy when 2 kg of water is heated from 0.01 °C to 100 °C.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= h_{f,100} - h_{f,0.01} \\
\Delta h &= 419.04 - 0 \\
\Delta h &= 419.04 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= m \times \Delta h \\
\Delta H &= 2 \times 419.04 \\
\Delta H &= 838.08 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Problem Type 3 — Triple Point vs Ice Point Distinction
Calculate the difference in absolute temperature between the ice point and the triple point of water.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{ice} - T_{tp} \\
\Delta T &= 273.15 - 273.16 \\
\Delta T &= -0.01 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

The ice point is **0.01 K below** the triple point.

### Problem Type 4 — Property Calculation with Reference State (Ideal Gas)
For air at 500 K and 200 kPa, calculate the specific entropy relative to a reference state of 300 K and 100 kPa. Given $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_2 - s_1 &= c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\
s_2 - s_1 &= 1.005 \times \ln\left(\frac{500}{300}\right) - 0.287 \times \ln\left(\frac{200}{100}\right) \\
s_2 - s_1 &= 1.005 \times 0.5108 - 0.287 \times 0.6931 \\
s_2 - s_1 &= 0.5134 - 0.1989 \\
s_2 - s_1 &= 0.3145 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Property-Reference Decision Matrix

| Substance / System | Reference Property | Reference Temperature | Reference Pressure | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water (Steam Tables) | $h_f$, $s_f$, $u_f$ = 0 | 0.01 °C (273.16 K) | 0.6113 kPa (sat. press.) | Power plants, boilers |
| Air (Ideal Gas Tables) | $h$, $u$, $s^\circ$ relative to 0 K | 0 K | 1 atm (101.325 kPa) | Gas turbines, jet engines |
| Refrigerant R-134a | $h_f$ = 0 at sat. liquid | −40 °C | saturation pressure | Refrigeration, AC |
| Ammonia (NH₃) | $h_f$ = 0 at sat. liquid | −40 °C | saturation pressure | Industrial refrigeration |
| Combustion Products | $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for stable element at reference | 25 °C (298.15 K) | 1 atm (101.325 kPa) | Combustion analysis |
| Cryogenic Gases (O₂, N₂) | $h_f = 0$ at sat. liquid at normal boiling point | −182.96 °C (O₂), −195.79 °C (N₂) | 1 atm | LNG plants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Reference Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[THERMODYNAMIC REFERENCE POINTS] --> B[Temperature Reference Points]
    A --> C[Property Reference States]
    A --> D[Process Reference States]

    B --> B1[Triple Point of Water - 273.16 K]
    B --> B2[Ice Point - 273.15 K at 1 atm]
    B --> B3[Steam Point - 373.15 K at 1 atm]
    B --> B4[Absolute Zero - 0 K theoretical]

    C --> C1[Steam Tables - Sat Liq at 0.01 C]
    C --> C2[Refrigerant Tables - Sat Liq at minus 40 C]
    C --> C3[Ideal Gas Tables - 0 K datum]
    C --> C4[Combustion - 25 C and 1 atm]

    D --> D1[Dead State - equilibrium with surroundings]
    D --> D2[Restricted Dead State]
    D --> D3[Atmospheric Reference - 1 atm 25 C]

    C1 --> E1[h sub f equals 0]
    C1 --> E2[s sub f equals 0]
    C1 --> E3[u sub f equals 0]
    C2 --> F1[h sub f equals 0 at minus 40 C]
    C3 --> G1[h equals 0 at 0 K]
    C3 --> G2[s sup 0 equals 0 at 0 K]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Reference Point Usage in a Rankine Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Boiler[BOILER]
        S1[State 1 - Sat Liq 0.01 C]
        S2[State 2 - Sat Liq at T2]
        S3[State 3 - Sat Vap at T3]
        S4[State 4 - Superheated at T4]
    end

    subgraph Reference[REFERENCE DATUM]
        R[Sat Liq at 0.01 C, h equals 0, s equals 0]
    end

    subgraph Turbine[TURBINE]
        S5[State 5 - Two phase exit]
    end

    subgraph Condenser[CONDENSER]
        S6[State 6 - Sat Liq at low T]
    end

    subgraph Pump[PUMP]
        S7[State 7 - Compressed Liq]
    end

    R -. Defines baseline for .-> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S1

    S4 --> H1[h equals 3433.8 kJ per kg]
    S5 --> H2[h equals 2380.3 kJ per kg]
    H1 --> WT[Turbine Work Wt equals h4 minus h5]
    H2 --> WT
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Reference to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the substance a pure substance?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, water| A1[Use Steam Tables - ref at 0.01 C]
    Q1 -->|Yes, refrigerant| A2[Use Refrigerant Tables - ref at minus 40 C]
    Q1 -->|Yes, ideal gas| A3[Use Ideal Gas Tables - ref at 0 K]
    Q1 -->|No, combustion| A4[Use Combustion Tables - ref at 25 C and 1 atm]

    A1 --> B1[hf, sf, uf all equal 0 at reference]
    A2 --> B2[hf equals 0 at reference]
    A3 --> B3[u equals 0 and h equals 0 at 0 K]
    A4 --> B4[Delta H formation equals 0 for stable elements]

    B1 --> C1[Read h and s for given state]
    B2 --> C2[Read h and s for given state]
    B3 --> C3[Compute delta h equals cp times delta T]
    B4 --> C4[Sum up h products minus h reactants]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Define reference point. Why is it necessary to fix a reference point for measuring thermodynamic properties such as internal energy, enthalpy, and entropy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Pattern):**

A reference point in thermodynamics is a fixed state assigned to a substance at which the values of selected thermodynamic properties are **arbitrarily set to zero**, against which all other values are measured.

It is necessary because:
1. **Internal energy ($u$), enthalpy ($h$), and entropy ($s$) are relative properties** — their absolute values cannot be determined experimentally. Only *changes* ($\Delta u$, $\Delta h$, $\Delta s$) can be measured (via the First Law and Clausius inequality).
2. To compute these changes numerically (e.g., from steam tables or ideal-gas tables), a **common datum** is required, so that all engineers across the world use the same starting point.
3. For steam tables, the reference state is **saturated liquid water at 0.01 °C (273.16 K)**, where $h_f = 0$, $u_f = 0$, and $s_f = 0$.
4. Without a fixed reference, comparison of thermodynamic data would be impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Tip:** Many students forget to explicitly mention that only *changes* are measurable — examiners specifically look for this phrasing. Award full 3 marks only when both the definition AND the measurability argument are present.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**
**Distinguish clearly between the triple point of water and the ice point. State their temperatures in Kelvin.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Pattern):**

| Property | Triple Point of Water | Ice Point |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | State where solid, liquid, and vapour phases of water coexist in equilibrium | Temperature at which pure water freezes at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm) |
| Temperature | **273.16 K** (exact, by SI definition) | **273.15 K** |
| Pressure | 611.657 Pa | 101.325 kPa |
| Phases present | Solid + Liquid + Vapour | Solid + Liquid (in equilibrium with air) |

The triple point is the *fundamental fixed point* used to define the Kelvin temperature scale. The ice point is a *practical reference* used historically for the Celsius scale.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the triple point of water. Explain why the triple point of water is chosen as the fundamental reference for the thermodynamic temperature scale, rather than the ice point or steam point.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 kg of water initially as saturated liquid at 0.01 °C. Heat is added until the water becomes saturated vapour at the same temperature. Using steam tables, determine:
   (i) The total change in enthalpy $\Delta H$ (in kJ).
   (ii) The total change in entropy $\Delta S$ (in kJ/K).
   (iii) Comment on the choice of reference point in solving this problem.

**Standard Values from Steam Tables at 0.01 °C:**
- $h_f = 0.00$ kJ/kg, $\;h_g = 2506.5$ kJ/kg
- $s_f = 0.0000$ kJ/kg·K, $\;s_g = 9.1562$ kJ/kg·K

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition [2 Marks]:**
The triple point of water is the unique thermodynamic state at which the solid (ice), liquid (water), and gaseous (water vapour) phases of pure water coexist in **mutual thermodynamic equilibrium**. At this point, all three phases have the same temperature and pressure simultaneously. Its values are exactly:

$$
T_{tp} = 273.16 \; \text{K}, \quad P_{tp} = 611.657 \; \text{Pa}
$$

**Why triple point is preferred [5 Marks]:**
1. **Uniqueness:** The triple point is a single, unique state — there is only one combination of $T$ and $P$ at which all three phases coexist. In contrast, the ice point and steam point depend on the ambient pressure (which varies slightly with weather and altitude), introducing measurement uncertainty.
2. **Reproducibility:** A triple-point cell can be constructed in any laboratory and achieves better than ±0.0001 K accuracy. Ice and steam points require precise pressure control (1 atm ± uncertainty).
3. **SI Definition:** Since 1954, the Kelvin has been *defined* by declaring the triple point of water to be exactly 273.16 K. This makes the Kelvin independent of any other property.
4. **No Air Dependency:** The triple point is measured in a sealed evacuated cell, so dissolved gases and atmospheric composition do not affect the reading.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Change in enthalpy [3 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= h_g - h_f \\
\Delta h &= 2506.5 - 0.00 \\
\Delta h &= 2506.5 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta H &= m \times \Delta h \\
\Delta H &= 0.5 \times 2506.5 \\
\Delta H &= 1253.25 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Change in entropy [3 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &= s_g - s_f \\
\Delta s &= 9.1562 - 0.0000 \\
\Delta s &= 9.1562 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= m \times \Delta s \\
\Delta S &= 0.5 \times 9.1562 \\
\Delta S &= 4.5781 \; \text{kJ/K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Comment on Reference Point [1 Mark]:**

Since the initial state (saturated liquid at 0.01 °C) is **itself the reference state** where $h_f = 0$ and $s_f = 0$, the calculated enthalpy and entropy values of the final state *are* the absolute values relative to the reference datum. This is the unique advantage of having the reference state coincide with one of the process states.

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the different reference points used in:
   (i) Steam tables
   (ii) Refrigerant tables (R-134a)
   (iii) Ideal-gas air tables
   (iv) Combustion analysis
   
   Tabulate the assigned zero property at each reference.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An ideal gas initially at state 1 ($T_1 = 350$ K, $P_1 = 150$ kPa) is compressed to state 2 ($T_2 = 500$ K, $P_2 = 400$ kPa). Using the ideal-gas reference state at 0 K with $s^\circ = 0$ at 1 atm, and given $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, calculate the change in specific enthalpy $\Delta h$, change in specific internal energy $\Delta u$, and change in specific entropy $\Delta s$ (per kg of gas).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Tabulated explanation:**

| Reference Table | Reference State | Properties Set to Zero | Why This Reference? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steam Tables** (Cengel convention) | Saturated liquid water at **0.01 °C (273.16 K)** | $h_f = 0$, $s_f = 0$, $u_f = 0$ | Coincides with the natural phase-change region; conveniently gives small positive values for typical processes. |
| **Refrigerant Tables** (R-134a, R-12) | Saturated liquid refrigerant at **−40 °C** | $h_f = 0$ | Avoids negative $h$ values in the operating range of most refrigeration cycles. |
| **Ideal Gas Air Tables** | **0 K** | $u = 0$, $h = 0$, $s^\circ = 0$ at $P = 1$ atm | Allows direct integration of $\Delta u = c_v \Delta T$ and $\Delta h = c_p \Delta T$ from absolute zero. |
| **Combustion Tables** | **25 °C (298.15 K), 1 atm** — most stable form of element | $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$, $\Delta G_f^\circ = 0$ | Standard state for thermochemistry; consistent with most laboratory reactions. |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Change in specific enthalpy [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= c_p \Delta T \\
\Delta h &= 1.005 \times (500 - 350) \\
\Delta h &= 1.005 \times 150 \\
\Delta h &= 150.75 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Change in specific internal energy [2 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u &= c_v \Delta T \\
c_v &= c_p - R = 1.005 - 0.287 = 0.718 \; \text{kJ/kg·K} \\
\Delta u &= 0.718 \times 150 \\
\Delta u &= 107.7 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Change in specific entropy [3 Marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta s &= c_p \ln\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \\
\Delta s &= 1.005 \times \ln\left(\frac{500}{350}\right) - 0.287 \times \ln\left(\frac{400}{150}\right) \\
\Delta s &= 1.005 \times 0.3567 - 0.287 \times 0.9808 \\
\Delta s &= 0.3585 - 0.2815 \\
\Delta s &= 0.0770 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Ice Point with Triple Point:** Many students write 273.16 °C instead of 273.16 K. Triple point is **273.16 K**, not °C. −1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the Reference State in Entropy Calculations:** If a problem asks "what is the entropy at state 2?", you cannot give a single number — entropy is *always* relative to a reference. −2 marks.
> 3. **Using Lowercase $t$ for Absolute Temperatures in Formulas:** The gas law $PV = mRT$ requires $T$ in Kelvin. Substituting Celsius directly will give the wrong answer. −1 mark.
> 4. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** Always write kJ/kg·K for entropy, K for temperature. KTU board examiners are strict about SI units.
> 5. **Using $C_v$ instead of $c_v$:** For per-unit-mass calculations, use lowercase specific heats ($c_p$, $c_v$). Uppercase ($C_p$, $C_v$) is molar. −1 mark.
> 6. **Stating "$h_f = 0$ for steam" without mentioning the temperature (0.01 °C) and state (saturated liquid):** −1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **reference point** is an arbitrary but universally fixed datum state of a substance where selected thermodynamic properties ($h$, $u$, $s$) are set to **zero**.
- [x] Reference points are necessary because $u$, $h$, $s$ are **relative** properties — only their *changes* are physically measurable.
- [x] The **Triple Point of Water** = 273.16 K (exact) is the SI-defined fundamental reference for the Kelvin scale.
- [x] The **Ice Point** = 273.15 K and **Steam Point** = 373.15 K are practical references at 1 atm.
- [x] **Steam Tables** use saturated liquid water at **0.01 °C (273.16 K)** as the reference state — all $h_f$, $s_f$, $u_f$ are zero there.
- [x] **Refrigerant Tables** use saturated liquid refrigerant at **−40 °C** as the reference state.
- [x] **Ideal Gas Tables** use **0 K** as the reference (with $s^\circ = 0$ at 1 atm).
- [x] **Combustion Tables** use **25 °C, 1 atm** with $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for elements in their stable form.
- [x] Always use **uppercase $T$** for absolute (Kelvin, Rankine) and **lowercase $t$** for relative (Celsius, Fahrenheit) temperatures.
- [x] Conversion formulas: $T(K) = t(°C) + 273.15$; $T(°R) = T(K) \times 1.8$; $°F = (9/5)°C + 32$.
- [x] Absolute zero = **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F** — unattainable in practice (Third Law of Thermodynamics).
- [x] Standard Ambient: SATP = 25 °C, 100 kPa; STP = 0 °C, 101.325 kPa; NTP = 20 °C, 101.325 kPa.
- [x] The **dead state** is the reference for exergy calculations — the state of complete thermodynamic equilibrium with the environment.
- [x] Reference points do not affect physical predictions — they only affect numerical values. Any consistent reference is acceptable in principle; the chosen one is purely conventional.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Scales
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Temperature Scales # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Temperature? **Temperature** is a fundamental physical quantity that quantitatively expresses the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. In thermodynamics, it is defined as the property that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies placed in thermal contact. Heat always flows from the body at **higher temperature** to the body at **lower temperature** until thermal equilibrium is reached. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** Temperature is a thermodynamic property which determines whether a system is in thermal equilibrium with other systems. Two systems are in thermal equilibrium if and only if they have the **same temperature** (Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Think of temperature as a **"ladder of hotness."** Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to arrange five buckets of water from coldest to hottest. You would dip your hand in each and rank them. Temperature is the *number* we assign to that subjective feeling of hotness. Just like we use a ruler to assign numbers to length, we use a **thermometer** to assign numbers to hotness. But here is the catch: the *feeling* of "twice as hot" is not accurate. So scientists needed a **reproducible, universal standard** — this is why we have multiple temperature scales. Each scale is essentially a different "ruler" with different zero points and different sizes of a degree. ## 1.3 The Zeroth Law and the Need for a Scale > [!NOTE] > **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics:** If body A is in thermal equilibrium with body B, and body B is in thermal equilibrium with body C, then body A and body C are also in thermal equilibrium. This transitive property allows us to use a thermometer as a **third reference body** to measure the temperature of any other body. This law gives temperature its meaning as a measurable, comparable quantity and is the *prerequisite* for constructing any temperature scale. ## 1.4 Thermometric Property To construct a temperature scale, we choose a physical property that varies **linearly and continuously** with hotness. This is called a **thermometric property** (e.g., length of a mercury column, pressure of a gas, resistance of a platinum wire, EMF of a thermocouple). The general relationship is: $$T = a \cdot X + b$$ where $X$ is the value of the thermometric property, and $a, b$ are constants fixed by assigning numerical values to two **fixed points** (reference temperatures). ## 1.5 Fixed Points (Reference Temperatures) > [!IMPORTANT] > A temperature scale requires **two reproducible fixed points** to be defined uniquely: > - **Lower Fixed Point (Ice Point):** The temperature of pure melting ice at standard atmospheric pressure (**1 atm = 101.325 kPa**) in equilibrium with air-saturated water. On the Celsius scale = **0 °C**. > - **Upper Fixed Point (Steam Point):** The temperature of pure boiling water at standard atmospheric pressure (**1 atm**). On the Celsius scale = **100 °C**. The interval between these two points is divided into a chosen number of **degrees**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Temperature Scales Used in Engineering | Scale | Symbol | Lower Fixed Point | Upper Fixed Point | Fundamental Interval | Absolute Zero on Scale | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Celsius (Centigrade) | °C | 0 °C | 100 °C | 100 | −273.15 °C | | Fahrenheit | °F | 32 °F | 212 °F | 180 | −459.67 °F | | Kelvin (Absolute) | K | 273.15 K | 373.15 K | 100 | 0 K | | Rankine (Absolute) | °R | 491.67 °R | 671.67 °R | 180 | 0 °R | | Réaumur | °Ré | 0 °Ré | 80 °Ré | 80 | −218.52 °Ré | > [!NOTE] > **Absolute scales (Kelvin and Rankine)** start at absolute zero, the theoretical temperature at which all molecular motion ceases. They are used in **thermodynamic calculations** because they avoid negative values. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### A. Direct Conversion Formulas | Conversion | Formula | |---|---| | Celsius → Fahrenheit | $F = \dfrac{9}{5} C + 32$ | | Fahrenheit → Celsius | $C = \dfrac{5}{9}(F - 32)$ | | Celsius → Kelvin | $K = C + 273.15$ | | Kelvin → Celsius | $C = K - 273.15$ | | Fahrenheit → Rankine | $R = F + 459.67$ | | Rankine → Fahrenheit | $F = R - 459.67$ | | Kelvin → Rankine | $R = \dfrac{9}{5} K$ | | Rankine → Kelvin | $K = \dfrac{5}{9} R$ | | Celsius → Réaumur | $Re = \dfrac{4}{5} C$ | | Réaumur → Celsius | $C = \dfrac{5}{4} Re$ | ### B. General Linear Conversion Formula (Master Formula) If a temperature scale $S$ has its lower fixed point at $L_S$ and upper fixed point at $U_S$ on its own scale, then any temperature reading $T_S$ corresponds to a Celsius temperature $C$ as: $$T_S = L_S + \dfrac{(U_S - L_S)}{100} \cdot C$$ Equivalently, for conversion between **any two scales** $S_1$ and $S_2$: $$\dfrac{T_{S_1} - L_{S_1}}{U_{S_1} - L_{S_1}} = \dfrac{T_{S_2} - L_{S_2}}{U_{S_2} - L_{S_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to use this:** The left side represents the *fractional position* of the temperature between the two fixed points on scale $S_1$. The right side represents the *same fractional position* on scale $S_2$. They must be equal because they describe the **same physical state**. ### C. Key Numerical Values (Memorize These) | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Triple point of water | **273.16 K** (exact) | | Absolute zero | **0 K = −273.15 °C** | | Standard atmospheric pressure | **1 atm = 101.325 kPa** | | 1 °C interval | = 1 K interval = 1.8 °F interval = 1.8 °R interval | | 1 °F interval | = 5/9 °C interval = 5/9 K interval | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility - **Celsius and Kelvin** are used in **scientific, SI-based engineering** work (heat transfer, power plants, refrigeration cycles). - **Fahrenheit and Rankine** are still used in **US customary systems** (HVAC, petrochemical in the US, some aerospace). - **Rankine** is the **absolute Fahrenheit** — used in US thermodynamic calculations. - **Réaumur** is now mostly **obsolete** but historically appeared in European sugar and alcohol industries. > [!TIP] > **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** If the answer to a temperature feels negative but the formula gives a positive number, you have likely **forgotten the offset** (e.g., the "+32" in F→C). Always check the offset separately from the multiplier. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Conversion Formula ### Setup Consider two temperature scales, **Scale A** and **Scale B**. Both measure the same physical hotness, but they may have: - Different **zero references** (e.g., Scale A: ice point = 0, Scale B: ice point = 32) - Different **degree sizes** (e.g., Scale A: 100 divisions, Scale B: 180 divisions) **Given Data:** - Lower fixed point on Scale A = $L_A$ - Upper fixed point on Scale A = $U_A$ - Lower fixed point on Scale B = $L_B$ - Upper fixed point on Scale B = $U_B$ ### Step 1: Define the Fractional Position When a body is at some intermediate temperature, it lies *somewhere* between the ice point and the steam point. The fraction of the way it is from the ice point to the steam point must be the same on both scales, because they describe the same physical state. Let $T_A$ and $T_B$ be the temperature readings of the body on scales A and B respectively. **Fractional position on Scale A:** $$f_A = \dfrac{T_A - L_A}{U_A - L_A}$$ This represents: "What fraction of the way is $T_A$ from $L_A$ to $U_A$?" **Fractional position on Scale B:** $$f_B = \dfrac{T_B - L_B}{U_B - L_B}$$ ### Step 2: Equate the Two Fractions Since both scales are linear and measure the same physical state: $$f_A = f_B$$ Therefore: $$\dfrac{T_A - L_A}{U_A - L_A} = \dfrac{T_B - L_B}{U_B - L_B}$$ This is the **Master Conversion Formula**. ### Step 3: Specialize to Celsius ↔ Fahrenheit For Celsius: $L_C = 0$, $U_C = 100$ For Fahrenheit: $L_F = 32$, $U_F = 212$ Substituting: $$\dfrac{T_C - 0}{100 - 0} = \dfrac{T_F - 32}{212 - 32}$$ $$\dfrac{T_C}{100} = \dfrac{T_F - 32}{180}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$180 \cdot T_C = 100 \cdot (T_F - 32)$$ $$\dfrac{9}{5} T_C = T_F - 32$$ $$\boxed{\,T_F = \dfrac{9}{5} T_C + 32\,}$$ This is the **Celsius-to-Fahrenheit** conversion. ### Step 4: Specialize to Celsius ↔ Kelvin For Celsius: $L_C = 0$, $U_C = 100$ For Kelvin: $L_K = 273.15$, $U_K = 373.15$ $$\dfrac{T_C - 0}{100} = \dfrac{T_K - 273.15}{100}$$ $$T_C = T_K - 273.15$$ $$\boxed{\,T_K = T_C + 273.15\,}$$ ### Step 5: Specialize to Celsius ↔ Rankine For Rankine: $L_R = 491.67$, $U_R = 671.67$ $$\dfrac{T_C}{100} = \dfrac{T_R - 491.67}{180}$$ $$1.8 \cdot T_C = T_R - 491.67$$ $$\boxed{\,T_R = 1.8 \cdot T_C + 491.67\,}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Absolute Zero in Celsius We know that the size of one Fahrenheit degree equals $\frac{5}{9}$ of one Celsius degree (since 180 °F = 100 °C). The relationship: $$T_F = \frac{9}{5} T_C + 32$$ To find the Celsius equivalent of $0 \, °F$: $$0 = \frac{9}{5} T_C + 32$$ $$\frac{9}{5} T_C = -32$$ $$T_C = -\dfrac{32 \times 5}{9} = -\dfrac{160}{9} = -17.78 \, °C$$ So $0 \, °F = -17.78 \, °C$. To find where the Fahrenheit scale crosses absolute zero (in K = 0 K), we use the fact that the **intervals** are proportional. From 0 °F to 32 °F is 32 °F. The same interval in Celsius is 17.78 °C. From 0 °F going *down* to absolute zero: how many Fahrenheit degrees? $$\Delta T_F = 32 - 0 = 32 \, °F$$ This equals in Celsius: $$\Delta T_C = 17.78 \, °C$$ So absolute zero is: $$T_C = -17.78 - 17.78 = -35.56 \, °C$$ Wait, this is incorrect. Let me redo using exact values. ### Correct Derivation Using 0 K → °C Absolute zero is $0 \, \text{K}$. By definition: $$T_C = T_K - 273.15 = 0 - 273.15 = -273.15 \, °C$$ To find absolute zero in Fahrenheit: $$T_F = \frac{9}{5}(-273.15) + 32 = -491.67 + 32 = -459.67 \, °F$$ To find absolute zero in Rankine: $$T_R = T_F + 459.67 = -459.67 + 459.67 = 0 \, °R$$ This confirms that **0 K = 0 °R** (both absolute scales share the same zero). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples ### Example 1: Convert 50 °C to Fahrenheit **Given:** $T_C = 50$ °C **Find:** $T_F$ $$T_F = \frac{9}{5}(50) + 32 = 90 + 32 = 122 \, °F$$ **Verification:** 50 °C is exactly halfway between 0 °C and 100 °C. The corresponding Fahrenheit interval is halfway between 32 °F and 212 °F, which is $(32 + 212)/2 = 122$ °F. ✓ ### Example 2: Convert −40 °C to Fahrenheit **Given:** $T_C = -40$ °C **Find:** $T_F$ $$T_F = \frac{9}{5}(-40) + 32 = -72 + 32 = -40 \, °F$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this:** **−40 °C = −40 °F**. This is the only temperature at which Celsius and Fahrenheit give the same numerical value. It is a popular KTU short-answer/trick question. **Mathematical proof:** Set $T_C = T_F = x$: $$x = \frac{9}{5}x + 32$$ $$x - \frac{9}{5}x = 32$$ $$-\frac{4}{5}x = 32$$ $$x = -40$$ ### Example 3: Convert 100 °F to Kelvin **Given:** $T_F = 100$ °F **Find:** $T_K$ Step 1 — Convert °F to °C: $$T_C = \frac{5}{9}(T_F - 32) = \frac{5}{9}(100 - 32) = \frac{5}{9}(68) = 37.78 \, °C$$ Step 2 — Convert °C to K: $$T_K = T_C + 273.15 = 37.78 + 273.15 = 310.93 \, \text{K}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU strategy:** When asked to convert between two non-Celsius scales, **always pivot through Celsius** unless the two scales share the same zero (e.g., K ↔ R, where $R = 1.8 K$). ### Example 4: A temperature reads the same on Celsius and Fahrenheit scales — find it. Set $T_C = T_F = x$: $$x = \frac{9}{5}x + 32 \implies -\frac{4}{5}x = 32 \implies x = -40$$ **Answer:** −40° (a unique common point). ### Example 5: Thermometric Property Problem A platinum resistance thermometer reads 90 Ω at ice point, 126 Ω at steam point, and 108 Ω at an unknown temperature. Find the unknown temperature on the Celsius scale. **Given:** - $R_{\text{ice}} = 90 \, \Omega$ at 0 °C - $R_{\text{steam}} = 126 \, \Omega$ at 100 °C - $R_x = 108 \, \Omega$ at $T_x$ °C Using linear interpolation: $$T_x = 0 + \frac{R_x - R_{\text{ice}}}{R_{\text{steam}} - R_{\text{ice}}} \times 100$$ $$T_x = \frac{108 - 90}{126 - 90} \times 100 = \frac{18}{36} \times 100 = 50 \, °C$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Master Reference for KTU Numericals | Question Type | Pivot Formula | Common Mistake | |---|---|---| | °C → °F | $F = 1.8 C + 32$ | Forgetting the **+32** offset | | °F → °C | $C = (5/9)(F - 32)$ | Forgetting the **−32** before multiplying | | °C → K | $K = C + 273.15$ | Using 273 instead of 273.15 (loses 0.15 mark) | | K → °F | First to °C, then to °F | Trying to do it in one step | | Find same-reading point | Set both equal and solve | Missing the **−40 °C = −40 °F** special case | | Thermometric property | $T = L + \frac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L} \times (U - L)$ | Using wrong fixed-point values | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Temperature Scales (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD A[All Temperature Scales]:::root --> B[Relative Scales]:::branch A --> C[Absolute Scales]:::branch B --> D[Celsius degC]:::rel B --> E[Fahrenheit degF]:::rel B --> F[Reaumur degRe]:::rel C --> G[Kelvin K]:::abs C --> H[Rankine degR]:::abs D -.->|add 273.15| G G -.->|multiply 1.8| H E -.->|add 459.67| H D -.->|multiply 1.8 add 32| E F -.->|multiply 1.25| D classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef branch fill:#2e7d8a,stroke:#0a3a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef rel fill:#d4a017,stroke:#8a6a00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef abs fill:#c84b31,stroke:#7a2a1a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1.5px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **top block** is the parent concept: "All Temperature Scales." - The **two main branches** are *Relative Scales* (with arbitrary zeros like 0 °C) and *Absolute Scales* (starting from absolute zero). - The **dashed arrows** show the *conversion paths* (the mathematical operations connecting them). ## 4.2 Conversion Flowchart (Celsius as the Pivot Hub) ```mermaid graph LR CEL[Celsius degC]:::hub FAH[Fahrenheit degF]:::rel KEL[Kelvin K]:::abs RAN[Rankine degR]:::abs REA[Reaumur degRe]:::rel CEL ===|multiply by 1.8 and add 32| FAH FAH ===|subtract 32 and multiply by 5/9| CEL CEL ===|add 273.15| KEL KEL ===|subtract 273.15| CEL CEL ===|multiply by 4/5| REA REA ===|multiply by 5/4| CEL KEL ===|multiply by 1.8| RAN RAN ===|multiply by 5/9| KEL FAH ===|add 459.67| RAN RAN ===|subtract 459.67| FAH classDef hub fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2.5px classDef rel fill:#d4a017,stroke:#8a6a00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1.5px classDef abs fill:#c84b31,stroke:#7a2a1a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1.5px ``` **How to use this flowchart for KTU problems:** 1. Identify your **source scale** and **target scale**. 2. If the direct conversion is not given, **pivot through Celsius** (the central hub). 3. For absolute-to-absolute (K ↔ R), use the *direct* multiplier 1.8 or 5/9. ## 4.3 Ice Point – Steam Point Comparison (Numerical Alignment) ```mermaid graph TD IP[Lower Fixed Point - Ice Point
Pure melting ice at 1 atm]:::fixed SP[Upper Fixed Point - Steam Point
Pure boiling water at 1 atm]:::fixed IP --> IP1[Celsius: 0 degC]:::val IP --> IP2[Fahrenheit: 32 degF]:::val IP --> IP3[Kelvin: 273.15 K]:::val IP --> IP4[Rankine: 491.67 degR]:::val IP --> IP5[Reaumur: 0 degRe]:::val SP --> SP1[Celsius: 100 degC]:::val SP --> SP2[Fahrenheit: 212 degF]:::val SP --> SP3[Kelvin: 373.15 K]:::val SP --> SP4[Rankine: 671.67 degR]:::val SP --> SP5[Reaumur: 80 degRe]:::val classDef fixed fill:#2e7d8a,stroke:#0a3a4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef val fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a17f00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** A common question asks: *"On what temperature do the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales read the same value?"* The answer is **−40°** (see derivation in Section 3.3). This value is the same on **both** scales, but it is the *only* such point. ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Construction of a Temperature Scale ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Choose a Thermometric Property X]:::step S2[Step 2: Select Two Fixed Points]:::step S3[Step 3: Assign Numerical Values]:::step S4[Step 4: Define the Mathematical Relation]:::step S5[Step 5: Interpolate for Unknown Temperatures]:::step S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S2 --> F1[Lower Fixed Point: Ice Point at 1 atm]:::fp S2 --> F2[Upper Fixed Point: Steam Point at 1 atm]:::fp classDef step fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef fp fill:#d4a017,stroke:#8a6a00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1.5px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Q1.** Define the terms *thermodynamic temperature*, *ice point*, and *steam point*. What are their SI values? **Model Answer:** > **Thermodynamic temperature** is the temperature measured on a scale that is independent of the properties of any particular substance. It is defined using the Second Law of Thermodynamics via a reversible heat engine (Kelvin's definition) and is measured in **Kelvin (K)**, the SI unit of temperature. > > **Ice point:** The temperature at which pure ice melts (and pure water freezes) in equilibrium with air-saturated water at **standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm = 101.325 kPa)**. > - Value: **0 °C = 273.15 K** > > **Steam point:** The temperature at which pure water boils (and steam condenses) at standard atmospheric pressure. > - Value: **100 °C = 373.15 K** --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]` **Q2.** State the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics** and explain its significance in defining temperature. **Model Answer:** > **Statement:** If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other. > > **Significance:** > 1. It establishes **temperature as a measurable property** — it provides the operational basis for using a thermometer. > 2. It guarantees the existence of a property (temperature) that is the *same* for all systems in mutual thermal equilibrium. > 3. It forms the logical foundation upon which the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics are built (hence it is called the "Zeroth" law — it was added later but is more fundamental). > 4. It justifies the **transitive comparison** of hotness: if A = B and B = C (in temperature), then A = C. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the general formula for converting a temperature reading on one scale to another, given their lower and upper fixed points. Hence, obtain the relation between Celsius and Fahrenheit scales. **(b) [7 Marks]** A constant-volume gas thermometer reads a pressure of 80.0 kPa at the ice point and 110.0 kPa at the steam point. (i) What is the temperature in °C when the pressure reads 95.0 kPa? (ii) What is the pressure at the triple point of water? (iii) What Celsius temperature corresponds to absolute zero on this thermometer? #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation** Let Scale-1 have lower fixed point $L_1$ and upper fixed point $U_1$. Let Scale-2 have lower fixed point $L_2$ and upper fixed point $U_2$. Let a body read $T_1$ on Scale-1 and $T_2$ on Scale-2. Since the temperature scales are linear, the **fractional position** of the body between the fixed points must be the same on both scales: $$\dfrac{T_1 - L_1}{U_1 - L_1} = \dfrac{T_2 - L_2}{U_2 - L_2} \quad \text{...(Master Formula)}$$ **Specialization to Celsius–Fahrenheit:** - $L_C = 0$, $U_C = 100$ - $L_F = 32$, $U_F = 212$ $$\dfrac{T_C - 0}{100} = \dfrac{T_F - 32}{180}$$ $$180 \, T_C = 100 (T_F - 32)$$ $$T_F - 32 = 1.8 \, T_C$$ $$\boxed{\,T_F = 1.8 \, T_C + 32\,}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical** Given: - $P_{\text{ice}} = 80.0$ kPa at $T_C = 0$ °C - $P_{\text{steam}} = 110.0$ kPa at $T_C = 100$ °C - Gas thermometer assumes $P = aT_K$ (linear in Kelvin), where $T_K = T_C + 273.15$. **(i) Temperature when $P = 95.0$ kPa:** Using the linear relation in °C: $$T_C = \dfrac{P - P_{\text{ice}}}{P_{\text{steam}} - P_{\text{ice}}} \times 100$$ $$T_C = \dfrac{95.0 - 80.0}{110.0 - 80.0} \times 100 = \dfrac{15.0}{30.0} \times 100 = 50.0 \, °C$$ **(ii) Pressure at the triple point of water (273.16 K):** First, find the gas constant $a$ in $P = a T_K$: At ice point: $P_{\text{ice}} = a \times 273.15$ $$a = \dfrac{80.0}{273.15} = 0.2929 \,\, \text{kPa/K}$$ At steam point check: $P_{\text{steam}} = 0.2929 \times 373.15 = 109.29$ kPa. (Close to 110.0; the discrepancy arises because pressure may not be exactly linear, but the linear-interpolation method in °C is sufficient for KTU.) **Using the °C method directly for the triple point:** Triple point = **0.01 °C**. $$P_{\text{triple}} = P_{\text{ice}} + \dfrac{0.01 - 0}{100 - 0}(P_{\text{steam}} - P_{\text{ice}})$$ $$P_{\text{triple}} = 80.0 + \dfrac{0.01}{100}(30.0) = 80.0 + 0.003 = 80.003 \,\, \text{kPa}$$ **(iii) Celsius temperature at absolute zero:** At absolute zero, $P = 0$ (extrapolate linearly): $$0 = P_{\text{ice}} + \dfrac{T_C}{100}(P_{\text{steam}} - P_{\text{ice}})$$ $$-80.0 = \dfrac{T_C}{100} \times 30.0$$ $$T_C = \dfrac{-80.0 \times 100}{30.0} = -266.67 \, °C$$ > [!NOTE] > This value (−266.67 °C) is **not exactly** −273.15 °C because a real constant-volume gas thermometer is not a *perfect* ideal-gas thermometer. The extrapolated value approaches −273.15 °C only as the gas pressure approaches zero. For KTU, the linear interpolation value of −266.67 °C is the expected answer. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of the **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)**. What are primary and secondary fixed points? List at least four primary fixed points used in ITS-90. **(b) [7 Marks]** The temperature of a furnace is measured by a thermocouple whose EMF varies linearly from 0 mV at 0 °C to 10.0 mV at 100 °C. (i) What is the temperature in °C when the EMF is 6.5 mV? (ii) If the same thermocouple reads 4.0 mV at the steam point of a new calibration, what is the discrepancy, and what should be the corrected furnace temperature for a 6.5 mV reading? #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — ITS-90** The **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)** is a practical, reproducible temperature scale adopted by the international metrology community. It is designed to closely approximate the true thermodynamic temperature scale. **Key features of ITS-90:** 1. It defines fixed points using **pure-substance phase transitions** (e.g., triple points, melting points, freezing points) that are highly reproducible. 2. It specifies the **interpolating instruments** to be used between fixed points (e.g., standard platinum resistance thermometer for low to mid range, thermocouples/Planck radiation law for high range). 3. It covers the range from **0.65 K to the highest temperatures** practically measurable (above 5000 K via radiation). **Primary Fixed Points (defined by phase transitions of pure substances):** | Substance | Equilibrium State | Temperature (K) | |---|---|---| | Helium-3 | Vapor pressure point | 0.65 – 3.2 | | Hydrogen | Triple point | 13.8033 | | Neon | Triple point | 24.5561 | | Oxygen | Triple point | 54.3584 | | Argon | Triple point | 83.8058 | | Mercury | Triple point | 234.3156 | | **Water** | **Triple point** | **273.16** | | **Water** | **Boiling point (1 atm)** | **373.124** | | Tin | Freezing point | 505.078 | | Zinc | Freezing point | 692.677 | | Silver | Freezing point | 1234.93 | | Gold | Freezing point | 1337.33 | **Secondary fixed points** are additional reproducible points used for calibration, but they are not the defining fixed points of the scale (e.g., normal boiling point of sulfur at 717.8 K). > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trick:** "Triple point of water = 273.16 K (exact by definition)." Memorize this — it is the *single most important fixed point* because it is the basis of the **Kelvin** itself. **Part (b) — Thermocouple Numerical** Given: - $E_0 = 0$ mV at $T_0 = 0$ °C - $E_{100} = 10.0$ mV at $T_{100} = 100$ °C - Linear relation: $E = aT_C + b$ From $T = 0$: $b = 0$ From $T = 100$: $a = 10.0/100 = 0.1$ mV/°C **General formula:** $$T_C = \dfrac{E - E_0}{E_{100} - E_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{E}{10.0} \times 100 = 10 \cdot E$$ **(i) For $E = 6.5$ mV:** $$T_C = 10 \times 6.5 = 65 \, °C$$ **(ii) Recalibration with steam point reading 4.0 mV:** Now the thermocouple has: - $E_0 = 0$ mV at 0 °C - $E_{100}^{\text{new}} = 4.0$ mV at 100 °C (this is the actual steam point) The **original calibration** assumed $E_{100} = 10.0$ mV, so a reading of 6.5 mV *originally* corresponded to: $$T_{\text{old}} = 65 \, °C$$ The **new calibration** gives: $$a_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{4.0}{100} = 0.04 \,\, \text{mV/°C}$$ For the same reading $E = 6.5$ mV: $$T_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{6.5}{0.04} = 162.5 \, °C$$ **Discrepancy:** $\Delta T = 162.5 - 65 = 97.5 \, °C$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation:** The thermocouple has *aged* or *drifted*, so its sensitivity has dropped. A 6.5 mV reading now corresponds to a *much higher* true temperature than it used to. This is why periodic recalibration of industrial thermocouples is critical. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Temperature-Scale Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the offset:** Students write $F = 1.8 \, C$ instead of $F = 1.8 \, C + 32$. This loses **1 full mark** in the formula step alone, and the final numerical answer will be wrong. > > 2. **Using 273 instead of 273.15 in K ↔ °C conversions:** The 0.15 K difference matters in precision-based questions. KTU expects **273.15** unless the question specifies otherwise. > > 3. **Order of operations:** For °F → K, you must **subtract 32 first**, then multiply by 5/9, then add 273.15. A common error is to add 273.15 to Fahrenheit directly — this is a *unit* violation. > > 4. **The "−40" trap:** A question may ask *"At what temperature are °C and °F the same?"* Students sometimes answer "0" (confusing it with the only-fahrenheit case). The correct answer is **−40°**. > > 5. **Thermometric property linearity assumption:** The KTU linear-interpolation formula ($T = L + \frac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L} \times (U - L)$) assumes the property is perfectly linear. For real instruments, this is only an approximation. State this assumption explicitly in your answer to score the *method* marks. > > 6. **Writing units inside the equation:** Never write "$T_F = 1.8 \, T_C + 32 \, °F$" with the °F on both sides. The "+32" is a *number*, not a temperature. Write: "$T_F (°F) = 1.8 \, T_C (°C) + 32$". ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Temperature Scales** > > - **Temperature** is a measure of the degree of hotness; it determines the direction of heat flow. > - **Zeroth Law** gives the operational basis for temperature measurement. > - **Two fixed points** are required to define a scale: **ice point (0 °C)** and **steam point (100 °C)** at **1 atm**. > - **Master formula:** $\dfrac{T_1 - L_1}{U_1 - L_1} = \dfrac{T_2 - L_2}{U_2 - L_2}$ > - **Celsius ↔ Fahrenheit:** $F = 1.8 C + 32$ > - **Celsius ↔ Kelvin:** $K = C + 273.15$ > - **Fahrenheit ↔ Rankine:** $R = F + 459.67$ > - **Kelvin ↔ Rankine:** $R = 1.8 \, K$ > - **Absolute zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F = 0 °R** > - **Triple point of water:** **273.16 K (exact)** — the basis of the Kelvin. > - **Special identity:** **−40 °C = −40 °F** (the only common point). > - **Linear interpolation for thermometric property:** $T = L + \dfrac{X - X_L}{X_U - X_L} \times (U - L)$ > - **ITS-90** is the modern practical scale; it uses **primary fixed points** (phase transitions of pure substances) and prescribed **interpolating instruments**. > - For multi-step conversions, **always pivot through Celsius** (or through any single scale you are confident about). > - **Check units in the final answer:** A temperature in K will never have a negative value. **Mnemonics for KTU:** - **"Fahrenheit Fifty, Celsi**u**s Zero"** → At 0 °C it is 32 °F; add 1.8 per degree. - **"Kelvin's Kinda Cold"** → K = C + 273 (the "kinda" = 0.15 difference matters in precision). - **"Rankine's Reference"** → R is to F as K is to C (add 459.67 / add 273.15).

topics.title: Energy and Work: Pdv Work and Other Types of Work Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy and Work: Pdv Work and Other Types of Work Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔋 Module 2 — Energy and Work: PdV Work and Other Types of Work Transfer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work (W)** in thermodynamics is defined as the **energy transferred across the boundary of a closed system** by means of a **mechanical interaction** between the system and its surroundings. Work is recognized at the boundary only when a force acts through a displacement.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT502) syllabus, work transfer is classified into two broad categories:

1. **Boundary Work (PdV Work / Displacement Work)** — the work done due to a change in system volume against a prevailing pressure.
2. **Non-PdV Work (Other Types of Work Transfer)** — which includes **shaft work, electrical work, spring work, work done in magnetic fields, work done in stretching of liquids, and work done by surface tension**.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Imagine a **piston-cylinder assembly** containing gas. When the gas expands, it pushes the piston upward against the atmosphere. The gas is doing **work on the surroundings**. Now imagine a **mixer grinder** running on electricity. Here, the system (the motor) does work on the surroundings (the blade) without any volume change.

> [!NOTE]
> 🔑 **Key Insight:** Not all thermodynamic work is PdV work. PdV work is *one specific type* of work where volume change is the mechanism. The KTU syllabus requires you to recognize and compute *any* form of work transfer that crosses the boundary.

### ⚖️ Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Direction of Energy Transfer | Sign | KTU Notation |
|------------------------------|------|--------------|
| Work done **BY** the system on surroundings | Positive | $+W$ |
| Work done **ON** the system by surroundings | Negative | $-W$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some textbooks (like Cengel) use the opposite sign convention. KTU 2024 Scheme adopts the standard **IIT/Mechanical Engineering convention** where work done by the system is **positive**. Always check the convention in the question paper.

### 🎯 Path Function Property
