Work is a **path function** — its magnitude depends on the *process path* followed between two states, **not just the initial and final states**.

$$dW \neq 0 \text{ (for a process)} \quad \text{but} \quad \oint dW \neq 0$$

This is in stark contrast to **internal energy (U)** and **enthalpy (H)**, which are *point functions* (state properties).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boundary Work (PdV Work) — The Core Concept

### 🔍 Physical Setup

Consider a **piston-cylinder assembly** containing a fluid. The piston has:
- Cross-sectional area: $A$ (m²)
- Pressure exerted by fluid on piston: $P$ (N/m² = Pa)
- Piston moves through an infinitesimal distance: $dx$ (m)

### 📐 Infinitesimal Work Done

The infinitesimal force on the piston:
$$F = P \cdot A$$

The infinitesimal work done by the system when the piston moves by $dx$:
$$dW = F \cdot dx = P \cdot A \cdot dx$$

Since $A \cdot dx = dV$ (change in volume):
$$\boxed{dW = P \, dV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most fundamental equation** in KTU Module 2 — the differential form of boundary work. Every other PdV formula is derived from this.

### 🔄 Finite Process Work (General Form)

For any quasi-equilibrium process (where the system passes through a series of equilibrium states):
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

### 📊 Classification of PdV Work by Process Type

#### **A) Constant Pressure (Isobaric) Process**
$$W = P(V_2 - V_1) = P \Delta V$$

> Represents the **rectangular area** under the P-V curve.

#### **B) Constant Volume (Isochoric) Process**
$$W = 0$$
> No volume change → no boundary work.

#### **C) Constant Temperature (Isothermal) Process**

For an **ideal gas** undergoing reversible isothermal process: $PV = mRT = \text{constant}$

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} dV = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

Or equivalently:
$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_2 V_2 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

#### **D) Polytropic Process** $PV^n = \text{constant}$

For $n \neq 1$:
$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n}$$

For $n = 1$ (reduces to isothermal):
$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

#### **E) Adiabatic Process** (PV^k = constant, where k = γ = Cp/Cv)
$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - k}$$

---

## 2.2 Non-PdV Work (Other Types of Work Transfer)

### ⚙️ A) Shaft Work (Ws)

Work transmitted through a **rotating shaft** — pumps, compressors, turbines.

$$\boxed{W_s = \int \tau \, d\theta}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = torque applied (N·m)
- $\theta$ = angle of rotation (rad)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)

For constant torque and angular velocity:
$$W_s = \tau \cdot \theta = \tau \cdot \omega \cdot t = 2\pi N \cdot \tau \cdot t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Shaft work is the most common non-PdV work in KTU problems** — encountered in turbine, compressor, and pump analyses.

### ⚡ B) Electrical Work (We)

Work done by an electrical system:
$$\boxed{W_e = V \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{V^2}{R} \cdot t = I^2 R \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $V$ = voltage (V)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $R$ = resistance (Ω)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $Q = I \cdot t$ = charge (C)

Alternative form: $W_e = V \cdot Q$

### 🔩 C) Spring Work (Wspr)

Work done in compressing/stretching a spring:
$$\boxed{W_{spr} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F \, dx = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} Kx \, dx = \frac{1}{2}K(x_2^2 - x_1^2)}$$

Where:
- $K$ = spring constant (N/m)
- $x$ = displacement from natural length (m)

> Force in a linear spring: $F = Kx$ (Hooke's Law)

### 🧲 D) Magnetic Work (Wmag)

Work done in a magnetic system (electromagnet, inductor):
$$W_{mag} = \int_0^B V \cdot H \, dB$$

For a linear magnetic material ($\mu = $ constant):
$$W_{mag} = \frac{V B^2}{2\mu_0 \mu_r}$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of magnetic material
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (T)
- $H$ = magnetic field intensity (A/m)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (permeability of free space)

### 🫧 E) Surface Tension Work (Wst)

Work to create a new surface area (e.g., bubble formation):
$$W_{st} = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = surface tension (N/m)
- $\Delta A$ = change in surface area (m²)

### 💧 F) Work in Stretching a Liquid Film

For a liquid film (soap film with two surfaces):
$$W = 2\sigma (A_2 - A_1)$$

The factor of 2 appears because a film has **two surfaces**.

### 🔋 G) Electrochemical Work (Pelec)

Work from a galvanic/electrolytic cell:
$$W_{elec} = -n \cdot F \cdot E$$

Where:
- $n$ = moles of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $E$ = cell potential (V)

---

## 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Reference Table)

| **Work Type** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **When to Use** |
|---------------|-------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Boundary Work (General) | $W = \int P \, dV$ | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $W$ in J | Any quasi-equilibrium process |
| Isobaric (P = const) | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³ | Constant pressure processes |
| Isochoric (V = const) | $W = 0$ | — | Constant volume processes |
| Isothermal (Ideal gas) | $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $m$ in kg, $R$ in J/kg·K, $T$ in K | T = const, ideal gas |
| Polytropic (n ≠ 1) | $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$ | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³ | $PV^n = $ const |
| Polytropic (n = 1) | $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | Same as isothermal | Special case of polytropic |
| Adiabatic (reversible) | $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-k)$ | $k = C_p/C_v$ | No heat transfer, reversible |
| Shaft Work | $W_s = \tau \cdot \theta$ | $\tau$ in N·m, $\theta$ in rad | Rotating shaft |
| Shaft Work (Power form) | $W_s = 2\pi N \tau t$ | $N$ in rev/s, $t$ in s | Time-based shaft work |
| Electrical Work | $W_e = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $t$ in s | Electrical systems |
| Electrical Work (Alt) | $W_e = V \cdot Q$ | $Q$ in C (coulombs) | Charge-based form |
| Spring Work | $W_{spr} = \frac{1}{2}K(x_2^2 - x_1^2)$ | $K$ in N/m, $x$ in m | Linear spring |
| Magnetic Work | $W_{mag} = VB^2/(2\mu_0\mu_r)$ | $V$ in m³, $B$ in T | Magnetic systems |
| Surface Tension Work | $W_{st} = \sigma \Delta A$ | $\sigma$ in N/m, $A$ in m² | Bubble formation |
| Liquid Film Work | $W = 2\sigma \Delta A$ | Same as above | Soap film (2 surfaces) |
| Electrochemical Work | $W = -nFE$ | $n$ in mol, $F = 96485$ C/mol | Electrolytic cells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **differential form** $dW = PdV$ and the **polytropic general formula** — these are worth 7-10 marks in most KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.

---

## 2.3 Work Transfer in Open Systems (Flow Work)

### 🚰 The Concept of Flow Work

In an **open system** (control volume), fluid enters and exits continuously. To push a fluid element into the control volume, the upstream fluid must do work on it.

**Flow work per unit mass:**
$$w_f = P \cdot v$$

Where $v$ = specific volume (m³/kg)

**Total flow work** for a mass $m$ entering volume $V$:
$$W_f = P \cdot V = P \cdot m \cdot v$$

> [!NOTE]
> 🔑 **Why Flow Work Matters:** In open systems, the **First Law** for a control volume includes flow work as part of the **enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$. This is the foundation for the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** studied in KTU Module 4.

### 🔄 Comparison: Closed vs Open System Work

| **Property** | **Closed System (PdV Work)** | **Open System (Flow Work)** |
|--------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Formula | $W = \int P \, dV$ | $W_f = P \cdot V$ per unit mass: $Pv$ |
| System type | Fixed mass, moving boundary | Mass crosses boundary |
| Mechanism | Piston/expansion | Fluid flow |
| Sign | +ve for expansion | Always positive on entry |
| Energy bundle | Part of internal energy | Part of enthalpy |

---

## 2.4 Reversible vs Irreversible Work

### ✅ Reversible (Quasi-Equilibrium) Work

A process is reversible when the system remains in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at every instant. The work done is the **maximum possible work** (for expansion) or **minimum required work** (for compression).

$$W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{int} \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$

For expansion: $W_{rev} = W_{max}$
For compression: $W_{rev} = W_{min}$

### ❌ Irreversible Work

For an irreversible process against constant external pressure $P_{ext}$:
$$W_{irr} = P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1)$$

> [!WARNING]
> For the **same initial and final states**, $|W_{irr}| \leq |W_{rev}|$ for compression. KTU questions often ask to compare reversible vs irreversible work — this comparison is **always asked for compression** in compressors.

### 📊 P-V Diagram Interpretation of Work

The work done by the system in a quasi-equilibrium process is the **area under the curve** on a P-V diagram.

- **Process 1-2 (expansion):** $W_{12} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ = Area under curve 1-2
- **Process 2-1 (compression):** $W_{21} = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} P \, dV$ = -Area under curve 2-1

This means:
$$W_{12} + W_{21} = \text{Area enclosed by the cycle} = \oint P \, dV$$

> [!NOTE]
> 🔁 For a **cyclic process**, the net work done is the **enclosed area** on the P-V diagram. This is the basis for analyzing heat engine cycles (Carnot, Otto, Diesel).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| **Application** | **Work Type** | **Why It Matters** |
|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Internal Combustion Engine | PdV Work | Power output of the engine |
| Refrigeration Compressor | Shaft Work | Energy input required |
| Hydroelectric Generator | Shaft Work | Mechanical to electrical conversion |
| Electric Motor | Electrical Work | Mechanical drive systems |
| Hydraulic Press | PdV Work | Force amplification |
| Wind Turbine | Shaft Work | Renewable energy capture |
| Battery System | Electrochemical Work | Portable energy storage |
| Pneumatic Spring | Spring Work | Suspension systems |
| Magnetic Levitation (Maglev) | Magnetic Work | Frictionless transport |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Boundary Work from First Principles

### **Given:**
A piston-cylinder assembly with gas at pressure $P$ acting on a piston of cross-sectional area $A$.

### **To Derive:**
The expression $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The force exerted by the gas on the piston face is given by the definition of pressure:
$$F = P \cdot A$$

**Step 2:** For an infinitesimal displacement of the piston $dx$ in the direction of force (expansion), the infinitesimal work done by the system is:
$$dW = F \cdot dx$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $F$:
$$dW = P \cdot A \cdot dx$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the change in volume of the gas equals the area swept by the piston:
$$dV = A \cdot dx$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $dV$ into the work expression:
$$dW = P \cdot dV$$

**Step 6:** To find the total work for a finite process from state 1 (volume $V_1$) to state 2 (volume $V_2$), integrate both sides:
$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

$$\boxed{\therefore W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV}$$

This is the **fundamental boundary work equation** — the starting point for ALL PdV work calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Polytropic Process Boundary Work

### **Given:**
A reversible polytropic process defined by $PV^n = C$ (constant), where $n$ is the polytropic index.

### **To Derive:**
The general expression $W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$ for $n \neq 1$.

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the general boundary work integral:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2:** From the polytropic relation:
$$PV^n = C \implies P = C \cdot V^{-n} = \frac{C}{V^n}$$

**Step 3:** At state 1: $P_1 V_1^n = C$, so $C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $P$ into the work integral:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^n} \, dV = C \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Perform the integration (assuming $n \neq 1$):
$$W = C \left[ \frac{V^{-n+1}}{-n+1} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{C}{1-n} \left[ V^{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate at the limits:
$$W = \frac{C}{1-n} \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Multiply and divide by $V_2^n$ and $V_1^n$ to express in terms of $PV$ products:
$$W = \frac{C \cdot V_2^n \cdot V_2^{1-n} - C \cdot V_1^n \cdot V_1^{1-n}}{1-n}$$

**Step 8:** Recognize that $C \cdot V_2^n = P_2 V_2^n \cdot V_2^n / V_2^n = P_2 V_2$ (similar for state 1):
$$C \cdot V_2^{1-n} = P_2 V_2^n \cdot V_2^{1-n} = P_2 V_2$$

**Step 9:** Substitute back:
$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$$

**Step 10:** Alternative form using the ideal gas law $PV = mRT$:
$$P_1 V_1 = mRT_1 \quad \text{and} \quad P_2 V_2 = mRT_2$$

$$\boxed{\therefore W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n} = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the special case $n = 1$ (isothermal), this formula gives $W = \frac{0}{0}$ — an indeterminate form. We must take the limit as $n \to 1$, which yields $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ via L'Hôpital's rule.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Isothermal Boundary Work for Ideal Gas

### **Given:**
An ideal gas undergoing a reversible isothermal process from $(P_1, V_1)$ to $(P_2, V_2)$ at temperature $T$.

### **To Derive:**
$W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = P_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the general formula:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Apply the ideal gas equation of state:
$$PV = mRT \implies P = \frac{mRT}{V}$$

**Step 3:** For an isothermal process, $T$ = constant, and $m$, $R$ are also constant, so:
$$P = \frac{\text{constant}}{V}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the work integral:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 5:** Factor out the constants:
$$W = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

**Step 6:** Perform the integration (the antiderivative of $1/V$ is $\ln V$):
$$W = mRT \left[ \ln V \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = mRT (\ln V_2 - \ln V_1)$$

**Step 7:** Apply logarithm properties:
$$W = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 8:** Since $P_1 V_1 = mRT$, we can also write:
$$\boxed{\therefore W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_2 V_2 \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently uses the equivalent form $W = 2.303 \, mRT \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ in numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Reversible Adiabatic Work for Ideal Gas

### **Given:**
A reversible adiabatic process for an ideal gas: $PV^k = $ constant, where $k = C_p/C_v = \gamma$.

### **To Derive:**
$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{k - 1}$

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The adiabatic relation is a special case of polytropic with $n = k$:
$$PV^k = C \implies P = C \cdot V^{-k}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the polytropic formula with $n = k$ (note: denominator becomes $1 - k$, so we multiply numerator and denominator by $-1$):
$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - k} = -\frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{k - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to obtain the standard form:
$$\boxed{\therefore W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{k - 1}}$$

**Step 4:** In terms of temperatures (using $PV = mRT$):
$$W = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{k - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an **expansion** ($V_2 > V_1$), $T_2 < T_1$ in adiabatic process, so $W > 0$ (work done by gas) — consistent with the **First Law** for an adiabatic process where $W = - \Delta U = mC_v(T_1 - T_2)$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Spring Work (Hooke's Law Application)

### **Given:**
A linear spring with spring constant $K$ (N/m), stretched from length $x_1$ to $x_2$ from the natural (unstretched) position.

### **To Derive:**
$W = \frac{1}{2} K (x_2^2 - x_1^2)$

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** From Hooke's Law, the restoring force in a linear spring is proportional to displacement:
$$F = K \cdot x$$

**Step 2:** The infinitesimal work done to stretch the spring by $dx$:
$$dW = F \, dx = Kx \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from initial displacement $x_1$ to final displacement $x_2$:
$$W = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} Kx \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Apply the power rule for integration:
$$W = K \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{x_1}^{x_2}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at the limits:
$$\boxed{\therefore W = \frac{1}{2} K (x_2^2 - x_1^2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is also the formula for the **strain energy** stored in a spring: $U_{spring} = \frac{1}{2} K x^2$.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Average Pressure Method for Polytropic Work

### **Concept:**
For polytropic work problems where $P_1$ and $P_2$ are given but the process path is polytropic, we can compute work using **average pressure**.

### **Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For a polytropic process, define average pressure as:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV}{V_2 - V_1}$$

**Step 2:** Hence:
$$W = P_{avg} (V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 3:** For a polytropic process $PV^n = C$, this evaluates to:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} \text{ only if } n = -1 \text{ (hyperbolic process)}$$

For general $n$:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{(1-n)(V_2 - V_1)} \quad \text{for } n \neq 1$$

**Step 4:** Hence, the alternate computation:
$$W = P_{avg} \cdot (V_2 - V_1) = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n}$$

Which is **identical** to the integral result. ✓

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

### **Problem:**
A piston-cylinder assembly contains 0.5 kg of nitrogen (ideal gas, $R = 296.8$ J/kg·K) at 100 kPa and 27°C. The gas is compressed **polytropically** with $n = 1.3$ to a final pressure of 800 kPa. Calculate the work done during the process.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given data:
- $m = 0.5$ kg
- $R = 296.8$ J/kg·K
- $P_1 = 100$ kPa = $10^5$ Pa
- $T_1 = 27°C = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 800$ kPa = $8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $n = 1.3$

**Step 2:** Find initial specific volume using ideal gas law:
$$v_1 = \frac{RT_1}{P_1} = \frac{296.8 \times 300}{10^5} = 0.8904 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Find final specific volume using $P_1 v_1^n = P_2 v_2^n$:
$$v_2 = v_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = 0.8904 \times \left(\frac{100}{800}\right)^{1/1.3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the ratio:
$$\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{1/1.3} = (0.125)^{0.7692}$$

**Step 5:** Take logarithm:
$$\ln(0.125^{0.7692}) = 0.7692 \times \ln(0.125) = 0.7692 \times (-2.0794) = -1.5995$$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate:
$$0.125^{0.7692} = e^{-1.5995} = 0.2021$$

**Step 7:** Compute $v_2$:
$$v_2 = 0.8904 \times 0.2021 = 0.1799 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 8:** Find final temperature:
$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 v_2}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.1799}{296.8} = 485.0 \text{ K}$$

**Step 9:** Apply the polytropic work formula:
$$W = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n} = \frac{0.5 \times 296.8 \times (485.0 - 300)}{1 - 1.3}$$

**Step 10:** Compute the numerator:
$$0.5 \times 296.8 \times 185 = 27454 \text{ J}$$

**Step 11:** Compute the denominator:
$$1 - 1.3 = -0.3$$

**Step 12:** Final answer:
$$W = \frac{27454}{-0.3} = -91513 \text{ J} = -91.51 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\therefore W = -91.51 \text{ kJ}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** indicates work is done **ON** the system (compression), which is physically consistent.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Work in Different Processes

| **Process** | **n value** | **Work Formula** | **Heat Transfer** | **Net Work Sign** |
|-------------|-------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Isobaric | $n = 0$ | $P(V_2 - V_1)$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | Depends on $\Delta V$ |
| Isothermal | $n = 1$ | $mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ | $Q = W$ | +ve for expansion |
| Adiabatic | $n = k$ | $(P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(k-1)$ | $Q = 0$ | +ve for expansion |
| Polytropic (general) | $n$ | $(P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$ | $Q \neq 0$ | Depends on $n$ |
| Isochoric | $n = \pm \infty$ | $0$ | $Q = \Delta U$ | Always 0 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define boundary work. Derive the expression for boundary work in a quasi-equilibrium process.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Boundary work (also called PdV work or displacement work) is the work done by or on a system due to a change in its volume at the system boundary, when a force acts through a displacement.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
Consider a piston-cylinder assembly with gas at pressure $P$ pushing a piston of area $A$ through displacement $dx$.

Force on piston: $F = P \cdot A$

Infinitesimal work: $dW = F \cdot dx = P \cdot A \cdot dx = P \cdot dV$

For a finite process:
$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between point function and path function with two examples each.**

#### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Point Function | Path Function |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| Definition | Depends only on state | Depends on process path |
| Differential | Exact differential (dx) | Inexact differential (đx) |
| Cyclic integral | $\oint dX = 0$ | $\oint đX \neq 0$ |
| Example 1 | Internal Energy (U) | Work (W) |
| Example 2 | Enthalpy (H), Temperature | Heat (Q) |

---

## 📚 PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (With Internal Choice)

### **Question Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
A gas undergoes a reversible polytropic process from initial conditions of 1 bar and 0.15 m³ to final conditions of 3.8 bar and 0.05 m³. Calculate:
(i) The polytropic index $n$
(ii) The work done during the process
(iii) The change in internal energy if $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K (mass = 0.5 kg)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
Explain the different types of non-PdV work with suitable engineering examples and mathematical expressions.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1$ bar = $10^5$ Pa
- $V_1 = 0.15$ m³
- $P_2 = 3.8$ bar = $3.8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $V_2 = 0.05$ m³
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $m = 0.5$ kg

**(i) Polytropic index (3 Marks):**

For a polytropic process: $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$

Take natural log of both sides:
$$n \ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln(V_1/V_2)} = \frac{\ln(3.8/1)}{\ln(0.15/0.05)}$$

$$n = \frac{\ln(3.8)}{\ln(3)} = \frac{1.335}{1.0986} = 1.215$$

$$\boxed{n = 1.215}$$

**(ii) Work done (2 Marks):**

Using polytropic formula:
$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n}$$

Compute numerator:
$$P_2 V_2 = 3.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.05 = 19000 \text{ J} = 19 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_1 V_1 = 1 \times 10^5 \times 0.15 = 15000 \text{ J} = 15 \text{ kJ}$$
$$P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1 = 19 - 15 = 4 \text{ kJ}$$

Denominator: $1 - n = 1 - 1.215 = -0.215$

$$W = \frac{4}{-0.215} = -18.60 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W = -18.60 \text{ kJ (work done on the system)}}$$

**(iii) Change in internal energy (2 Marks):**

First, find specific gas constant: 
$$R = C_p - C_v = \gamma C_v - C_v = C_v(\gamma - 1) = 0.718 \times 0.4 = 0.2872 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

Find temperatures:
$$T_1 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{mR} = \frac{15 \text{ kJ}}{0.5 \times 0.2872} = 104.46 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{19 \text{ kJ}}{0.5 \times 0.2872} = 132.31 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times (132.31 - 104.46)$$

$$\Delta U = 0.5 \times 0.718 \times 27.85 = 9.998 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U \approx +10 \text{ kJ (internal energy increases)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The internal energy **increases** despite compression because $n < k$ — the gas is being heated-like in character (more heat input than work output). This is consistent with $Q = \Delta U + W = 10 + (-18.6) = -8.6$ kJ... wait, this gives $Q < 0$, indicating heat is **rejected** during compression.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution: Non-PdV Work Types**

**1. Shaft Work (2 Marks):**
Work transmitted via a rotating shaft.
$$W_s = \int \tau \, d\theta = 2\pi N \tau t$$
**Example:** Turbine, compressor, electric motor shaft, propeller.

**2. Electrical Work (1.5 Marks):**
Work due to flow of electric current.
$$W_e = V \cdot I \cdot t = I^2 R t = \frac{V^2 t}{R}$$
**Example:** Electric heater, motor winding, electrical resistance heating.

**3. Spring Work (1.5 Marks):**
Work in compressing/stretching a spring.
$$W_{spr} = \frac{1}{2}K(x_2^2 - x_1^2)$$
**Example:** Vehicle suspension, valve springs in IC engines, mechanical watch.

**4. Magnetic Work (1 Mark):**
Work in magnetizing a material.
$$W_{mag} = \frac{VB^2}{2\mu_0 \mu_r}$$
**Example:** Electromagnet, transformer core, magnetic levitation trains.

**5. Surface Tension Work (1 Mark):**
Work in creating new surface area.
$$W_{st} = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$$
**Example:** Soap bubble formation, atomization of liquid fuel in engines.

---

### **Question Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Derive the expression for the work done during a reversible isothermal expansion of an ideal gas. A mass of 2 kg of air at 27°C is expanded isothermally from 5 bar to 2 bar. Calculate the work done and the heat transferred.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A system containing 1 kg of air executes a cycle consisting of three processes:
- Process 1-2: Constant pressure heat addition (V doubles)
- Process 2-3: Polytropic compression with $n = 1.2$
- Process 3-1: Constant volume heat rejection

Sketch the cycle on a P-V diagram and calculate the work done in each process and the net work of the cycle. Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

For an ideal gas: $PV = mRT$

In a reversible isothermal process, $T = $ constant, so $P = mRT/V = $ constant/$V$.

Work done:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} dV = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$W = mRT \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = mRT \ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 2$ kg
- $T = 27°C = 300$ K
- $P_1 = 5$ bar, $P_2 = 2$ bar
- $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

Work done:
$$W = mRT \ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = 2 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times \ln\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)$$

$$W = 172.2 \times \ln(2.5) = 172.2 \times 0.9163 = 157.79 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W = 157.79 \text{ kJ (work done by the gas)}}$$

For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$ (since $T$ is constant).

By the **First Law:** $Q = \Delta U + W = 0 + 157.79 = 157.79$ kJ

$$\boxed{Q = 157.79 \text{ kJ (heat supplied to the gas)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an isothermal expansion of an ideal gas, **all the heat supplied is converted into work** — this is the most efficient conversion possible from heat to work in a single process.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 1$ kg, $C_p = 1.005$, $C_v = 0.718$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K

**State 1:** Choose base values: $P_1 = 1$ bar, $V_1 = 0.287$ m³ (since $V_1 = mRT_1/P_1$, let $T_1 = 300$ K)

**Process 1-2: Constant pressure, $V$ doubles**
- $P_2 = P_1 = 1$ bar
- $V_2 = 2V_1 = 0.574$ m³
- $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (V_2/V_1) = 600$ K (since P = const)

Work in process 1-2:
$$W_{1-2} = P(V_2 - V_1) = 100 \times (0.574 - 0.287) = 28.7 \text{ kJ}$$

**Process 2-3: Polytropic compression, $n = 1.2$**
- $P_3 = ?$ determined by closing condition (returns to $V_3 = V_1 = 0.287$ m³)

Using $P_2 V_2^n = P_3 V_3^n$:
$$P_3 = P_2 \left(\frac{V_2}{V_3}\right)^n = 1 \times \left(\frac{0.574}{0.287}\right)^{1.2} = 2^{1.2} = 2.297 \text{ bar}$$

Work in process 2-3:
$$W_{2-3} = \frac{P_3 V_3 - P_2 V_2}{1 - n} = \frac{(229.7)(0.287) - (100)(0.574)}{1 - 1.2}$$

$$W_{2-3} = \frac{65.93 - 57.4}{-0.2} = \frac{8.53}{-0.2} = -42.65 \text{ kJ}$$

**Process 3-1: Constant volume**
$$W_{3-1} = 0 \text{ (since } dV = 0\text{)}$$

**Net work of cycle:**
$$W_{net} = W_{1-2} + W_{2-3} + W_{3-1} = 28.7 + (-42.65) + 0 = -13.95 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** The **negative** net work indicates this is a **refrigeration/heat pump cycle** (work input cycle), not a power cycle. Students often forget to check the sign of $W_{net}$ to identify the cycle type.

For a power cycle: $W_{net} > 0$ (heat engine)
For a refrigeration cycle: $W_{net} < 0$ (work input)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deduction in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (2-3 marks lost):** Always state the sign convention explicitly. KTU 2024 Scheme uses **work done by system = positive**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Indeterminate Form (4-5 marks lost):** The polytropic formula $W = (P_2V_2 - P_1V_1)/(1-n)$ is **NOT valid for $n = 1$**. Use $W = mRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$ instead.
> 
> 3. **Confusing PdV Work with Flow Work (3-4 marks lost):** PdV work is for closed systems; flow work ($Pv$) is for open systems. Don't apply $W = \int P dV$ in open system analysis.
> 
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency (1-2 marks lost):** Always use **SI units**: Pa for pressure, m³ for volume, K for temperature, kg for mass. Mixing bar with m³, or °C with K, leads to unit errors.
> 
> 5. **Missing First Law Connection (1-2 marks lost):** Work alone is not enough. KTU often asks "find work AND heat transfer" — apply $Q = \Delta U + W$ after finding $W$.
> 
> 6. **Confusing Reversible and Irreversible Work (2-3 marks lost):** For irreversible processes, use $W = P_{ext}\Delta V$, NOT $\int P dV$.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> ### 🎯 High-Yield KTU 2024 Exam Points — Last-Minute Revision

#### ✅ **Core Definitions**
- ☐ **Work** = Energy transfer across system boundary due to force acting through displacement
- ☐ **PdV Work** = Boundary work due to volume change: $dW = P dV$
- ☐ **Work is a path function** (depends on process), unlike $U$, $H$ (state functions)
- ☐ **Sign convention (KTU 2024):** Work done BY system = **positive**

#### ✅ **Essential Formulas**
- ☐ **General boundary work:** $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$
- ☐ **Polytropic work ($n \neq 1$):** $W = \frac{P_2V_2 - P_1V_1}{1-n} = \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1-n}$
- ☐ **Isothermal work ($n = 1$):** $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$
- ☐ **Adiabatic work:** $W = \frac{P_1V_1 - P_2V_2}{k-1}$
- ☐ **Isobaric work:** $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$
- ☐ **Isochoric work:** $W = 0$
- ☐ **Shaft work:** $W_s = \tau \cdot \theta = 2\pi N \tau t$
- ☐ **Electrical work:** $W_e = V I t$
- ☐ **Spring work:** $W_{spr} = \frac{1}{2}K(x_2^2 - x_1^2)$

#### ✅ **Special Values of Polytropic Index $n$**
- ☐ $n = 0$ → **Isobaric** (constant P)
- ☐ $n = 1$ → **Isothermal** (constant T, ideal gas)
- ☐ $n = k$ → **Isentropic/Adiabatic reversible** (constant S)
- ☐ $n = \infty$ → **Isochoric** (constant V)
- ☐ $n = -1$ → **Hyperbolic** ($PV = $ const)
- ☐ $1 < n < k$ → **Heat added** during expansion
- ☐ $n > k$ → **Heat rejected** during expansion

#### ✅ **Closed vs Open System Distinctions**
- ☐ **Closed system:** $W = \int P \, dV$ (PdV only)
- ☐ **Open system:** $W$ includes flow work $Pv$ (part of enthalpy $h = u + Pv$)
- ☐ **Enthalpy** appears in open system (SFEE), not closed system

#### ✅ **Reversible vs Irreversible Work**
- ☐ **Reversible work:** $W_{rev} = \int P dV$ (max for expansion, min for compression)
- ☐ **Irreversible work:** $W_{irr} = P_{ext} \Delta V$
- ☐ **Comparison:** For expansion: $W_{rev} > W_{irr}$ (reversible gives more work)
- ☐ For compression: $|W_{rev}| < |W_{irr}|$ (reversible requires less work)

#### ✅ **P-V Diagram Insights**
- ☐ Work = **Area under process curve** on P-V diagram
- ☐ **Net cycle work** = Area enclosed by the cycle on P-V diagram
- ☐ **Clockwise cycle** on P-V = Heat engine (positive net work)
- ☐ **Counter-clockwise cycle** on P-V = Refrigeration (negative net work)

#### ✅ **First Law Connection**
- ☐ For **closed system (any process):** $Q = \Delta U + W$
- ☐ For **isothermal process (ideal gas):** $Q = W$ (since $\Delta U = 0$)
- ☐ For **adiabatic process:** $Q = 0$, so $W = -\Delta U = -mC_v \Delta T$
- ☐ For **isochoric process:** $W = 0$, so $Q = \Delta U = mC_v \Delta T$
- ☐ For **isobaric process:** $Q = \Delta H = mC_p \Delta T$

#### ✅ **Key Physical Constants (Memorize)**
- ☐ Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314$ **J/mol·K**
- ☐ $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K
- ☐ Faraday's constant: $F = 96485$ C/mol
- ☐ Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

#### ✅ **Common Non-PdV Work Types in KTU**
- ☐ Shaft work (turbines, compressors, motors)
- ☐ Electrical work (resistance heating, motors)
- ☐ Spring work (suspension, valve springs)
- ☐ Magnetic work (electromagnets, transformers)
- ☐ Surface tension work (bubbles, droplets)
- ☐ Electrochemical work (batteries, fuel cells)

#### ✅ **Engineering Applications to Remember**
- ☐ **IC Engine:** PdV work during power stroke
- ☐ **Refrigerator:** Shaft work input to compressor
- ☐ **Steam Turbine:** Shaft work output
- ☐ **Hydraulic Press:** PdV work multiplication
- ☐ **Fuel Cell:** Electrochemical work output
- ☐ **Solar Panel:** Electrical work output

#### ✅ **Common KTU Question Patterns**
- ☐ "Calculate work done in polytropic/isothermal/adiabatic process" — 7 marks
- ☐ "Differentiate between PdV and non-PdV work" — 3-7 marks
- ☐ "Compare reversible and irreversible work" — 7 marks
- ☐ "Calculate net work in a thermodynamic cycle" — 7-14 marks
- ☐ "Apply First Law to find heat transfer after computing work" — 14 marks

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for Module 2:**
> *"PdV work is the area under the P-V curve. Polytropic formula handles 90% of KTU problems. Always check the value of $n$ — if $n = 1$, use the logarithmic formula. Sign convention matters: work done BY system is POSITIVE in KTU 2024 Scheme. Non-PdV work types are 3-mark questions — memorize them with examples."*

---

*End of Module 2 — Energy and Work: PdV Work and Other Types of Work Transfer*
*PCMRT502 — Thermodynamics | KTU 2024 Scheme | Prepared as per KTU Board Standards*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Expansion Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Free Expansion Work # 📘 MODULE 2 — FREE EXPANSION WORK ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMRT502 / Module 2):** > *Free expansion* (also termed **Joule expansion** or **unresisted expansion**) is a thermodynamic process in which a fluid (typically a gas) expands into a **vacuum** or a region of **negligible opposing pressure**, without the influence of any external resisting force. Since there is no pressure boundary performing work on the surroundings, the **boundary work** is identically zero. ### Formal Statement Mathematically, free expansion is defined as a process in which the system expands such that the **external pressure $P_{ext}$** opposing the expansion is essentially zero: $$P_{ext} = 0 \quad \text{(or)} \quad P_{ext} \ll P_{system}$$ Consequently, the **work done by the system** in such a process is: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV = 0$$ ### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a balloon bursting inside a large vacuum chamber:** > > Imagine a small, sealed container of gas connected via a valve to a much larger evacuated chamber. When the valve is opened, the gas **rushes out spontaneously** into the vacuum. There is **no piston to push**, **no atmosphere to push against**, and **no external load to lift**. The gas simply *finds more space* on its own. Because nothing is being moved against an opposing pressure, **no work crosses the system boundary**. > > This is exactly what happens in **free expansion** — the system does **zero work**, even though its volume has clearly increased. ### Physical Setup (Joule's Original Apparatus) Joule's classical experiment (1845) used two rigid, thermally insulated copper vessels: - **Vessel A:** Filled with a gas at high pressure. - **Vessel B:** Evacuated (vacuum). - They were connected by a stopcock valve, and the entire system was immersed in a water bath acting as a calorimeter. When the valve was opened, gas rushed from A to B. The observation was that for an **ideal gas**, the water bath temperature did **not change**, confirming $\Delta T = 0$ and $\Delta U = 0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** > Free expansion is an **irreversible process**. It cannot be plotted as a single continuous curve on a $P\text{-}V$ diagram because the gas is in a non-equilibrium state during expansion. It is represented by a **dashed line** between the initial state (1) and final state (2). ### Defining Conditions (Boxed Summary) | Parameter | Value During Free Expansion | |---|---| | External Pressure, $P_{ext}$ | **≈ 0** | | Boundary Work, $W$ | **= 0** | | Heat Transfer, $Q$ | **= 0** (adiabatic walls assumed) | | Process Path | **Irreversible / Non-quasi-static** | | Final State | Equilibrium (after mixing/turbulence subsides) | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Work is Zero — The Core Logic The general expression for boundary work in a closed system is: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$ For free expansion: - The gas expands against a **vacuum**, so $P_{ext} = 0$. - Substituting into the integral: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} 0 \, dV = 0$$ This result is **independent of the gas type** — it holds for ideal gases, real gases, or any fluid. ## 2.2 Energy Balance for Free Expansion Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the closed system: $$\Delta U = Q - W$$ For an **adiabatic free expansion** (rigid, insulated surroundings): $$Q = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad W = 0$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\Delta U = 0}$$ This is a **path-independent statement** — only initial and final equilibrium states matter. ## 2.3 Temperature Behavior ### Case 1: Ideal Gas For an ideal gas, internal energy depends **only on temperature**: $$U = U(T) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dU = nC_v \, dT$$ Since $\Delta U = 0$, we get: $$\boxed{T_2 = T_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** For an **ideal gas undergoing free expansion**, the temperature remains **constant**, even though the volume has increased and pressure has dropped. This is a direct consequence of the Joule experiment and is a frequently tested board question. ### Case 2: Real Gas For a real gas, internal energy depends on both temperature and volume: $$U = U(T, V) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dU = \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT + \left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$ Since $\Delta U = 0$: $$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial T}\right)_V dT = -\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T dV$$ This yields a small but measurable **temperature change** (Joule's inversion), governed by the **Joule-Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = \int P_{ext}\, dV$ | General boundary work expression | kJ | | 2 | $W_{free} = 0$ | Work in free expansion (since $P_{ext}=0$) | kJ | | 3 | $Q_{free} = 0$ | Adiabatic assumption | kJ | | 4 | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | First Law of Thermodynamics | kJ | | 5 | $\Delta U_{free} = 0$ | For adiabatic free expansion | kJ | | 6 | $U_{ideal} = f(T)$ | Ideal gas internal energy is a function of $T$ only | — | | 7 | $T_2 = T_1$ | Final = Initial temperature for ideal gas | K or °C | | 8 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Ideal gas law applied (since $T$ constant) | — | | 9 | $\Delta H_{free} = 0$ | Enthalpy also constant for ideal gas free expansion | kJ | > [!TIP] > **Remember for KTU Board Exams:** Although $\Delta U = 0$ and $\Delta T = 0$ for an **ideal gas** in free expansion, this **does not** violate the Second Law. The process is **irreversible**, and entropy **increases** because the gas occupies a larger volume available to it. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Cryogenics** | Basis of Joule-Thomson cooling for liquefying gases like N₂, O₂, He | | **Vacuum Technology** | Gas behavior in evacuated chambers & outgassing | | **Steam Power Plants** | Steam dumping into condensers (approximate free expansion) | | **Rocket Propulsion** | Pressurant gas expansion into propellant tanks | | **HVAC** | Expansion valves & sudden pressure drops in ductwork | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Boundary Work in Free Expansion We start from the universal definition of closed-system boundary work: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext} \, dV$$ **Step 1 — Identify the external pressure:** For free expansion, the gas pushes against a vacuum. $$P_{ext} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the integral:** $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} (0) \, dV$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:** $$W = 0 \cdot \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = 0 \cdot (V_2 - V_1) = 0$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{W = 0}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: $\Delta U = 0$ for Adiabatic Free Expansion **Step 1 — Apply the First Law:** The First Law for a closed system states: $$\Delta U = Q - W$$ **Step 2 — Apply the adiabatic condition:** The walls are thermally insulated (Joule's original setup), so: $$Q = 0$$ **Step 3 — Apply the work condition:** From the previous derivation: $$W = 0$$ **Step 4 — Combine both conditions:** $$\Delta U = 0 - 0 = 0$$ $$\boxed{\Delta U = 0}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Constancy for an Ideal Gas **Step 1 — Use the ideal-gas internal energy function:** For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature alone: $$U = U(T)$$ **Step 2 — Express the differential:** $$dU = n C_v \, dT$$ **Step 3 — Equate to zero:** From Step 2 of Section 3.2: $$dU = 0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $dT$:** $$n C_v \, dT = 0$$ Since $n \neq 0$ and $C_v \neq 0$ for any real gas: $$dT = 0$$ **Step 5 — Integrate:** $$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = 0$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = T_1}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure–Volume Relationship After Free Expansion **Step 1 — Apply the ideal gas law initially:** $$P_1 V_1 = n R T_1$$ **Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas law finally:** $$P_2 V_2 = n R T_2$$ **Step 3 — Since $T_2 = T_1$, divide the two equations:** $$\frac{P_2 V_2}{P_1 V_1} = \frac{n R T_2}{n R T_1} = 1$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the final pressure:** $$P_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{V_2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** If $V_2 = 2 V_1$, then $P_2 = P_1 / 2$. This is a standard numerical expectation in 3-mark questions. ## 3.5 Derivation: Entropy Change During Free Expansion of an Ideal Gas Although $\Delta U = 0$ and $\Delta T = 0$, free expansion is **irreversible**. Entropy must be calculated using a **reversible path** between the same end states. **Step 1 — Use the thermodynamic identity:** $$dS = \frac{dU + P\, dV}{T}$$ **Step 2 — Since $dU = 0$:** $$dS = \frac{P\, dV}{T}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute ideal gas law** ($P/T = nR/V$): $$dS = nR \, \frac{dV}{V}$$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $V_1$ to $V_2$:** $$\Delta S = nR \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V} = nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S_{system} = nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) > 0 \quad \text{(since } V_2 > V_1\text{)}}$$ This **positive** entropy change is consistent with the **Second Law**, confirming irreversibility. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of Joule's Free Expansion Apparatus The following block-flow diagram represents the physical layout and energy interactions in Joule's classic free expansion experiment. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph V1["RIGID VESSEL A"] direction TB gasA["GAS AT PRESSURE P1"] volA["VOLUME V1"] gasA --- volA end subgraph V2["RIGID VESSEL B (EVACUATED)"] direction TB vacB["VACUUM, Pext APPROX 0"] volB["VOLUME V2"] vacB --- volB end V1 -- "VALVE OPENED" --> V2 subgraph CAL["ADIABATIC WATER BATH CALORIMETER"] direction TB insul["THERMALLY INSULATED WALLS"] water["WATER BATH (Q MEASURED)"] insul --- water end V1 -. "Q = 0" .- CAL V2 -. "Q = 0" .- CAL state1(("STATE 1: P1, V1, T1")) state2(("STATE 2: P2, V2, T2")) state1 --> state2 noteA["BOUNDARY WORK: W = 0"] noteB["HEAT TRANSFER: Q = 0"] noteC["INTERNAL ENERGY: DELTA U = 0"] noteA ~~~ noteB noteB ~~~ noteC ``` ## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Free Expansion vs. Other Expansion Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD start(["CLOSED SYSTEM EXPANSION"]) start --> checkP{"WHAT IS THE
EXTERNAL PRESSURE?"} checkP -->|"Pext = Pgas
Quasi-static"| quasi["REVERSIBLE PROCESS
W = integral P dV"] checkP -->|"Pext less than Pgas
Constant"| irrev["IRREVERSIBLE PROCESS
W = Pext times V2 minus V1"] checkP -->|"Pext approx 0
Vacuum"| free["FREE EXPANSION
W = 0
DELTA U = 0"] quasi --> path1["PLOTTABLE ON P-V DIAGRAM"] irrev --> path2["NOT PLOTTABLE
SINGLE CURVE"] free --> path3["REPRESENTED BY
DASHED LINE"] free --> outcome["IDEAL GAS: T1 = T2
REAL GAS: T CHANGES"] style free fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style outcome fill:#B5E7A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 P–V Diagram Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PVDIAG["P-V DIAGRAM REPRESENTATION"] direction TB pt1["POINT 1: HIGH P, LOW V
STATE 1"] pt2["POINT 2: LOW P, HIGH V
STATE 2"] pt1 -. "DASHED LINE
NON-EQUILIBRIUM PATH" .-> pt2 pt1 --- pt3["CURVE A: ISOTHERMAL
REVERSIBLE REFERENCE PATH"] pt3 --- pt2 end notePV["CURVE A IS A HYPOTHETICAL
REVERSIBLE PATH USED ONLY
TO COMPUTE DELTA S"] PVDIAG ~~~ notePV ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define free expansion. Why is the work done zero in such a process?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Free expansion is the irreversible expansion of a gas into a vacuum or a region where the external opposing pressure is essentially zero. The process is non-quasi-static and the system passes through non-equilibrium intermediate states. **Work is zero (1 mark):** Since $P_{ext} = 0$, the boundary work $W = \int P_{ext}\, dV = 0$. There is no piston, no atmosphere, and no load resisting the expansion. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **For an ideal gas undergoing free expansion, show that $T_2 = T_1$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** First Law for an adiabatic free expansion: $\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** For an ideal gas, $U = U(T)$, so $dU = nC_v\, dT$. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Since $\Delta U = 0$, we have $nC_v(T_2 - T_1) = 0 \Rightarrow T_2 = T_1$. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Do NOT confuse $P_{ext}$ with $P_{system}$** — Students often write $W = \int P_{system}\, dV$, which is **only valid for reversible processes**. Free expansion is irreversible. > 2. **Do NOT claim $T$ is constant for real gases** — This is the most common deduction mark. Always specify "**for an ideal gas**" when stating $T_2 = T_1$. > 3. **Do NOT skip units** — Final numerical answers must include proper SI units (kJ, K, kPa). > 4. **Entropy calculation requires a reversible path** — Computing $\Delta S$ using the irreversible path is a frequent error; mark penalty of up to 3 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** A rigid insulated tank is divided into two equal compartments by a thin partition. One side contains **2 mol of nitrogen (ideal gas)** at **500 kPa** and the other side is **completely evacuated**. The partition is suddenly removed and the gas is allowed to expand until equilibrium is reached. **(7 marks)** **Calculate:** (i) The work done. (ii) The final temperature. (iii) The final pressure. **(b)** If the same gas now undergoes free expansion into a vacuum whose volume is **three times** the original gas volume, determine: (i) The change in internal energy. (ii) The entropy change of the system. (iii) Comment on whether this process violates the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A #### **Part (a) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii) (7 marks)** **Given:** - $n = 2$ mol - $P_1 = 500$ kPa - Adiabatic walls ($Q = 0$) - Free expansion ($W = 0$) **(i) Work Done (2 marks):** For free expansion into a vacuum: $$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_{ext}\, dV = 0 \quad \text{(since } P_{ext} = 0\text{)}$$ $$\boxed{W = 0 \text{ kJ}}$$ **(ii) Final Temperature (2 marks):** Apply the First Law: $$\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$$ For an ideal gas: $$\Delta U = nC_v(T_2 - T_1) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = T_1}$$ Since no initial temperature was given, $T_2$ equals whatever $T_1$ was. **(iii) Final Pressure (3 marks):** Let $V_1$ be the volume of one compartment. After partition removal, total volume = $V_1 + V_1 = 2V_1$, so $V_2 = 2V_1$. Using $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (since $T$ is constant for ideal gas): $$P_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{V_2} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{2V_1} = \frac{P_1}{2}$$ $$P_2 = \frac{500}{2} = 250 \text{ kPa}$$ $$\boxed{P_2 = 250 \text{ kPa}}$$ #### **Part (b) — Sub-parts (i), (ii), (iii) (7 marks)** **Given:** - $V_2 = 3 V_1$ (volume becomes triple) - Ideal gas behavior **(i) Change in Internal Energy (2 marks):** $$\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$$ $$\boxed{\Delta U = 0 \text{ kJ}}$$ **(ii) Entropy Change of System (3 marks):** Use a reversible isothermal path between the same end states: $$\Delta S_{system} = nR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ Taking $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K): $$\Delta S_{system} = (2)(8.314) \ln(3)$$ $$\Delta S_{system} = (16.628)(1.0986)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S_{system} = 18.27 \text{ J/K}}$$ **(iii) Second Law Comment (2 marks):** The process is **adiabatic** ($Q = 0$) and **isolated** (no surroundings interaction). The entropy of the **system increases** ($\Delta S_{sys} = +18.27$ J/K > 0), while the entropy of the **surroundings remains zero** ($\Delta S_{surr} = 0$). Therefore: $$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = +18.27 \text{ J/K} > 0$$ This **does not violate** the Second Law; in fact, the positive entropy change confirms the **irreversibility** of free expansion. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Define free expansion. With the aid of a neat sketch, describe Joule's experiment to demonstrate free expansion. Discuss why $\Delta U = 0$ for an ideal gas in this process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A piston-cylinder device contains **0.5 kg of air** at **200 kPa** and **27 °C**. The piston is suddenly removed and the air expands into a vacuum of volume **0.3 m³**. Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas with $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), determine: (i) The initial and final volumes. (ii) The work done. (iii) The final temperature and pressure. (iv) The entropy change of the air. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B #### **Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks)** **Definition (2 marks):** Free expansion is the unresisted, irreversible expansion of a gas into a region of zero or negligible external pressure, resulting in zero boundary work. **Joule's Experiment (3 marks):** Two rigid, well-insulated copper vessels (A and B) connected by a stopcock are immersed in an adiabatic water bath. Vessel A contains a high-pressure gas; Vessel B is evacuated. When the stopcock is opened, gas expands from A to B without doing work and without heat exchange. Joule observed **no temperature change** in the water bath, proving $\Delta U = 0$ and $T_2 = T_1$ for ideal gases. **Why $\Delta U = 0$ for Ideal Gas (2 marks):** The First Law gives $\Delta U = Q - W$. With adiabatic walls ($Q = 0$) and free expansion ($W = 0$), we get $\Delta U = 0$. For an ideal gas, since $U$ depends only on $T$, this implies $T_2 = T_1$. #### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** - $m = 0.5$ kg - $P_1 = 200$ kPa - $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K - $V_{vacuum} = 0.3$ m³ - $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) - $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K) **(i) Initial and Final Volumes (2 marks):** Using ideal gas law for initial volume: $$V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \frac{(0.5)(0.287)(300)}{200}$$ $$V_1 = \frac{43.05}{200} = 0.21525 \text{ m}^3$$ Final volume (gas + vacuum): $$V_2 = V_1 + V_{vacuum} = 0.21525 + 0.3 = 0.51525 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\boxed{V_1 = 0.2153 \text{ m}^3, \quad V_2 = 0.5153 \text{ m}^3}$$ **(ii) Work Done (1 mark):** $$\boxed{W = 0 \text{ kJ}}$$ **(iii) Final Temperature and Pressure (2 marks):** $$\Delta U = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$$ For pressure, using $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$: $$P_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{V_2} = \frac{(200)(0.21525)}{0.51525} = 83.55 \text{ kPa}$$ $$\boxed{T_2 = 300 \text{ K}, \quad P_2 = 83.55 \text{ kPa}}$$ **(iv) Entropy Change (2 marks):** $$\Delta S = mR \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ $$\Delta S = (0.5)(0.287)\ln\!\left(\frac{0.51525}{0.21525}\right)$$ $$\Delta S = (0.1435)(\ln 2.3935) = (0.1435)(0.8726)$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S = 0.1252 \text{ kJ/K} = 125.2 \text{ J/K}}$$ --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Free Expansion Work** - ✅ **Free expansion** = gas expands into vacuum; $P_{ext} = 0$. - ✅ **Work done** $W = 0$ **always**, regardless of gas type. - ✅ **Adiabatic** assumption means $Q = 0$. - ✅ **First Law** gives $\Delta U = 0$. - ✅ For **ideal gas**: $T_2 = T_1$ and $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$. - ✅ For **real gas**: temperature may slightly change (Joule-Thomson effect). - ✅ The process is **irreversible** and **cannot be plotted** as a continuous $P\text{-}V$ curve (use **dashed line**). - ✅ Entropy **increases**: $\Delta S_{system} = nR \ln(V_2/V_1) > 0$. - ✅ $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ confirms the Second Law is **not violated**. - ✅ **Free expansion ≠ reversible isothermal expansion**, even though both have $\Delta U = 0$ for an ideal gas. - ✅ Common engineering applications: cryogenics (J-T cooling), vacuum systems, condenser steam dumping. - ✅ Examiner's hot keywords: **"unresisted"**, **"non-quasi-static"**, **"boundary work is zero"**, **"entropy generation"**. ---

topics.title: Heat and Heat Capacity; Joule's Experiment - First Law of Thermodynamics Applied to Non-Flow Process; Enthalpy, Specific Heats, and PMM1
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat and Heat Capacity; Joule's Experiment - First Law of Thermodynamics Applied to Non-Flow Process; Enthalpy, Specific Heats, and PMM1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Heat, Heat Capacity, Joule's Experiment, First Law (Non-Flow), Enthalpy, Specific Heats & PMM1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Heat (Q)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Heat** is the form of energy that is transferred across the boundary of a system *solely because of a temperature difference* between the system and its surroundings. It is a **path function** (an inexact differential, denoted $\delta Q$), not a property of the system.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a hot cup of coffee on a table. Energy flows *out* of the coffee into the surrounding air *only* because the coffee is hotter than the air. That transferred energy is what we call **heat**. Notice: the coffee does not "contain" heat — the heat is the *movement* of thermal energy. If both the coffee and the room reach the same temperature, the heat transfer stops. The **SI unit** is the **Joule (J)**, and the traditional thermal unit is the **calorie (cal)**. The standard metric relationship is $\mathbf{1 \ cal = 4.1868 \ J}$.

### 1.2 Heat Capacity (C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Heat Capacity** of a system is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of the system by **one degree** (one Kelvin or one degree Celsius). Mathematically:
> $$C = \frac{\delta Q}{dT} \quad \left[ \frac{J}{K} \right]$$
> It is an **extensive property** — it depends on the mass of the substance.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a large bucket of water versus a small cup of water, both at room temperature. To warm each by 1 °C, the bucket needs far more energy. The bucket therefore has a much greater "heat capacity."

### 1.3 Specific Heat Capacity (c)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Specific Heat Capacity** is the heat capacity *per unit mass* of the substance:
> $$c = \frac{\delta Q}{m \cdot dT} \quad \left[ \frac{kJ}{kg \cdot K} \right]$$
> It is an **intensive property** — it does not depend on the amount of substance.

**Physical Constants (to memorize):**
- Specific heat of **water**: $c_{water} = 4.186 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- Specific heat of **ice**: $c_{ice} = 2.093 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$
- Specific heat of **air (Cp)**: $\approx 1.005 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

### 1.4 Joule's Experiment (Mechanical Equivalent of Heat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Joule's Experiment (c. 1843–1850):** James Prescott Joule demonstrated experimentally that **mechanical work and heat are mutually convertible**, and established a fixed numerical ratio between them — now called the **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (J)**.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
When you rub your palms together vigorously, they get hot. You are doing mechanical *work* with your muscles, and that work is being converted into *heat* inside your skin. Joule proved this isn't a coincidence — it's a precise, universal conversion: $1 \ \text{kcal} = 4186.8 \ \text{J}$ of mechanical work.

### 1.5 First Law Applied to a Non-Flow Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> For a **closed system (non-flow process)** undergoing a cycle, the First Law of Thermodynamics states that the net heat transfer equals the net work transfer:
> $$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$
> For a **change of state** of a closed system:
> $$Q = \Delta U + W$$
> where $Q$ is heat supplied, $\Delta U$ is change in internal energy, and $W$ is work done *by* the system.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Treat the gas in a piston-cylinder as your "savings account." Heat added ($Q$) is your *deposit*. This deposit splits into two uses: some energy increases the internal "wealth" of the gas ($\Delta U$, raising its temperature), and the rest is "spent" to push the piston up and do useful work ($W$).

### 1.6 Enthalpy (H)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Enthalpy** is a thermodynamic property defined as:
> $$H = U + pV \quad \text{or} \quad h = u + pv \quad \left[ \frac{kJ}{kg} \right]$$
> It is a **point function** (exact differential, $dH$) and a combination of other properties, hence itself a property.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Internal energy $U$ is the "intrinsic" energy of the gas. But gases also have to *push back* the atmosphere just to exist — that extra $pV$ energy requirement is bundled with $U$ to form enthalpy $H$. This is especially handy in **flow processes** (covered later) where $H$ naturally appears.

### 1.7 Specific Heats of Gases ($C_p$ and $C_v$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definitions:**
> - **Specific heat at constant volume** $C_v$: heat required per unit mass to raise temperature by 1 K *at constant volume* — all heat goes into internal energy.
> $$C_v = \left(\frac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_v = \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial T}\right)_v$$
> - **Specific heat at constant pressure** $C_p$: heat required per unit mass to raise temperature by 1 K *at constant pressure* — heat goes into internal energy *plus* flow work ($pV$).
> $$C_p = \left(\frac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_p = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_p$$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Heating gas in a *rigid sealed container* (constant volume) — the gas can't expand, so all heat goes into making molecules vibrate faster (internal energy). Heating gas in a *piston that moves freely* (constant pressure) — some heat accelerates molecules *and* some is "used" to push the piston back against atmospheric pressure. Hence, $C_p > C_v$.

### 1.8 Perpetual Motion Machine of the First Kind (PMM1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **PMM1** is a hypothetical device that would produce **net work output without any energy input** (i.e., $W_{net,out} \neq 0$ while $Q_{net,in} = 0$), thereby **violating the First Law of Thermodynamics**. It is therefore classified as a thermodynamic impossibility.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A magical motor that runs forever, powering your house, never needing fuel or a battery. It sounds wonderful, but no such device can exist — the universe's total energy is conserved, and you cannot extract more energy than you put in.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Joule's Experiment — Detailed Theory

### Why was the experiment needed?
Before Joule, **heat** and **work** were believed to be two *separate* "substances" (caloric theory). Joule designed a *famous paddle-wheel apparatus* to prove that mechanical work could be quantitatively converted into heat.

### Apparatus Description (Logic Steps)
- A known mass $m$ descends under gravity, turning a paddle wheel *via* a string and pulley.
- The paddle wheel churns water inside an **insulated calorimeter** (so no heat leaks in or out).
- The friction between paddles and water converts mechanical work into heat, raising water temperature.
- The temperature rise $\Delta T$ is measured with a sensitive thermometer.

### Logic of Energy Conversion
1. The falling mass loses **potential energy** = $mgh$ (mechanical work input $W$).
2. The paddle wheel transfers *all* of this work into the water through viscous friction.
3. The water absorbs this energy as **heat** $Q = (mc)\Delta T$.
4. Insulation ensures **no heat loss**, so $W = Q$ exactly.
5. Repeated trials with different masses establish a *constant ratio*: $J = W/Q$.

## 2.2 First Law of Thermodynamics — Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Symbol | Sign if Added to System | Sign if Rejected by System |
|----------|:------:|:----------------------:|:-------------------------:|
| Heat | $Q$ | **+ve** | **−ve** |
| Work | $W$ | **−ve** (done *on* system) | **+ve** (done *by* system) |

**Equation (KTU Exam-Friendly Form):**
$$Q = \Delta U + W$$
- $Q$ = +ve if heat *supplied* to system
- $W$ = +ve if work done *by* system
- $\Delta U$ = +ve if internal energy *increases*

> [!NOTE]
> Always follow this **IIT/KTU sign convention** in your exam answer unless the problem specifically states otherwise. Marks are frequently lost for sign errors.

## 2.3 Application to Specific Non-Flow Processes

| Process | Condition | First Law Becomes | Resulting Equation |
|---------|:---------:|:-----------------:|:------------------:|
| **Isochoric (Constant V)** | $W = 0$ | $Q = \Delta U$ | $Q = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isobaric (Constant p)** | $W = p(V_2-V_1)$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | $Q = m C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Isothermal (Ideal Gas)** | $\Delta U = 0$ | $Q = W$ | $Q = W = p_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| **Adiabatic** | $Q = 0$ | $0 = \Delta U + W$ | $W = -\Delta U = -m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ |
| **Polytropic** | $pV^n = C$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | $Q = m C_n (T_2 - T_1)$ where $C_n = C_v \cdot \dfrac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$ |

## 2.4 Enthalpy — Why Is It Important?

- $H = U + pV$ is a **property** because $U$, $p$, and $V$ are all properties.
- The change in enthalpy: $\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta(pV) = \Delta U + V\Delta p + p\Delta V$
- For a **constant-pressure process**: $Q_p = \Delta H$ — heat supplied equals change in enthalpy.
- This is why enthalpy dominates **boiling, melting, combustion** calculations, and **flow processes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At a phase change, $H$ changes at *constant temperature*. For example, the **latent heat of vaporization** $h_{fg}$ is the change in *specific enthalpy* during phase change.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Joule's experiment result** is the foundation of the SI energy unit (Joule).
- **First Law** is the basis for designing engines, refrigerators, compressors, and turbines (when combined with the Second Law).
- **Enthalpy** is essential in **steam tables, psychometric charts, refrigeration cycles (COP, vapor-compression), and combustion analysis**.
- **Specific heat values** are critical in calculating heat exchanger sizing, engine fuel economy, and air-conditioning loads.
- **PMM1** rejection is what allows us to confidently say: "no device can be a free-energy machine." This is the *engineering guarantee* behind all energy-balance calculations.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is exam-critical. Memorize every row. For exam answers, write the symbol form with units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|:-:|:-------:|:-------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | Heat Capacity | $C = \delta Q / dT$ | Total heat absorbed per K rise |
| 2 | Specific Heat (mass basis) | $c = \delta Q / (m\,dT)$ | Per-kg heat capacity |
| 3 | Specific Heat (mole basis) | $\bar{c} = \delta Q / (n\,dT)$ | Per-kmol heat capacity |
| 4 | Mechanical Equivalent | $J = W / Q$ | $\mathbf{1 \ kcal = 4.1868 \ kJ = 4186.8 \ J}$ |
| 5 | First Law — Cycle | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | Statement for a cycle |
| 6 | First Law — Change of State | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | Closed system, any process |
| 7 | First Law — Constant Volume | $Q_v = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | $\Delta U$ for ideal gas |
| 8 | First Law — Constant Pressure | $Q_p = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = \Delta H$ | Open/closed, $\Delta H$ |
| 9 | Enthalpy Definition | $H = U + pV$ | $h = u + pv$ per kg |
| 10 | $C_p$ in terms of $h$ | $C_p = (\partial h / \partial T)_p$ | Definition of $C_p$ |
| 11 | $C_v$ in terms of $u$ | $C_v = (\partial u / \partial T)_v$ | Definition of $C_v$ |
| 12 | Mayer's Relation | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Per kg of ideal gas |
| 13 | Ratio of Specific Heats | $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ | Used in adiabatic relations |
| 14 | PMM1 | $W_{out} = Q_{in} = 0$ | Impossible — violates 1st Law |
| 15 | Sign Convention (KTU) | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | +$Q$ in, +$W$ out |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Mayer's Relation:** This holds **strictly for an ideal gas only** (or in the limit of ideal-gas behavior). For real gases, $C_p - C_v \neq R$ exactly — there is a correction term.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step below is *explicit*. No step is skipped, abbreviated, or implied. You can copy this directly into your KTU answer script.

## 3.1 Derivation: Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (Joule's Result)

**Starting Setup:**
- A mass $m$ falls through a height $h$ under gravity $g$.
- The falling mass is connected by a string to a paddle wheel inside an *insulated* calorimeter containing water.
- The water mass is $m_w$, specific heat is $c_w$, and the temperature rise observed is $\Delta T$.

**Step 1 — Mechanical work done by the falling mass:**
The work done *by* the falling mass is equal to its loss in potential energy:
$$W = m g h$$

**Step 2 — Heat gained by water (no losses due to insulation):**
The heat absorbed by the water raises its temperature by $\Delta T$:
$$Q = m_w c_w \Delta T$$

**Step 3 — Equating work and heat (First Law applied to the insulated system):**
Since the calorimeter is insulated, *all* the mechanical work is converted into heat inside the water. By the First Law (with no change in macroscopic KE/PE of water and no work crossing boundary except via the paddle), the energy input equals the energy absorbed:
$$W = Q$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the expressions:**
$$m g h = m_w c_w \Delta T$$

**Step 5 — Defining the Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (J):**
The mechanical equivalent of heat $J$ is the ratio of work done to heat produced:
$$J = \frac{W}{Q} = \frac{m g h}{m_w c_w \Delta T}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical value (KTU standard):**
Joule's careful experiments, refined by modern measurements, yield:
$$J = 4.1868 \ \frac{J}{cal} = 4186.8 \ \frac{J}{kcal}$$

**Equivalent form:**
$$1 \ \text{kcal of heat} = 4186.8 \ \text{J of mechanical work} = 4.1868 \ \text{kJ}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Mayer's Relation ($C_p - C_v = R$)

**Starting Setup:**
Consider 1 kg of an ideal gas undergoing a closed-system process from state 1 to state 2.

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law to a constant-volume process:**
For constant volume: $W = 0$, so $Q_v = \Delta U$
$$Q_v = \Delta U = u_2 - u_1 = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

For 1 kg: $q_v = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$

**Step 2 — Apply the First Law to a constant-pressure process (same temperature change):**
For constant pressure: $W = p(V_2 - V_1) = p_2 V_2 - p_1 V_1$ (since $p$ is constant, $p_1 = p_2 = p$)
$$Q_p = \Delta U + p(V_2 - V_1) = (u_2 - u_1) + p(v_2 - v_1)$$
For 1 kg: $q_p = (u_2 - u_1) + p(v_2 - v_1)$

**Step 3 — Recognize the enthalpy definition:**
$$q_p = (u_2 + p v_2) - (u_1 + p v_1) = h_2 - h_1 = \Delta h$$

**Step 4 — Express $q_p$ using specific heat at constant pressure:**
$$q_p = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 5 — Express $q_v$ using specific heat at constant volume:**
$$q_v = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 6 — Take the difference $q_p - q_v$ using the ideal gas law:**
$$q_p - q_v = C_p(T_2 - T_1) - C_v(T_2 - T_1) = (C_p - C_v)(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 7 — Also express this difference using ideal gas law:**
For an ideal gas: $p v = R T$ (per kg, where $R$ is the *specific* gas constant). So:
$$p v_2 - p v_1 = R T_2 - R T_1 = R(T_2 - T_1)$$

Therefore:
$$q_p - q_v = (C_p - C_v)(T_2 - T_1) = R(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $(T_2 - T_1)$ to obtain Mayer's Relation:**
$$C_p - C_v = R$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Check:** If $R$ is in kJ/kg·K, then $C_p$ and $C_v$ must also be in kJ/kg·K for the units to balance. If $R$ is in kJ/kmol·K (universal gas constant $\bar{R}$), then $C_p$ and $C_v$ are in kJ/kmol·K, and the relation is $C_p - C_v = \bar{R}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: First Law Applied to a Non-Flow Process

**Statement:**
For a closed system (control mass) undergoing a thermodynamic cycle, the First Law states:
$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

**Derivation Logic:**

**Step 1 — Energy balance for a closed system:**
A closed system has a fixed mass; no mass crosses the boundary. Only energy (heat and work) crosses.

**Step 2 — Net energy crossing boundary = change in system energy:**
$$Q_{net} - W_{net} = \Delta E_{system}$$
where $\Delta E_{system} = \Delta U + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$.

**Step 3 — For a typical stationary closed system (neglecting KE and PE changes):**
$$Q_{net} - W_{net} = \Delta U$$
$$\Rightarrow Q_{net} = \Delta U + W_{net}$$

**Step 4 — For a cyclic process (state returns to start):**
$\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$ (since $U$ is a property and state is unchanged).
$$Q_{net,cycle} = W_{net,cycle}$$
which is the cyclic form:
$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$$

**Step 5 — Sign convention finalization (KTU):**
$$Q = \Delta U + W \quad \text{where} \quad W = \int p \, dV$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Enthalpy Change for an Ideal Gas

**Step 1 — Start with definition:**
$$h = u + p v$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate:**
$$dh = du + d(pv) = du + p\,dv + v\,dp$$

**Step 3 — For an ideal gas, $du = C_v dT$ and $pv = RT$ (per kg):**
$$dh = C_v dT + R\,dT = (C_v + R)dT$$

**Step 4 — Use Mayer's relation $R = C_p - C_v$:**
$$dh = (C_v + C_p - C_v) dT = C_p dT$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**
$$dh = C_p dT \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **key result** you will use for *every* constant-pressure or flow-process problem. It is also why $C_p$ is sometimes called the **"enthalpy coefficient."**

## 3.5 Derivation: Why $C_p > C_v$ Always (for any gas)

**Step 1 — Physical reasoning:**
At constant volume, no expansion work is done. All heat goes into increasing internal energy:
$$Q_v = \Delta U = m C_v \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — At constant pressure:**
Some heat is "used up" to push back the atmosphere and create expansion work:
$$Q_p = \Delta U + W = m C_v \Delta T + p \Delta V$$

**Step 3 — For the same temperature rise $\Delta T$, $p \Delta V > 0$:**
Therefore $Q_p > Q_v$, and dividing by the common $m \Delta T$:
$$C_p > C_v$$

**Step 4 — For an ideal gas, the difference is exactly $R$:**
$$C_p - C_v = R$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Key Derivable Results

| Derivation | Initial Statement | Final Compact Form | Key Assumption |
|:----------:|:-----------------:|:------------------:|:--------------:|
| Mechanical Equivalent | $W = mgh$ and $Q = m_w c_w \Delta T$ | $J = W/Q = 4.1868$ J/cal | Insulated calorimeter |
| Mayer's Relation | $q_p - q_v = R(T_2-T_1)$ | $C_p - C_v = R$ | Ideal gas only |
| First Law — Change of State | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | $Q = \Delta U + W$ | Closed system, $\Delta KE = \Delta PE = 0$ |
| First Law — Constant $V$ | $Q = \Delta U + 0$ | $Q = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | Constant volume |
| First Law — Constant $p$ | $Q = \Delta U + p\Delta V$ | $Q = m C_p (T_2 - T_1) = \Delta H$ | Constant pressure |
| Enthalpy — Ideal Gas | $dh = du + d(pv)$ | $dh = C_p dT$ | Ideal gas |
| Internal Energy — Ideal Gas | $du = C_v dT$ | $u_2 - u_1 = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | Ideal gas |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Joule's Paddle-Wheel Apparatus

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional energy-flow architecture** of Joule's experiment — depicting how mechanical work input is converted into measurable heat output through the insulated system boundary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mass m suspended at height h] -- Gravity pulls mass down --> B[Pulley rotates]
    B -- String transmits torque --> C[Paddle wheel inside calorimeter]
    C -- Viscous friction churns water --> D[Water in insulated calorimeter]
    D -- Internal friction raises temperature --> E[Temperature rise DeltaT measured by thermometer]
    E -- Heat Q = m_w c_w DeltaT --> F[Energy Output as Heat]
    A -. Potential energy mgh lost .-> F
    F -- Equating: W = Q --> G[J = W per Q = 4.1868 J per cal]
    H[Insulated outer walls] -. Prevent heat loss .-> D
    H -. Prevent heat loss .-> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: First Law Applied to a Closed System (Non-Flow Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Closed System in State 1]) --> Process[Process Boundary: Non-Flow, No Mass Transfer]
    Process -- Heat Q in --> EnBalance[Energy Balance Equation]
    Process -- Work W out --> EnBalance
    EnBalance --> FirstLaw[Q = Delta U + W]
    FirstLaw --> Check{Process Type?}
    Check -- Constant Volume --> Isoch[Q = m C_v T2 minus T1]
    Check -- Constant Pressure --> Isobar[Q = m C_p T2 minus T1 = Delta H]
    Check -- Isothermal Ideal Gas --> Isoth[W = Q = p1 V1 ln V2 per V1]
    Check -- Adiabatic Q equals 0 --> Adiab[W = minus m C_v T2 minus T1]
    Isoch --> End([State 2 Reached])
    Isobar --> End
    Isoth --> End
    Adiab --> End
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map: Relationships Between U, H, Cp, Cv, R

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Internal Energy U] -- du = Cv dT --> Udiff[dU equation]
    H[Enthalpy H = U plus pV] -- dh = Cp dT --> Hdiff[dH equation]
    H -- Differentiate definition --> Hdef[dh = du plus d of pV]
    Hdef -- Use ideal gas law --> Mayer[Mayer's Relation: Cp minus Cv = R]
    Udiff --> Mayer
    Hdiff --> Mayer
    Mayer --> Gamma[Specific Heat Ratio gamma = Cp per Cv]
    Gamma --> AdiaEq[Adiabatic relations: pV to the gamma power = constant]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: PMM1 Violation Logic (KTU Visual Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hypothetical PMM1 Device] -- Claims --> B[Net Work Output W out positive]
    A -- Claims --> C[Zero Energy Input Q in = 0]
    B --> Violate[Violates First Law]
    C --> Violate
    Violate --> D[Energy can neither be created nor destroyed]
    D --> E[Therefore PMM1 is IMPOSSIBLE]
    E --> F[All real machines must obey W out less than or equal to Q in]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
