# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-recall questions modeled on the **KTU University Exam Part A pattern**. Write the **definition, formula, and one application example** in 3–4 lines for full marks.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define (a) Heat, (b) Heat Capacity, and (c) Specific Heat Capacity. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Heat (Q):** Heat is the form of energy that is transferred across the boundary of a system *due to a temperature difference* between the system and its surroundings. It is a path function (inexact differential $\delta Q$). **SI unit: Joule (J).**
- **(b) Heat Capacity (C):** It is the amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a body by one degree (1 K). $C = \delta Q / dT$. **SI unit: J/K.**
- **(c) Specific Heat Capacity (c):** It is the heat capacity per unit mass, $c = C/m = \delta Q/(m \cdot dT)$. **SI unit: J/(kg·K).** For water, $c_w = 4186.8$ J/(kg·K).

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the First Law of Thermodynamics for a non-flow process. Explain the meaning of each term with a sign convention.**

**Model Answer:**
For a closed system undergoing any non-flow (no mass transfer) process, the First Law states:
$$Q = \Delta U + W$$
where:
- $Q$ = heat transferred *to* the system (**positive** if supplied, negative if rejected).
- $\Delta U$ = change in internal energy of the system (**positive** if it increases).
- $W$ = work done *by* the system (**positive** if done by the system, negative if done on it).
**Sign Convention (KTU/IIT):** $Q_{in} = +ve$, $Q_{out} = -ve$, $W_{by\,system} = +ve$, $W_{on\,system} = -ve$.
For a **cyclic process**, $\Delta U = 0$, hence $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question below is structured exactly like a KTU 2024 End Semester Exam (ESE) Module-2 question. There are **two independent choices (A or B)**. Pick ONE and answer. Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain Joule's experiment to determine the **mechanical equivalent of heat** with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for Joule's constant and state its value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 2 kg of air is heated at constant volume from 300 K to 600 K. Determine (i) the heat supplied, (ii) the change in internal energy, and (iii) the change in enthalpy. Take $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K and $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Joule's Experiment Explanation:**
*(Sketch already shown in Mermaid Section 4.1; replicate on paper as: a falling mass — string — pulley — paddle wheel — insulated calorimeter — thermometer.)*

Joule's apparatus consists of a known mass $m$ attached to a string that passes over a frictionless pulley and winds around a paddle wheel submerged in water contained in an insulated calorimeter. When the mass is allowed to fall through a known height $h$, the paddle wheel rotates and churns the water vigorously.

**Derivation of Joule's Mechanical Equivalent (J):**

- Mechanical work done by falling mass:
$$W = m g h \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

- Heat gained by the water (insulated calorimeter, no heat loss):
$$Q = m_w \, c_w \, \Delta T \quad \text{[calories or Joules]}$$

- By conservation of energy (First Law applied to the insulated system, with no kinetic energy change in water and no boundary work):
$$W = Q$$
$$m g h = m_w \, c_w \, \Delta T$$

- Joule's Mechanical Equivalent:
$$J = \frac{W}{Q} = \frac{m g h}{m_w \, c_w \, \Delta T} = 4.1868 \ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{cal}}$$

- **Equivalent statement:** $1 \ \text{kcal} = 4.1868 \ \text{kJ} = 4186.8 \ \text{J}$

**Conclusion:** Mechanical work and heat are interconvertible, and the ratio between them is a universal constant.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_2 = 600$ K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**(i) Heat supplied at constant volume:**
$$Q_v = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$
$$Q_v = 2 \times 0.718 \times (600 - 300)$$
$$Q_v = 2 \times 0.718 \times 300$$
$$Q_v = 430.8 \ \text{kJ}$$

**(ii) Change in internal energy:**
For a closed system at constant volume, $\Delta U = Q_v$ (since $W = 0$).
$$\Delta U = 430.8 \ \text{kJ}$$

**Cross-check using $C_v$ directly:**
$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = 2 \times 0.718 \times 300 = 430.8 \ \text{kJ} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Change in enthalpy:**
For an ideal gas, $\Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1)$:
$$\Delta H = 2 \times 1.005 \times 300$$
$$\Delta H = 603 \ \text{kJ}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q = 430.8$ kJ
- (ii) $\Delta U = 430.8$ kJ
- (iii) $\Delta H = 603$ kJ

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):** Students frequently confuse which $C$ to use. At **constant volume**, the heat and $\Delta U$ both use $C_v$. The **enthalpy change** *always* uses $C_p$, regardless of the actual process — because $H$ is a *state property* depending only on initial and final states for an ideal gas. Writing $\Delta H = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ will fetch **zero marks** for that part.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Define **enthalpy**. Derive the relation $C_p - C_v = R$ for an ideal gas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas undergoes a non-flow isothermal compression from $V_1 = 0.3$ m³ to $V_2 = 0.1$ m³ at $T = 400$ K. The initial pressure is 100 kPa. Calculate (i) the work done, (ii) the heat transferred, and (iii) the change in internal energy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Enthalpy Definition and Mayer's Relation Derivation:**

**Definition of Enthalpy:**
Enthalpy is a thermodynamic property of a system defined as:
$$H = U + pV \quad \text{(total)} \qquad h = u + pv \quad \text{(specific, per kg)}$$
where $U$ is internal energy, $p$ is pressure, and $V$ is volume. Since $U$, $p$, and $V$ are all properties, $H$ is also a property (point function, exact differential $dH$).

**Derivation of $C_p - C_v = R$:**

Consider 1 kg of an ideal gas undergoing a process from state 1 to state 2.

**Step 1 — Constant-volume process** (work = 0):
$$q_v = \Delta u = u_2 - u_1 = C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Constant-pressure process** (work = $p \Delta v$):
$$q_p = \Delta u + p \Delta v = (u_2 - u_1) + p(v_2 - v_1) = (h_2 - h_1) = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 — Difference** $q_p - q_v$:
$$q_p - q_v = C_p(T_2 - T_1) - C_v(T_2 - T_1) = (C_p - C_v)(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 4 — Also from ideal gas law** ($pv = RT$ per kg):
$$p(v_2 - v_1) = R(T_2 - T_1)$$
So:
$$q_p - q_v = p(v_2 - v_1) = R(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 5 — Equating the two expressions:**
$$(C_p - C_v)(T_2 - T_1) = R(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 6 — Final Result — Mayer's Relation:**
$$C_p - C_v = R$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given:** $V_1 = 0.3$ m³, $V_2 = 0.1$ m³, $T = 400$ K (isothermal), $p_1 = 100$ kPa.

**(i) Work done during isothermal compression:**
For an ideal gas isothermal process, $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2 = mRT$ (constant). The work done *by* the system:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} p \, dV = p_1 V_1 \ln \left( \frac{V_2}{V_1} \right)$$

$$W = 100 \times 0.3 \times \ln \left( \frac{0.1}{0.3} \right)$$

$$W = 30 \times \ln(0.3333)$$

$$W = 30 \times (-1.0986)$$

$$W = -32.96 \ \text{kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates work is done *on* the system (compression).

**(ii) Heat transferred:**
For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$. From the First Law:
$$Q = \Delta U + W = 0 + W = W$$
$$Q = -32.96 \ \text{kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates heat is *rejected* by the system (the surroundings absorbed the heat equivalent of work done on the gas).

**(iii) Change in internal energy:**
For an ideal gas, internal energy is a function of temperature *only*. Since $T$ is constant (isothermal):
$$\Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1) = m C_v \cdot 0 = 0 \ \text{kJ}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $W = -32.96$ kJ (work done *on* system)
- (ii) $Q = -32.96$ kJ (heat rejected)
- (iii) $\Delta U = 0$ kJ

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> - **Sign of the logarithm:** $\ln(0.1/0.3) = \ln(1/3) = -\ln 3 = -1.0986$. Do *not* drop the negative sign.
> - **Forgetting the units of $p_1 V_1$:** Since $p$ is in kPa and $V$ in m³, the product $pV$ is in **kJ** directly — no need to multiply by $10^3$.
> - **Isothermal + ideal gas =** $\Delta U = 0$ *always* — don't write $m C_v \Delta T$ with $\Delta T$ calculated wrongly. If $T$ is constant, the answer is 0 *without calculation*.
> - Students often write $Q = +32.96$ kJ because they forget the sign convention. In KTU, **-ve means heat is rejected**, which is correct for compression.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this as your **last-minute revision sheet** the night before the exam. Each bullet is a guaranteed test-point.

- **Heat (Q)** is energy transfer *due to temperature difference only* — it is a **path function** (inexact differential: $\delta Q$).
- **Heat Capacity (C)** is *extensive*; **Specific Heat (c)** is *intensive*; they relate as $C = m \cdot c$.
- For water: $c_w = 4.1868$ kJ/kg·K; for air: $C_p \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v \approx 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.
- **Joule's Mechanical Equivalent of Heat:** $1$ kcal $= 4186.8$ J $= 4.1868$ kJ.
- **Joule's Experiment:** Falling mass → paddle wheel → insulated water; $W = mgh = m_w c_w \Delta T = Q$.
- **First Law — Cycle:** $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$.
- **First Law — Change of State (KTU Sign Convention):** $Q = \Delta U + W$ (with $Q_{in} = +ve$, $W_{by\,sys} = +ve$).
- **Enthalpy:** $H = U + pV$ (or $h = u + pv$). A property, point function, exact differential $dH$.
- **$C_p$** = heat per unit mass per K rise at **constant pressure** = $(\partial h / \partial T)_p$.
- **$C_v$** = heat per unit mass per K rise at **constant volume** = $(\partial u / \partial T)_v$.
- **Mayer's Relation (ideal gas only):** $C_p - C_v = R$ (per kg basis) or $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = \bar{R}$ (per kmol basis).
- **Specific heat ratio:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v \geq 1$ always; for air, $\gamma \approx 1.4$.
- **Constant-volume process:** $Q_v = \Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$ and $W = 0$.
- **Constant-pressure process:** $Q_p = \Delta H = m C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ and $W = p \Delta V$.
- **Isothermal process (ideal gas):** $\Delta U = 0$, $\Delta H = 0$, $Q = W = p_1 V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
- **Adiabatic process:** $Q = 0$, $W = -\Delta U = -m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$.
- **Ideal-gas internal energy change:** $du = C_v dT$ — depends *only* on temperature change.
- **Ideal-gas enthalpy change:** $dh = C_p dT$ — depends *only* on temperature change.
- **PMM1** is *impossible* — it would violate the First Law by producing work with zero energy input.
- **Units reminder:** $R$ for air $= 0.287$ kJ/kg·K (per kg); $\bar{R} = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K (per kmol).
- **Always show units** in your final answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- **KTU favorite derivations to memorize:** (1) Mayer's relation, (2) $Q = \Delta U + W$ from cycle integral, (3) $dh = C_p dT$ for ideal gas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First Law Applied to Flow Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** First Law Applied to Flow Process # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In engineering practice, most thermodynamic systems involve **fluid flowing through a device** — steam expanding in a turbine, air being compressed, fuel-air mixture entering a nozzle, refrigerant flowing through a throttling valve, etc. Unlike a closed system (control mass) where the boundary is fixed and no mass crosses it, an **open system (control volume)** has mass crossing its boundary along with energy. The **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a flow process** states that, for a control volume operating under steady-state, steady-flow conditions, the net rate of energy transfer (by heat and work) equals the net rate of energy carried out by the fluid mass flow. The governing equation is the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ where all terms are expressed per unit mass of fluid (kJ/kg). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The First Law for flow processes is the foundational tool for analyzing all steady-flow devices like **turbines, compressors, nozzles, diffusers, boilers, condensers, and throttling valves**. Nearly every numerical problem in Module 2 reduces to an application of the SFEE. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a **garden water fountain system** with a hose connected to a pump: - **Mass (water)** continuously enters the hose and continuously leaves the nozzle — your boundary (the hose + nozzle + pump system) has water crossing it constantly. - A **pump** adds energy (work in) → water pressure rises. - A **nozzle** speeds up the water (kinetic energy rises, enthalpy drops). - A **valve** you partially close restricts flow (enthalpy stays nearly same — throttling). - A **turbine** extracts energy from moving water (work out, enthalpy drops). The SFEE simply says: *"Whatever energy goes in (heat + work) must show up somewhere — in the outgoing fluid's heat content (enthalpy), its speed (kinetic energy), or its elevation (potential energy)."* Nothing is created or destroyed — just **redistributed** among different forms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Universal gas constant: **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** > - Specific heat of water (approx.): **c_p = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** > - 1 kW = **1 kJ/s** ## 1.3 Control Mass vs Control Volume — The Critical Distinction | Aspect | Control Mass (Closed System) | Control Volume (Open System) | |---|---|---| | **Boundary** | Fixed, no mass crosses | Fixed in space; mass crosses it | | **Mass** | Constant | Varies with time (steady ⇒ constant at instant) | | **First Law** | Q = ΔU + W | ṁ(h₂ + V₂²/2 + gz₂) − ṁ(h₁ + V₁²/2 + gz₁) = Q̇ − Ẇ | | **Examples** | Piston-cylinder, balloon | Turbine, pump, nozzle, boiler | | **Energy carried** | Only heat & work | Heat, work, AND enthalpy of mass flow | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Derivation Logic of SFEE — Step-by-Step Reasoning Consider a generic engineering device (pump, turbine, nozzle, etc.) through which fluid flows steadily: **Step 1 — Mass balance (continuity equation):** At steady state, the mass entering per unit time equals the mass leaving per unit time. If there is only one inlet (1) and one outlet (2): $$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$$ This ensures the mass contained inside the control volume does not change with time. **Step 2 — Energy balance (First Law for open system):** The total energy of a flowing fluid per unit mass is the sum of: - **Internal energy (u)** - **Flow energy (p·v)** — energy required to push fluid into the control volume against system pressure - **Kinetic energy (V²/2)** - **Potential energy (gz)** Total specific energy: $e_{total} = u + pv + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz = h + \dfrac{V^2}{2} + gz$ **Step 3 — Apply First Law:** $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$ Dividing by ṁ gives the **per-unit-mass SFEE**: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why h appears and not u:** Because in a flow process, the fluid entering carries with it the *flow work* (p·v) required to push it inside. The sum u + pv = h (enthalpy) automatically accounts for this. This is the **single most important conceptual step** in open system analysis. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference | Device | Assumptions | Governing Equation | Useful Work / Effect | |---|---|---|---| | **Nozzle** | q = 0, w = 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | V₂ > V₁, h drops ⇒ KE rises | | **Diffuser** | q = 0, w = 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$ | V₂ < V₁, h rises ⇒ KE recovered | | **Turbine** | q = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $w_s = h_1 - h_2$ | Work output, h₁ > h₂ | | **Compressor** | q = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | Work input, h₂ > h₁ | | **Pump (liquid)** | q = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $w_p = v(h_2 - h_1) = v \cdot \Delta p$ | Work input, for incompressible fluids | | **Boiler** | w = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $q = h_2 - h_1$ | Heat supplied externally | | **Condenser** | w = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $q = h_2 - h_1$ | Heat rejected | | **Throttling Valve** | q = 0, w = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Isenthalpic, pressure drops | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams — "QW = E + KE + PE":** Heat in minus Work out equals change in Enthalpy + change in Kinetic Energy + change in Potential Energy. Then **zero out** whatever doesn't apply for each device. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Steam Power Plants (Rankine Cycle):** SFEE applied to boiler (heat addition), turbine (work extraction), condenser (heat rejection), and pump (work input) is the foundation of **power generation design**. - **Gas Turbine / Jet Engines:** SFEE for compressor (work in), combustion chamber (heat in), and turbine (work out) drives the entire **aerospace and power industry**. - **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Throttling valve and compressor analyses are central to **VCRS/HCRS cycle** calculations. - **Rocket & Jet Nozzles:** SFEE governs rocket thrust calculation: $F = \dot{m}(V_{exit} - V_{inlet})$ — derived directly from momentum + SFEE. - **Hydraulic Turbines (Pelton, Francis):** PE → KE → Work conversion in nozzles and runners — SFEE underpins **hydroelectric power**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of SFEE Starting from the general First Law for an open system (control volume), we consider a system with **one inlet (1)** and **one outlet (2)** operating under **steady-flow, steady-state** conditions. ### 3.1.1 Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation) The general mass balance for a control volume is: $$\frac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$ At **steady state**, $\dfrac{dm_{cv}}{dt} = 0$, so for one inlet and one outlet: $$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$$ This is the **steady-flow continuity equation**. ### 3.1.2 Energy Balance (First Law) The First Law for a control volume in rate form is: $$\dot{Q}_{cv} - \dot{W}_{cv} = \frac{dE_{cv}}{dt} + \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in}$$ **Step 1:** Apply steady state condition $\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$. **Step 2:** Substitute $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$. $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$ **Step 3:** Divide both sides by ṁ to obtain the per-unit-mass SFEE: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 4:** In many engineering devices, kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible compared to enthalpy change. Thus the **practical engineering form** is: $$q - w = \Delta h = h_2 - h_1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "flow work" appears:** Consider fluid entering a control volume at pressure p₁. To push a unit mass of volume v₁ into the system, an external agent must do work equal to p₁v₁ on the fluid. Similarly, the system does work p₂v₂ on the outgoing fluid. Net flow work = p₂v₂ − p₁v₁. Combined with internal energy change Δu: Δu + (p₂v₂ − p₁v₁) = (u₂ + p₂v₂) − (u₁ + p₁v₁) = h₂ − h₁ = Δh. **This is why h replaces u in flow processes.** ## 3.2 Worked Derivations — Typical KTU Numerical Patterns ### 3.2.1 Nozzle Velocity Derivation **Problem Statement:** Show that for an adiabatic nozzle with negligible PE change, the exit velocity is given by: $$V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)}$$ **Derivation:** Start with SFEE: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ Apply nozzle assumptions: - q = 0 (adiabatic) - w = 0 (no shaft work) - g(z₂ − z₁) ≈ 0 (ΔPE negligible) Equation reduces to: $$0 = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$ Rearrange: $$\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} = -(h_2 - h_1) = (h_1 - h_2)$$ $$\frac{V_2^2}{2} = \frac{V_1^2}{2} + (h_1 - h_2)$$ $$V_2^2 = V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)$$ $$\boxed{V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **famous nozzle exit velocity equation** that KTU frequently tests. For incompressible fluids with negligible inlet velocity, it reduces to **Torricelli's equation**: $V_2 = \sqrt{2gh}$ or its energy equivalent $V_2 = \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta h}$. ### 3.2.2 Turbine Work Derivation **Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for specific work output of an adiabatic turbine neglecting KE and PE changes. **Derivation:** Apply SFEE with turbine assumptions (q = 0, ΔKE ≈ 0, ΔPE ≈ 0): $$0 - w_s = (h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0$$ $$w_s = -(h_2 - h_1) = h_1 - h_2$$ $$\boxed{w_s = h_1 - h_2}$$ For steam and gas turbines, this is the **work done per kg of fluid**. Total power: $$\dot{W}_s = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_2) \quad \text{(kW, if ṁ in kg/s)}$$ ### 3.2.3 Compressor Work Derivation (with Isentropic Efficiency) For a **reversible (isentropic) compressor**: $$w_{c,s} = h_{2s} - h_1$$ For an **actual compressor** with isentropic efficiency $\eta_c$: $$\eta_c = \frac{w_{c,s}}{w_{c,actual}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$$ Therefore: $$w_{c,actual} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_c}$$ ### 3.2.4 Throttling Process — Why h is Constant In a throttling valve (e.g., expansion valve in refrigeration): - q = 0 (adiabatic, fast process) - w = 0 (no shaft work) - ΔKE ≈ 0 (large cross-section, low velocity) - ΔPE ≈ 0 SFEE reduces to: $$0 = (h_2 - h_1)$$ $$\boxed{h_1 = h_2}$$ This **isenthalpic** process causes a pressure drop accompanied by temperature drop (Joule-Thomson effect) — the working principle of refrigeration expansion valves. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Steady-Flow Device Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet State 1
h1, V1, z1, p1] --> B{Control Volume
SFEE Applied} B --> C[Outlet State 2
h2, V2, z2, p2] Q[Heat Transfer Q] -.-> B W[Work Transfer W] <--.-> B M[Mass Flow m_dot] --> B B --> D[Energy Balance:
q - w = delta h + delta KE + delta PE] style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000 style B fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000 style Q fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000 style W fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000 style M fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00796B,color:#000 style D fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right SFEE Form ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Identify the Device]) --> Q1{Is heat
transfer involved?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Apply q = h2 - h1
Boiler or Condenser] Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is shaft
work involved?} Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Work is in
or out?} Q4 -- Out --> Q5[Turbine: ws = h1 - h2] Q4 -- In --> Q6{Is fluid
compressible?} Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Compressor: wc = h2 - h1] Q6 -- No --> Q8[Pump: wp = v times delta p] Q3 -- No --> Q9{Velocity
changes?} Q9 -- Increases --> Q10[Nozzle: V2 = sqrt V1 squared + 2h1 minus h2] Q9 -- Decreases --> Q11[Diffuser: h rises, KE drops] Q9 -- No --> Q12[Throttling Valve: h1 = h2] style Start fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style Q1 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style Q2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q3 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style Q4 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style Q5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q6 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style Q7 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q8 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q9 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style Q10 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q11 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style Q12 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Application Map — Where SFEE is Used in Real Systems ```mermaid flowchart LR SFEE[Steady Flow
Energy Equation] --> P1[Steam Power Plant
Rankine Cycle] SFEE --> P2[Gas Turbine Plant
Brayton Cycle] SFEE --> P3[Refrigeration
Vapour Compression] SFEE --> P4[Jet Propulsion
Rocket Engines] SFEE --> P5[Hydraulic Systems
Pumps and Turbines] P1 --> P1A[Boiler: q = delta h] P1 --> P1B[Turbine: ws = h1 minus h2] P1 --> P1C[Condenser: q = delta h] P1 --> P1D[Pump: wp = v delta p] P2 --> P2A[Compressor: wc = h2s minus h1] P2 --> P2B[Combustion Chamber: q in] P2 --> P2C[Turbine: ws = h1 minus h2] P2 --> P2D[Nozzle: V2 = sqrt 2 delta h] P3 --> P3A[Compressor] P3 --> P3B[Condenser] P3 --> P3C[Throttle: h1 = h2] P3 --> P3D[Evaporator] style SFEE fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style P1 fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 style P2 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 style P3 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style P4 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 style P5 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#004D40,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE). State its significance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) is the mathematical statement of the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open system operating under steady-flow, steady-state conditions with one inlet and one outlet: $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ where q = heat transfer per kg, w = work transfer per kg, h = specific enthalpy, V = velocity, z = elevation, g = acceleration due to gravity. **Significance:** - It forms the foundation for analyzing **all steady-flow devices** — turbines, compressors, nozzles, diffusers, pumps, boilers, condensers. - It enables determination of **work output/input**, **heat transfer**, **exit velocities**, and **state changes** in flow processes. - It is the **backbone of all thermodynamic cycle analyses** (Rankine, Brayton, Refrigeration). --- ### Question 2: What is a throttling process? Show that it is an isenthalpic process. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **throttling process** is a flow process in which a fluid is forced through a restriction (such as a partially closed valve, porous plug, or capillary tube), resulting in a **drop in pressure** without any external work or heat transfer. **Proof of Isenthalpic Nature:** Applying SFEE to a throttling valve with assumptions: - q = 0 (adiabatic — process is rapid) - w = 0 (no shaft work) - ΔKE ≈ 0 (negligible velocity change due to large area) - ΔPE ≈ 0 (negligible elevation change) $$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0$$ $$0 = h_2 - h_1$$ $$\boxed{h_1 = h_2}$$ Hence, the throttling process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert enthalpy from kJ/kg to J/kg (or vice versa) when calculating velocity. Remember: if h is in kJ/kg, multiply by **1000** before using $\frac{V^2}{2}$. > - **Common Mistake #2:** For pumps (incompressible fluid), use $w = v\Delta p$ NOT $w = \Delta h$. Using Δh gives wrong answers. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Sign convention confusion: Turbine work is **output (positive)**, compressor/pump work is **input (negative if "w" denotes work done BY system)**. Read the question's sign convention carefully. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Students write SFEE but forget to **state assumptions** for each device. Examiners **demand** explicit assumption listing (q=0, w=0, ΔKE≈0, ΔPE≈0) for full marks. --- ### Question A: First Law Application to Turbine and Compressor `[14 Marks]` **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Steam enters an adiabatic turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C with a velocity of 60 m/s and leaves at 50 kPa with a velocity of 200 m/s. The mass flow rate is 10 kg/s. Determine: - (i) The specific work output of the turbine. - (ii) The power output in kW. Assume elevation change is negligible. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An air compressor compresses air adiabatically from 100 kPa, 20 °C to 800 kPa with an isentropic efficiency of 85%. Determine the actual work input per kg of air. For air, take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K). --- ### Model Solution — Question A: **Part (a) — Turbine Analysis:** **Step 1: State the SFEE for an adiabatic turbine:** $$q - w_s = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 2: Apply assumptions:** - q = 0 (adiabatic) - ΔPE ≈ 0 (z₂ − z₁ ≈ 0) - g(z₂ − z₁) term vanishes Equation becomes: $$-w_s = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$ $$w_s = (h_1 - h_2) + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2}$$ **Step 3: Find enthalpy values from steam tables:** At **Inlet (3 MPa, 350 °C)** — Superheated steam: $$h_1 = 3043.4 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(from standard steam tables)}$$ At **Outlet (50 kPa)** — Need to determine quality. From steam tables at 50 kPa: $$h_f = 340.5 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2305.4 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2645.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Without additional information on exit quality, assume exit is **saturated vapor** (typical assumption when not specified): $$h_2 = h_g \text{ at } 50 \text{ kPa} = 2645.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Substitute numerical values:** Enthalpy change: $$h_1 - h_2 = 3043.4 - 2645.9 = 397.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ Kinetic energy change (must use J/kg, so multiply kJ/kg by 1000): $$\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2} = \frac{(60)^2 - (200)^2}{2} = \frac{3600 - 40000}{2} = \frac{-36400}{2} = -18200 \text{ J/kg}$$ Converting to kJ/kg: $-18200 / 1000 = -18.2$ kJ/kg **Step 5: Calculate specific work:** $$w_s = 397.5 + (-18.2) = 379.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{w_s \approx 379.3 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 6: Calculate power output:** $$\dot{W}_s = \dot{m} \times w_s = 10 \text{ kg/s} \times 379.3 \text{ kJ/kg} = 3793 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{W}_s \approx 3793 \text{ kW} \approx 3.79 \text{ MW}}$$ --- **Part (b) — Compressor with Isentropic Efficiency:** **Step 1: For isentropic compression of an ideal gas:** $$T_{2s} = T_1 \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values:** $$T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293 \text{ K}$$ $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{800}{100} = 8$$ $$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ $$T_{2s} = 293 \times (8)^{0.2857}$$ Calculate $(8)^{0.2857}$: $$\ln(8) = 2.0794$$ $$0.2857 \times 2.0794 = 0.5941$$ $$e^{0.5941} = 1.8114$$ Therefore: $$T_{2s} = 293 \times 1.8114 = 530.74 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Calculate isentropic work input:** $$w_{c,s} = c_p (T_{2s} - T_1) = 1.005 \times (530.74 - 293)$$ $$w_{c,s} = 1.005 \times 237.74 = 238.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Apply isentropic efficiency:** $$\eta_c = \frac{w_{c,s}}{w_{c,actual}}$$ $$w_{c,actual} = \frac{w_{c,s}}{\eta_c} = \frac{238.93}{0.85}$$ $$\boxed{w_{c,actual} \approx 281.09 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ --- ### Question B: First Law Application to Nozzle and Throttling `[14 Marks]` **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Air at 200 kPa and 150 °C enters an adiabatic nozzle with negligible velocity and exits at 100 kPa. Assuming the process is reversible and air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$, find: - (i) The exit temperature. - (ii) The exit velocity of air. **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Refrigerant R-134a enters the throttling valve of a refrigeration system at 1.0 MPa as saturated liquid and exits at 200 kPa. Determine: - (i) The enthalpy at inlet and outlet. - (ii) The temperature at outlet. - (iii) The quality of refrigerant at outlet, if applicable. --- ### Model Solution — Question B: **Part (a) — Nozzle Analysis:** **Step 1: Apply SFEE for adiabatic nozzle with negligible inlet velocity and ΔPE:** $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ With V₁ ≈ 0: $$V_2 = \sqrt{2(h_1 - h_2)} = \sqrt{2 \cdot c_p (T_1 - T_2)}$$ **Step 2: For reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process of ideal gas:** $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$ **Step 3: Substitute values:** $$T_1 = 150 + 273 = 423 \text{ K}$$ $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$ $$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = 0.2857$$ $$T_2 = 423 \times (0.5)^{0.2857}$$ Calculate $(0.5)^{0.2857}$: $$\ln(0.5) = -0.6931$$ $$0.2857 \times (-0.6931) = -0.1980$$ $$e^{-0.1980} = 0.8204$$ Therefore: $$T_2 = 423 \times 0.8204 = 346.97 \text{ K} \approx 347 \text{ K}$$ Or in Celsius: $$T_2 = 347 - 273 = 74 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{T_2 \approx 347 \text{ K} \approx 74 \text{ °C}}$$ **Step 4: Calculate exit velocity:** $$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times c_p \times (T_1 - T_2)}$$ $$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times (423 - 347)}$$ $$V_2 = \sqrt{2 \times 1005 \times 76}$$ $$V_2 = \sqrt{152760}$$ $$\boxed{V_2 \approx 390.8 \text{ m/s}}$$ --- **Part (b) — Throttling Valve Analysis:** **Step 1: Find enthalpy at inlet (saturated liquid at 1.0 MPa):** From R-134a saturation tables at $p_1 = 1.0$ MPa: $$h_1 = h_f \text{ at } 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 101.61 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$T_{sat} \text{ at } 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 39.37 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 2: For throttling, $h_1 = h_2$:** $$h_2 = h_1 = 101.61 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{h_1 = h_2 = 101.61 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 3: Find outlet temperature at $p_2 = 200$ kPa:** At p₂ = 200 kPa (from R-134a tables): $$h_f = 38.43 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 241.30 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad T_{sat} = -10.09 \text{ °C}$$ Since $h_f < h_2 < h_g$, the refrigerant at outlet is a **saturated mixture**. $$\boxed{T_2 = T_{sat} \text{ at } 200 \text{ kPa} = -10.09 \text{ °C}}$$ **Step 4: Calculate quality at outlet:** $$h_{fg} = h_g - h_f = 241.30 - 38.43 = 202.87 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$h_2 = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$ $$101.61 = 38.43 + x \cdot 202.87$$ $$x = \frac{101.61 - 38.43}{202.87} = \frac{63.18}{202.87}$$ $$\boxed{x \approx 0.3115 \text{ or } 31.15\%}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:** - **SFEE (Master Equation):** $q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$ - **Enthalpy h = u + pv** is the key property in flow processes (replaces internal energy u). - **Steady state assumptions** required for SFEE: $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$ and $\dfrac{dE_{cv}}{dt} = 0$. - **Nozzle:** $V_2 = \sqrt{V_1^2 + 2(h_1 - h_2)}$ — enthalpy drops, KE rises. - **Diffuser:** Opposite of nozzle — KE drops, enthalpy rises. - **Turbine:** $w_s = h_1 - h_2$ — work output, $h_1 > h_2$. - **Compressor:** $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ — work input, $h_2 > h_1$. - **Pump (liquid):** $w_p = v(p_2 - p_1) = v \cdot \Delta p$ — NOT $\Delta h$ for incompressible fluids. - **Boiler / Condenser:** $q = h_2 - h_1$ — heat transfer only. - **Throttling Valve:** $h_1 = h_2$ — isenthalpic process. - **Unit Conversion Trap:** If h is in kJ/kg and velocity in m/s, multiply h by **1000** before using $\frac{V^2}{2}$. - **Always state assumptions** before applying the simplified SFEE — examiners **demand** this for full marks. - **Power equation:** $\dot{W} = \dot{m} \times w$ (kW when ṁ in kg/s and w in kJ/kg). - **Isentropic efficiency** for compressor: $\eta_c = \dfrac{w_{c,s}}{w_{c,actual}}$; for turbine: $\eta_t = \dfrac{w_{actual}}{w_s}$. - **Continuity equation:** $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \dfrac{AV}{v}$ — relates mass flow, area, and velocity.

topics.title: Mass and Energy Balance in Simple Steady Flow Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mass and Energy Balance in Simple Steady Flow Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Control Volume and Control Surface

A **Control Volume (CV)** is a fixed, well-defined region in space selected for the thermodynamic analysis of a flowing fluid. The fluid streams and energy interactions are evaluated as they cross the imaginary boundary enclosing this region.

The **Control Surface (CS)** is the closed, imaginary boundary that envelops the control volume. It acts as the interface through which mass and energy are exchanged with the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In engineering thermodynamics, a *system* is often a device (turbine, compressor, nozzle) through which fluid flows. To apply the First Law to such devices, engineers switch from the *closed system* view to an *open system* (control volume) view.

### 1.2 Steady Flow Process

A process is classified as a **Steady Flow Process** when all properties of the fluid (pressure, temperature, density, velocity) at any fixed point inside the control volume remain constant with respect to time. Mathematically:

$$\left(\frac{\partial X}{\partial t}\right)_{CV} = 0$$

where $X$ represents any fluid property. The rate of mass and energy entering the CV equals the rate leaving the CV at every instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constraint of Steady Flow:** The total mass and total energy *contained* inside the CV at any moment are *constant*. There is *no accumulation* or *depletion* of mass/energy inside the device.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Tap

Imagine a transparent garden hose connected to a municipal tap. Once the tap valve is set to a fixed position:

- The velocity of water at the inlet of the hose remains **5 m/s** at all times.
- The velocity of water at the outlet remains **5 m/s** at all times.
- The mass of water *inside* the flexible hose is constant (the hose is not bulging or collapsing).

If we draw an imaginary boundary around a 1-meter length of the hose, that section becomes our **control volume**. The hose wall is the **control surface**. Water molecules entering at one end (State 1) are different from those leaving the other end (State 2), but the *rate* of flow stays the same. This is the essence of a **steady flow process**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Examples:** Steam flowing through a turbine, air moving through a jet engine, refrigerant circulating in a chiller, water through a pump — *every one* of these is a steady flow process in practice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mass Balance — The Continuity Equation

The **Law of Conservation of Mass** for a control volume states that the net rate of mass accumulation inside the CV equals the net mass inflow minus the net mass outflow.

$$\frac{dm_{CV}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$

For a **steady flow process**, $dm_{CV}/dt = 0$, giving the **Continuity Equation**:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$

For a single-stream (one inlet, one outlet) device:

$$\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 V_1}{v_1} = \frac{A_2 V_2}{v_2}$$

For an **incompressible fluid** ($\rho$ = constant), this reduces to the volumetric form:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

### 2.2 Energy Balance — The First Law for a Control Volume

The First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an open system (control volume) is:

$$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out}$$

where each fluid stream carries three forms of energy per unit mass:
- **Enthalpy** $h$ (internal energy + flow work)
- **Kinetic Energy** $V^2/2$
- **Potential Energy** $gz$

For **steady flow**, $dE_{CV}/dt = 0$, which yields the most important equation in engineering thermodynamics — the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.

### 2.3 The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in}$$

On a **per-unit-mass basis** (dividing by $\dot{m}$), the SFEE is:

$$q - w = \left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}\right) + g\left(z_2 - z_1\right)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Special Cases of SFEE

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important summary** for solving KTU problems on this topic. Memorize it before any exam.

| Device | Heat $q$ | Work $w$ | $\Delta KE$ | $\Delta PE$ | Simplified SFEE | Key Output |
|:------:|:--------:|:--------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:----------------|:-----------|
| **Nozzle** | 0 | 0 | Non-zero | Neglected | $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ | High velocity jet |
| **Diffuser** | 0 | 0 | Non-zero | Neglected | $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ | Pressure recovery |
| **Turbine** | 0 | $h_1 - h_2$ | Neglected | Neglected | $w = h_1 - h_2$ | Power output |
| **Compressor** | 0 | $h_2 - h_1$ | Neglected | Neglected | $w_{in} = h_2 - h_1$ | Power input |
| **Throttle Valve** | 0 | 0 | Neglected | Neglected | $h_1 = h_2$ | Pressure drop |
| **Heat Exchanger** | $h_2 - h_1$ | 0 | Neglected | Neglected | $q = h_2 - h_1$ | Heat transfer |
| **Mixing Chamber** | 0 | 0 | Neglected | Neglected | $\sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$ | Stream mixing |
| **Boiler/Condenser** | $h_2 - h_1$ | 0 | Neglected | Neglected | $q = h_2 - h_1$ | Phase change |

### 2.5 Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

- $q > 0$ ⇒ Heat **supplied to** the fluid
- $q < 0$ ⇒ Heat **rejected by** the fluid
- $w > 0$ ⇒ Work **done by** the fluid (turbines, expanders)
- $w < 0$ ⇒ Work **done on** the fluid (compressors, pumps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The SFEE is the foundation for designing every energy-conversion device — from the steam turbines in a 1000 MW thermal power plant to the tiny turbochargers in automobiles. Mastering it unlocks the analysis of the entire field of *power plant engineering* and *refrigeration*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Steady Flow Energy Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the General First Law for an Open System**

The First Law of Thermodynamics for a control volume (open system) is expressed in rate form. The total energy of a flowing fluid stream comprises internal energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, *and* flow work. This combined quantity is called the **specific total enthalpy** $\theta$:

$$\theta = h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz = \left(u + Pv\right) + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz$$

The differential of total energy stored inside the CV is:

$$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out}$$

where $\dot{W}_{CV}$ is the **shaft work** (boundary work is replaced by flow work inside $h$).

**Step 2 — Apply the Steady-Flow Condition**

By the definition of a steady flow process, the total energy stored inside the CV cannot change with time. Therefore:

$$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$$

Substituting this into the general first law:

$$0 = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Obtain the SFEE**

Bring all mass-flow terms to the right-hand side:

$$\dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in}$$

Expanding the total enthalpy $\theta$:

$$\dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in}\left(h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz\right)_{in}$$

This is the **Steady Flow Energy Equation** in rate form.

**Step 4 — Convert to the Per-Unit-Mass Form**

For a single-stream device (one inlet, one outlet) where $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$, divide the entire equation by $\dot{m}$:

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_{CV}}{\dot{m}} - \frac{\dot{W}_{CV}}{\dot{m}} = \left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}\right) + g\left(z_2 - z_1\right)$$

Letting $q = \dot{Q}/\dot{m}$ and $w = \dot{W}/\dot{m}$:

$$\boxed{\,q - w = \left(h_2 - h_1\right) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g\left(z_2 - z_1\right)\,}$$

This is the **final, board-exam-ready SFEE** that every KTU student must commit to memory.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Walkthrough — Adiabatic Turbine

**Problem Setup:** Steam enters a turbine at $P_1 = 30$ bar, $T_1 = 350°C$, $V_1 = 60$ m/s. It exhausts at $P_2 = 0.1$ bar, $V_2 = 200$ m/s. Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s. The turbine is adiabatic, and elevation change is negligible.

**Step A — Select the Correct SFEE Variant**

From the formula table, for a turbine under adiabatic, no-$\Delta$PE conditions:

$$w = h_1 - h_2$$

**Step B — Read Steam-Table Values**

From superheated steam tables (at 30 bar, 350°C):
$$h_1 = 3126.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Assuming saturated vapor at the exit (0.1 bar):
$$h_2 = h_g = 2584.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step C — Apply the SFEE Including Kinetic Energy**

For accuracy, we include the kinetic energy terms since $V_1$ and $V_2$ are given:

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

With $q = 0$:

$$-w = (2584.6 - 3126.3) + \frac{200^2 - 60^2}{2 \times 1000}$$

The kinetic energy term is converted to kJ/kg by dividing by 1000:

$$\frac{200^2 - 60^2}{2000} = \frac{40000 - 3600}{2000} = 18.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Therefore:

$$-w = -541.7 + 18.2 = -523.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,w = 523.5 \text{ kJ/kg}\,}$$

**Step D — Compute the Power Output**

$$\dot{W}_{turbine} = \dot{m} \times w = 5 \times 523.5 = 2617.5 \text{ kW} \approx 2.62 \text{ MW}$$

### 3.3 Worked Walkthrough — Throttling Valve

**Problem Setup:** Wet steam at 5 bar with dryness fraction $x_1 = 0.9$ is throttled to 1 bar. Find $x_2$.

**Step A — Apply the Throttling Condition**

For a throttle valve: $h_1 = h_2$.

**Step B — Compute $h_1$ from Steam Tables**

At 5 bar: $h_f = 640.23$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2108.5$ kJ/kg.

$$h_1 = h_f + x_1 h_{fg} = 640.23 + 0.9 \times 2108.5 = 2537.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step C — Compute $x_2$ at 1 bar**

At 1 bar: $h_f = 417.46$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2258.0$ kJ/kg.

$$h_2 = h_1 = 2537.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \frac{2537.88 - 417.46}{2258.0} = \frac{2120.42}{2258.0} \approx 0.939$$

$$\boxed{\,x_2 \approx 0.94\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Observation:** Throttling wet steam produces *more wet* steam (dryness fraction decreases). This is the operating principle behind the **throttling calorimeter** used in steam power plants to measure moisture content.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Control Volume with Mass and Energy Streams

The block diagram below illustrates a generic open system (control volume) with the full set of mass, heat, and work interactions. This is the **conceptual backbone** of the SFEE and is universally applicable to turbines, compressors, nozzles, and throttling devices.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inStream[INLET FLUID m1, h1, V1, z1] -->|FLOW DIRECTION| cvBlock[CONTROL VOLUME]
    cvBlock -->|FLOW DIRECTION| outStream[OUTLET FLUID m2, h2, V2, z2]
    qIn[HEAT TRANSFER Q] -->|q_in| cvBlock
    cvBlock -->|q_out| qOut[HEAT REJECTION]
    cvBlock -->|w_shaft| wOut[SHAFT WORK W]
    cvBlock -.-> csBoundary[CONTROL SURFACE]
    style cvBlock fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style csBoundary fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#666,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style inStream fill:#90EE90
    style outStream fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **dashed cyan boundary** (`CONTROL SURFACE`) is the imaginary envelope.
- The **inlet stream** (green) carries mass flow $\dot{m}_1$ at enthalpy $h_1$, velocity $V_1$, and elevation $z_1$.
- The **outlet stream** (pink) carries mass flow $\dot{m}_2$ at enthalpy $h_2$, velocity $V_2$, and elevation $z_2$.
- Heat $Q$ may enter or leave.
- Shaft work $W$ may be extracted (turbine) or supplied (compressor).
- **Steady-flow assumption:** $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$ for a single-stream device.

### 4.2 Mapping of Special Devices onto the Control Volume

The following mapping helps the student *visually* identify which terms of the SFEE survive for a given device.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[GENERIC CONTROL VOLUME] --> B[NOZZLE: q=0, w=0, KE up]
    A --> C[DIFFUSER: q=0, w=0, KE down]
    A --> D[TURBINE: q=0, h drops, w out]
    A --> E[COMPRESSOR: q=0, h rises, w in]
    A --> F[THROTTLE: q=0, w=0, h constant]
    A --> G[HEAT EXCHANGER: q in or out, w=0]
    A --> H[MIXING CHAMBER: 2+ inlets, q=0, w=0]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#E6E6FA
    style C fill:#E6E6FA
    style D fill:#E6E6FA
    style E fill:#E6E6FA
    style F fill:#E6E6FA
    style G fill:#E6E6FA
    style H fill:#E6E6FA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery Tip:** When you see a device name in a problem, immediately **mentally cancel** the irrelevant terms of the SFEE using this map. This single habit will save you 3-4 minutes per problem in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define the following with one example each: (i) Control volume, (ii) Control surface, (iii) Steady flow process. Write the continuity equation for a single-stream steady flow device.

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Control Volume:** A fixed region in space selected for thermodynamic analysis of a flowing fluid. *Example:* A turbine casing enclosing the rotor.
- **(ii) Control Surface:** The imaginary closed boundary of a control volume across which mass and energy flow. *Example:* The inner walls of the turbine casing.
- **(iii) Steady Flow Process:** A process in which fluid properties at every point inside the CV do not change with time. *Example:* Steam flow through a condenser under constant load.
- **Continuity equation:** $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2$, or equivalently, $\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
What is a throttling process? State the assumptions and write the simplified steady flow energy equation (SFEE) for a throttling valve.

**Model Answer:**

A **throttling process** is an irreversible, adiabatic, isenthalpic expansion of a fluid through a restriction (e.g., valve, porous plug, narrow orifice), where a significant pressure drop occurs without any work extraction.

**Assumptions:**
- $q = 0$ (adiabatic; rapid process, no time for heat transfer)
- $w = 0$ (no shaft work)
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$ and $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (small flow area, low velocity)

**Simplified SFEE:**

$$h_1 = h_2$$

This single result is the foundation of devices like **throttling calorimeters** and **expansion valves in refrigeration cycles**.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** State the first law of thermodynamics for a control volume. From this, derive the steady flow energy equation (SFEE) for a single-stream control volume with one inlet and one outlet. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal compressor receives air at **100 kPa, 305 K** with a velocity of **5 m/s**. The air leaves the compressor at **500 kPa, 550 K** with a velocity of **10 m/s**. The mass flow rate is **0.5 kg/s**, and the heat loss to the surroundings is **15 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
    (i) The change in enthalpy of the air.
    (ii) The power required to drive the compressor.
    Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Derivation:**

The First Law for a control volume (open system) in rate form is:

$$\frac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \sum \dot{m}_{in}\theta_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out}\theta_{out}$$

where $\theta = h + V^2/2 + gz$ is the total specific enthalpy.

**Assumptions for SFEE:**
1. **Steady flow:** $\dfrac{dE_{CV}}{dt} = 0$
2. **Single inlet and single outlet:** $\dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}$
3. **Fluid properties uniform** at each cross-section

Substituting the steady-flow condition into the first law:

$$0 = \dot{Q}_{CV} - \dot{W}_{CV} + \dot{m}\left[\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) - \left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right)\right]$$

Rearranging and dividing throughout by $\dot{m}$:

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)$$

This is the required **SFEE**.

**(b) Compressor Calculation:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 305$ K, $V_1 = 5$ m/s, $P_2 = 500$ kPa, $T_2 = 550$ K, $V_2 = 10$ m/s, $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s, $q = -15$ kJ/kg (heat loss), $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K.

**(i) Change in Enthalpy:**

For an ideal gas with constant specific heat:

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$$

$$\Delta h = 1.005 \times (550 - 305) = 1.005 \times 245 = 246.225 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Power Required:**

Apply the SFEE per unit mass:

$$q - w = \Delta h + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

Compute the kinetic energy change (converting J/kg to kJ/kg):

$$\Delta KE = \frac{10^2 - 5^2}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{100 - 25}{2000} = 0.0375 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Substitute all known values (with $q = -15$ kJ/kg since heat is lost):

$$-15 - w = 246.225 + 0.0375$$

$$-15 - w = 246.2625$$

$$w = -15 - 246.2625 = -261.2625 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

The **negative sign** indicates work is done **on** the fluid (compressor work input).

**Power required:**

$$\dot{W}_{in} = \dot{m} \times \vert w \vert = 0.5 \times 261.2625$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{in} = 130.63 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Steam enters a turbine at **30 bar, 350°C** with a velocity of **60 m/s**. It leaves the turbine at **0.1 bar** with a velocity of **200 m/s**. The mass flow rate is **5 kg/s**. Assuming adiabatic conditions and negligible change in potential energy, calculate the power output of the turbine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Steam at **5 bar** with a dryness fraction of **0.9** is throttled to **1 bar**. Determine the dryness fraction of steam at the exit of the throttle valve. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Turbine Power Output:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 30$ bar, $T_1 = 350°C$, $V_1 = 60$ m/s, $P_2 = 0.1$ bar, $V_2 = 200$ m/s, $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s, $q = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$.

**Step 1 — Identify the SFEE form for a turbine:**

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Look up steam-table values:**

At 30 bar, 350°C (superheated steam):

$$h_1 = 3126.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

At 0.1 bar, saturated vapor (assumed at exit):

$$h_2 = h_g = 2584.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kinetic energy change:**

$$\Delta KE = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{200^2 - 60^2}{2000} = \frac{40000 - 3600}{2000} = 18.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the SFEE:**

With $q = 0$:

$$-w = (h_2 - h_1) + \Delta KE$$

$$-w = (2584.6 - 3126.3) + 18.2 = -541.7 + 18.2 = -523.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w = +523.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

The **positive sign** indicates work is done **by** the fluid (turbine work output).

**Step 5 — Calculate the power output:**

$$\dot{W}_{turbine} = \dot{m} \times w = 5 \times 523.5$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{turbine} = 2617.5 \text{ kW} \approx 2.62 \text{ MW}}$$

**(b) Throttling Dryness Fraction:**

**Given:** $P_1 = 5$ bar, $x_1 = 0.9$, $P_2 = 1$ bar.

**Step 1 — Apply the throttling condition:**

$$h_1 = h_2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $h_1$ from steam tables at 5 bar:**

At 5 bar: $h_f = 640.23$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2108.5$ kJ/kg.

$$h_1 = h_f + x_1 \cdot h_{fg} = 640.23 + 0.9 \times 2108.5$$

$$h_1 = 640.23 + 1897.65 = 2537.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $x_2$ at 1 bar:**

At 1 bar: $h_f = 417.46$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2258.0$ kJ/kg.

$$h_2 = h_1 = 2537.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_f}{h_{fg}} = \frac{2537.88 - 417.46}{2258.0}$$

$$x_2 = \frac{2120.42}{2258.0}$$

$$\boxed{x_2 \approx 0.939}$$

**Inference:** The steam becomes *slightly wetter* during throttling (from $x = 0.90$ to $x \approx 0.94$... wait, $0.94 > 0.90$, so it is actually *drier*; this confirms the physical reality that throttling is an isenthalpic expansion, and for a fixed enthalpy, the dryness fraction changes with pressure).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Cost: 1-2 marks):** The most common mistake is forgetting that *work is positive when done BY the fluid* and *heat is positive when added TO the fluid*. In compressor problems, work comes out negative — students often report the power output as a *negative* number, losing 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in KE and PE (Cost: 1 mark):** Kinetic energy is naturally in J/kg, while enthalpies are in kJ/kg. Students forget to divide by 1000 when adding $\Delta KE$ to $\Delta h$. This single error cascades and produces wrong answers.
>
> 3. **Wrong Steam-Table Lookup (Cost: 2-3 marks):** For superheated steam, students often use saturated-steam tables. Always verify whether the steam is saturated (use $T_{sat}$) or superheated (use the $T > T_{sat}$ table).
>
> 4. **Forgetting Mass Conservation (Cost: 1 mark):** Even in steady flow, the problem may have multiple inlets or outlets. Always write $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ explicitly *before* applying the SFEE.
>
> 5. **Treating Throttling Wrongly (Cost: 2 marks):** Throttling is *not* isothermal or isobaric. It is **isenthalpic** ($h_1 = h_2$). Forgetting this in mixing-chamber or calorimeter problems is a frequent error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Control Volume** is the fundamental analysis region for any device handling flowing fluid; it is bounded by the **Control Surface**.
- A **Steady Flow Process** has zero time-derivative of mass and energy inside the CV — *no accumulation*.
- The **Continuity Equation** for steady flow: $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$; for incompressible flow: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.
- The **SFEE** is the energy counterpart: $q - w = \Delta h + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$.
- For **turbines** (adiabatic, $\Delta KE = 0$): $w = h_1 - h_2 > 0$.
- For **compressors** (adiabatic, $\Delta KE = 0$): $w = h_2 - h_1 < 0$; power required is $\vert w \vert \cdot \dot{m}$.
- For **nozzles and diffusers** ($q = 0$, $w = 0$): $h_1 + V_1^2/2 = h_2 + V_2^2/2$ — enthalpy converts to kinetic energy (or vice versa).
- For **throttling devices** ($q = 0$, $w = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$): $h_1 = h_2$ — the **isenthalpic principle**.
- For **heat exchangers** ($w = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$): $q = h_2 - h_1$.
- For **mixing chambers** ($q = 0$, $w = 0$): $\sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$.
- The **standard gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81$ m/s² must be used when $\Delta PE$ is non-negligible.
- **KE and PE are often neglected** in turbines, compressors, and boilers because $\Delta h$ dominates; however, in nozzles and diffusers, **KE cannot be neglected**.
- **Sign discipline is everything** in KTU valuation: write $w = +523.5$ kJ/kg for turbines and $w = -261.26$ kJ/kg for compressors — the *sign itself carries the physics*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitations of First Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limitations of First Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** is the principle of conservation of energy applied to thermodynamic systems. Mathematically, for a closed system undergoing a cyclic process:

$$\oint \mathrm{d}Q = \oint \mathrm{d}W$$

For a change of state:

$$\mathrm{d}Q = \mathrm{d}U + \mathrm{d}W$$

where $Q$ is heat transferred, $W$ is work done, and $U$ is internal energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Standard):** The First Law asserts that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another, and the total energy of an isolated system is constant.

However, despite being universally valid, the First Law has **inherent limitations** — it cannot answer three pivotal engineering questions:

| # | Question | Answered by First Law? |
|---|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Will the process actually occur (direction)? | ❌ No |
| 2 | To what extent can heat be converted to work? | ❌ No |
| 3 | What is the quality / availability of energy? | ❌ No |

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Account Story:**
> 
> Imagine a student who earns money (energy input) and spends money on rent, food, and books (energy output). The **First Law is like an accountant** — it tracks every rupee in and every rupee out perfectly. The total balance is always conserved.
> 
> **But the accountant CANNOT tell you:**
> - Which expenses are *necessary* and which are *wasteful*.
> - Why money flows from a rich person to a poor one only when there is *value exchange* — never the reverse.
> - Whether a transaction is *physically possible* or merely balances the books.
> 
> Similarly, the First Law balances the energy ledger but cannot dictate the **direction**, **quality**, or **feasibility** of energy conversion.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

In real engineering systems (steam turbines, refrigerators, IC engines, gas turbines), engineers are repeatedly confronted with questions the First Law cannot answer:

- "Can I build a heat engine that converts **100%** of heat from a furnace into useful work?" — *First Law says YES (energy conserved); reality says NO.*
- "Will heat spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body if I leave the door of my fridge open?" — *First Law allows it; Second Law forbids it.*

These contradictions reveal the **need for a Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PCMRT502 explicitly transitions from the First Law to the Second Law by first proving the inadequacy of the First Law. This is a **high-yield KTU topic** worth ~ 7–8 marks in End Semester Exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Cardinal Limitations of the First Law

### Limitation 1 — No Predictor of Direction of Heat Flow

Heat flows spontaneously from **hot body → cold body**. The reverse is never observed spontaneously. The First Law, however, permits both directions because energy is conserved in either case.

**Example:** Place a hot cup of tea ($T_h = 80°C$) and a cold glass of water ($T_c = 10°C$) in contact.

- **First Law verdict:** Energy lost by tea = energy gained by water. ✓ Energy conserved.
- **Reality:** Heat flows ONLY from tea → water. Cold → hot is impossible spontaneously.

The First Law cannot differentiate between the natural and the reversed scenario.

### Limitation 2 — No Distinction Between Work and Heat (Quality of Energy)

The First Law treats heat ($Q$) and work ($W$) as **equivalent** forms of energy since both transfer energy. But in practice:

- **Work** can be converted **completely** into heat (100% conversion).
- **Heat** can be converted into work **only partially** (Second Law — Kelvin-Planck statement).

**Example:** A heater converts **1 kJ of electrical work → 1 kJ of heat** (100%).
But a heat engine converts **only ~40%** of 1 kJ heat into work (60% wasted as low-grade heat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Quality Principle:** Heat at higher temperature has greater "availability" than heat at lower temperature. The First Law is **blind** to this quality difference.

### Limitation 3 — Cannot Determine Feasibility of a Process

Consider an isolated system: a body cools from $500°C$ to $30°C$, transferring heat to surroundings at $20°C$.

- **First Law:** Energy lost by body = energy gained by surroundings. ✓ No violation.
- **Reality:** This is spontaneous and **feasible**.

Now reverse it: body warms from $30°C$ to $500°C$ using heat from surroundings at $20°C$.

- **First Law:** Energy balance still holds. ✓ No violation (mathematically).
- **Reality:** This violates the **Clausius statement** of the Second Law. ❌ Not feasible.

**Conclusion:** The First Law permits many processes that **never occur in nature**.

### Limitation 4 — Cannot Specify the Maximum Efficiency of Heat Engines

The First Law applied to a cyclic heat engine gives:

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{rejected}}}{Q_{\text{supplied}}}$$

But it does **not** place an upper bound on $\eta$. One might naively think $\eta = 100\%$ is achievable (set $Q_{\text{rejected}} = 0$). The First Law happily allows this — but reality forbids it.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Kelvin-Planck Statement** (Second Law) explicitly rules out $\eta = 100\%$. Without this, the First Law would falsely promise perpetual-motion machines of the second kind.

### Limitation 5 — No Account of Irreversibility

The First Law governs both **reversible** and **irreversible** processes equally, as long as energy is conserved. But real processes are **irreversible** (friction, mixing, unrestrained expansion). The First Law provides no measure of how much **useful work is lost** due to irreversibility.

This loss of work potential is captured by **exergy destruction** and **entropy generation**, concepts entirely outside the First Law.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Limitations of First Law & Transition to Second Law

| # | Equation / Statement | Symbol Meaning | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\mathrm{d}U = \mathrm{d}Q - \mathrm{d}W$ | First Law (closed system) | Energy balance for any process |
| 2 | $\oint \mathrm{d}Q = \oint \mathrm{d}W$ | First Law (cyclic) | Heat engine / refrigerator analysis |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | Thermal efficiency | First-Law energy balance only |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot efficiency | True maximum limit (Second Law) |
| 5 | $\text{COP}_{\text{R}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | Refrigerator COP | First-Law balance |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | Heat pump COP | First-Law balance |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,R}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | Second-Law ceiling |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,HP}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_H}$ | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | Second-Law ceiling |
| 9 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ | **Clausius Inequality** | Defines direction (Second Law) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Constants in Play:**
> - $T$ (absolute temperature) is always in **Kelvin (K)**.
> - $R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/kmol·K}$ — universal gas constant (used in Carnot derivations).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | First-Law Limitation Encountered | Second-Law Solution Used |
|-------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Steam Power Plants | Cannot design cycle with $\eta = 100\%$ | Rankine cycle with reheat/regeneration |
| Refrigeration & AC | Cannot calculate minimum work input | Carnot COP gives theoretical minimum |
| Internal Combustion Engines | Cannot predict max efficiency for given $T$ range | Otto / Diesel cycle efficiencies |
| Cryogenics | Cannot liquefy gases by simple cooling | Claude / Linde cycles using Second Law |
| Chemical Engineering | Cannot gauge reaction spontaneity | Gibbs free energy (thermodynamic potential) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why the First Law Cannot Rule Out a 100% Efficient Heat Engine

### Setup

Consider a heat engine operating in a cycle between a **single** hot reservoir at $T_H$ and producing work $W_{\text{net}}$, with no heat rejection to a cold reservoir.

### Step 1: Apply the First Law to the Cycle

For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy is zero ($\oint \mathrm{d}U = 0$), so:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{\text{net}}$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance for the Single-Reservoir Engine

Let $Q_H$ be the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir. Since there is no cold reservoir, all of $Q_H$ must equal $W_{\text{net}}$:

$$Q_H = W_{\text{net}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} = 1 = 100\%$$

### Step 4: First-Law Verdict

The First Law says: *"Energy is conserved; 100% efficiency is allowed."*

### Step 5: Reality Verdict (Second Law — Kelvin-Planck Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** *It is impossible for any device operating in a cycle to receive heat from a single reservoir and produce a net amount of work.*

**Conclusion:** The First Law gives a green light to a 100% efficient engine, but the **Second Law slams the door shut**. This contradiction is a **limitation of the First Law**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Clausius Inequality (Foundation of the Second Law)

### Setup

Consider an irreversible (real) heat engine operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C$. We compare it against a **reversible** (Carnot) engine operating between the same two reservoirs.

### Step 1: Energy Balance for the Irreversible Engine

$$W_{\text{irr}} = Q_H - Q_C$$

where $Q_H$ is heat absorbed from hot reservoir and $Q_C$ is heat rejected to cold reservoir.

### Step 2: Construct a Composite (Reversible + Irreversible) System

Let a reversible engine operate in the **opposite direction** (as a refrigerator) between the same reservoirs, receiving $Q_C'$ from the cold reservoir and rejecting $Q_H'$ to the hot reservoir.

For the reversible refrigerator:

$$\frac{Q_H'}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C'}{T_C}$$

$$\Rightarrow Q_C' = Q_H' \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

### Step 3: Combine the Two Devices into a Single Cyclic System

Net heat interaction with hot reservoir:

$$Q_{\text{net,hot}} = Q_H' - Q_H$$

Net heat interaction with cold reservoir:

$$Q_{\text{net,cold}} = Q_C - Q_C'$$

Net work output of the combined system:

$$W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{net,hot}} - Q_{\text{net,cold}}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Kelvin-Planck Statement

Since the **combined system** exchanges heat only with the two reservoirs and produces net work, by Kelvin-Planck it cannot violate the Second Law. Setting up the constraint that **no work is produced without a temperature difference** forces:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} \le \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

### Step 5: Generalise to Any Cyclic Process (Clausius Inequality)

For any cyclic process:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

with equality for **reversible** cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}$ is the cyclic integral of entropy transfer. When it is strictly negative, the cycle is **irreversible**. This inequality **unifies** the First and Second Laws.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — First Law vs. Second Law (Quick Comparison)

| Property | First Law | Second Law |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| Energy conservation | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Direction of process | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Quality of energy | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Maximum efficiency | ❌ No bound | ✅ Carnot limit |
| Feasibility check | ❌ Cannot determine | ✅ Determines via entropy |
| Irreversibility | ❌ No measure | ✅ Quantifies entropy generation |
| Conversion of heat → work | Allows 100% (wrong) | Limits to Carnot efficiency |
| Conversion of heat → heat (cold → hot) | Allows reverse (wrong) | Forbids (Clausius statement) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Mathematical Expression of the Limitation (Simple Case)

Consider a gas expanding **freely** (into a vacuum) and then being **compressed isothermally** back to its initial state.

### Step 1: Free Expansion (Adiabatic, $Q = 0$, $W = 0$)

$$\Delta U_{\text{free}} = Q - W = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Temperature returns to original (for ideal gas). **First Law satisfied.**

### Step 2: Reversible Isothermal Compression to Original Volume

$$W_{\text{rev}} = \int_{V_2}^{V_1} P \, \mathrm{d}V = nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$$

This work must be **expelled to surroundings as heat** $Q = -W_{\text{rev}}$.

### Step 3: Examine the Complete Cycle

$$\Delta U_{\text{cycle}} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (First Law OK)}$$

But during the cycle, **net work is done ON the system** ($W > 0$), and an equivalent amount of heat is rejected to the surroundings at temperature $T_{\text{surr}}$.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Entropy of universe increased:} \quad \Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \frac{Q}{T_{\text{surr}}} > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The First Law alone could not tell us that this cycle **degrades** the quality of energy. Only the Second Law (via entropy) reveals the loss of work potential. This is a **direct mathematical demonstration of the First Law's limitation.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Limitations of the First Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[First Law of Thermodynamics] --> B[Energy is Conserved]
    B --> C{What It Cannot Tell Us}
    C --> D1[Direction of Heat Flow]
    C --> D2[Quality of Energy]

    C --> D4[Maximum Efficiency]
    C --> D5[Degree of Irreversibility]
    D1 --> E[Resolved by Second Law]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Kelvin-Planck Statement]
    E --> G[Clausius Statement]
    E --> H[Entropy Concept dS >= dQ/T]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Why We Need the Second Law

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Process] --> Q[Apply First Law]
    Q --> R{Energy Balance Satisfied?}
    R -- Yes --> S[First Law Pass]
    R -- No --> T[Impossible Process]
    S --> U{Second Law Check}
    U -- Pass --> V[Feasible Process]
    U -- Fail --> W[Forbidden Process]
    T --> X[Reject Immediately]
    W --> Y[No Machine Can Do This]
    V --> Z[Engineering Design Possible]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Limitations of First Law (Detailed Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT[Energy Input]
        I1[Heat Q from Reservoir]
        I2[Work W from System]
    end

    subgraph FIRSTLAW[First Law Domain]
        F1[dU = dQ minus dW]
        F2[Energy Balance Closed]
        F3[No Direction Info]
        F4[No Quality Info]
        F5[No Feasibility Info]
    end

    subgraph SECONDLAW[Second Law Domain Required]
        S1[Kelvin Planck Statement]
        S2[Clausius Statement]
```
