```mermaid
        S5[Exergy and Availability]
    end

    I1 --> F1
    I2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F2 --> F4
    F2 --> F5

    F3 --> S1
    F4 --> S2
    F5 --> S4
    F3 --> S3
    F4 --> S5
    F5 --> S4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first block diagram traces how the First Law passes a hypothetical 100% efficient heat engine, while the Second Law catches and rejects it. The architecture flow shows how each First-Law limitation maps onto a specific Second-Law concept that resolves it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State any two limitations of the First Law of Thermodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No indication of direction of heat flow:** The First Law permits heat to flow from a cold body to a hot body as long as energy is conserved. It does not forbid the reverse of natural processes. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **No distinction between heat and work as energy transfer modes:** Although heat and work are both forms of energy, work can be completely converted into heat, but heat cannot be completely converted into work. The First Law treats them as equivalent and fails to account for this asymmetry in quality. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Why is a 100% efficient heat engine impossible according to the Second Law?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 100% efficient heat engine would convert **all** heat absorbed from a single hot reservoir into work, rejecting **zero** heat to a cold reservoir. This directly contradicts the **Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law:

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single body and the production of an equivalent amount of work."*

Hence, every cyclic heat engine must reject some heat to a lower-temperature sink, making its efficiency strictly less than 100%. The maximum achievable efficiency is the **Carnot efficiency**:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Explain the limitations of the First Law of Thermodynamics. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The First Law of Thermodynamics states that energy is conserved in any thermodynamic process:

$$\mathrm{d}Q = \mathrm{d}U + \mathrm{d}W$$

While universally true, it suffers from the following **major limitations**:

**Limitation 1 — No prediction of direction:**
*(2 marks)*

The First Law treats heat transfer between two bodies as energy exchange only. It does not differentiate between natural and reversed heat flow. For instance, when a hot body at $T_H$ and a cold body at $T_C$ are in contact, both directions of heat flow satisfy the First Law, yet only $T_H \rightarrow T_C$ occurs spontaneously.

**Limitation 2 — No distinction between work and heat:**
*(2 marks)*

Although $Q$ and $W$ are both energy transfer modes, work can be completely converted into heat (e.g., friction), while heat cannot be entirely converted to work in a cyclic process. The First Law treats them as equivalent, ignoring this critical asymmetry.

**Limitation 3 — Cannot predict feasibility:**
*(1.5 marks)*

Many processes that satisfy the First Law are physically impossible (e.g., heat flowing from cold to hot without external work). The First Law cannot identify these.

**Limitation 4 — No efficiency bound:**
*(1.5 marks)*

Applying the First Law to a cyclic heat engine, $\eta = 1 - Q_{\text{out}}/Q_{\text{in}}$. If $Q_{\text{out}} = 0$, the First Law permits $\eta = 100\%$, which the **Kelvin-Planck statement** forbids.

---

#### Part (b) — State and explain the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Kelvin-Planck Statement:**
*(3.5 marks)*

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

**Explanation:** Every heat engine must reject some heat to a low-temperature sink. A 100% efficient engine is ruled out. Schematic:

```
       ┌───────────────┐
   Q_H │               │  W_net
  ────▶│ HEAT ENGINE   │───────▶
       │               │
       └───────┬───────┘
               │ Q_C
               ▼
          Cold Reservoir
```

$$\eta_{\text{th}} < 1 \quad \text{(always)}$$

**2. Clausius Statement:**
*(3.5 marks)*

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

**Explanation:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot. Work input is mandatory for refrigeration.

```
       ┌───────────────┐
   W    │               │  Q_H
  ────▶│ REFRIGERATOR  │───────▶
       │               │
       └───────┬───────┘
               │ Q_L
               ▼
          Cold Reservoir
```

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} < \infty$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Discuss why the First Law fails to predict the direction of a heat transfer process with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The First Law applied to two bodies in thermal contact states:

$$Q_{\text{lost by hot}} = Q_{\text{gained by cold}}$$

This single equation is symmetric — it does not change if we replace $T_H$ with $T_C$.

**Example 1 — Hot tea & cold water:** *(2 marks)*

A cup of tea at $80°C$ and water at $10°C$ are placed in contact. The First Law states energy lost by tea = energy gained by water. If we reverse the scenario, the equation still holds. Yet, only the natural direction (tea → water) occurs.

**Example 2 — Refrigerator door left open:** *(2.5 marks)*

If the door of a refrigerator is left open, the First Law permits heat from the room ($30°C$) to flow into the refrigerator ($4°C$) and then back to the room — energy balance is maintained. But this violates the Clausius statement.

**Example 3 — Heat engine with no cold reservoir:** *(2.5 marks)*

A ship sailing on the ocean and extracting heat from seawater to produce work — the First Law permits it, but Kelvin-Planck forbids it.

**Conclusion:** The First Law identifies *what* happens in terms of energy but cannot tell us *which way* a process unfolds. This directional ambiguity is the principal limitation of the First Law.

---

#### Part (b) — State the Clausius Inequality. Explain its physical significance in distinguishing reversible and irreversible processes. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the Clausius Inequality:** *(2.5 marks)*

For any cyclic process (reversible or irreversible), the cyclic integral of $\delta Q / T$ satisfies:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

where $T$ is the absolute temperature of the boundary at which $\delta Q$ is transferred.

**Equality Case:** *(1.5 marks)*

When the cycle is **completely reversible**:

$$\oint \left( \frac{\delta Q}{T} \right)_{\text{rev}} = 0$$

**Strict Inequality Case:** *(1.5 marks)*

When the cycle contains **any irreversibility** (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, finite temperature difference heat transfer):

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$$

**Physical Significance:** *(1.5 marks)*

1. It establishes **entropy** as a thermodynamic property: $\mathrm{d}S = \delta Q_{\text{rev}} / T$.
2. It provides a **mathematical criterion** for feasibility: a real process must yield $\Delta S_{\text{universe}} \ge 0$.
3. It **unifies** the First and Second Laws, defining the limit beyond which the First Law's balance is supplemented by the directionality of energy transformation.

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \Delta S_{\text{system}} + \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} \ge 0$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Lose Marks):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing limitation with violation:** Students often write *"the First Law is violated by certain processes"*. **Wrong!** The First Law is **never violated**. It is merely **insufficient**. Always say *"the First Law does not forbid / does not predict…"*
> 
> 2. **Mixing Kelvin-Planck with Clausius:** Kelvin-Planck governs **heat engines** (work ↔ heat). Clausius governs **refrigerators** (cold ↔ hot heat transfer). Don't swap them in the exam.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the word "cycle":** Both Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements apply to **devices operating in a cycle**. A non-cyclic process is not covered.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring temperature in Kelvin:** Always convert °C to **K** in Carnot efficiency. Marks are deducted silently if $T$ is in °C.
> 
> 5. **Writing $\eta = 100\%$ for Carnot:** Carnot efficiency is **less than 100%** unless $T_C = 0\,\text{K}$ (which is unattainable by the Third Law).
> 
> 6. **Missing "spontaneously":** Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold. With **external work**, it can be forced from cold to hot (refrigerator). Examiners expect the qualifier.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **First Law** is the **energy conservation** principle — it is **always valid** but **not sufficient**.
- ✅ Its main limitation: it **cannot predict direction, quality, feasibility, or maximum efficiency**.
- ✅ A **100% efficient heat engine** is allowed by the First Law but **forbidden** by the **Kelvin-Planck statement**.
- ✅ **Spontaneous heat flow** is only from **hot → cold** (Clausius statement); reverse requires **work input**.
- ✅ **Work → Heat** is fully possible; **Heat → Work** is partially possible.
- ✅ The **Clausius Inequality** $\oint \delta Q / T \le 0$ bridges First and Second Laws and defines **entropy** as a property.
- ✅ **Carnot efficiency** $\eta = 1 - T_C / T_H$ is the **upper bound** for any heat engine — the Second Law's gift.
- ✅ Always use **absolute temperature (K)** in efficiency and COP calculations.
- ✅ Limitations of First Law are **conceptual**, not mathematical — never write "First Law is wrong."
- ✅ **Irreversibility** (friction, mixing, free expansion) cannot be quantified by the First Law — use **entropy generation**.
- ✅ **Refrigerator COP** uses **work input**, while **heat pump COP** uses **heat delivered** — don't confuse them.
- ✅ The Second Law introduces the concept of **exergy / availability** — the maximum useful work obtainable — which the First Law cannot predict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Second Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Second Law of Thermodynamics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is the Second Law of Thermodynamics? The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** is a fundamental physical principle that establishes the **direction** of natural processes and introduces the concept of **entropy** (qualitatively) and **irreversibility**. While the First Law enforces **energy conservation** (energy cannot be created or destroyed), it places no restriction on the *direction* of energy transfer. The Second Law fills this critical gap. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that *processes occur in a certain direction and not in the reverse direction*. It asserts that a spontaneous transformation of heat completely into work (Kelvin-Planck) or of heat from a cold body to a hot body without external work (Clausius) is **impossible**. The Second Law is, therefore, an **empirical generalization** based on experimental observations about how energy actually behaves in nature. It identifies **quality** of energy, not just quantity — distinguishing between *high-grade* energy (organized, useful work) and *low-grade* energy (disorganized, dissipated heat). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cup of Coffee" Intuition Imagine a hot cup of coffee left on a table: - **What happens naturally?** It cools down — heat flows from the **hot coffee** to the **cooler room air**. - **What never happens (without external effort)?** The coffee does **not** spontaneously reheat itself by extracting heat from the room. - The First Law would happily allow both scenarios (energy is conserved either way), but the Second Law **forbids** the second. **Why does this matter in engineering?** All heat engines, refrigerators, and turbines operate *between* temperature reservoirs. The Second Law sets a hard theoretical ceiling on their efficiency — a ceiling no engineering ingenuity can ever surpass (the **Carnot efficiency**). ## 1.3 The Two Classical Statements > [!NOTE] > The Second Law has two classical, completely equivalent statements, each addressing a different physical impossibility. ### 1.3.1 Kelvin–Planck Statement (Heat Engine Perspective) > **"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work."** In simpler terms: **You cannot build a 100% efficient heat engine** that converts all supplied heat into work using a single thermal reservoir. At least *some* heat *must* be rejected to a lower-temperature sink. ### 1.3.2 Clausius Statement (Refrigerator Perspective) > **"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."** In simpler words: **Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot**. To move heat against its natural temperature gradient, external **work input** is mandatory — this is why your refrigerator needs electricity. ## 1.4 Heat Engine, Refrigerator, and Heat Pump — The Three Devices | Device | Purpose | Working Direction | Key Output | |---|---|---|---| | **Heat Engine** | Convert heat → work | Hot reservoir → Cold reservoir | Net Work ($W_{net}$) | | **Refrigerator** | Cool a cold space | Cold reservoir → Hot reservoir (with work input) | Cooling Effect ($Q_L$) | | **Heat Pump** | Heat a hot space | Cold reservoir → Hot reservoir (with work input) | Heating Effect ($Q_H$) | ## 1.5 Reversible vs. Irreversible Processes (Foundational Concept) > [!NOTE] > This distinction is **critical** because the Second Law is best articulated in terms of what *can* be perfectly reversed and what *cannot*. - **Reversible Process**: A process that can be exactly retraced in the opposite direction such that the system and surroundings return to their *initial states* without leaving any net change in the universe. - **Irreversible Process**: A process that *cannot* be reversed without leaving changes in the surroundings. All natural/spontaneous processes are irreversible. **Examples of Irreversibility:** Friction, unrestrained expansion, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference, mixing of fluids, chemical reactions, electrical resistance. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Heat Engine — Schematic and Energy Balance A heat engine receives heat $Q_H$ from a **high-temperature source** (at $T_H$), converts a portion to work $W_{net}$, and rejects the remaining heat $Q_L$ to a **low-temperature sink** (at $T_L$). Operating cyclically, the working fluid returns to its initial state. **First Law applied to the cyclic engine:** $$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$ $$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$ ### Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) Thermal efficiency is the **performance metric** of a heat engine — the fraction of input heat converted to useful work. $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kelvin-Planck Implication:** Since the Second Law forbids $Q_L = 0$, it is impossible to achieve $\eta_{th} = 1$ (i.e., 100%). The Second Law thus sets an upper limit: $0 \le \eta_{th} < 1$. ## 2.2 The Refrigerator and Heat Pump — COP Metrics A refrigerator absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a cold space and, with work input $W_{net}$, rejects heat $Q_H$ to a hot space. From the First Law: $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$. ### Coefficient of Performance — Refrigerator ($COP_R$) $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ ### Coefficient of Performance — Heat Pump ($COP_{HP}$) $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ ### Relationship between $COP_{HP}$ and $COP_R$ $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clausius Implication:** Since heat cannot flow from cold to hot *spontaneously*, the Second Law requires $W_{net} > 0$ for any refrigerator. Thus $COP_R$ is always finite, and is **greater than 1** for practical systems (more heat removed than work input). ## 2.3 Reversible and Irreversible Processes — The Hierarchy | Property | Reversible | Irreversible | |---|---|---| | Direction | Can be reversed without net change in universe | Cannot be reversed without net change | | Equilibrium | Quasi-static (system passes through equilibrium states) | System not in equilibrium during process | | Friction | Frictionless | Friction present | | Heat transfer | Across **infinitesimal** temperature difference | Across **finite** temperature difference | | Unrestrained expansion | Absent | Present | | Work | Maximum work for a given change | Less work obtained | > [!NOTE] > A **reversible process** is an *idealization* — no real process is perfectly reversible, but it serves as a *theoretical benchmark* (e.g., the Carnot cycle) for evaluating real engines. ## 2.4 The Carnot Cycle — The Ideal Benchmark The **Carnot cycle** is a hypothetical, fully reversible thermodynamic cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$. It comprises four reversible processes: 1. **Process 1-2: Isothermal Expansion at $T_H$** — System absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir. 2. **Process 2-3: Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion** — Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$. 3. **Process 3-4: Isothermal Compression at $T_L$** — System rejects heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir. 4. **Process 4-1: Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression** — Temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$. **P-V and T-S Diagrams of the Carnot Cycle:** On a **P-V diagram**: The cycle is a "rounded rectangle" with two isotherms (curves) and two adiabats (steeper curves). The enclosed area represents the net work. On a **T-S diagram**: The cycle is a **perfect rectangle** — this is the cleanest representation. The horizontal width is the entropy change $\Delta S$, and the vertical sides are the isotherms at $T_H$ and $T_L$. ## 2.5 Carnot Theorems (The Two Pillars) > [!NOTE] > These two theorems are *frequently asked* in KTU examinations and are the cornerstone of the Second Law. **Carnot's Theorem I (Efficiency Theorem):** > The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs is **independent of the working fluid** and depends **only on the reservoir temperatures**. **Carnot's Theorem II (Optimality Theorem):** > No irreversible heat engine operating between the same two reservoirs can be **more efficient** than a reversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs. In other words, **the reversible (Carnot) engine has the highest possible efficiency**. **Corollary:** All reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the **same efficiency** — the **Carnot efficiency**. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | First Law for cyclic engine | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | All in kJ | | 2 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Dimensionless, $> 1$ | | 4 | COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless, $> 1$ | | 5 | Relation: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | — | Useful shortcut | | 6 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Absolute temps in **K** | | 7 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | $T$ in K | | 8 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | $T$ in K | | 9 | Heat Ratio (Reversible Engine) | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | For Carnot engine only | | 10 | PMM2 (Perpetual Motion Machine 2) | Impossible by Second Law | Exists in theory only | | 11 | Reversible Work Maximum | $W_{rev} \ge W_{actual}$ | For the same $\Delta U$ | | 12 | Increase of Entropy Principle | $\Delta S_{universe} \ge 0$ | Equality = reversible | > [!WARNING] > **MOST COMMON EXAM MISTAKE:** Students often plug Celsius values into the Carnot efficiency formula. **Always use absolute temperature (Kelvin)**. Conversion: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. A 1-degree error in Kelvin will yield a wrong efficiency. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Plants (Steam, Gas, Nuclear):** The Carnot efficiency sets the **thermodynamic ceiling** for conversion of heat to work. - **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning:** The Carnot COP gives the **upper limit** for refrigeration; real systems use refrigerants and compressors. - **Otto, Diesel, Brayton Cycles:** All are compared *against* the Carnot cycle to evaluate how close they approach ideal performance. - **Thermodynamic Temperature Scale:** Lord Kelvin's thermodynamic temperature scale is defined by the Carnot cycle — making the Second Law the **foundation of thermometry**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Carnot Efficiency (From Carnot Cycle Analysis) We derive the efficiency of the reversible Carnot cycle using the First Law and the property of isothermal processes for an ideal gas. ### Step 1: Apply First Law to a Cycle For any cyclic device (engine), the net work equals net heat transfer: $$W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ ### Step 2: Express Thermal Efficiency By definition, thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work to heat supplied: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ ### Step 3: Compute Heat Transfer in Isothermal Processes (Reversible Engine, Ideal Gas) For the isothermal expansion (Process 1-2) at $T_H$, using $dU = 0$ (ideal gas isothermal), the First Law gives: $$Q_H = W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = n R T_H \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ For the isothermal compression (Process 3-4) at $T_L$: $$Q_L = W_{3-4} = n R T_L \ln\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Apply Adiabatic Relations for Processes 2-3 and 4-1 For a reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process of an ideal gas, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$. Applying this to Process 2-3 (from $T_H$ to $T_L$): $$T_H V_2^{\gamma-1} = T_L V_3^{\gamma-1}$$ $$\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \left(\frac{V_3}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ Similarly, for Process 4-1 (from $T_L$ to $T_H$): $$T_L V_4^{\gamma-1} = T_H V_1^{\gamma-1}$$ $$\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ ### Step 5: Equate the Two Adiabatic Relations From Steps 4a and 4b: $$\left(\frac{V_3}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ This implies: $$\frac{V_3}{V_2} = \frac{V_4}{V_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{V_3}{V_4}$$ ### Step 6: Substitute into the Efficiency Expression Using the result from Step 5: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{n R T_L \ln\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)}{n R T_H \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ ### Step 7: Final Carnot Efficiency Expression $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The remarkable result: **Efficiency depends ONLY on reservoir temperatures**, not on the working fluid (validates Carnot Theorem I). ## 3.2 Derivation of Equivalence Between Kelvin-Planck and Clausius Statements This proof shows that violation of one statement implies violation of the other. ### Step 1: Setup Assume a **Clausius-violating refrigerator (R')** that transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input**. Couple it with a regular heat engine (E) operating between the same reservoirs, receiving $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir. ### Step 2: Define Engine Performance The engine extracts $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, does work $W$, and rejects $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir. By the First Law: $$W = Q_H - Q_L$$ The efficiency is $\eta = W / Q_H$. ### Step 3: Couple the Two Devices Let the Clausius refrigerator deliver exactly $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir (the same amount that the engine rejects). The refrigerator takes $Q$ from the cold reservoir and delivers $Q + 0 = Q$ to the hot reservoir (no work input). > Wait — for consistency, we set the refrigerator to deliver $Q$ to the hot reservoir and absorb $Q$ from the cold reservoir. ### Step 4: Net Effect on the Cold Reservoir The cold reservoir **loses** $Q_L$ to the engine and **gains** $Q$ from the refrigerator. If we set $Q = Q_L$, the cold reservoir experiences **zero net change**. ### Step 5: Net Effect on the Hot Reservoir The hot reservoir **loses** $Q_H$ to the engine and **gains** $Q$ from the refrigerator. Net change: it receives $Q - Q_H$. ### Step 6: Net Effect on the Combined System - Cold reservoir: net change = 0 - Hot reservoir: net heat = $Q - Q_H$ - Net work output = $W = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - Q$ **Combined Effect:** The composite system **extracts net work** $W = Q_H - Q$ from a **single reservoir** (the hot reservoir) — with no other effect. This is a **violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement**. ### Step 7: Conclusion If the Clausius statement is violated, the Kelvin-Planck statement is *also* violated. The reverse proof (K-P violation → Clausius violation) follows by symmetric construction. **The two statements are equivalent.** $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Carnot COP for a Refrigerator For a reversible (Carnot) refrigerator, the heat ratio follows the same temperature relation: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ From the COP definition: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ Divide numerator and denominator by $Q_H$: $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L/Q_H}{1 - Q_L/Q_H}$$ Substitute $Q_L/Q_H = T_L/T_H$: $$COP_R = \frac{T_L/T_H}{1 - T_L/T_H}$$ Simplify: $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ Similarly, for a Carnot heat pump: $$\boxed{COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$$ ## 3.4 Proof of Carnot Theorem II (Optimality) ### Step 1: Hypothesis Setup Let **Engine A** be a reversible (Carnot) engine, and **Engine B** be any (possibly irreversible) engine operating between the same $T_H$ and $T_L$. Both receive the same $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir. ### Step 2: Assume Contradiction Suppose engine B is *more* efficient than engine A: $$\eta_B > \eta_A$$ This means engine B produces *more* work than A from the same $Q_H$: $$W_B > W_A$$ ### Step 3: Reverse Engine A Since engine A is reversible, run it *in reverse* as a refrigerator/heat pump. As a reversed engine, it consumes $W_A$ and delivers $Q_H = Q_L + W_A$ to the hot reservoir. ### Step 4: Couple the Devices Couple B (forward) with A (reversed). Let B drive A in reverse by providing $W_A$ from its work output. Since $W_B > W_A$, B has surplus work to spare. ### Step 5: Net Effects - **Hot reservoir:** Receives $Q_H$ from A (reversed) and gives up $Q_H$ to B → **net change = 0**. - **Cold reservoir:** A (reversed) takes $Q_L$ from cold, B rejects $Q_L' < Q_L$ (less, because B is more efficient) → **net heat delivered TO cold reservoir = $Q_L - Q_L' > 0$**. - **External work:** B produces $W_B$, A (reversed) consumes $W_A$; surplus $W_B - W_A$ is delivered as net work. ### Step 6: Identify the Violation The composite system **extracts heat $Q_L - Q_L'$ from the cold reservoir** and **converts it entirely into net work $W_B - W_A$**, with no other effect. This is a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement! ### Step 7: Conclusion by Contradiction Since a Kelvin-Planck violation cannot occur, our assumption $\eta_B > \eta_A$ must be **false**. Therefore: $$\eta_B \le \eta_A = \eta_{Carnot}$$ Equality holds only when B is also reversible. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > **Example:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at 600 K and a cold reservoir at 300 K. The engine receives 1000 kJ of heat from the hot reservoir. Calculate: (a) Thermal efficiency, (b) Net work output, (c) Heat rejected. **Solution:** **Part (a): Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 = 50\%$$ **Part (b): Net Work Output** $$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.50 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$ **Part (c): Heat Rejected to Cold Reservoir** $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 500 = 500 \text{ kJ}$$ **Verification using the reversible relation:** $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{600} = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500 \text{ kJ} \checkmark$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of a Heat Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR HotRes[Hot Reservoir T sub H] -- Heat Q sub H --> Engine[Heat Engine] Engine -- Work W sub net --> WorkOut[Net Work Output] Engine -- Heat Q sub L --> ColdRes[Cold Reservoir T sub L] style HotRes fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style ColdRes fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style Engine fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style WorkOut fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of a Refrigerator / Heat Pump ```mermaid flowchart LR ColdRes2[Cold Reservoir T sub L] -- Heat Q sub L --> Refrig[Refrigerator] Refrig -- Work W sub net IN --> WorkIn[Work Input] Refrig -- Heat Q sub H OUT --> HotRes2[Hot Reservoir T sub H] style HotRes2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style ColdRes2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style Refrig fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style WorkIn fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Carnot Cycle on T-S Diagram (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[State 1: Start of Isothermal Expansion at T sub H] -->|Heat Q sub H IN

Use COP sub R] Q3 -->|NO| HeatPump[Heat Pump - Use COP sub HP] Q2 -->|NO| Other[Not a standard thermal device] Engine --> Eff[Compute Efficiency eta th] Refrig --> COPR[Compute COP sub R] HeatPump --> COPHP[Compute COP sub HP] Eff --> CarnotCheck{Compare with eta Carnot = 1 minus T sub L over T sub H} COPR --> CarnotCheck COPHP --> CarnotCheck CarnotCheck -->|Actual LESS than Carnot| Result1[Second Law SATISFIED] CarnotCheck -->|Actual GREATER than Carnot| Result2[VIOLATION of Second Law - IMPOSSIBLE] style Result1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style Result2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Why are these two statements considered equivalent?** **Model Answer:** **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and performing an equivalent amount of work. *Interpretation:* A heat engine must reject some heat to a low-temperature sink; 100% conversion of heat to work is impossible (PMM2 impossibility). **Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than transferring heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. *Interpretation:* Heat does not flow spontaneously from cold to hot; external work is required for refrigeration. **Equivalence:** Violation of one statement implies violation of the other. A Clausius-violating refrigerator coupled with a normal engine can construct a composite device that violates Kelvin-Planck, and vice versa. Hence both statements are two ways of expressing the same fundamental law. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a reversible process. List any four causes of irreversibility with examples.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A reversible process is one that can be exactly retraced in the opposite direction such that both the system and surroundings return to their original states without leaving any net change in the universe. **Causes of Irreversibility:** 1. **Friction** — e.g., sliding block on a rough surface generates heat. 2. **Unrestrained expansion** — e.g., gas rushing into a vacuum. 3. **Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference** — e.g., hot fluid mixing with cold fluid. 4. **Mixing of two different gases** — e.g., diffusion of oxygen and nitrogen. 5. **Inelastic deformation / plastic deformation** — energy dissipated as heat. 6. **Electrical resistance** — current flow through a resistor generates heat (Joule heating). A reversible process is an idealization; all real (natural) processes are irreversible to some degree. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at 800 K and a cold reservoir at 300 K. The engine delivers 500 kJ of work per cycle. Determine: (i) The thermal efficiency of the engine. (ii) The heat supplied from the hot reservoir. (iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir. (iv) The COP of a reversible refrigerator operating between the same two reservoirs. **Model Solution:** **Part (i) Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$ **Part (ii) Heat Supplied from Hot Reservoir:** Using $\eta = W_{net} / Q_H$: $$Q_H = \frac{W_{net}}{\eta} = \frac{500}{0.625} = 800 \text{ kJ}$$ **Part (iii) Heat Rejected to Cold Reservoir:** By First Law for a cycle: $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 800 - 500 = 300 \text{ kJ}$$ **Verification using reversible heat ratio:** $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{800} = 0.375$$ $$Q_L = 0.375 \times 800 = 300 \text{ kJ} \checkmark$$ **Part (iv) COP of Reversible Refrigerator:** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{300}{800 - 300} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6$$ The reversible refrigerator delivers 0.6 kJ of cooling per kJ of work input. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** State and prove the two Carnot theorems. **Model Solution:** **Carnot's Theorem I (Efficiency Theorem):** > The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs is independent of the working fluid and depends only on the reservoir temperatures. *Justification (brief):* The Carnot efficiency derivation shows $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$, which contains no property of the working fluid (no $c_p$, $c_v$, $R$, or $\gamma$ appears). Hence the efficiency is *universal* for all reversible engines between the same reservoirs. **Carnot's Theorem II (Optimality Theorem):** > No irreversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible engine operating between the same reservoirs. **Proof (concise):** Let Engine A be reversible (Carnot) and Engine B be any irreversible engine, both receiving the same $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir $T_H$. Assume, for contradiction, that $\eta_B > \eta_A$. Then $W_B > W_A$. Now reverse Engine A (using its work output as a heat pump), and couple it with Engine B. The composite system would have the following net effects: - Hot reservoir: net change = 0 (Engine A reversed takes $Q_H$ from hot; Engine B delivers $Q_H$ to hot — actually B rejects, hmm, let's clarify). Re-clarified standard proof: - Engine B (forward): takes $Q_H$ from hot, does $W_B$, rejects $Q_L'$ to cold. - Engine A (reversed): consumes $W_A$, takes $Q_L$ from cold, delivers $Q_H$ to hot. - Couple: B's work $W_B$ drives A in reverse (with surplus $W_B - W_A$ as net output). - Net on cold reservoir: $Q_L - Q_L'$ absorbed (since $Q_L > Q_L'$ due to A being more efficient at the same $Q_H$ — wait, with same $Q_H$ and lower $\eta_A$, A rejects more $Q_L$). Hmm, need careful setup. **Standard corrected proof setup:** Let both engines A (reversible) and B (irreversible) deliver the *same* work $W$. Then since $\eta_B > \eta_A$, B requires *less* $Q_H$ than A. Reverse A, couple B's work output to drive A in reverse (with $W$ matching). Then: - Net on hot reservoir: B takes $Q_{H,B}$; A reversed delivers $Q_{H,A}$ to hot. Since $Q_{H,A} > Q_{H,B}$, net heat is delivered **TO** the hot reservoir. - Net on cold reservoir: A reversed extracts $Q_{L,A}$; B rejects $Q_{L,B}$. Since $Q_{L,A} < Q_{L,B}$ (A takes less from cold than B rejects — wait, both must equal since work is same... let me recheck). Standard canonical proof uses **same $Q_H$** assumption. Let me present that cleanly: **Clean Proof:** Let A and B both absorb the same $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir. Then $W_A = \eta_A Q_H$ and $W_B = \eta_B Q_H$. Suppose $\eta_B > \eta_A$, so $W_B > W_A$. Run A in reverse. It consumes $W_A$, absorbs $Q_{L,A}$ from cold reservoir, and rejects $Q_H = Q_{L,A} + W_A$ to hot reservoir. Now use B to drive A in reverse: B produces $W_B$, of which $W_A$ is consumed by A reversed, leaving surplus $W_B - W_A$ as **net work output** of the composite. Net effects: - **Hot reservoir:** Loses $Q_H$ (to B) and gains $Q_H$ (from A reversed) → **net change = 0**. - **Cold reservoir:** Loses $Q_{L,A}$ (to A reversed) and gains $Q_{L,B}$ (from B). - Since $\eta_B > \eta_A$, $Q_{L,B} < Q_{L,A}$ → net **heat lost by cold reservoir = $Q_{L,A} - Q_{L,B} > 0$**. - **Net work output:** $W_B - W_A > 0$. The composite system **converts heat extracted from a single reservoir (the cold one) entirely into work** with no other effect — **a violation of Kelvin-Planck statement**. This is impossible, so $\eta_B > \eta_A$ is false. $$\therefore \eta_B \le \eta_A = \eta_{Carnot}$$ Equality holds iff B is also reversible. $\blacksquare$ --- ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** A heat pump is used to maintain a building at 22°C when the outside temperature is 5°C. The heat pump has a COP of 3.5. Determine: (i) The heat supplied to the building per kWh of electrical energy consumed. (ii) Compare this COP with that of a Carnot (reversible) heat pump operating between the same temperature limits. (iii) Is the given heat pump's performance realistic? Justify. **Model Solution:** **Convert temperatures to Kelvin:** $$T_H = 22 + 273.15 = 295.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_L = 5 + 273.15 = 278.15 \text{ K}$$ **Part (i) Heat Supplied to the Building:** The COP of a heat pump is: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = 3.5$$ For $W_{net} = 1$ kWh: $$Q_H = COP_{HP} \times W_{net} = 3.5 \times 1 = 3.5 \text{ kWh}$$ The heat pump delivers **3.5 kWh of heat** to the building per kWh of electrical energy consumed. (Energy balance check: $Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 3.5 - 1 = 2.5$ kWh absorbed from outside air.) **Part (ii) Carnot COP for Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{295.15}{295.15 - 278.15} = \frac{295.15}{17} \approx 17.36$$ **Part (iii) Realism Check:** The actual $COP_{HP} = 3.5$ is **much less than** the Carnot $COP_{HP} \approx 17.36$. This is **realistic** because: - All real heat pumps suffer from irreversibilities (friction, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, compressor inefficiencies). - The Second Law guarantees actual $COP \le$ Carnot COP, which is satisfied here ($3.5 < 17.36$). - Typical real-world heat pumps for residential heating have COPs in the range 2.5–4.5, consistent with the given value. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$. Show that the efficiency depends only on the reservoir temperatures. **Model Solution:** The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal processes (at $T_H$ and $T_L$) and two adiabatic (isentropic) processes. Working fluid: ideal gas (without loss of generality). **Step 1:** For any heat engine, from the First Law (cyclic process): $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ $$\eta = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ **Step 2:** Heat transfers in the isothermal processes (ideal gas, $dU = 0$): $$Q_H = nRT_H \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) \quad \text{(isothermal expansion at } T_H)$$ $$Q_L = nRT_L \ln\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right) \quad \text{(isothermal compression at } T_L)$$ **Step 3:** Adiabatic relations: $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$ For Process 2-3: $T_H V_2^{\gamma-1} = T_L V_3^{\gamma-1}$ For Process 4-1: $T_L V_4^{\gamma-1} = T_H V_1^{\gamma-1}$ Dividing: $(V_3/V_2)^{\gamma-1} = (V_4/V_1)^{\gamma-1}$, giving $V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4$. **Step 4:** Substituting into efficiency: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{nRT_L \ln(V_3/V_4)}{nRT_H \ln(V_2/V_1)} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ (since $V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4$, the logarithms cancel) **Step 5:** Final expression: $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ **Independence from working fluid:** The final expression contains only the reservoir temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$. The specific gas constant $R$, the specific heats, and $\gamma$ have all cancelled out. Hence the efficiency is *universal* for any working fluid — validating Carnot's First Theorem. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Second Law Questions (KTU Valuation):** > > 1. **Using °C instead of K in Carnot efficiency:** Guaranteed 1-mark penalty. **Always convert to Kelvin.** A 1°C offset in a typical problem (e.g., 300 K vs 301 K) changes the answer by ~0.3%, which is enough to lose a mark for "incorrect final answer." > > 2. **Confusing COP of refrigerator with COP of heat pump:** They differ by exactly 1 ($COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$). Mixing them up is a 1–2 mark deduction. > > 3. **Forgetting that efficiency/COP are dimensionless ratios:** Do not write units. Write as a decimal (0.50) or percentage (50%). > > 4. **Wrong sign convention for work in refrigerator:** For an engine, $W_{out} > 0$. For a refrigerator, $W_{in} > 0$ (work is consumed). The COP definition is "desired output / required input," so for a refrigerator, the desired output is $Q_L$ (cooling), and the input is $W_{net}$ (work). > > 5. **In equivalence proof, failing to show both directions:** A complete proof requires showing Clausius → Kelvin-Planck *and* Kelvin-Planck → Clausius. Showing only one direction = -3 marks. > > 6. **In Carnot Theorem II proof, not assuming the contradiction clearly:** State the contradiction hypothesis explicitly: "Assume $\eta_B > \eta_A$" — then show the violation. Without the explicit assumption, the proof is logically incomplete. > > 7. **PMM2 confusion:** A "Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind" (PMM2) is a device that violates the Second Law (single-reservoir heat engine). This is *different* from PMM1, which violates the First Law. Confusing them costs marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Second Law of Thermodynamics (Module 3)** - **Two Statements:** Kelvin-Planck (engine) and Clausius (refrigerator). They are *equivalent* and *negations* of impossibility. - **Heat Engine Efficiency:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$, **always less than 1**. - **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ — depends *only* on reservoir temperatures. - **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_R = Q_L/W_{net} = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ for Carnot refrigerator. - **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = Q_H/W_{net} = T_H/(T_H - T_L)$ for Carnot heat pump. - **Key relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$. - **Reversible Process:** Quasi-static, frictionless, infinitesimal temperature differences — an *ideal* benchmark. - **Irreversible Process:** Friction, unrestrained expansion, finite ΔT heat transfer, mixing, chemical reactions, electrical resistance. - **Carnot Theorems:** (I) Efficiency is *independent* of working fluid, depends only on reservoir temps. (II) No engine can be *more* efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine between the same reservoirs. - **PMM2:** A 100% efficient single-reservoir engine — *impossible* by Second Law. - **Maximum Work Principle:** Reversible processes deliver *maximum* work (or require *minimum* work input) for a given change of state. - **Energy Quality vs. Quantity:** Second Law introduces the concept that energy has *quality* — high-temperature heat is more useful than low-temperature heat, even if the *quantity* (in kJ) is the same. - **Thermodynamic Temperature Scale:** Defined via the Carnot cycle using $T_H/T_L = Q_H/Q_L$ — an absolute scale independent of any working fluid property. - **Real Devices:** Always have $\eta_{real} < \eta_{Carnot}$ and $COP_{real} < COP_{Carnot}$. - **Cycle Direction:** Heat engine runs *hot → cold*; refrigerator/heat pump runs *cold → hot* (with work input). - **Internal Energy in Cycle:** $\oint dU = 0$ for any cyclic process — net change in system state is zero. - **Key Difference from First Law:** First Law is about *quantity* (energy balance); Second Law is about *direction* and *quality* of processes. - **Remember the Limits:** $0 < \eta < 1$ (engine); $COP_R > 1$, $COP_{HP} > 1$ (always, by Second Law).

topics.title: Thermal Reservoir
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Reservoir

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Thermal Reservoir** (also called a *Heat Reservoir* or *Thermal Energy Reservoir*) is defined as a hypothetical body of **infinitely large (or very large) thermal energy capacity** that can supply to, or absorb from, a system an unlimited quantity of heat without undergoing any appreciable change in its thermodynamic temperature.

Mathematically, the defining condition is:

$$C_{reservoir} = \frac{\delta Q}{dT} \rightarrow \infty \quad \text{with} \quad dT \rightarrow 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A thermal reservoir is a body of sufficiently large heat capacity such that it can absorb or reject finite amounts of heat without any change in its temperature."*

## Two Principal Types of Thermal Reservoirs

| Type | Role | Temperature | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Supplies heat to the system | High $T_H$ | Furnace, Boiler, Nuclear reactor core, Solar collector |
| **Heat Sink** | Absorbs heat from the system | Low $T_L$ | Atmosphere, Rivers, Oceans, Cooling tower pond |

## Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Infinite Ocean**
> Imagine a vast ocean. If you take a small bucket of warm water from a ship and pour it into the ocean, the ocean's temperature does **not change at all**. Conversely, if you draw a bucket of cool water from the ocean, the ocean stays the same temperature. The ocean acts as a **thermal reservoir** because its massive heat capacity overwhelms any small heat exchange.

**Another quick mental model:**
- Think of the **atmosphere** as a giant air-conditioned room that never gets warmer or cooler no matter how much heat a small engine dumps into it.
- Think of a **boiler furnace** as a furnace so large that it keeps burning at the same flame temperature even after heating steam for hours.

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Specific Heat of Ocean Water:** $c_p \approx 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- **Mean Atmospheric Heat Capacity:** Effectively infinite per unit mass exchanged
- **Standard Reference Reservoir Temperatures:**
    - $T_{\text{sink}} = 298 \text{ K} = 25^\circ\text{C}$ (ambient standard)
    - $T_{\text{sink}} = 300 \text{ K} = 27^\circ\text{C}$ (typical tropical sink)

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Mnemonic:** *"A reservoir is a body that takes the heat, never changes its state."* Use this line if you must write a one-line answer in 2 minutes during the exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A thermal reservoir, despite being a *hypothetical* construct, satisfies three cornerstone operational conditions:

1. **Capacity Condition (Heat Capacity):**
   The reservoir's effective heat capacity $C_{res}$ is so large that it is treated as infinite.
   $$C_{res} \to \infty$$

2. **Stability Condition (Constant Temperature):**
   The reservoir's temperature $T_{res}$ is treated as spatially uniform and time-invariant.
   $$\frac{dT_{res}}{dt} = 0$$

3. **Interaction Condition (Finite Heat Transfer):**
   The reservoir can supply or absorb a *finite* amount of heat $\delta Q$ while still satisfying both conditions above.
   $$\delta Q = C_{res} \cdot dT \quad \text{with} \quad C_{res}\cdot dT = \text{finite} \Rightarrow dT \to 0$$

## Why the Concept is Essential in Engineering

- **Boundary Condition Specifier:** Reservoirs define fixed-temperature boundaries for heat transfer problems (e.g., $T = 300 \text{ K}$ wall).
- **Cyclic Device Analysis:** In Carnot, Rankine, and Brayton cycles, the source and sink reservoirs anchor the $T_H$ and $T_L$ limits.
- **Entropy Accounting:** The entropy change of a reservoir is the only "external" entropy change that defines the Second Law inequality for a system operating between reservoirs.

## Entropy Change of a Thermal Reservoir (Key Derivation Foundation)

Since the reservoir undergoes an internally reversible, isothermal heat transfer:

$$dS_{res} = \frac{\delta Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

Integrating (with $T_{res} = \text{const}$):

$$\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

## Energy Balance: System ↔ Reservoir

For a **closed system** exchanging only heat with a single reservoir:

$$Q_{system} + Q_{reservoir} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{reservoir} = -Q_{system}$$

For a **control volume (open system)** at steady state with one reservoir:

$$\dot{Q}_{CV} + \dot{Q}_{res} = 0$$

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Condition | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{res} \to \infty$ | Defining condition (infinite heat capacity) | J/K |
| 2 | $dT_{res} = 0$ | Isothermal nature of reservoir | K |
| 3 | $\Delta S_{res} = Q_{res}/T_{res}$ | Entropy change of reservoir (integrated) | J/K |
| 4 | $dS_{res} = \delta Q_{res}/T_{res}$ | Differential entropy transfer | J/K |
| 5 | $Q_{res} = -Q_{sys}$ | Energy balance (closed system) | J |
| 6 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ | Reservoir-tempered cycle limit | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + Q_{res}/T_{res} \ge 0$ | Second Law with reservoir | J/K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** KTU uses the convention that $Q > 0$ means heat **into** the system. If the system receives $+Q$, the reservoir loses the same $Q$, so $Q_{res} = -Q$. Mixing signs is the #1 reason students lose 1-2 marks per question.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** The condenser cooling water is treated as a low-temperature sink reservoir.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** The outdoor ambient air acts as the high-temperature sink for the rejected heat.
- **Heat Exchangers:** The cold/hot utility streams are modeled as reservoirs in pinch analysis.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** Combustion products are approximated as a high-temperature source.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: The Defining Condition of a Thermal Reservoir

**Statement to Prove:** *A body with $C \to \infty$ can exchange finite heat $\delta Q$ while $dT \to 0$.*

**Starting Point — General Heat Capacity Definition:**

$$C = \frac{\delta Q}{dT}$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to express the heat exchanged in terms of capacity and temperature change:

$$\delta Q = C \cdot dT$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit of the product as $C$ becomes infinite and $dT$ becomes infinitesimally small:

$$\lim_{C \to \infty} \delta Q = \lim_{C \to \infty,\, dT \to 0} (C \cdot dT)$$

**Step 3:** Apply L'Hôpital-style limiting form (indeterminate $\infty \cdot 0$):

$$\delta Q = \lim_{C \to \infty} C \cdot \lim_{dT \to 0} dT = \infty \cdot 0 = \text{finite}$$

**Step 4:** Conclude — the heat exchanged remains finite while temperature is unchanged:

$$\boxed{\delta Q = \text{finite}, \quad dT = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{res} = \text{constant}}$$

## Derivation 2: Entropy Change of a Thermal Reservoir

**Starting Point — Clausius Definition of Entropy:**

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

**Step 1:** For a reservoir, the process is **internally reversible** (since temperature is uniform and constant), so:

$$dS_{res} = \frac{\delta Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

**Step 2:** Because $T_{res}$ is independent of time and position, it can be treated as a constant and pulled out of the integral:

$$\Delta S_{res} = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{res}}{T_{res}} = \frac{1}{T_{res}} \int_{1}^{2} \delta Q_{res}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral of the heat term (which is simply the total heat transferred):

$$\int_{1}^{2} \delta Q_{res} = Q_{res}$$

**Step 4:** Final integrated form:

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}}$$

**Step 5 (Sign Check):** If the reservoir *supplies* heat ($Q_{res} < 0$ from reservoir's perspective, i.e., the reservoir loses heat), then $\Delta S_{res} < 0$. The reservoir's entropy *decreases*.

## Derivation 3: Energy Balance Between System and a Single Reservoir

**Setup:** A closed system undergoes a process while in thermal contact with a single thermal reservoir at $T_{res}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the First Law to the **combined system** (system + reservoir) as an isolated supersystem:

$$\Delta U_{total} = Q_{total} - W_{total}$$

**Step 2:** Since the combined supersystem is isolated, $Q_{total} = 0$ and $W_{total} = 0$:

$$\Delta U_{total} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expand the total internal energy:

$$\Delta U_{sys} + \Delta U_{res} = 0$$

**Step 4:** For the reservoir, the change in internal energy equals the heat added (since its temperature is constant and no work crosses its boundary, assuming rigid walls):

$$\Delta U_{res} = Q_{res}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$\Delta U_{sys} + Q_{res} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{res} = -\Delta U_{sys}$$

**Step 6:** For the special case of a closed system at constant volume doing only boundary work (or no work), the heat transferred *to the system* equals the negative of the reservoir's heat:

$$\boxed{Q_{sys} = -Q_{res}}$$

## Derivation 4: Total Entropy Change for a System Operating Between Two Reservoirs

**Setup:** A heat engine receives $Q_H$ from a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ and rejects $Q_L$ to a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$.

**Step 1:** Write the entropy change of the universe (system + both reservoirs):

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{H,res} + \Delta S_{L,res}$$

**Step 2:** For a complete cycle, the system returns to its initial state, so:

$$\Delta S_{sys} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Apply the reservoir entropy formula to each:

$$\Delta S_{H,res} = \frac{-Q_H}{T_H} \quad \text{(reservoir loses heat)}$$

$$\Delta S_{L,res} = \frac{+Q_L}{T_L} \quad \text{(reservoir gains heat)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Second Law of Thermodynamics:

$$\Delta S_{univ} \ge 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} \ge 0$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the Carnot inequality:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} \le \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} \ge \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$

This is the foundational inequality that limits all real heat engines.

## Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Result

| Scenario | Applicable Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Reservoir's entropy change | $\Delta S_{res} = Q_{res}/T_{res}$ | Single heat transfer step |
| System + 1 reservoir (closed) | $Q_{sys} = -Q_{res}$ | First Law sanity check |
| System + 2 reservoirs (cyclic) | $\Delta S_{univ} = -Q_H/T_H + Q_L/T_L$ | Carnot / Second Law |
| Source-only contact | $T_{sys} = T_H$ | Isothermal expansion/compression |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Mermaid Block Diagram: Conceptual Architecture of a Thermal Reservoir

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[THERMAL RESERVOIR CONCEPT] --> B[DEFINING PROPERTIES]
    A --> C[CLASSIFICATION]
    A --> D[INTERACTION WITH SYSTEM]
    A --> E[ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS]
    B --> B1[Heat Capacity C tends to infinity]
    B --> B2[Temperature dT equals zero]
    B --> B3[Heat Transfer delta Q is finite]
    C --> C1[Heat Source - High T H]
    C --> C2[Heat Sink - Low T L]
    D --> D1[Supplies heat to system - Q out of res positive to sys]
    D --> D2[Absorbs heat from system - Q into res]
    D --> D3[Isothermal process maintained at T res]
    C1 --> F1[Examples Furnace Boiler Reactor Core]
    C2 --> F2[Examples Atmosphere Ocean Cooling Pond]
    E --> E1[Power Plant Condenser]
    E --> E2[Refrigeration Outdoor Coil]
    E --> E3[Carnot Cycle Heat Exchange]
    E --> E4[Heat Exchanger Utility Streams]
    D1 --> G1[Entropy of res decreases]
    D2 --> G2[Entropy of res increases]
    G1 --> H1[Delta S res equals minus Q res by T res]
    G2 --> H1
```

## Mermaid Block Diagram: System ↔ Reservoir Energy and Entropy Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    SYS[CLOSED SYSTEM AT T sys] -- Heat Q sys --> RES[THERMAL RESERVOIR AT T res]
    RES -- Heat Q res equals minus Q sys --> SYS
    SYS -- Entropy generation S gen --> ENV[SURROUNDINGS]
    RES -- Entropy change delta S res --> ENV
    H1[Heat Source T H] -. Furnace, Boiler, Reactor .-> SYS
    H2[Heat Sink T L] -. Atmosphere, Ocean, Cooling Water .-> RES
    SYS --> W[Work Output W]
    SYS --> H3[Internal Energy Change delta U]
    H3 --> SYS
```

## Mermaid Block Diagram: Role of Reservoirs in a Heat Engine Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    ENG[HEAT ENGINE CYCLE] --> SRC[HIGH T RESERVOIR - SOURCE - T H]
    ENG --> SNK[LOW T RESERVOIR - SINK - T L]
    SRC -- Q H into engine --> ENG
    ENG -- Q L out of engine --> SNK
    ENG -- W net out --> OUT[USEFUL WORK OUTPUT]
    SRC -. Delta S H res equals minus Q H by T H .-> CHK[SECOND LAW CHECK]
    SNK -. Delta S L res equals plus Q L by T L .-> CHK
    CHK --> RES2[Delta S universe greater than or equal to zero]
    RES2 --> EFF[eta less than or equal to eta Carnot equals 1 minus T L by T H]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing a reservoir on your answer sheet, always draw it as a **rectangle (or trapezoid)** with a *fixed temperature label* ($T_H$ or $T_L$) inside. Use **straight arrows** for heat flow and **curly/wavy arrows** for entropy change. Examiners award 1-2 marks just for a *clean, well-labeled* diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a thermal reservoir. Distinguish between a heat source and a heat sink with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **thermal reservoir** is a body of sufficiently large heat capacity that can supply or absorb a finite amount of heat without any change in its temperature.

**Distinction:**

- **Heat Source:** A thermal reservoir at *high temperature* that *supplies* heat to the system.  
  *Example:* Furnace combustion chamber in a boiler plant.
- **Heat Sink:** A thermal reservoir at *low temperature* that *absorbs* heat rejected by the system.  
  *Example:* Atmospheric air used in an air-cooled condenser.

*Allocation: 1 mark – definition; 1 mark – source + example; 1 mark – sink + example.*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Write the mathematical condition that defines a thermal reservoir and derive the expression for the entropy change of a reservoir.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The defining condition is:

$$C_{res} = \frac{\delta Q}{dT} \rightarrow \infty \quad \text{with} \quad dT \to 0$$

Since the reservoir undergoes an internally reversible isothermal process:

$$dS_{res} = \frac{\delta Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

Integrating with $T_{res} = \text{const}$:

$$\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}}$$

*Allocation: 1 mark – condition; 1 mark – differential form; 1 mark – integrated form.*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A heat engine operates between a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$ and a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. The engine receives $Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$ of heat from the source per cycle.  
**(i)** Determine the *maximum possible* efficiency and the corresponding work output per cycle.  
**(ii)** Calculate the total entropy change of the universe per cycle for a *reversible* operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of thermal reservoirs in defining the limits of real heat engines, with reference to the Carnot efficiency expression. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i):** Maximum (Carnot) efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

Maximum work output:

$$W_{max} = \eta_{max} \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (a)(ii):** For a reversible cycle, $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$.

The heat rejected to the low-temperature reservoir:

$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{max} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

Entropy change of source:

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{1000}{800} = -1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Entropy change of sink:

$$\Delta S_L = +\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = +\frac{375}{300} = +1.25 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Total entropy change of universe:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_L = -1.25 + 1.25 = 0 \text{ kJ/K} \;\checkmark$$

*Allocation: (i) 1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark answer; (ii) 1 mark $Q_L$ + 1 mark $\Delta S_H$ + 1 mark $\Delta S_L$ + 1 mark conclusion.*

**Part (b):**  
Thermal reservoirs define the **temperature boundaries** $T_H$ and $T_L$ of a heat engine. The **Carnot efficiency** sets the *theoretical upper limit* for any heat engine operating between these two reservoirs:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

No real engine can exceed this value, as proven by the Second Law. Reservoirs also provide a *clean accounting framework* for entropy: the entropy generated within the engine is balanced against the entropy changes of the source and sink.

*Allocation: 1 mark – reservoir as boundary; 1 mark – Carnot formula; 1 mark – explanation of limit; 1 mark – entropy accounting; 3 marks – coherent explanation/derivation links.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** A refrigeration cycle removes $Q_L = 500 \text{ kJ}$ of heat from a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L = 250 \text{ K}$ and rejects heat to a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H = 350 \text{ K}$.  
**(i)** Calculate the *minimum work input* required per cycle.  
**(ii)** Determine the coefficient of performance (COP) and the total entropy change of the universe for a reversible cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the energy and entropy interactions between a closed system and a single thermal reservoir. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i):** For a reversible refrigeration cycle (COP is maximum):

$$COP_{R,max} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{250}{350 - 250} = 2.5$$

The relationship between heat removed and work input:

$$COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{in,min} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_{R,max}} = \frac{500}{2.5} = 200 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (a)(ii):** Coefficient of performance:

$$COP_{R,max} = 2.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

Heat rejected to high-temperature reservoir:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 500 + 200 = 700 \text{ kJ}$$

Entropy change of low-temperature reservoir (loses heat):

$$\Delta S_L = -\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = -\frac{500}{250} = -2.0 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Entropy change of high-temperature reservoir (gains heat):

$$\Delta S_H = +\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = +\frac{700}{350} = +2.0 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Total entropy change (reversible cycle):

$$\Delta S_{univ} = -2.0 + 2.0 = 0 \text{ kJ/K} \;\checkmark$$

*Allocation: 1 mark – COP formula; 1 mark – $W_{in}$; 1 mark – $Q_H$; 1 mark – $\Delta S_L$; 1 mark – $\Delta S_H$; 2 marks – $\Delta S_{univ}$ and conclusion.*

**Part (b):**  
Consider a closed system at temperature $T_{sys}$ in contact with a single thermal reservoir at $T_{res}$.

**Energy Interaction:**
The heat lost by the reservoir equals the heat gained by the system:

$$Q_{res} = -Q_{sys}$$

**Entropy Interaction:**
The reservoir undergoes an internally reversible isothermal process:

$$\Delta S_{res} = \frac{Q_{res}}{T_{res}} = -\frac{Q_{sys}}{T_{res}}$$

The total entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{res} \ge 0$$

A labeled block diagram should show the system and reservoir as two distinct boxes with **heat flow arrow** (energy) and **entropy flow arrow** (entropy generation).

*Allocation: 1 mark – diagram with labels; 1 mark – $Q$ balance; 1 mark – $\Delta S_{res}$ formula; 1 mark – $T_{res}$ constant justification; 1 mark – $\Delta S_{univ}$ inequality; 2 marks – coherent writing.*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in Thermal Reservoir Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** Writing $\Delta S_{univ} = +Q_H/T_H + Q_L/T_L$ instead of $-Q_H/T_H + Q_L/T_L$. Cost: 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing reservoir and system temperatures:** The reservoir's temperature is constant; the system's temperature can change. Cost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $T$ in °C** in the entropy formula $\Delta S = Q/T$. Always convert to Kelvin first.
> 4. **Not specifying the sign convention** in word problems. State "heat into the system is positive" before solving.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** KTU examiners often award 1-2 marks *just* for a well-labeled system-reservoir diagram. Don't lose easy marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A thermal reservoir is a body of *infinite heat capacity* with *constant temperature* that can absorb or supply finite heat.
- **Two Types:** *Heat Source* (high $T$, supplies heat) and *Heat Sink* (low $T$, absorbs heat).
- **Key Formula:** $\Delta S_{res} = Q_{res}/T_{res}$ (since $T_{res}$ is constant).
- **Energy Balance:** $Q_{sys} = -Q_{res}$ (for a closed system in contact with one reservoir).
- **Second Law Tool:** $\Delta S_{univ} = -Q_H/T_H + Q_L/T_L \ge 0$.
- **Carnot Limit:** $\eta_{max} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ — the *theoretical maximum* efficiency of any heat engine between two reservoirs.
- **Reversible vs. Irreversible:** $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$ (reversible), $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ (irreversible).
- **Engineering Anchors:** Furnace = source; atmosphere/ocean = sink; condenser cooling water = sink.
- **Unit Vigilance:** Always use **Kelvin** in entropy formulas. $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$.
- **Mnemonic for Sign:** *"Reservoir gives heat → reservoir's entropy falls → $\Delta S_{res}$ is negative."*
- **Exam Tip:** Draw the system-reservoir diagram *first*, then solve. This guarantees the 1-2 mark "diagram" credit and keeps your solution organized.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Heat Engine # 🔥 Heat Engine — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Heat Engine** is a thermodynamic operating device or system that continuously converts **heat energy (thermal energy)** supplied from a high-temperature source into **useful mechanical work (shaft work)**, while rejecting the remaining unused heat to a low-temperature sink, thereby operating in a **cyclic process** where the working fluid returns to its initial state after every cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > A heat engine is a device operating in a thermodynamic cycle that performs a net positive work output by transferring heat from a high-temperature reservoir (source) to a low-temperature reservoir (sink), in accordance with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics (Kelvin–Planck Statement)**. ### Three Essential Components of Every Heat Engine: 1. **Heat Source (Hot Reservoir)** — Supplies heat $Q_H$ at temperature $T_H$ 2. **Heat Sink (Cold Reservoir)** — Receives rejected heat $Q_L$ at temperature $T_L$ 3. **Working Substance** — The medium (gas, vapour, or combustion products) that undergoes the cyclic process ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel Intuition 💧 Imagine a **water wheel placed between two reservoirs at different heights**: - The **high-level water source** drops water onto the wheel (analogous to high-temperature heat $Q_H$). - The wheel **spins**, performing useful mechanical work (analogous to $W_{net}$). - The water finally falls into a **low-level reservoir** (analogous to low-temperature heat rejection $Q_L$). - A pump lifts water back to the top to repeat the cycle (this is why the working fluid must return to its initial state). > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Just as water naturally flows from high to low elevation, heat naturally flows from hot to cold. A heat engine *harnesses* this natural flow to extract work — it never reverses the flow spontaneously (Second Law of Kelvin–Planck). ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Weightage ⭐) | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Heat Supplied | $Q_H$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | Energy input from source | | Heat Rejected | $Q_L$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | Energy dumped to sink | | Net Work Output | $W_{net}$ | **kJ** or **kJ/kg** | Useful work extracted | | Source Temperature | $T_H$ | **K (Kelvin)** | Absolute temperature mandatory | | Sink Temperature | $T_L$ | **K (Kelvin)** | Absolute temperature mandatory | | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | **Dimensionless (%)** | Performance indicator | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin (K)** before substituting in efficiency formulas. Using °C will cost 1–2 marks instantly! # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Heat Engine — Stepwise Breakdown The functioning of a heat engine can be broken down into a logical sequence as required by the KTU valuation key: - **Step 1 — Heat Absorption:** The working substance receives heat $Q_H$ from the high-temperature source during the heat addition process (e.g., combustion in a cylinder, or heat transfer through walls in a steam boiler). - **Step 2 — Expansion & Work Production:** The working substance expands (e.g., gas pushes a piston), converting part of the absorbed heat into **expansion work** $W_{exp}$. - **Step 3 — Heat Rejection:** The remaining unused heat $Q_L$ is expelled to the low-temperature sink (e.g., exhaust gases, condenser cooling water). - **Step 4 — Compression:** The working substance is compressed back to its initial state, requiring compression work $W_{comp}$. - **Step 5 — Cyclic Closure:** Net work done = $W_{exp} - W_{comp}$ = $W_{net}$, and the working substance returns to its initial thermodynamic state, satisfying $\oint dU = 0$. ### 2.1.1 Energy Balance (First Law Applied to the Cycle) Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics** over a complete cycle: $$Q_{net} = W_{net}$$ $$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$ This is the **single most important equation** in any heat engine problem on the KTU exam. ### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind Cyclic Operation The 'why' is rooted in the fact that internal energy $U$ is a **point function** (state property). After a complete cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\oint dU = 0$, which is what allows continuous net work extraction from a single charge of working fluid. ## 2.2 Thermal Efficiency — The Performance Parameter **Thermal efficiency** is defined as the ratio of net work output to heat supplied: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ The **Carnot (Reversible) Efficiency** is the theoretical upper limit for any heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kelvin–Planck Statement (KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight):** > *It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work.* > **Consequence:** No heat engine can have 100% efficiency. $\eta_{th} = 1$ is **impossible**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | # | Formula | Description | Units | KTU Board Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$ | First Law energy balance for cycle | kJ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency (general) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) efficiency | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ | Efficiency from work | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot cycle heat ratio | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Heat pump COP (Carnot) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | $COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Refrigerator COP (Carnot) | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 8 | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - r^{\gamma - 1}$ | Otto cycle efficiency | dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 9 | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle efficiency | dimensionless | ⭐⭐ | | 10 | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Brayton cycle efficiency | dimensionless | ⭐⭐ | Where: - $r$ = Compression ratio $= V_1 / V_2$ - $\rho$ = Cut-off ratio - $r_p$ = Pressure ratio - $\gamma$ = Specific heat ratio ($C_p / C_v$) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Heat engines power nearly all **stationary power generation** (thermal power plants using Rankine cycles), **transportation** (Otto, Diesel, Brayton cycles in cars, trucks, jets), and **portable machinery**. The thermal efficiency directly determines **fuel economy, emissions, and operating cost**, making it the central performance metric in **mechanical, automobile, and power engineering** disciplines. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Efficiency in Terms of Heat (General Heat Engine) **Starting Point:** By the First Law of Thermodynamics applied over a complete cyclic process: $$dQ_{net} = dW_{net}$$ Since internal energy is a state function, for a complete cycle: $$\oint dU = 0 \implies Q_{net} = W_{net}$$ The net heat transfer is the algebraic sum of heat added and heat rejected: $$Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ Therefore, the net work output is: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Step 1 — Definition of Thermal Efficiency:** By definition, the thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ is the ratio of net work output to the heat supplied from the high-temperature source: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Expression for $W_{net}$:** Replacing $W_{net}$ with the First Law result: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}$$ **Step 3 — Separate the Numerator:** Splitting the fraction into two parts: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H} - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** The first term equals unity, yielding the canonical form: $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}}$$ This is the **most general expression** for the thermal efficiency of any heat engine, applicable to all cycles — reversible or irreversible. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carnot (Reversible) Thermal Efficiency **Starting Point:** Consider a Carnot cycle operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$. For a reversible isothermal heat transfer process, the heat $Q$ exchanged is related to entropy change $S$ as: $$Q = T \cdot \Delta S$$ **Step 1 — Heat Supplied (Isothermal Expansion at $T_H$):** During isothermal expansion at the higher temperature $T_H$, the working substance receives heat: $$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S_H$$ where $\Delta S_H$ is the entropy change during heat addition. **Step 2 — Heat Rejected (Isothermal Compression at $T_L$):** During isothermal compression at the lower temperature $T_L$, the working substance rejects heat: $$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S_L$$ **Step 3 — Entropy Balance for a Reversible Cycle:** For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the total entropy change over a complete cycle is zero (entropy is a state function): $$\oint dS = 0 \implies \Delta S_H = \Delta S_L = \Delta S$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the General Efficiency Formula:** Using the result from Derivation 1: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ Plugging in the reversible heat expressions: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{T_H \cdot \Delta S}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel the Common Entropy Term:** The $\Delta S$ cancels out: $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** This derivation makes it clear that the maximum possible efficiency depends **only** on the absolute temperatures of the two reservoirs — a beautiful result of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relation $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$ for Carnot Cycle **Step 1 — Start with the First Law for the Cycle:** $$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$$ **Step 2 — Carnot Cycle Has Two Isothermal and Two Adiabatic Processes:** - Isothermal expansion at $T_H$: receives $Q_H$, does work $W_1 = Q_H$ - Adiabatic expansion: temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$, work done $W_2$ - Isothermal compression at $T_L$: rejects $Q_L$, work input $W_3 = Q_L$ - Adiabatic compression: temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$, work input $W_4$ **Step 3 — For the Isothermal Processes:** Work done during isothermal expansion of an ideal gas: $$W_1 = Q_H = nRT_H \ln\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$ Work input during isothermal compression: $$W_3 = Q_L = nRT_L \ln\left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Adiabatic Relations:** From adiabatic relations, $TV^{\gamma - 1} = \text{constant}$: $$T_H V_2^{\gamma - 1} = T_L V_3^{\gamma - 1}$$ $$T_L V_4^{\gamma - 1} = T_H V_1^{\gamma - 1}$$ Dividing these two equations: $$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{V_3}{V_4}$$ **Step 5 — Take the Ratio of $Q_L$ to $Q_H$:** $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)}{T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)} = \frac{T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)}{T_H \ln(V_3/V_4)}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}}$$ This proves that for a Carnot cycle, the heat ratio equals the absolute temperature ratio. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table (Common KTU Question Type) | Cycle | Heat Addition | Heat Rejection | Compression | Efficiency Formula | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Carnot** | Isothermal | Isothermal | Isentropic | $1 - T_L/T_H$ | | **Otto** | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | Isentropic | $1 - r^{\gamma - 1}$ | | **Diesel** | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume | Isentropic | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma -
