1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | | **Brayton** | Constant Pressure | Constant Pressure | Isentropic | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | | **Rankine** | Constant Pressure (Boiler) | Constant Pressure (Condenser) | Isentropic (Pump) | Depends on turbine inlet conditions | # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — Schematic of a Generalized Heat Engine The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the functional architecture of a generalized heat engine, including the energy flows between the source, working substance, sink, and work output. This is the most commonly expected diagram in KTU Module 3 exam answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HOT RESERVOIR
Source at TH"] B["WORKING SUBSTANCE
Cyclic Device"] C["COLD RESERVOIR
Sink at TL"] D["SHAFT
Net Work Output"] A -- "QH Heat In" --> B B -- "QL Heat Out" --> C B -- "Wnet Useful Work" --> D style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffd166,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style C fill:#4d96ff,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style D fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Description:** Heat $Q_H$ flows from the hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$ into the cyclic device containing the working substance. Inside the cyclic device, the working substance undergoes a thermodynamic cycle, producing net work $W_{net}$ as the output and rejecting the unused heat $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — P-V and T-S Diagram Mapping for Carnot Cycle The Carnot cycle is the reference standard for all heat engines. The diagram below maps the four processes of the Carnot cycle on both **P-V (Pressure-Volume)** and **T-S (Temperature-Entropy)** planes. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PV["P-V DIAGRAM (PRESSURE vs VOLUME)"] P1["1: Start of Isothermal Expansion"] P2["2: End of Isothermal Expansion
Beginning of Adiabatic Expansion"] P3["3: End of Adiabatic Expansion
Start of Isothermal Compression"] P4["4: End of Isothermal Compression
Start of Adiabatic Compression"] P1 ---|Isothermal Expansion at TH| P2 P2 ---|Adiabatic Expansion TL Achieved| P3 P3 ---|Isothermal Compression at TL| P4 P4 ---|Adiabatic Compression TH Restored| P1 end subgraph TS["T-S DIAGRAM (TEMPERATURE vs ENTROPY)"] S1["1: High T Start"] S2["2: Isothermal Expansion Horizontal Line"] S3["3: Adiabatic Drop Vertical Line"] S4["4: Isothermal Compression Horizontal Line at TL"] S1 --- S2 S2 --- S3 S3 --- S4 S4 ---|Adiabatic Compression Vertical Rise| S1 end style P1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style P2 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style P3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style P4 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style S1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style S2 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style S3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style S4 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` **Key Insight:** On the T-S diagram, the Carnot cycle forms a **perfect rectangle** because the isothermal processes are horizontal lines and the isentropic (adiabatic, reversible) processes are vertical lines. The **area enclosed** by the rectangle on the T-S diagram equals the **net work done** in one cycle: $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$. --- ## 4.3 Classification Flowchart — Types of Heat Engines ```mermaid flowchart TB A["HEAT ENGINES"] B["EXTERNAL COMBUSTION
EC Engine"] C["INTERNAL COMBUSTION
IC Engine"] D["Steam Engine
Stirling Engine"] E["Otto Cycle SI Engine"] F["Diesel Cycle CI Engine"] G["Brayton Cycle Gas Turbine"] A --> B A --> C B --> D C --> E C --> F C --> G style A fill:#ffe066,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style B fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style C fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style D fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style E fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style F fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style G fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` **Explanation:** - **External Combustion Engines:** Combustion occurs outside the working fluid cylinder (e.g., steam power plants, Stirling engines). - **Internal Combustion Engines:** Combustion occurs inside the cylinder with the working fluid (e.g., petrol/diesel car engines, jet engines, gas turbines). # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define a Heat Engine. State the Kelvin–Planck statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **heat engine** is a thermodynamic device that operates in a cycle and converts heat energy supplied from a high-temperature source into useful mechanical work, rejecting the remaining heat to a low-temperature sink. **Kelvin–Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. (3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for Kelvin–Planck statement) --- ### **Question 2: Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Answer:** For a Carnot engine, from the First Law applied to the cycle: $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$ Thermal efficiency is defined as: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$ For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the heat ratio equals the absolute temperature ratio: $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ Substituting: $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$ (3 Marks: 1 for First Law, 1 for substitution, 1 for final boxed expression) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### ✅ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **A Carnot heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. The engine receives 1000 kJ of heat from the source per cycle.** **(a) Calculate the thermal efficiency, net work output, and heat rejected per cycle. (7 Marks)** **(b) If the same engine is redesigned to operate between 900 K and 300 K, find the new efficiency and the additional work that can be obtained per cycle. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Given:** - $T_H = 800$ K - $T_L = 300$ K - $Q_H = 1000$ kJ **Step 1 — Calculate Carnot Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 \text{ or } 62.5\%$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Net Work Output:** $$W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Heat Rejected:** $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$ **Answers (a):** $\eta_{th} = 62.5\%$, $W_{net} = 625$ kJ, $Q_L = 375$ kJ --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **New Given:** $T_H' = 900$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 1000$ kJ **Step 1 — Calculate New Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th}' = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667 \text{ or } 66.67\%$$ **Step 2 — Calculate New Net Work:** $$W_{net}' = \eta_{th}' \times Q_H = 0.6667 \times 1000 = 666.67 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 3 — Additional Work:** $$\Delta W = W_{net}' - W_{net} = 666.67 - 625 = 41.67 \text{ kJ}$$ **Answers (b):** $\eta_{th}' = 66.67\%$, Additional work $\Delta W = 41.67$ kJ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Using temperatures in °C (500 °C and 27 °C) instead of converting to Kelvin. **Always convert to Kelvin first!** > - **Common Mistake 2:** Computing additional work as the *new* work (666.67 kJ) instead of the *difference* (41.67 kJ). The question asks for **additional** work. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to show the unit (kJ) and percentage sign properly. --- ### ✅ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]` **A heat engine receives 500 kJ of heat from a high-temperature source at 1200 K and rejects 200 kJ of heat to a sink at 300 K per cycle.** **(a) Determine the thermal efficiency, the net work output, and verify whether the engine is reversible, irreversible, or impossible. (7 Marks)** **(b) Calculate the maximum possible efficiency (Carnot) for the given temperature limits, and find the actual $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ ratio and compare it with $\frac{T_L}{T_H}$. (7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Given:** - $Q_H = 500$ kJ - $Q_L = 200$ kJ - $T_H = 1200$ K - $T_L = 300$ K **Step 1 — Calculate Net Work:** $$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = 500 - 200 = 300 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Actual Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{actual} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{200}{500} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ or } 60\%$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Carnot (Maximum) Efficiency:** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{1200} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%$$ **Step 4 — Comparison & Verdict:** Since $\eta_{actual} = 60\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 75\%$, and $\eta_{actual} > 0$, the engine is operating in a **possible but irreversible** manner. (It is not violating the Second Law, but it is not maximally efficient.) **Answers (a):** $W_{net} = 300$ kJ, $\eta_{actual} = 60\%$, Engine is **Irreversible (Possible)**. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Step 1 — Carnot Efficiency (Already Computed):** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 75\%$$ **Step 2 — Actual $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ Ratio:** $$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical $\frac{T_L}{T_H}$ Ratio:** $$\frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{1200} = 0.25$$ **Step 4 — Comparison:** Since $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 0.4 > \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 0.25$, more heat is being rejected per unit heat input than the reversible minimum. This confirms the engine is **irreversible** — irreversibilities cause additional heat rejection beyond the theoretical minimum. **Answers (b):** $\eta_{Carnot} = 75\%$, $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 0.4$, $\frac{T_L}{T_H} = 0.25$, Engine is **irreversible** because the heat ratio exceeds the temperature ratio. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Option B:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Stating the engine is "impossible" just because it is not Carnot. The Second Law only forbids $\eta = 100\%$, not $\eta = 60\%$. The engine is possible and irreversible. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Failing to compare $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ with $\frac{T_L}{T_H}$ — the inequality direction is the **proof of irreversibility**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating the verdict clearly. The conclusion sentence is worth 1 mark by itself. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 - **Definition:** A heat engine operates in a cycle, taking $Q_H$ from a hot source, producing $W_{net}$, and rejecting $Q_L$ to a cold sink. - **First Law Energy Balance:** $Q_H - Q_L = W_{net}$ — the *master equation* for any heat engine cycle. - **Thermal Efficiency (General):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ — always expressed as a decimal or percentage. - **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$ — theoretical maximum, depends *only* on absolute temperatures. - **Absolute Temperatures:** $T_H$ and $T_L$ must always be in **Kelvin**. The conversion is $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. - **Kelvin–Planck Statement:** No heat engine can convert all absorbed heat into work ($\eta = 1$ is impossible). - **Reversibility Check:** For a Carnot engine, $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$. If $\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} > \frac{T_L}{T_H}$, the engine is **irreversible**. - **Sign Convention:** $Q_H$ is supplied (input, +ve), $Q_L$ is rejected (output, but treated as energy leaving the working fluid), $W_{net}$ is output (always +ve for a heat engine). - **Three Components:** Source, Working Substance, Sink — every heat engine has exactly these three. - **Real Engines:** Otto (petrol), Diesel, Brayton (jet), Rankine (steam plant) are all examples — each with its own efficiency formula in the KTU cheat sheet. - **Common Board Favourite:** "Calculate efficiency, work, and heat rejected" — memorize all three steps; KTU asks this in nearly every exam. - **Pitfall to Avoid:** Never compute $W$ as $Q_H + Q_L$ — it is **$Q_H - Q_L$** (subtraction, not addition).

topics.title: Heat Pump, Kelvin-Planck and Clausius Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Pump, Kelvin-Planck and Clausius Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Heat Pump — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Heat Pump):**
> A **Heat Pump** is a thermodynamic device that operates on a **reversed thermodynamic cycle** and transfers energy in the form of **heat from a low-temperature reservoir to a high-temperature reservoir**, with the continuous input of **external work** to the system.

Mathematically, the Heat Pump is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a cyclic device:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W$$

where:
- $Q_H$ = Heat rejected to the high-temperature (hot) reservoir (in kJ or kW)
- $Q_L$ = Heat absorbed from the low-temperature (cold) reservoir (in kJ or kW)
- $W$ = Net work input supplied to the cycle (in kJ or kW)

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a Heat Pump is the most important metric — it is defined as the ratio of the **desired effect** (heat delivered to the hot body) to the **work input** required.

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_H}{W}$$

## 1.2 The Refrigerator vs. The Heat Pump — A Critical Distinction

Although both devices operate on the **same reversed thermodynamic cycle** and obey the same energy equation, their **purpose** (and hence their "desired effect") is fundamentally different.

| Device | Purpose | Desired Effect | COP Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerator** | To maintain a body at a temperature **lower** than its surroundings | Heat removed from the cold body $Q_L$ | $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W$ |
| **Heat Pump** | To maintain a body at a temperature **higher** than its surroundings | Heat delivered to the hot body $Q_H$ | $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W$ |

> [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** A refrigerator and a heat pump are **physically the same machine** with the same components. The only difference is the **location of the useful coil** — the coil inside the conditioned space makes it a refrigerator; the coil inside the conditioned space for a house being *heated* makes it a heat pump.

## 1.3 The Kelvin–Planck Statement of the Second Law

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Kelvin–Planck Statement (KTU Board Examination Standard Wording):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

In simpler words: **No heat engine can convert all the heat it receives from a single reservoir entirely into work.** At least *some* heat must be rejected to a lower-temperature sink.

A device that would violate this — i.e., a 100% efficient heat engine with $W = Q_H$ and $Q_L = 0$ — is called a **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)**.

## 1.4 The Clausius Statement of the Second Law

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Clausius Statement (KTU Board Examination Standard Wording):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

In simpler words: **Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body** without the continuous expenditure of external work.

A device that would violate this — i.e., a self-acting refrigeration cycle with $W = 0$ — is also a **PMM2**.

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP] > **Analogy 1 — The Heat Pump as a "Heat Elevator":** Imagine a bucket that lifts water *uphill* (from a low well to a high tank). The water is $Q_L$, the muscle/electric energy you put in is $W$, and the water that arrives at the top tank is $Q_H$. The bucket can never lift water on its own — it needs you. The **COP** tells you how much water arrives at the top *per unit* of your effort. A COP of 4 means for every 1 kJ of work, 4 kJ of heat is delivered to the hot space.

> [!TIP] > **Analogy 2 — Kelvin–Planck as a "Water Wheel Without Spillage":** Imagine a water wheel in a river. The Kelvin–Planck statement says you can never build a wheel that converts *all* the river's kinetic energy into useful rotation while returning *zero* water downstream. Some energy (analogous to $Q_L$) must always be "wasted" to the surroundings.

> [!TIP] > **Analogy 3 — Clausius as "Heat Doesn't Climb by Itself":** Think of a metal spoon on a hot cup of tea. Heat naturally travels *downhill* (hot → cold), never the reverse. Clausius says: a refrigerator can force heat to climb back "uphill," but only by paying an energy tax ($W$).

## 1.6 Why These Statements Matter in Engineering

Every real engine, refrigerator, and heat pump is **bounded** by these two statements. They define the **maximum possible efficiency** and **maximum possible COP** for any thermal device. Without the Second Law, one could (in principle) build a 100%-efficient engine or a self-cooling box — both of which would upend energy economics and violate countless observed phenomena.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Heat Pump — Step-by-Step

A heat pump is, by construction, a **reversed heat engine**. To understand its logic, we trace the heat and work interactions:

1. **Cold Reservoir Interaction:** The working fluid (refrigerant) absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the low-temperature space (e.g., outside air at $T_L$).
2. **Work Input:** An amount of work $W$ is supplied to the compressor (the most energy-intensive component).
3. **Hot Reservoir Interaction:** The working fluid rejects heat $Q_H = Q_L + W$ to the high-temperature space (e.g., inside the room at $T_H$).
4. **Cycle Repetition:** The fluid returns to its initial state, and the process repeats continuously.

The **Carnot Heat Pump** — the theoretical benchmark — is a reversible cycle operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$ (absolute temperatures, in Kelvin). Its COP is:

$$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

This is the **upper bound**; no real heat pump can exceed it.

## 2.2 Relationship Between $\text{COP}_{HP}$ and $\text{COP}_R$

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. The relationship is:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$$

### Why is this true? (Conceptual 'Why')

Because the heat pump and refrigerator share the same work input $W$, but the heat pump "counts" the **total** heat rejected ($Q_H$) as its useful output, while the refrigerator only "counts" the heat removed from the cold space ($Q_L$). Since $Q_H = Q_L + W$, the difference is exactly $W$ — and dividing this difference by $W$ gives the **+1** term.

## 2.3 Why Two Statements for One Law?

Both the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements are **logically equivalent** formulations of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. They appear different because they address two different physical impossibilities:
- **Kelvin–Planck** addresses the impossibility of a 100% heat-to-work converter.
- **Clausius** addresses the impossibility of self-acting refrigeration.

Yet, a violation of one **necessarily implies** a violation of the other. This equivalence is one of the most elegant results in classical thermodynamics and is frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 2.4 PMM2 — The Forbidden Machine

A **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)** is a hypothetical device that would violate the Second Law. There are two versions:

- **PMM2 (Kelvin–Planck type):** Operates in a cycle, exchanges heat with a single reservoir, and produces net work ($W = Q_H$, $Q_L = 0$).
- **PMM2 (Clausius type):** Operates in a cycle, transfers heat from cold to hot with no work input ($Q_H = Q_L$, $W = 0$).

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A **PMM2** does *not* violate the **First Law** (energy is conserved) — it only violates the **Second Law**. This is a common confusion point in exams.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_H = Q_L + W$ | First Law for heat pump / refrigerator (cyclic device) | kJ or kW |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W}$ | Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Pump | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ | Coefficient of Performance of a Refrigerator | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Universal relationship between HP and Refrigerator COPs | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum (theoretical) COP of a reversible heat pump | $T_H, T_L$ in **K** (always absolute) |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum (theoretical) COP of a reversible refrigerator | $T_H, T_L$ in **K** |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum thermal efficiency of a heat engine | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q_L \neq 0$ for any cyclic heat engine | Kelvin–Planck implication (at least one sink required) | — |
| 9 | $W \neq 0$ for any cold-to-hot heat transfer | Clausius implication (work tax required) | — |
| 10 | $\text{COP}_{HP} \geq 1$ always | Heat pump always delivers *more* heat than the work put in | Lower bound |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder — Use Absolute Temperature:** All Carnot COP and efficiency formulas require temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**. KTU examiners regularly test students by giving temperatures in °C to trap them. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15 \approx T(°C) + 273$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building HVAC systems** use heat pumps to provide heating in winter and cooling in summer (reversible cycle).
- **Industrial process heating** (e.g., food drying, paper manufacturing) uses high-temperature heat pumps to recover waste heat.
- **Refrigeration and air-conditioning** industries apply the same principles to design efficient refrigerators and chillers.
- The **Carnot COP** sets the *benchmark* against which the **Seasonal Coefficient of Performance (SCOP)** of real units is measured for energy-star ratings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$

**Given:** A heat pump and a refrigerator share the same energy equation in a cycle.

**Step 1 — Write the First Law for the cyclic device:**

$$Q_H = Q_L + W$$

This equation states that the heat rejected to the hot body equals the heat absorbed from the cold body plus the work input.

**Step 2 — Write the COP of the Heat Pump:**

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W}$$

By definition, this is the ratio of the desired effect (heat delivered to the hot space) to the work input.

**Step 3 — Write the COP of the Refrigerator:**

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W}$$

By definition, this is the ratio of the desired effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input.

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_H = Q_L + W$ into the heat pump COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W} \\
&= \frac{Q_L + W}{W} \\
&= \frac{Q_L}{W} + \frac{W}{W} \\
&= \frac{Q_L}{W} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $Q_L / W$ is exactly the refrigerator's COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP} &= \text{COP}_R + 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The COP of a heat pump is exactly 1 greater than the COP of a refrigerator operating between the same two thermal reservoirs with the same work input. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: $\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$

**Given:** A reversible (Carnot) heat pump cycle operating between two reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_L$ (in Kelvin).

**Step 1 — Write the Carnot cycle efficiency for the equivalent forward heat engine:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 2 — Express efficiency in terms of heat:**

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions and solve for $Q_L / Q_H$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} &= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \\
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $Q_H / Q_L$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} &= \frac{T_H}{T_L} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the First Law to relate work:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= Q_H - Q_L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the COP definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{Q_H}{W} \\
&= \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}} \\
&= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The Carnot COP of a heat pump depends only on the reservoir temperatures. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalence of Kelvin–Planck and Clausius Statements

This is a **contrapositive proof**. We show that a violation of one statement implies a violation of the other.

### Part A: Violation of Kelvin–Planck $\Rightarrow$ Violation of Clausius

**Assumption (for contradiction):** Suppose a **PMM2 of Kelvin–Planck type** exists. This device takes heat $Q_H$ from a single hot reservoir at $T_H$ and converts it *entirely* into work $W = Q_H$, with no heat rejected ($Q_L = 0$).

**Construction of the violating Clausius device:**

- Couple this PMM2 to a **real (Clausius-violating would-be) refrigerator** that needs $W$ as input.
- The real refrigerator absorbs $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and delivers $Q_L + W = Q_L + Q_H$ to the hot reservoir.

**Combined system (PMM2 + Refrigerator) analysis:**

- **Work input of refrigerator:** $W = Q_H$ (supplied by the PMM2)
- **Heat absorbed from cold reservoir:** $Q_L$
- **Heat rejected to hot reservoir:** $Q_L + W = Q_L + Q_H$
- **Net heat rejected to hot reservoir:** $(Q_L + Q_H) - Q_H = Q_L$

**Net effect of the combined system:**

- Work input from outside: **Zero** (PMM2 supplies exactly the work needed).
- Heat absorbed from cold reservoir: $Q_L$.
- Heat rejected to hot reservoir: $Q_L$.

**Conclusion:** The combined system transfers heat $Q_L$ from a cold body to a hot body **without any external work input** — this is precisely a **violation of the Clausius statement**.

Hence: **Kelvin–Planck violation $\Rightarrow$ Clausius violation.** $\blacksquare$

### Part B: Violation of Clausius $\Rightarrow$ Violation of Kelvin–Planck

**Assumption (for contradiction):** Suppose a **PMM2 of Clausius type** exists. This device transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ with no work input ($W = 0$).

**Construction of the violating Kelvin–Planck device:**

- Couple this PMM2 to a **real heat engine** that operates between $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- The real engine absorbs $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, does work $W$, and rejects $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir.

**Design choice:** Adjust the heat engine such that $Q_L$ (its rejected heat) equals $Q$ (the heat absorbed by the PMM2 from the cold reservoir).

**Combined system (PMM2 + Heat Engine) analysis:**

- **Heat absorbed by PMM2 from cold reservoir:** $Q$
- **Heat supplied by PMM2 to hot reservoir:** $Q$ (no work exchanged)
- **Heat rejected by engine to cold reservoir:** $Q$ (same as PMM2 absorbs — they cancel)
- **Heat absorbed by engine from hot reservoir:** $Q_H$
- **Net heat taken from hot reservoir:** $Q_H - Q$
- **Work output:** $W = Q_H - Q$

**Net effect of the combined system:**

- Heat absorbed: $Q_H - Q$ (from a single hot reservoir at $T_H$)
- Work produced: $W = Q_H - Q$
- No heat rejected anywhere (PMM2 cancels the engine's sink heat).

**Conclusion:** The combined system extracts heat from a *single* reservoir and converts it *entirely* into work — this is a **violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement**.

Hence: **Clausius violation $\Rightarrow$ Kelvin–Planck violation.** $\blacksquare$

### Combined Result

Since each violation implies the other, the two statements are **logically equivalent**:

$$\text{Kelvin–Planck is false} \iff \text{Clausius is false}$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{Kelvin–Planck is true} \iff \text{Clausius is true}$$

This establishes the **unified Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison

| Aspect | Kelvin–Planck Statement | Clausius Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Device addressed** | Heat Engine | Refrigerator / Heat Pump |
| **Direction of heat flow** | Hot $\rightarrow$ Work $\rightarrow$ some to Cold | Cold $\rightarrow$ Hot (forced) |
| **Forbidden scenario** | 100% conversion of $Q_H$ to $W$ | Spontaneous $Q$ flow cold $\to$ hot without $W$ |
| **PMM2 type** | Single-reservoir engine | Self-acting refrigerator |
| **Test for violation** | $Q_L = 0$ in a cycle | $W = 0$ in a cycle |
| **Real-world consequence** | No 100%-efficient engine possible | No passive refrigeration possible |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Working Principle of a Heat Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOT["HIGH TEMPERATURE RESERVOIR AT TH"]
        direction TB
        HOTBODY["Hot Space / Room Being Heated"]
    end

    subgraph COLD["LOW TEMPERATURE RESERVOIR AT TL"]
        direction TB
        COLDBODY["Cold Space / Outside Air / Ground"]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE["HEAT PUMP CYCLE"]
        direction TB
        EVAP["Evaporator Coil"]
        COMP["Compressor W Input"]
        COND["Condenser Coil"]
        EXVAL["Expansion Valve"]
    end

    QL["Heat QL Absorbed"]:::heatflow
    W["Work W Input"]:::workflow
    QH["Heat QH Rejected"]:::heatflow

    COLDBODY --> QL --> EVAP
    EVAP --> COMP
    COMP --> W
    COMP --> COND
    COND --> QH --> HOTBODY
    COND --> EXVAL --> EVAP

    classDef heatflow stroke:#d62728,stroke-width:2px,fill:#ffe5e5,color:#000
    classDef workflow stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px,fill:#e5f0ff,color:#000
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - The **Evaporator** absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir.
> - The **Compressor** receives work $W$ from an external source (electricity).
> - The **Condenser** rejects heat $Q_H = Q_L + W$ to the hot reservoir.
> - The **Expansion Valve** drops the pressure so the cycle can repeat.
> - The net balance is the First Law for a cyclic device.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Equivalence Proof (Kelvin–Planck Violation $\Rightarrow$ Clausius Violation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASSUMPTION["ASSUMPTION FOR CONTRADICTION"]
        direction TB
        PMM2KP["PMM2 KELVIN PLANCK TYPE Heat QH from Single Reservoir Converted Fully to Work W QH"]
    end

    subgraph COMBINE["COMBINED SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        REALREF["REAL REFRIGERATOR Absorbs QL from Cold Rejects QL plus W to Hot W Supplied by PMM2"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NET EFFECT OF COMBINED SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        NETRESULT["Heat QL flows from Cold Reservoir to Hot Reservoir With ZERO External Work Input"]
    end

    subgraph CONCLUSION["CONCLUSION"]
        direction TB
        CLAUSVIOL["CLAUSIUS STATEMENT IS VIOLATED Equivalence Established"]
    end

    PMM2KP --> REALREF
    REALREF --> NETRESULT
    NETRESULT --> CLAUSVIOL
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The proof constructs a composite system by coupling a hypothetical PMM2 (Kelvin–Planck violator) to a real refrigerator. The PMM2's work output exactly powers the refrigerator, and the refrigerator's rejected heat to the cold sink exactly matches the heat absorbed from the cold sink. The composite device then performs "self-acting refrigeration" — a Clausius violation.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Equivalence Proof (Clausius Violation $\Rightarrow$ Kelvin–Planck Violation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ASSUMPTION2["ASSUMPTION FOR CONTRADICTION"]
        direction TB
        PMM2CL["PMM2 CLAUSIUS TYPE Heat Q from Cold to Hot With Zero Work Input W Zero"]
    end

    subgraph COMBINE2["COMBINED SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        REALENG["REAL HEAT ENGINE Absorbs QH from Hot Does Work W Rejects QL to Cold QL Equals Q from PMM2"]
    end

    subgraph NET2["NET EFFECT OF COMBINED SYSTEM"]
        direction TB
        NETRESULT2["Heat QH minus Q extracted from Single Hot Reservoir Converted Fully to Work No Heat Rejected"]
    end

    subgraph CONCLUSION2["CONCLUSION"]
        direction TB
        KPVIOL["KELVIN PLANCK STATEMENT IS VIOLATED Equivalence Confirmed"]
    end

    PMM2CL --> REALENG
    REALENG --> NETRESULT2
    NETRESULT2 --> KPVIOL
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** The symmetric proof. A hypothetical PMM2 (Clausius violator) is coupled to a real heat engine. The PMM2's cold-to-hot heat flow cancels the engine's hot-to-cold rejection, leaving a single-reservoir device that converts heat entirely into work — a Kelvin–Planck violation.

---

## 4.4 Heat Engine vs. Heat Pump — Directional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENG["HEAT ENGINE FORWARD CYCLE"]
        ENG_HOT["Hot Reservoir TH"] -->|"QH"| ENG_DEV["Engine Cycle"]
        ENG_DEV -->|"QL"| ENG_COLD["Cold Reservoir TL"]
        ENG_DEV -->|"W OUT"| ENG_SHAFT["Shaft Work Output"]
    end

    subgraph HP["HEAT PUMP REVERSED CYCLE"]
        HP_COLD["Cold Reservoir TL"] -->|"QL IN"| HP_DEV["Pump Cycle"]
        HP_DEV -->|"QH OUT"| HP_HOT["Hot Reservoir TH"]
        HP_ELEC["Electrical Work W IN"] -->|"W IN"| HP_DEV
    end

    ENG -.Reversal of Direction.-> HP
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** A heat engine takes $Q_H$ from a hot source, produces work $W$, and rejects $Q_L$ to the cold. Reversing the direction (and adding work input) yields a heat pump. The mathematical formulations are mirror images — this is why the Carnot formulas for efficiency and COP are so closely related.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Kelvin–Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. (1½ marks)

**Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. (1½ marks)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Heat Pump and a Refrigerator. Show that $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Difference:**

| Heat Pump | Refrigerator |
|---|---|
| Delivers heat $Q_H$ to the hot space (heating) | Extracts heat $Q_L$ from the cold space (cooling) |
| $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W$ | $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W$ |

**Derivation (1½ marks):**

From First Law, $Q_H = Q_L + W$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_L + W}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + 1 = \text{COP}_R + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts. Students must answer **ONE full question** (either Q-A or Q-B) out of the module's internal choice.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State and explain the Kelvin–Planck statement of the Second Law. Why is a 100% efficient heat engine impossible? (7 marks)

**(b)** A reversible heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. Determine: (i) the thermal efficiency, (ii) the work output if 1000 kJ of heat is supplied, and (iii) the heat rejected to the sink. If the same cycle is reversed to act as a heat pump operating between the same reservoirs, find the COP of the heat pump. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Statement (2 marks):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single thermal reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

**Explanation (3 marks):**
A heat engine operating in a cycle receives heat $Q_H$ from a source at $T_H$, produces work $W$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to a sink at $T_L < T_H$. The First Law gives $Q_H = W + Q_L$. For 100% efficiency, $Q_L = 0$ and $W = Q_H$. The Kelvin–Planck statement forbids this. A hypothetical 100%-efficient engine is called a **PMM2**. Such a device would convert the vast low-grade heat of oceans, atmosphere, etc. into useful work — violating extensive experimental evidence.

**Why is it impossible? (2 marks):**
Any real engine must reject some heat to a lower-temperature sink. This is a fundamental consequence of the Second Law, supported by all experimental observations. A PMM2 would also enable self-acting refrigeration (see Clausius equivalence), leading to contradictions with the natural direction of heat flow.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 1000$ kJ.

**(i) Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 \\
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 62.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Work output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \eta \cdot Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat rejected to sink:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_L &= Q_H - W = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**COP of the reversed-cycle heat pump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{800}{800 - 300} = \frac{800}{500} = 1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature in °C, not K:** Many students forget to convert and use $T_L = 300$ °C, which is meaningless in Carnot formulas. **Always convert to Kelvin first.**
> 2. **Mixing up Q_L and Q_H:** The 1000 kJ supplied is $Q_H$ (from the hot source), not $Q_L$.
> 3. **Confusing heat engine with heat pump formulas:** $\eta = W/Q_H$ is for engines; $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H/W$ is for heat pumps.
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** All heat quantities should be **positive magnitudes** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** State the Clausius statement of the Second Law. Show that a violation of the Clausius statement implies a violation of the Kelvin–Planck statement. (7 marks)

**(b)** A refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10$ °C and a hot space at 35 °C. It removes 1500 kJ of heat per cycle from the cold space. Assuming a reversible (Carnot) cycle: (i) calculate the COP of the refrigerator, (ii) calculate the work input required per cycle, and (iii) calculate the heat rejected to the hot space per cycle. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Statement (2 marks):**
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

**Implication:** If this were possible, one could build a self-acting refrigerator requiring no work input (a PMM2 of Clausius type). The Second Law forbids this.

**Proof of Equivalence (5 marks):**

*Assumption:* Suppose a Clausius-violating device (PMM2-C) exists that transfers heat $Q$ from a cold reservoir at $T_L$ to a hot reservoir at $T_H$ with $W = 0$.

*Construction:* Couple this PMM2-C to a **real heat engine** operating between the same two reservoirs. The real engine absorbs $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir, produces work $W$, and rejects $Q_L$ to the cold reservoir. Choose the engine such that $Q_L = Q$ (the heat absorbed by the PMM2 from the cold reservoir).

*Analysis of the combined system:*

- The PMM2-C absorbs $Q$ from the cold reservoir.
- The engine rejects $Q$ to the cold reservoir.
- **Net heat exchange with the cold reservoir: ZERO** (they cancel).
- The engine absorbs $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir.
- The PMM2-C delivers $Q$ to the hot reservoir.
- **Net heat taken from the hot reservoir: $Q_H - Q$.**
- **Net work output: $W = Q_H - Q$.**

*Conclusion:* The combined system extracts heat $Q_H - Q$ from a **single** hot reservoir and converts it **entirely** into work $W = Q_H - Q$, with **no heat rejected to any cold sink**. This is a direct violation of the **Kelvin–Planck statement**.

Therefore, a violation of Clausius implies a violation of Kelvin–Planck. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_L &= -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K} \\
T_H &= 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Given:** $Q_L = 1500$ kJ per cycle.

**(i) COP of the refrigerator (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45} \\
\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} &\approx 5.844
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Work input per cycle (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{1500}{5.844} \approx 256.7 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat rejected to the hot space (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= Q_L + W = 1500 + 256.7 \approx 1756.7 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes in This Question Type:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K:** The most frequent error. With $T_L = -10$ and $T_H = 35$, students who skip conversion get a nonsensical negative $T_H - T_L = 45$ °C, but more critically, ratios become meaningless. **Always Kelvin.**
> 2. **Confusing $Q_L$ and $Q_H$ in the COP formula:** $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W$, not $Q_H / W$. This is the most common sign-of-confusion indicator for examiners.
> 3. **Rounding too early:** Carry 4 significant figures until the final answer. Premature rounding leads to 0.5–1 mark deductions.
> 4. **Mis-stating the Clausius statement:** A vague paraphrase ("heat doesn't flow from cold to hot") is worth only 1 of 2 marks. The KTU key requires the precise wording including *"operates in a cycle"* and *"no other effect."*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Heat Pump:** Reversed-cycle device; delivers heat $Q_H$ to a hot space using work input $W$.
- **First Law (cyclic):** $Q_H = Q_L + W$ — the single most important equation for both refrigerators and heat pumps.
- **COP of Heat Pump:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W$. This is the **desired effect** metric.
- **COP of Refrigerator:** $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W$.
- **Key Identity:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ (derive on exam day if memory fails).
- **Carnot COP of Heat Pump:** $\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ — temperatures **must be in Kelvin**.
- **Carnot COP of Refrigerator:** $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.
- **Carnot Efficiency of Heat Engine:** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_L / T_H = (T_H - T_L) / T_H$.
- **Kelvin–Planck:** No cyclic device can convert heat from a *single* reservoir fully into work. ($Q_L \neq 0$.)
- **Clausius:** No cyclic device can transfer heat from cold to hot without external work. ($W \neq 0$.)
- **Equivalence:** Violation of Kelvin–Planck $\iff$ Violation of Clausius (both proven via composite-device arguments).
- **PMM2:** A device that violates the Second Law (but not the First Law). Two types: Kelvin–Planck type and Clausius type.
- **Numerical hygiene:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin before plugging into Carnot formulas. Carry full precision to the final step. Match each heat quantity to its reservoir (hot vs. cold).
- **COP $\geq 1$** is always true for any heat pump — the device delivers *more* heat than the work you put in (the rest is "free" from the cold reservoir).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of Two Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Equivalence of Two Statements # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Two Classical Statements of the Second Law The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** is a fundamental principle that governs the direction of natural processes and the efficiency of energy conversion systems. Unlike the First Law (which deals with the *conservation* of energy), the Second Law places restrictions on the *direction* of energy transfer and the *quality* of energy. Historically, this law was formulated in two distinct, seemingly independent ways by two pioneering physicists. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > The Second Law of Thermodynamics is a cornerstone topic. The equivalence of its two classical statements (Kelvin-Planck and Clausius) is a **high-weightage, frequently-asked topic** in KTU university examinations. Students are expected to know both statements verbatim and reproduce both proofs. ### 1.1.1 Kelvin-Planck Statement > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (Kelvin-Planck Statement):** > *It is impossible for any device that operates on a cycle to receive heat from a single reservoir and produce a net amount of work.* In other words, a heat engine cannot convert heat completely into work without some heat being rejected to a lower-temperature reservoir. A hypothetical engine that violates this statement is called a **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)**. ### 1.1.2 Clausius Statement > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (Clausius Statement):** > *It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.* In simpler terms, heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body without any external work input. A hypothetical refrigerator/heat pump that violates this statement is called a **Perfect Refrigerator (or PMM2 in cooling mode)**. ## 1.2 The Claim of Equivalence The **Equivalence of the Two Statements** asserts that: 1. A violation of the **Clausius statement** implies a violation of the **Kelvin-Planck statement** (and vice-versa). 2. A violation of the **Kelvin-Planck statement** implies a violation of the **Clausius statement** (and vice-versa). Both statements are, therefore, logically equivalent — they are simply two different ways of expressing the same fundamental physical truth. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "River and Dam" Picture Imagine a river flowing downhill — nature has a clear **preferred direction** for water flow. - The **Kelvin-Planck statement** is like saying: *"You cannot build a water wheel that takes water from the bottom of the river, lifts all of it back to the top without any leftover water, and extracts useful mechanical work in the process."* You will always lose some water (energy) as it flows past. - The **Clausius statement** is like saying: *"Water at the bottom of the river will never spontaneously climb back to the top all by itself."* The equivalence proof shows that **if you could build a machine that makes water spontaneously climb uphill, you could also build a water wheel that produces work from a single reservoir of water at one level**. The two impossibilities are the same impossibility viewed from two different angles. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics - **Thermal Efficiency of a Heat Engine:** $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ - **Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a Refrigerator:** $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ - **Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Pump:** $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For an engine to satisfy the Kelvin-Planck statement: $Q_L \neq 0$ and therefore $\eta_{th} < 100\%$. > For a refrigerator to satisfy the Clausius statement: $W_{net} \neq 0$ and therefore $COP_R < \infty$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logic of Equivalence — The Big Picture To prove that two propositions **A** and **B** are logically equivalent, we must prove two conditional statements: 1. **Part I:** $A \implies B$ (i.e., if A is true, then B is true) 2. **Part II:** $B \implies A$ (i.e., if B is true, then A is true) Equivalently, by **proof by contradiction** (the method used in thermodynamics), we prove: 1. **Part I:** If A is **violated** (i.e., A is false), then B is also **violated** (B is false). 2. **Part II:** If B is **violated**, then A is also **violated**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect students to label both parts of the proof clearly. A common mistake is to prove only one part and lose **7 out of 14 marks**. ## 2.2 Strategy Used in the Proofs The proofs are constructed by **combining two devices**: one that is assumed to **violate** one statement, and another (a normal, law-abiding device) that, when combined with the violating device, creates a **net device that violates the other statement**. This is an application of the **Reductio ad Absurdum** method. | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $Q_H$ | Heat transferred from the high-temperature reservoir | | $Q_L$ | Heat transferred to the low-temperature reservoir | | $W_{net}$ | Net work output (for engine) or work input (for refrigerator) | | $\eta_{th}$ | Thermal efficiency of a heat engine | | $COP$ | Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator/heat pump | | **PMM2** | Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind — a hypothetical violation of the Second Law | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Statement | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---| | Kelvin-Planck Statement | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ with $Q_L > 0$ | $\eta_{th} < 100\%$ always | | Clausius Statement | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ with $W_{net} > 0$ | $COP_R < \infty$ always | | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | $0 < \eta_{th} < 1$ | | COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | $COP_R > 1$ is possible | | COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | $COP_{HP} > 1$ is possible | | First Law (Cyclic Device) | $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$ | Energy is conserved | | PMM2 Condition | $\eta_{th} = 100\%$ | Impossible by Kelvin-Planck | | Perfect Refrigerator | $COP_R = \infty$ | Impossible by Clausius | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The equivalence of the two statements is not merely a theoretical curiosity. It has profound engineering implications: - **Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning:** Engineers use the Clausius statement to design refrigerators and heat pumps. The equivalence tells us that any refrigerator must consume work to move heat from cold to hot — there is no "free lunch." - **Power Plants:** The Kelvin-Planck statement sets the upper limit on thermal efficiency (the **Carnot efficiency**, $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$). The equivalence confirms that improving this efficiency beyond the Carnot limit would be tantamount to building a perfect refrigerator, which is impossible. - **Cryogenics and Liquefaction of Gases:** Engineers designing systems to reach ultra-low temperatures rely on the Clausius statement to know that work input is mandatory. > [!IMPORTANT] > The equivalence is also a powerful **diagnostic tool**: if you ever derive a design that would let you build a PMM2, you can immediately conclude that a perfect refrigerator is also possible — and both are forbidden. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Part I: Violation of Clausius Statement $\implies$ Violation of Kelvin-Planck Statement ### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumption **Assumption (to be contradicted):** Let us assume that the **Clausius statement is violated**. This means there exists a hypothetical refrigerator (**Refrigerator R'**) that: - Operates in a cycle. - Transfers heat $Q_L$ from a **low-temperature reservoir** at $T_L$ to a **high-temperature reservoir** at $T_H$. - Requires **no net work input** ($W_{R'} = 0$). ### 3.1.2 Constructing the Counter-Example Now consider a **real, law-abiding heat engine (Engine E)** that: - Receives heat $Q_H$ from the high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$. - Produces net work output $W_E$. - Rejects heat $Q_L$ to the low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$. For Engine E (First Law applied): $$ W_E = Q_H - Q_L $$ ### 3.1.3 Coupling the Two Devices We now **couple** the two devices such that: - The work output of Engine E ($W_E$) is used to **drive** the violating refrigerator R' (in our setup, $W_{R'} = 0$, so this is automatic). - The heat $Q_L$ rejected by Engine E is **exactly equal** to the heat $Q_L$ extracted by Refrigerator R' from the low-temperature reservoir. The combined system has the following net effect: | Quantity | From Engine E | From Refrigerator R' | Net (Combined) | |---|---|---|---| | Heat from $T_H$ | $Q_H$ (in) | $Q_L$ (out, dumped back) | $Q_H - Q_L$ | | Heat from $T_L$ | $Q_L$ (out) | $Q_L$ (in) | $0$ | | Work | $W_E$ (out) | $0$ (in) | $W_E$ (out) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The low-temperature reservoir experiences **no net heat exchange**. The work output is $W_E$, and the only net heat input comes from the high-temperature reservoir, in the amount $Q_H - Q_L = W_E$. ### 3.1.4 The Contradiction The combined system therefore acts as a heat engine that: - Operates in a cycle (composition of two cycles is a cycle). - Exchanges heat **only with a single reservoir** ($T_H$). - Produces a net amount of work $W_E = Q_H - Q_L > 0$. This is a **direct violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement**, which states that no engine can produce net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir. **Conclusion of Part I:** A violation of the Clausius statement **implies** a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Part II: Violation of Kelvin-Planck Statement $\implies$ Violation of Clausius Statement ### 3.2.1 Setup and Assumption **Assumption (to be contradicted):** Let us assume that the **Kelvin-Planck statement is violated**. This means there exists a hypothetical heat engine (**Engine E'**) that: - Operates in a cycle. - Receives heat $Q_H$ from a **single high-temperature reservoir** at $T_H$. - Converts it **entirely into work**, with $W_{E'} = Q_H$ and $Q_L = 0$. - Its efficiency is therefore $\eta_{th} = 100\%$. ### 3.2.2 Constructing the Counter-Example Now consider a **real, law-abiding refrigerator (Refrigerator R)** that: - Consumes net work input $W_R$. - Extracts heat $Q_L$ from the low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$. - Rejects heat $Q_H$ to the high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$. For Refrigerator R (First Law applied): $$ Q_H = Q_L + W_R $$ ### 3.2.3 Coupling the Two Devices We now **couple** the two devices such that: - The work output of the violating Engine E' is used to **drive** the law-abiding Refrigerator R. - The heat $Q_H$ dumped by Refrigerator R into the high-temperature reservoir is **exactly equal** to** the heat $Q_H$ extracted by Engine E' from the same reservoir. The combined system has the following net effect: | Quantity | From Engine E' | From Refrigerator R | Net (Combined) | |---|---|---|---| | Heat from $T_H$ | $Q_H$ (in) | $Q_H$ (out) | $0$ | | Heat from $T_L$ | $0$ | $Q_L$ (in) | $Q_L$ (in) | | Work | $W_{E'} = Q_H$ (out) | $W_R$ (in) | $Q_H - W_R = Q_L$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The high-temperature reservoir experiences **no net heat exchange**. The only net effect is that heat $Q_L$ is transferred from the low-temperature reservoir to... well, it must go somewhere. By the First Law, the only destination is the work output, but we accounted for the work: $W_{E'} = Q_H$ was supplied to drive the refrigerator. ### 3.2.4 Net Effect of the Combined System The combined system is a refrigerator that: - Operates in a cycle (composition of two cycles is a cycle). - Requires **no external work input** (the work needed by R is provided by E' from the single reservoir). - Transfers heat $Q_L$ from the low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ to the high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$. This is a **direct violation of the Clausius statement**, which forbids any device from transferring heat from a cold body to a hot body without external work input. **Conclusion of Part II:** A violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement **implies** a violation of the Clausius statement. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Final Synthesis Combining both parts: $$ \text{Violation of Clausius} \iff \text{Violation of Kelvin-Planck} $$ Therefore, the two statements are **logically equivalent**. They are two sides of the same physical law — the Second Law of Thermodynamics. ## 3.4 The Master Equation The Second Law, in either statement, can be compactly expressed through the **Clausius Inequality**: $$ \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 $$ with equality only for **reversible cycles**. This single inequality encapsulates both the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements as special cases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Clausius Inequality derivation is a separate but related topic. Be prepared to derive it from either the Kelvin-Planck or Clausius statement in your exam. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards The following Mermaid diagrams use purely alphanumeric node identifiers, clean uppercase text labels, and no markdown formatting inside the labels. ## 4.2 Part I: Violation of Clausius $\implies$ Violation of Kelvin-Planck ```mermaid flowchart LR resH["HIGH TEMP RESERVOIR TH"] resL["LOW TEMP RESERVOIR TL"] engineE["REAL HEAT ENGINE E
INPUT QH, OUTPUT QE, REJECTS QL"] refrigR["HYPOTHETICAL REFRIGERATOR R PRIME
INPUT QL, OUTPUT QL, NO WORK"] workOut["NET WORK OUTPUT WE = QH MINUS QL"] resH -- QH --> engineE engineE -- QL --> resL resL -- QL --> refrigR refrigR -- QL --> resH engineE -- WE --> workOut ``` **Reading the diagram:** The dashed-coupling logic is — Engine E dumps $Q_L$ into Reservoir $T_L$, and Refrigerator R' lifts the **same** $Q_L$ back to Reservoir $T_H$ using zero work. The high-temperature reservoir loses $Q_H - Q_L$ in net, which equals the work output. This is a single-reservoir engine — a PMM2 — forbidden by Kelvin-Planck. ## 4.3 Part II: Violation of Kelvin-Planck $\implies$ Violation of Clausius ```mermaid flowchart LR resH["HIGH TEMP RESERVOIR TH"] resL["LOW TEMP RESERVOIR TL"] engineEp["HYPOTHETICAL ENGINE E PRIME
INPUT QH, OUTPUT QH, ETA = 100 PERCENT"] refrigR["REAL REFRIGERATOR R
INPUT QL PLUS WR, REJECTS QH"] coolEffect["NET COOLING EFFECT QL
NO EXTERNAL WORK"] resH -- QH --> engineEp engineEp -- QH EQUALS WORK --> refrigR refrigR -- QH --> resH resL -- QL --> refrigR refrigR -- QL --> coolEffect ``` **Reading the diagram:** Engine E' converts $Q_H$ fully into work with 100% efficiency. This work drives Refrigerator R, which extracts $Q_L$ from $T_L$ and rejects $Q_H$ to $T_H$. The high-temperature reservoir experiences no net change. The net effect is heat transfer from cold to hot with no external work — a perfect refrigerator — forbidden by Clausius. ## 4.4 Combined Block Architecture: The Equivalence Master Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph law1["STATEMENT A: KELVIN PLANCK"] kp["NO ENGINE CAN CONVERT HEAT FROM
A SINGLE RESERVOIR ENTIRELY INTO WORK"] end subgraph law2["STATEMENT B: CLAUSIUS"] cl["NO REFRIGERATOR CAN TRANSFER HEAT
FROM COLD TO HOT WITHOUT WORK INPUT"] end subgraph proof1["PART I PROOF"] p1["ASSUME B IS VIOLATED
COUPLE WITH REAL ENGINE E
RESULT: A IS VIOLATED"] end subgraph proof2["PART II PROOF"] p2["ASSUME A IS VIOLATED
COUPLE WITH REAL REFRIGERATOR R
RESULT: B IS VIOLATED"] end law1 --> proof2 law2 --> proof1 proof1 --> law1 proof2 --> law2 ``` **Reading the diagram:** This meta-diagram shows the logical flow of the equivalence proof. Statement A (Kelvin-Planck) and Statement B (Clausius) are linked by two arrows: one showing "violation of B $\Rightarrow$ violation of A" and another showing "violation of A $\Rightarrow$ violation of B." Together, they establish the bidirectional logical equivalence. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **State the Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and performing an equivalent amount of work. In other words, no heat engine can have a thermal efficiency of 100%. > > **Clausius Statement:** It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. In other words, heat cannot spontaneously flow from a low-temperature region to a high-temperature region without external work input. > > *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for each statement.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **What is a Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)? Why is it impossible?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A **Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2)** is a hypothetical device that would operate in a cycle, exchange heat with a single thermal reservoir, and convert that heat completely into work (i.e., achieve 100% thermal efficiency). > > It is impossible because its existence would directly **violate the Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. The Kelvin-Planck statement asserts that at least some heat must be rejected to a lower-temperature sink, so $Q_L > 0$ and $\eta_{th} < 100\%$. > > *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the violation, 1 mark for the connection to Kelvin-Planck.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern (ESE Module 3):** Part B questions are worth 14 marks and offer an internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B below are designed in this exact pattern. **Answer ANY ONE.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** State the Clausius and Kelvin-Planck statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Show that a violation of the Clausius statement leads to a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Show that a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement leads to a violation of the Clausius statement. Hence establish the equivalence of the two statements. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Step-by-step:** **Step 1: State the Clausius statement.** > It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body. *(1 mark)* **Step 2: State the Kelvin-Planck statement.** > It is impossible for any device that operates on a cycle to receive heat from a single reservoir and produce a net amount of work. *(1 mark)* **Step 3: Assume violation of Clausius.** > Assume a hypothetical refrigerator (R') that transfers heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ to a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ **without any work input** ($W_{R'} = 0$). *(1 mark)* **Step 4: Introduce a real heat engine E.** > Consider a real, law-abiding heat engine E that receives heat $Q_H$ from $T_H$, produces work $W_E$, and rejects heat $Q_L$ to $T_L$, so that $W_E = Q_H - Q_L$. *(1 mark)* **Step 5: Couple the devices.** > Couple E and R' such that the heat rejected by E ($Q_L$) is exactly the heat extracted by R' from $T_L$. The net heat exchange with $T_L$ is **zero**, and the net heat exchange with $T_H$ is $Q_H - Q_L = W_E$. The combined system produces work while exchanging heat with a **single reservoir**. *(2 marks)* **Step 6: Conclude the contradiction.** > This combined system violates the Kelvin-Planck statement. Therefore, a violation of the Clausius statement **implies** a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. *(1 mark)* **Part (b) — Step-by-step:** **Step 1: Assume violation of Kelvin-Planck.** > Assume a hypothetical heat engine (E') that receives heat $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at $T_H$ and converts it **entirely** into work, with $Q_L = 0$ and $W_{E'} = Q_H$. *(1 mark)* **Step 2: Introduce a real refrigerator R.** > Consider a real refrigerator R that consumes work $W_R$, extracts heat $Q_L$ from $T_L$, and rejects heat $Q_H$ to $T_H$, with $Q_H = Q_L + W_R$. *(1 mark)* **Step 3: Couple the devices.** > Use the work output of E' ($W_{E'} = Q_H$) to drive R. Set the heat rejected by R to $T_H$ equal to $Q_H$ — the heat input to E'. The net heat exchange with $T_H$ is **zero**, and the net effect is that heat $Q_L$ flows from $T_L$ to... the work output, which is recycled. The system requires **no external work**. *(3 marks)* **Step 4: Conclude the contradiction.** > This combined system violates the Clausius statement (heat flows from cold to hot without external work). Therefore, a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement **implies** a violation of the Clausius statement. *(1 mark)* **Step 5: State the equivalence.** > Since violation of Clausius $\Rightarrow$ violation of Kelvin-Planck **and** violation of Kelvin-Planck $\Rightarrow$ violation of Clausius, the two statements are **logically equivalent**. *(1 mark)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** State the two classical statements of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Explain, with the help of a neat sketch, how a violation of the Clausius statement leads to a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, prove that a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement leads to a violation of the Clausius statement. State the final conclusion regarding the equivalence of the two statements. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Step-by-step:** **Step 1: State both statements (as in Part A Question 1).** *(2 marks)* **Step 2: Sketch and explain the setup.** Draw a block diagram showing: - A high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ (top). - A low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ (bottom). - A real heat engine E between them, taking $Q_H$ from $T_H$, producing work $W_E$, and dumping $Q_L$ into $T_L$. - A hypothetical violating refrigerator R' between them, taking $Q_L$ from $T_L$ and dumping it into $T_H$ with no work input. *(2 marks)* **Step 3: Apply First Law to each device.** For Engine E: $W_E = Q_H - Q_L$. For Refrigerator R': $W_{R'} = 0$, and the heat dumped to $T_H$ is $Q_L$. *(1 mark)* **Step 4: Compute the net effect of the combined system.** The $T_L$ reservoir sees zero net heat exchange ($Q_L$ in, $Q_L$ out). The $T_H$ reservoir sees net heat $Q_H - Q_L$ going out. By the First Law, this $Q_H - Q_L$ must be the work output. The combined system is a single-reservoir engine. *(1 mark)* **Step 5: Conclude the violation.** A single-reservoir engine that produces work **violates the Kelvin-Planck statement**. Therefore, a violation of the Clausius statement **implies** a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement. *(1 mark)* **Part (b) — Step-by-step:** **Step 1: Sketch the setup.** Draw a block diagram showing: - A high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$. - A low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$. - A hypothetical violating engine E' that takes $Q_H$ from $T_H$ and produces $Q_H$ as work (100% efficiency). - A real refrigerator R that uses this work to extract $Q_L$ from $T_L$ and dump $Q_H$ into $T_H$. *(2 marks)* **Step 2: Apply First Law to each device.** For Engine E': $W_{E'} = Q_H$ (since $Q_L = 0$). For Refrigerator R: $Q_H = Q_L + W_R$. *(1 mark)* **Step 3: Couple and compute net effect.** Use $W_{E'} = Q_H$ to drive R. The heat $Q_H$ rejected by R to $T_H$ equals the heat $Q_H$ taken by E' from $T_H$. The $T_H$ reservoir sees **zero net heat exchange**. The system operates as a refrigerator requiring no external work. *(2 marks)* **Step 4: Identify the violation.** A refrigerator that transfers heat from a cold body to a hot body without any external work **violates the Clausius statement**. *(1 mark)* **Step 5: State the final conclusion.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion of Equivalence:** Since a violation of the Clausius statement implies a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement (Part a) **and** a violation of the Kelvin-Planck statement implies a violation of the Clausius statement (Part b), the two statements are **logically and physically equivalent**. They are merely two different ways of expressing the same underlying physical law — the Second Law of Thermodynamics. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to state the assumption clearly** — Always start the proof by saying "Assume that the Clausius (or Kelvin-Planck) statement is violated, i.e., suppose a hypothetical device exists that..." Examiners specifically look for this. **(-2 marks)** > > 2. **Skipping the First Law equation for the real device** — You must write $W_E = Q_H - Q_L$ or $Q_H = Q_L + W_R$ explicitly. Don't just describe it in words. **(-2 marks)** > > 3. **Not drawing a block diagram** — A neat sketch with proper labeling of $T_H$, $T_L$, $Q_H$, $Q_L$, $W$ is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU. A textual description alone is insufficient. **(-2 marks)** > > 4. **Confusing the direction of heat flow in the violating device** — A violating refrigerator takes heat FROM $T_L$ and dumps it INTO $T_H$. A violating engine takes heat FROM $T_H$ and produces ALL of it as work. Mixing these up is a fatal error. **(-3 marks)** > > 5. **Not stating the final equivalence conclusion** — At the end of Part (b), explicitly write "Therefore the two statements are equivalent." Many students forget this final sentence. **(-1 mark)** > > 6. **Confusing the two statements with the First Law** — The First Law says energy is conserved ($\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$). The Second Law says *direction* of energy transfer is restricted. Do not conflate them. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equivalence of Two Statements** - [x] **Kelvin-Planck Statement:** No cyclic device can convert heat from a single reservoir entirely into work. Implies $\eta_{th} < 100\%$. - [x] **Clausius Statement:** No cyclic device can transfer heat from cold to hot without external work. Implies $COP_R < \infty$. - [x] **PMM2:** A hypothetical 100%-efficient engine. Its existence violates Kelvin-Planck. - [x] **Perfect Refrigerator:** A refrigerator with $COP_R = \infty$ (no work needed). Its existence violates Clausius. - [x] **Part I Proof:** Assume Clausius violated $\to$ couple violating refrigerator with real engine $\to$ net single-reservoir engine $\to$ Kelvin-Planck violated. - [x] **Part II Proof:** Assume Kelvin-Planck violated $\to$ couple violating engine with real refrigerator $\to$ net work-free refrigerator $\to$ Clausius violated. - [x] **Equivalence:** Established by the bidirectional implication in both parts. - [x] **First Law must be applied** to each device individually in every proof. - [x] **Block diagrams are mandatory** in KTU exams for full marks. - [x] **Final conclusion sentence** ("the two statements are equivalent") must appear. - [x] The **Clausius Inequality** $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$ is a compact mathematical form of the Second Law. - [x] **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$ is the maximum possible efficiency of any heat engine operating between two reservoirs. - [x] The equivalence is a **logical, mathematical, and physical** equivalence — not just a philosophical one.

topics.title: Reversibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reversibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Reversible Process** is a thermodynamic process that, after having taken place, can be precisely reversed in such a manner that the system and its surroundings pass through exactly the same intermediate states as in the direct process, and at the conclusion of the reverse process, both the system and surroundings are restored to their original states, leaving no net change in the universe.

In the language of classical thermodynamics, a process is reversible if and only if it is **quasi-static (quasi-equilibrium)** AND **frictionless (non-dissipative)** AND involves **no heat transfer across a finite temperature difference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A process is reversible if it can be retraced exactly in the opposite direction such that the system and surroundings undergo no net change when the process is completed and reversed. **Reversible processes are idealized — they do not actually occur in nature**, but serve as the theoretical benchmark against which real (irreversible) processes are measured.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English

Imagine pushing a heavy wooden block across a **smooth, frictionless marble floor at an infinitesimally slow speed**. Now imagine the process in reverse — pulling the block back along the *exact same path* at the *exact same slow speed*. If you were to film both motions in slow motion and play them backward, you could not tell which was forward and which was reversed. The system and the floor end up exactly where they started.

That — in essence — is a **reversible process**.

Now picture the *same* wooden block sliding across a **rough, carpeted floor** with friction. Friction heats up both the block and the carpet. Even if you pull the block back to its starting position, the carpet fibers remain bent, heat has been generated, and the universe is *not* in its original state. That is an **irreversible process**.

### 1.3 Conditions that Cause Irreversibility (Un-reversibility)

The following naturally occurring phenomena render a process irreversible:

- **Friction** (mechanical, viscous, fluid)
- **Unrestrained expansion** of a gas into a vacuum or lower-pressure region
- **Heat transfer** across a finite temperature difference (ΔT > 0)
- **Mixing** of two different substances (e.g., gases, miscible liquids)
- **Chemical reactions** (especially spontaneous, exothermic ones)
- **Electrical resistance** in current flow
- **Inelastic deformation** of solids
- **Hysteresis** effects in magnetic or elastic materials

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A reversible process is the *limiting case* of an actual process when the various potential gradients (pressure, temperature, chemical potential) driving the process are made infinitesimally small. The smaller the gradient, the closer the actual process approaches reversibility.

### 1.4 Why Reversibility Matters in Engineering

Reversible processes deliver the **maximum possible work** (for work-producing systems like engines) and require the **minimum possible work** (for work-consuming systems like refrigerators/compressors). All real thermodynamic equipment — turbines, compressors, engines, refrigerators — is *designed* to approach reversibility as closely as economically feasible, because reversible = ideal efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Reversible Process — Structured Logic

1. **Quasi-Equilibrium (Quasi-Static) Operation:** The system is never more than infinitesimally removed from equilibrium at any instant. State properties (P, T, v) are well-defined at every point.
2. **No Friction / No Dissipation:** There is no mechanical friction, no viscous dissipation, no electrical resistance, and no inelastic deformation.
3. **No Heat Transfer Across Finite ΔT:** All heat transfer must occur across an *infinitesimal* temperature gradient.
4. **No Unrestrained Expansion:** No spontaneous mixing or free expansion into vacuum.
5. **Exact Reversibility:** The system and surroundings can both be restored to their initial states with no net change in the universe.
6. **Maximum Work Output / Minimum Work Input:** Reversible work is the theoretical upper limit of useful work extractable from any system transitioning between two equilibrium states.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: "Why" and "How" Behind Reversibility

- **Why quasi-static?** Because if the system departs from equilibrium, its state cannot be described by intensive properties — work and heat become path-dependent, and the process cannot be reversed without leaving the surroundings changed.
- **Why no friction?** Friction converts useful mechanical work irreversibly into internal energy (heat). The energy is conserved (1st Law), but the *quality* of energy degrades (2nd Law). Once friction occurs, the dissipated heat cannot be fully reconverted into work.
- **Why no finite ΔT?** Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference is governed by the Clausius inequality and produces entropy in the universe. To reverse it, the universe's entropy would have to decrease, which violates the 2nd Law.

### 2.3 The Two Halves of Any Process

For any process, we can think of it as:

$$\text{Total Process} = \underbrace{\text{Reversible Portion}}_{\text{Ideal, Frictionless, Quasi-static}} + \underbrace{\text{Irreversibility}}_{\text{Friction, ΔT, Mixing, etc.}}$$

The work done in an actual process is:

$$W_{actual} = W_{reversible} - W_{lost}$$

where $W_{lost}$ is the work destroyed by irreversibilities (Clausius/Gouy-Stodola theorem: $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Identity | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | $W_{rev} = \int_{1}^{2} P \, dV$ | Reversible boundary work (closed system) | kJ |
| 2 | $W_{rev,1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$ | Integration of PdV work | kJ |
| 3 | $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$ (Clausius) | Inequality — equality holds for reversible | kJ/K |
| 4 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ | 2nd Law — entropy of universe never decreases | kJ/K |
| 5 | $S_{gen,rev} = 0$ | Entropy generated in a reversible process | kJ/K |
| 6 | $S_{gen,irrev} > 0$ | Entropy generated in any irreversible process | kJ/K |
| 7 | $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Gouy–Stodola theorem (lost work) | kJ |
| 8 | $\eta_{rev} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $COP_{rev,HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible heat pump COP | dimensionless |
| 10 | $COP_{rev,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible refrigerator COP | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Reversible (Carnot) efficiency sets the *upper limit* of thermal efficiency. Real Rankine/Brayton cycles are benchmarked against it.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** COP of reversible refrigeration cycle defines the theoretical ceiling for any real refrigerator.
- **Compressors & Turbines:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression/expansion defines the ideal work — actual work is always higher/lower (compressor/turbine) by the irreversibility penalty.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Reversible reactors approach maximum equilibrium conversion — irreversibility reduces yield.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reversible Boundary Work in a Closed System

Starting from the 1st Law for a closed system undergoing a quasi-equilibrium process (only boundary work, no other work modes):
