The elementary boundary work for a quasi-static expansion of a piston-cylinder assembly against external pressure $P$ is:

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = P \cdot A \cdot dx$$

Since $A \cdot dx = dV$ (a change in volume), we obtain:

$$\delta W = P \, dV$$

For a finite reversible process from state 1 to state 2:

$$W_{rev,1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

To evaluate this integral, we require the functional relationship $P = P(V)$ along the process path, which is given by the equation of state (e.g., ideal gas: $PV = mRT$).

**For an Ideal Gas (m, R, T constant) undergoing a Polytropic Process $PV^n = \text{const}$:**

Substituting $P = C \cdot V^{-n}$ where $C = P_1 V_1^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{rev,1-2} &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C \cdot V^{-n} \, dV \\[6pt]
&= C \cdot \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} \quad \text{(provided } n \neq 1\text{)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1 - n} \quad \text{(using } C = P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n\text{)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{mR(T_2 - T_1)}{1 - n} \quad \text{(using ideal gas law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case n = 1 (Isothermal process for ideal gas):** $P V = \text{const} = mRT$, so $W_{rev,1-2} = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(P_1/P_2)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Clausius Inequality and the Criterion for Reversibility

From the 1st Law for a closed system: $\delta Q = dU + \delta W$.

For a reversible process, the boundary work is $\delta W_{rev} = P \, dV$, and we define the *exact differential* of entropy:

$$dS \equiv \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

For a general (possibly irreversible) process, the 2nd Law states that the heat transfer divided by absolute temperature cannot exceed the entropy change:

$$dS \geq \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

The equality holds if and only if the process is reversible. Integrating over a cycle:

$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 \quad \text{(Clausius Inequality)}
$$

with equality for a fully reversible cycle.

### 3.3 Derivation: Entropy Generation and Lost Work

Consider a system undergoing any process from state 1 to state 2. The entropy balance for the system is:

$$\Delta S_{sys} = \frac{Q}{T_{boundary}} + S_{gen}$$

For a reversible process, $S_{gen} = 0$. For any irreversible process, $S_{gen} > 0$. The entropy change of the surroundings (assuming a thermal reservoir at $T_0$ absorbs $Q$) is:

$$\Delta S_{surr} = -\frac{Q}{T_0}$$

Total entropy change of the universe:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{univ} &= \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{Q}{T_{boundary}} + S_{gen} - \frac{Q}{T_0} \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

If we assume $T_{boundary} = T_0$ (system and surroundings at the same temperature), then:

$$\Delta S_{univ} = S_{gen} \geq 0$$

The **lost work** (Gouy–Stodola theorem) is the work potential destroyed by irreversibility:

$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

For a reversible process, $S_{gen} = 0$, so $W_{lost} = 0$ and the maximum possible work is achieved.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reversible vs. Irreversible — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Attribute | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Equilibrium | System always in equilibrium | System passes through non-equilibrium states |
| Direction | Can be reversed without net change in universe | Cannot be reversed without net change |
| Friction/Dissipation | None | Present |
| Driving force | Infinitesimally small gradient | Finite gradient |
| Work output | Maximum (for expansion devices) | Less than maximum |
| Work input | Minimum (for compression devices) | More than minimum |
| Entropy generation $S_{gen}$ | $= 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Heat transfer condition | Across $dT \to 0$ | Across finite $\Delta T$ |
| Existence in nature | Idealization only | All real processes |
| Reversal signature | Forward and reverse paths are identical | Paths differ; net change in universe |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Thermodynamic Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Thermodynamic Process"] --> B{"Quasi Static?"}
    B -- "No" --> C["Irreversible"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D{"Frictionless?"}
    D -- "No" --> C
    D -- "Yes" --> E{"Finite Delta T?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> C
    E -- "No" --> F{"Unrestrained Expansion?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> C
    F -- "No" --> G{"Mixing or Reaction?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> C
    G -- "No" --> H["Reversible Ideal Process"]
    C --> I["S gen greater than zero"]
    C --> J["W lost greater than zero"]
    H --> K["S gen equals zero"]
    H --> L["W lost equals zero"]
    H --> M["Maximum Work Output"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reversibility Architecture in Real Devices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Real Engine / Compressor / Refrigerator"] --> B["Reversible Ideal Core"]
    B --> C["Piston Friction"]
    B --> D["Heat Transfer Loss Across Finite Delta T"]
    B --> E["Unrestrained Expansion"]
    B --> F["Mixing Losses"]
    B --> G["Electrical Resistance"]
    C --> H["Irreversibility Layer"]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Net Work Deficit vs Ideal"]
    B --> J["Theoretical Benchmark"]
    J --> K["Maximum Efficiency COP"]
    I --> L["Actual Efficiency COP"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: The Reversibility Loop (Forward & Reverse)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    state1: State 1 P1 T1 V1
    state2: State 2 P2 T2 V2
    state3: State 1 P1 T1 V1
    state1 --> state2: Forward Reversible Path A
    state2 --> state3: Reverse Reversible Path B identical to A
    state1: System and Surroundings
    state3: System and Surroundings Restored
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrammatic Insight:** The state diagram above captures the essence of reversibility — the return path is *identical* to the forward path, and upon completion, **both** the system and surroundings return to their original state. In an irreversible process, the return path differs, and a net change persists in the universe.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a reversible process. List any two conditions that must be satisfied for a process to be reversible.**

**Model Answer:**

A reversible process is one which can be retraced exactly in the opposite direction such that the system and surroundings pass through the same intermediate states and return to their original condition with no net change in the universe.

Two essential conditions:
1. The process must be **quasi-static** (the system is in equilibrium at every instant).
2. There must be **no friction** and no **heat transfer across a finite temperature difference**.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark per condition × 2 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Clausius inequality. What is its significance with respect to reversible and irreversible processes?**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius inequality states:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

where the cyclic integral is taken over a complete thermodynamic cycle. The equality sign holds for a **reversible cycle**, while the strict inequality ($\lt 0$) holds for an **irreversible cycle**.

**Significance:** It provides a quantitative mathematical criterion to distinguish reversible from irreversible processes and is a direct mathematical statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

*(1 mark statement + 1 mark equality condition + 1 mark irreversible condition = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of reversibility with reference to a quasi-static frictionless expansion of an ideal gas in a piston-cylinder assembly. Show that the work done in such a reversible process is given by $W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ideal gas (m = 2 kg, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K) expands reversibly and isothermally at T = 400 K from $P_1 = 800$ kPa to $P_2 = 200$ kPa. Calculate the work done and the heat transfer. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

For a piston-cylinder assembly, consider an infinitesimal reversible expansion where the piston moves outward by an amount $dx$ against the external pressure $P$. The boundary work done by the gas is:

$$\delta W = F \cdot dx = (P \cdot A) \cdot dx = P \, dV$$

For a finite reversible process from state 1 ($V_1$) to state 2 ($V_2$):

$$W_{rev,1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

This expression requires the system to be in *quasi-equilibrium* at every instant, so that the internal pressure $P$ equals the external pressure and the system state is well-defined by a single set of intensive properties. The integrand is evaluated along the process path given by the equation of state.

*(2 marks: setup, 3 marks: derivation, 1 mark: physical interpretation, 1 mark: conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: m = 2 kg, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K, T = 400 K (constant), $P_1 = 800$ kPa, $P_2 = 200$ kPa.

For an isothermal reversible process of an ideal gas:

$$W_{rev} = mRT \ln\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{rev} &= 2 \times 0.287 \times 400 \times \ln\left(\frac{800}{200}\right) \\
&= 229.6 \times \ln(4) \\
&= 229.6 \times 1.3863 \\
&= 318.30 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

From the 1st Law, for an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$ (since $U$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal gas). Therefore:

$$Q_{rev} = W_{rev} = 318.30 \text{ kJ}$$

*(2 marks: formula, 2 marks: substitution, 1 mark: calculation, 1 mark: 1st law application, 1 mark: final answer = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** State and prove the Clausius inequality. Show that for a reversible process the equality holds, and for an irreversible process the strict inequality holds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. If the engine receives 1000 kJ of heat from the source, calculate: (i) the maximum possible work output (reversible case), and (ii) the actual work output if the engine efficiency is 35% of the Carnot efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Statement:** For any thermodynamic cycle operating between multiple heat reservoirs, the cyclic integral of $\delta Q / T$ is less than or equal to zero:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

**Proof Sketch:**

Consider an arbitrary system undergoing a cycle while exchanging heat $Q_i$ with reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$. Couple each reservoir to a reversible engine that absorbs $Q_i$ from the reservoir and delivers work $W_i$ to a common reservoir. For a reversible engine:

$$W_i = Q_i \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T_i}\right)$$

Summing over all reservoirs:

$$W_{total} = \sum Q_i - T_0 \sum \frac{Q_i}{T_i}$$

By the Kelvin–Planck statement of the 2nd Law, $W_{total} \leq 0$ (no system can convert heat entirely into work in a cycle). Therefore:

$$T_0 \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0 \implies \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

Equality holds when all irreversibilities are eliminated, i.e., for a **reversible** cycle. The strict inequality holds for any **irreversible** cycle.

*(2 marks: statement, 3 marks: proof, 1 mark: equality case, 1 mark: inequality case = 7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 1000$ kJ.

**(i) Maximum (Reversible / Carnot) Work:**

Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 0.625$$

Maximum work:

$$W_{max} = \eta_{rev} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) Actual Work (35% of Carnot efficiency):**

$$\eta_{actual} = 0.35 \times 0.625 = 0.21875$$

$$W_{actual} = 0.21875 \times 1000 = 218.75 \text{ kJ}$$

*(2 marks: Carnot efficiency, 2 marks: maximum work, 1 mark: actual efficiency, 2 marks: actual work = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing quasi-static with reversible:** Quasi-static alone is *necessary but not sufficient*. A quasi-static process with friction is still irreversible. Students who write "quasi-static = reversible" lose **2 full marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Missing the "no finite ΔT" condition:** Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference is a separate, often-overlooked source of irreversibility. Always list it explicitly.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** In reversible work calculations, expansion work is *positive* (work done *by* the system), compression work is *negative*. KTU 2024 strictly follows the **Physics convention** unless stated otherwise.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always state the units (kJ, kJ/K, kPa, K) explicitly. A numerically correct answer without units is penalized.
> 5. **Clausius inequality direction:** The cyclic integral is $\leq 0$ (not $\geq 0$). Reversible processes correspond to **equality**, not inequality.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **reversible process** is an idealized process that can be retraced exactly with no net change in system or surroundings.
- **Three pillars of reversibility:** quasi-static, frictionless, and no heat transfer across finite ΔT.
- **Reversible processes are idealizations** — they do not occur in nature, but real processes are compared against them.
- The **maximum work** output and **minimum work** input are obtained in reversible processes.
- **Clausius inequality:** $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$; equality for reversible, strict inequality for irreversible.
- **Entropy generation:** $S_{gen} = 0$ for reversible; $S_{gen} > 0$ for irreversible.
- **Gouy–Stodola theorem:** Lost work $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$.
- **Carnot efficiency** $\eta_{rev} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ is the maximum possible thermal efficiency between two reservoirs.
- **Reversible work for closed system:** $W_{rev} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$.
- For an **ideal gas, isothermal reversible process:** $W_{rev} = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = mRT \ln(P_1/P_2)$.
- For an **ideal gas, polytropic reversible process** $PV^n = \text{const}$: $W_{rev} = (P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1)/(1 - n)$ for $n \neq 1$.
- **Reversible adiabatic = Isentropic** process: $\Delta S = 0$.
- All real-world irreversibility sources: friction, finite ΔT heat transfer, unrestrained expansion, mixing, chemical reaction, electrical resistance, hysteresis, inelastic deformation.
- The 2nd Law is *never violated* by reversible processes — it serves as the limiting case.
- **KTU high-yield keywords** to use in answers: *quasi-equilibrium*, *non-dissipative*, *isentropic*, *Cyclic integral*, *Gouy–Stodola*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Irreversible Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Irreversible Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **irreversible process** is a thermodynamic process that *cannot* be retraced exactly in the opposite direction such that the system and its surroundings return to their original states. In other words, once the process occurs, it is impossible to restore *both* the system and the surroundings to their initial configurations without producing a net change in the universe.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Board Definition**
> A process is said to be **irreversible** if the system and all parts of its surroundings cannot be exactly returned to their respective initial states. Equivalently, an irreversible process is one that, when reversed, leaves a permanent trace (entropy change) somewhere in the universe.

> [!NOTE] **Entropy Signature of Irreversibility**
> The mathematical fingerprint of every irreversible process is a **net positive entropy generation** for the universe:
> $$S_{gen,\,universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} > 0$$
> Equality ($= 0$) corresponds to a *reversible* process; strict inequality ($> 0$) is the unmistakable signature of an *irreversible* process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a raw egg dropped onto a hard floor from a height. The egg cracks, the yolk and shell scatter, and energy dissipates as sound, heat, and deformation. Can you un-crack the egg, lift the fragments back to your hand, and reabsorb the sound and heat into the egg to restore the original state? **No — and that is irreversibility in its purest form.**

> **Real-world analogy — A spilled glass of water on a tilted table:**
> The water flows downhill (from high to low gravitational potential) and spreads across the floor. To "undo" this, you would have to evaporate every droplet from the floor, condense the vapor, and pour the water back into the glass — all without adding any net energy to the universe. This is impossible without external work, because the *spontaneous* direction is one-way.

> [!TIP] **Plain-English Heuristic for Exams**
> If a process involves **friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing of different substances, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference, or chemical reaction**, it is **irreversible**. Reversibility is an *idealization*; *all real (natural) processes are irreversible*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Referenced

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_0 = 101.325 \; \text{kPa}$
- **Entropy Unit (SI):** $\text{J/K}$ (specific entropy: $\text{J/(kg·K)}$)
- **Stagnation (Reference) Temperature for Heat Transfer:** $T_0 = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ (KTU convention)
- **Degree of Irreversibility (I):** a non-negative scalar defined as $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logical Breakdown — Anatomy of an Irreversible Process

A thermodynamic process is rendered irreversible whenever one or more of the following "drivers of irreversibility" is present. Each driver produces an internally irreversible microscopic path that cannot be perfectly retraced.

1. **Unrestrained (Free) Expansion** — gas expanding into a vacuum chamber has no opposing pressure; the system passes through non-quasi-static intermediate states that are undefined in the $(P, v)$ plane.
2. **Friction & Viscous Dissipation** — mechanical energy degrades into internal energy; work input is greater than work output across any cycle.
3. **Heat Transfer Across a Finite Temperature Difference** — spontaneous heat flow from hot to cold, governed by the **Clausius statement** of the Second Law; an infinitesimal $\Delta T$ is required for *reversibility*.
4. **Mixing of Different Substances (Diffusion)** — once two gases mix, separating them requires the input of *work* (or infinite time via semipermeable membranes, idealized).
5. **Chemical Reactions & Phase Changes at Non-Equilibrium** — combustion, rusting, freezing at non-equilibrium pressure/temperature.
6. **Hysteresis & Plastic Deformation** — stress–strain loops that dissipate mechanical energy as heat.

> **The "Why" of Irreversibility (KTU High-Yield Insight):**
> Irreversibility is fundamentally a *microscopic-statistical* phenomenon. Although the underlying molecular dynamics (Newton's laws) are time-reversible, the *macroscopic* description coarse-grains over $\sim 10^{23}$ degrees of freedom. The probability of the system spontaneously returning to its initial microstate is effectively **zero** — this is the **Boltzmann / Statistical Second Law**.

### 2.2 Two Sub-Classes: Internal vs External Irreversibility

KTU examiners specifically test this distinction. Memorize it:

| Class | Location of Cause | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Internally Irreversible** | *Within* the system boundaries | Friction inside a piston–cylinder, viscous flow in a pipe, chemical reaction, mixing |
| **Externally Irreversible** | *Outside* the system boundaries (in surroundings) | Heat transfer across $\Delta T \neq 0$ between system and reservoir, shaft work with bearing friction outside the system |
| **Totally Irreversible** | Both internal and external | A real engine with friction *and* a finite $\Delta T$ across its cylinder walls |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master this table — it covers ~70% of Module 3 derivation questions.**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta S_{system} \geq \int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}}$ | **Clausius Inequality** (2nd Law, KTU form) | $=$ reversible, $>$ irreversible |
| 2 | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | **Cyclic Clausius Inequality** | $=$ reversible cycle, $<$ irreversible cycle |
| 3 | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k}$ | Entropy generation of universe | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ always |
| 4 | $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | **Degree of Irreversibility / Lost Work** | $I \geq 0$ in real processes |
| 5 | $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | **Lost Work** (Bejan) | $W_{lost} = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ |
| 6 | $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$ | Differential Clausius | Equality only on reversible path |
| 7 | $\Delta S_{iso} \geq 0$ | Isolated-system entropy principle | $>$ irreversible, $=$ reversible |
| 8 | $W_{rev} = W_{actual} + W_{lost}$ | Useful-work identity | Used in KTU part (b) problems |

> **Note on notation:** In the table, $T_{boundary}$ denotes the temperature *at the system boundary where heat crosses*, not necessarily the system bulk temperature. This distinction is *essential* for irreversible heat-transfer problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IC Engines:** The irreversibility between combustion flame ($\sim 2500 \; \text{K}$) and cylinder wall ($\sim 500 \; \text{K}$) is the single largest source of lost work; engineers reduce it via **regenerative heat exchangers** and **low-heat-rejection coatings**.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Lost work in compressor valves and condenser tubes is quantified via $W_{lost} = T_0 S_{gen}$ to compute the **Second-Law Efficiency (Exergetic Efficiency)** $\eta_{II} = W_{actual}/W_{rev}$.
- **Power Plants (Rankine, Brayton):** Irreversibility analysis pinpoints where the *first-law efficiency* hides losses that the *second-law* reveals — crucial for plant optimization.
- **Chemical & Process Industries:** Pinch Analysis uses $\Delta T_{min}$ between hot/cold streams precisely to **minimize $S_{gen}$**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Clausius Inequality from the Kelvin–Planck Statement

This is a **core KTU derivation** worth up to 7 marks.

**Step 1 — Setup.** Consider a system executing a *cyclic* process by exchanging heat $Q_i$ with reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$. Let a *reversible* engine $R$ operate between the same reservoirs to deliver the same heat as the irreversible cycle $I$ produces.

**Step 2 — Construct a combined cycle.** Run the irreversible engine $I$ in forward direction, then couple it with a reversible engine $R$ that returns the working fluid to its initial state. The combination is a cycle.

**Step 3 — Apply First Law (energy balance).** For the combined cycle,
$$
\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W \quad \text{(since } \Delta U_{cycle} = 0\text{)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Kelvin–Planck statement.** The combined cycle cannot deliver net positive work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir. Hence the net work of the combined cycle is $\leq 0$, which implies
$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0
$$

**Step 5 — Decompose into reversible + irreversible parts.** Any cycle can be split into a reversible path (the return) and an irreversible forward path. The reversible leg satisfies the equality, so the *irreversible* part alone satisfies the strict inequality:
$$
\oint_{irrev} \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0
$$

> **Why this matters (KTU board pattern):** Examiners often give the *Kelvin–Planck* statement and ask you to derive the *Clausius* inequality; the steps above are the standard expected answer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Entropy Generation for an Irreversible Adiabatic Process

**Given:** A gas in a piston–cylinder expands from state 1 $(P_1, V_1, T_1)$ to state 2 $(P_2, V_2, T_2)$ against a constant external pressure $P_{ext} < P_1$ (so the process is *unrestrained* and *irreversible*). The system is thermally insulated: $Q = 0$.

**Step 1 — First Law (closed system).**
$$
Q - W = \Delta U \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -W = \Delta U = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual (irreversible) work.**
$$
W_{actual} = \int_1^2 P_{ext}\, dV = P_{ext} (V_2 - V_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to obtain $T_2$ in terms of $P_{ext}, V_1, V_2$.**
$$
- P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1) = m c_v (T_2 - T_1)
$$

$$
T_2 = T_1 - \frac{P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1)}{m c_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta S_{system}$ (this is a state function, so it is path-independent).**
$$
\Delta S_{system} = m \left[ c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Delta S_{surroundings}$.** The surroundings (atmosphere) give heat $Q$ to the system; here $Q = 0$, so
$$
\Delta S_{surr} = -\frac{Q}{T_0} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Entropy generation of the universe.**
$$
S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surr} = m \left[ c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 7 — Sign check (KTU board expects this verification).** For the expansion described, $T_2 < T_1$ if $P_{ext}$ is small, but $v_2 \gg v_1$. The $R\ln(v_2/v_1)$ term dominates and $S_{gen} > 0$ ✓. This is the *direct proof* the process is irreversible.

> **Examiner's heuristic:** When the problem states the process is irreversible but does *not* specify the path, compute entropy generation as $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} - Q/T_{surr}$ using the *boundary* temperature for heat transfer. Always state the **sign of $S_{gen}$** explicitly — it is the litmus test for the answer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lost Work (Bejan's Identity)

**Statement (KTU Module 3 high-yield):** The difference between the maximum (reversible) work and the actual work obtained from a system operating between two reservoirs is called the *lost work*, and it equals $T_0 S_{gen}$.

**Step 1 — Definition.**
$$
W_{lost} \equiv W_{rev} - W_{actual}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Second-Law expression for $W_{rev}$.** For a closed system delivering work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir at $T_0$:
$$
W_{rev} = \Delta U - T_0 \Delta S + P_0 \Delta V
$$

**Step 3 — Actual work from First Law.**
$$
W_{actual} = Q - \Delta U
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.** Setting $Q = T_0 \Delta S - T_0 S_{gen}$ (definition of $S_{gen}$ rearranged), we get
$$
W_{lost} = T_0 \, S_{gen}
$$

This identity is the **analytical workhorse of KTU Module 3 part (b) problems** involving two-reservoir devices, mixing tanks, and throttling valves.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Reversible vs Irreversible

| Property | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of return | System & surroundings both restored | At least one cannot be restored |
| Quasi-static? | Yes (infinitely slow) | Not necessarily |
| Driving potential | Infinitesimal (e.g., $dT \to 0$, $dP \to 0$) | Finite (e.g., $\Delta T$, $\Delta P$) |
| $\oint \delta Q / T$ | $= 0$ | $< 0$ |
| $\Delta S_{universe}$ | $= 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Work output (cycle) | Maximum | Less than maximum |
| Heat input for same work | Minimum | More than minimum |
| Reversed without external effect? | Yes | No |
| Friction present? | No (frictionless idealization) | Yes (in real processes) |
| Realizable? | Idealization only | All natural/engineering processes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Irreversible Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IRREVERSIBLE PROCESS"]:::root
    A --> B["INTERNALLY IRREVERSIBLE"]:::branch
    A --> C["EXTERNALLY IRREVERSIBLE"]:::branch
    A --> D["TOTALLY IRREVERSIBLE"]:::branch
    B --> B1["Friction and Viscous Dissipation"]
    B --> B2["Unrestrained Expansion"]
    B --> B3["Chemical Reaction"]
    B --> B4["Mixing of Different Substances"]
    C --> C1["Heat Transfer across Finite DeltaT"]
    C --> C2["Shaft Work with External Friction"]
    C --> C3["Electrical Resistance Heating in Surroundings"]
    D --> D1["Real Engine: Friction PLUS Finite DeltaT"]
    D --> D2["Real Compressor: Valve Losses PLUS Condenser DeltaT"]
    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1e45,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Irreversibility Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1["Identify Process Type"]:::stage --> I2["List Drivers of Irreversibility"]:::stage
    I2 --> I3["Apply First Law: Compute W_actual and Q"]:::stage
    I3 --> I4["Compute DeltaS_system via State Function"]:::stage
    I4 --> I5["Compute DeltaS_surroundings = minus Q over T0"]:::stage
    I5 --> I6["S_gen = DeltaS_sys + DeltaS_surr"]:::stage
    I6 --> I7["Sign Test: S_gen greater than 0 confirms Irreversibility"]:::stage
    I7 --> I8["Lost Work: W_lost = T0 times S_gen"]:::stage
    I8 --> I9["Second Law Efficiency: eta_II = W_actual over W_rev"]:::stage
    classDef stage fill:#d1fae5,color:#064e3b,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Reversible vs Irreversible Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reversible_RT ["REVERSIBLE HEAT TRANSFER"]
        R1["Reservoir at T plus dT"] --> R2["System at T"]
        R2 --> R1
    end
    subgraph Irreversible_RT ["IRREVERSIBLE HEAT TRANSFER"]
        J1["Hot Reservoir at T_h"] --> J2["System at T_c"]
        J2 --> J3["Cold Reservoir at T_c"]
        J2 -. "Finite DeltaT drives spontaneous flow" .-> J1
    end
    classDef revStyle fill:#bbf7d0,color:#14532d,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef irrevStyle fill:#fecaca,color:#7f1d1d,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Information-Energy Cascade (Microscopic Origin of Irreversibility)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Ordered Macroscopic Energy KEbulk and PEbulk"]:::mac
    M2["Disordered Molecular Motion Thermal Internal Energy"]:::mol
    M3["Statistical Mixing Higher Entropy Microstates"]:::stat
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 -. "Cannot spontaneously return to M1: 2nd Law".-> M1
    classDef mac fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mol fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stat fill:#ede9fe,color:#4c1d95,stroke:#6d28d9,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define an irreversible process. Give two examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A thermodynamic process is *irreversible* if the system and all its surroundings cannot be exactly restored to their respective initial states when the process is reversed.
>
> **Examples (½ mark each, any two):**
> 1. Unrestrained expansion of a gas into a vacuum.
> 2. Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference (e.g., hot coffee cooling in a room).
> 3. Friction between sliding surfaces.
> 4. Spontaneous mixing of two different gases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Clausius inequality. What is its physical significance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** For any cyclic process,
> $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$
> where the equality holds for a *reversible* cycle and the strict inequality holds for an *irreversible* cycle.
>
> **Significance (2 marks):** (i) It is a concise mathematical statement of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**; (ii) It enables the definition of the state property *entropy* via $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$; (iii) The strict inequality quantifies irreversibility through $S_{gen} > 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q1]**

(a) **Distinguish between reversible and irreversible processes with five points.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **Air at $200 \; \text{kPa}$, $300 \; \text{K}$ occupies a $0.5 \; \text{m}^3$ rigid, insulated tank. A valve opens and the air leaks into an evacuated identical tank until equilibrium is reached. Calculate the entropy change of the air and the entropy generated. Take $c_v = 0.718 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $R = 0.287 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$.** *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Distinction (7 marks — 1.4 marks per point, pick any 5)**

| S.No. | Reversible Process | Irreversible Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Can be reversed without leaving any net change in universe | Cannot be reversed without net change |
| 2 | Quasi-static (infinitely slow) | Generally non-quasi-static |
| 3 | No friction, no finite $\Delta T$ | Friction and/or finite $\Delta T$ present |
| 4 | $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$ | $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$ |
| 5 | Yields *maximum* work output | Yields *less* than maximum work |
| 6 | Idealization; never fully realizable | All real (natural) processes are irreversible |
| 7 | $\oint \delta Q / T = 0$ | $\oint \delta Q / T < 0$ |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass and initial state (1 mark).**
$$
m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 300} = 1.161 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Final state of the air in the combined volume (1 mark).** Rigid, insulated, no work, no heat. By First Law, $\Delta U = 0$, so for an ideal gas $T_2 = T_1 = 300 \; \text{K}$ (free expansion of ideal gas is isothermal internally).

The final volume is $V_2 = V_1 + V_1 = 1.0 \; \text{m}^3$ (two tanks, identical).

**Step 3 — Entropy change of the system (3 marks).** For an ideal gas with $T$ constant:
$$
\Delta S = m R \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 1.161 \times 0.287 \times \ln(2)
$$
$$
\Delta S = 0.3332 \times 0.6931 = 0.2309 \; \text{kJ/K}
$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of surroundings (1 mark).** Insulated system, $Q = 0$:
$$
\Delta S_{surr} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Entropy generated (1 mark).**
$$
S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.2309 \; \text{kJ/K} > 0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

The process is confirmed **irreversible**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q1 Alt]**

(a) **Derive the Clausius inequality from the Kelvin–Planck statement of the Second Law.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800 \; \text{K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_C = 300 \; \text{K}$. The actual thermal efficiency is $35\%$, while the Carnot (reversible) efficiency is $62.5\%$. Determine: (i) the heat supplied, $Q_H$, if the net work output is $W = 50 \; \text{kW}$; (ii) the rate of entropy generation; (iii) the lost work per second.** *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks — distribute as: setup 2 marks, construction 2 marks, final step 3 marks)**

*Step 1 — Statement of Kelvin–Planck (1 mark):* It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces net work while exchanging heat with a *single* thermal reservoir.

*Step 2 — Construct a combined cycle (2 marks):* Let a reversible engine $R$ and an irreversible engine $I$ operate between the same two reservoirs at $T_H$ and $T_C$. Couple them so that the irreversible engine drives the reversible engine in reverse. The combination is a cyclic device.

*Step 3 — Energy balance on the combined cycle (2 marks):*
$$
W_{net} = Q_{H,total} - Q_{C,total}
$$
where the subscripts refer to the *net* heat exchanges with the two reservoirs after coupling.

*Step 4 — Apply Kelvin–Planck (2 marks):* The combined cycle exchanges heat with two reservoirs; by K-P, if it produced net work, it would violate K-P unless at least one reservoir experiences zero net heat exchange. Detailed algebraic manipulation yields
$$
\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0
$$
with equality for the reversible sub-cycle and strict inequality for the irreversible sub-cycle.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Heat supplied (2 marks):**
$$
\eta_{actual} = \frac{W}{Q_H} \;\Rightarrow\; Q_H = \frac{W}{\eta_{actual}} = \frac{50}{0.35} = 142.86 \; \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Rate of entropy generation (3 marks):**
$$
Q_C = Q_H - W = 142.86 - 50 = 92.86 \; \text{kW}
$$
$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{92.86}{300} - \frac{142.86}{800}
$$
$$
\dot{S}_{gen} = 0.3095 - 0.1786 = 0.1310 \; \text{kW/K}
$$
Since $\dot{S}_{gen} > 0$ ✓, the cycle is confirmed irreversible.

**(iii) Lost work per second (2 marks):**
$$
\dot{W}_{lost} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{gen} = 300 \times 0.1310 = 39.29 \; \text{kW}
$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary temperature distinction:** In irreversible heat transfer, $T$ in $\delta Q/T$ is the *boundary* temperature where heat crosses, **not** the bulk reservoir temperature. Marks are lost when students blindly use reservoir temperatures.
> 2. **Confusing state-function vs path-function entropy:** $\Delta S$ is a state function (path-independent); the *generation* $S_{gen}$ depends on the actual path. Do not compute $S_{gen}$ using the reversible path.
> 3. **Sign errors in Clausius inequality:** It is $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$, **not** $\geq 0$. Reversible gives equality; irreversible gives strict $<$ — examiners deduct for confused direction.
> 4. **Lost work must be non-negative:** If you obtain $W_{lost} < 0$, you have violated the Second Law; recheck sign conventions.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Always convert to **watts** or **kW** before computing $S_{gen}$ rate; mixing $\text{kW}$ with $\text{K}$ without unit discipline is the most common 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

- **Definition:** Irreversible = system **and** surroundings cannot both be restored; *positive* entropy generation of the universe is the *defining* signature.
- **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$. Equality = reversible; strict $<$ = irreversible.
- **Entropy Generation:** $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} - \sum Q_k/T_{k,boundary} \geq 0$.
- **Lost Work (Bejan):** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = W_{rev} - W_{actual} \geq 0$.
- **Two classes:** *Internal* (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing) vs *External* (heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$); *Total* = both.
- **Real processes** are *always* irreversible; reversibility is an *idealization* used as a *benchmark* for maximum performance.
- **Carnot cycle** is the *only* cycle operating between two reservoirs that is reversible.
- **Free expansion** of an ideal gas: $Q = 0$, $W = 0$, $\Delta U = 0 \Rightarrow T_2 = T_1$, but $\Delta S_{sys} > 0$, so $S_{gen} > 0$ → irreversible.
- **Mixing of ideal gases** (initially at same $T$ and $P$): $\Delta S_{mix} = -R \sum y_i \ln y_i > 0$ → irreversible.
- **Throttling** (isenthalpic): $h_1 = h_2$, but for an ideal gas $T_1 = T_2$, and $\Delta S = 0$ only if ideal gas *and* no temperature change; real throttling of a non-ideal gas is irreversible with $S_{gen} > 0$.
- **Remember the Second-Law Efficiency** for use in 14-mark problems: $\eta_{II} = \eta_{actual} / \eta_{rev}$ (for work-producing devices).
- **Always end an irreversibility problem with the sign check** $S_{gen} \geq 0$ — examiners reward the explicit verification.
- **Boundary temperature vs bulk temperature** — distinguish carefully in heat-transfer problems.
- **Memorize the "5 drivers of irreversibility":** friction, finite $\Delta T$, unrestrained expansion, mixing, chemical reaction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes of Irreversibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Causes of Irreversibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Irreversibility** is the inability of a system and its surroundings to be returned to their respective initial states after a process has occurred. A process that *cannot* be reversed to restore the original state without leaving changes in the universe is called an **irreversible process**.

Mathematically, irreversibility is quantified by **entropy generation** $(S_{gen})$:

$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \ge 0$$

The equality $S_{gen} = 0$ holds only for an **internally reversible** process, while $S_{gen} > 0$ confirms an **irreversible** process. The SI unit is **J/K** and it is an **extensive property** of the universe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversible Process:** A hypothetical, idealized process that can be retraced exactly in the opposite direction such that both the system and surroundings return to their original states, with no net change anywhere in the universe.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you drop a **raw egg** on a hard floor. The yolk splatters, shell cracks, and energy dissipates as heat and sound. You can never collect the broken pieces, the heat, and the sound back together to form the original egg. This is the **one-way nature of irreversibility**.

In contrast, imagine a **ball rolling on a frictionless surface**. If it is given a tiny push in the opposite direction, the ball will retrace its path perfectly. That is *reversible*.

**Engineering analogy for KTU students:** When a car brakes, the kinetic energy of the car is converted into heat in the brake pads (friction). The brake pads become hot but you can never extract that heat back into motion without spending *more* energy. This frictional dissipation is a **classic engineering cause of irreversibility**.

---

## 1.3 The Two Foundations of Irreversibility

Any actual (real) process is irreversible because of one or both of the following:

| Foundation | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **External Irreversibility** | Caused by interactions between the system and surroundings (e.g., heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$) |
| **Internal Irreversibility** | Caused by phenomena *within* the system (e.g., friction, unrestrained expansion, chemical reaction) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always identify *where* the irreversibility occurs (system-side or surroundings-side). External irreversibilities can sometimes be eliminated by better insulation; internal ones require fundamental redesign of the process equipment.

---

## 1.4 Why Studying Irreversibility Matters in Engineering

Engineers do not design *reversible* processes (they are ideals), but they use the **reversible limit** as a benchmark. The closer a real process gets to a reversible one, the **higher the efficiency** and the **lower the entropy generation**. Understanding causes of irreversibility helps us:

1. Maximize thermal efficiency of heat engines (Carnot benchmark).
2. Minimize compressor work in refrigerators and air-conditioners.
3. Calculate the **exergy (available energy) destroyed** in industrial processes, as $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$, where $T_0$ is the **dead-state temperature** of **298.15 K**.

---

## 1.5 Common Misconceptions Cleared

> [!WARNING]
> **A process being quasi-equilibrium is NOT the same as being reversible.** A quasi-equilibrium process involves infinitesimal gradients *within* the system, but if the heat transfer crosses a *finite* temperature difference, the process is still irreversible.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Major Causes of Irreversibility

A process becomes irreversible whenever any one of the following effects is present:

### (i) Friction
When two surfaces slide over each other, mechanical work is dissipated as heat (internal energy) at the interface. This heat cannot be fully converted back to work.

- **Real-world example:** Vehicle braking, drilling metal, bearings in rotating shafts.
- **Why irreversible:** Direction of energy conversion (work $\rightarrow$ heat) is *one-way* under the Second Law.

### (ii) Unrestrained (Free) Expansion
A gas expands into a vacuum or a lower-pressure region without doing any useful work on a piston or boundary.

- **Real-world example:** Opening a valve between a charged and an evacuated cylinder.
- **Why irreversible:** System passes through non-equilibrium states with no defined pressure or temperature inside.

### (iii) Heat Transfer Across a Finite Temperature Difference
Whenever heat flows from a body at $T_H$ to a body at $T_L$ (with $T_H > T_L$), entropy is generated.

- **Real-world example:** Heat passing from hot combustion gases to cold boiler water in a steam plant.
- **Why irreversible:** A finite temperature difference is a *driving potential* that cannot be spontaneously reversed by the surroundings.

### (iv) Mixing of Two Different Substances (or Gases)
When two distinguishable gases (or liquids) of different composition or temperature are allowed to mix, they never spontaneously separate.

- **Real-world example:** Perfume diffusing across a room; cream mixing into coffee.
- **Why irreversible:** The mixture is a more probable (higher-entropy) state; reverse would require *work input*.

### (v) Chemical Reaction
Most chemical reactions proceed spontaneously in one direction (e.g., combustion of methane), generating entropy and heat. The reverse reaction is only possible with substantial external energy (e.g., electrolysis of water).

- **Real-world example:** Combustion in a furnace, rusting of iron.
- **Why irreversible:** Spontaneous direction follows the Gibbs free energy decrease, $\Delta G < 0$.

### (vi) Electrical Resistance (Joule Heating)
When current passes through a resistor, electrical energy is irreversibly converted to heat ($Q = I^2 R t$).

- **Real-world example:** Heating element in a geyser, transmission line losses.
- **Why irreversible:** Heat produced at the resistor cannot be fully converted back into electrical work.

### (vii) Inelastic Deformation / Plasticity
Plastic deformation of solids (e.g., bending a metal rod) dissipates mechanical work as heat at the molecular level.

- **Real-world example:** Metal forging, car crash crumple zones.
- **Why irreversible:** Internal lattice rearrangements are statistically irreversible.

### (viii) Hysteresis Effects
Magnetization, dielectric polarization, and elastic deformation all display hysteresis loops, where the energy input is greater than recoverable energy output.

- **Real-world example:** Transformer core losses, rubber band stretching.
- **Why irreversible:** Energy trapped in microscopic domain realignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU exams:** Whenever you see the words "free expansion," "throttling," "sudden," "with friction," "across a finite $\Delta T$," or "mixing" — the process is **definitely irreversible**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Cause of Irreversibility | Entropy Generation Expression | Key Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$ | $S_{gen} = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right)$ | $T_H > T_L$, $Q > 0$ from hot to cold |
| 2 | Unrestrained expansion of ideal gas | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{universe} = nR \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right)$ | $W = 0$, adiabatic |
| 3 | Friction (work dissipation) | $S_{gen} = \dfrac{W_{friction}}{T_{boundary}}$ | $T_{boundary}$ = sink temperature |
| 4 | Mixing of two ideal gases | $S_{gen} = -nR \sum_i y_i \ln y_i$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction of gas $i$ |
| 5 | Electrical resistance heating | $S_{gen} = \dfrac{I^2 R t}{T_{resistor}}$ | Steady current flow |
| 6 | Polytropic expansion with friction | $S_{gen} = \dfrac{W_{lost}}{T_{surroundings}}$ | $W_{lost} = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$ |
| 7 | Throttling (Joule-Thomson) | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} > 0$ | $h_1 = h_2$ |
| 8 | Heat addition at variable $T$ | $S_{gen} = \int \delta Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_{sink}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{source}} \right)$ | General heat interaction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Rule:** For *any* process, $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{universe} \ge 0$. This is the **only** directionality test you need for a Second Law problem.

---

## 2.3 Clausius Inequality (The Master Theorem)

The Clausius inequality, foundational to all entropy analysis, is:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \le 0$$

- Equality $\Rightarrow$ **Reversible** cycle.
- Strict inequality $\Rightarrow$ **Irreversible** cycle.

For a *cycle* operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$, with heat $Q_H$ absorbed and $Q_L$ rejected, the Clausius inequality reduces to:

$$\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} \le 0$$

The quantity $\dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} - \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} \ge 0$ is the entropy generated per cycle.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Power Plant Design** | Minimizing $S_{gen}$ between flue gas and steam maximizes Rankine cycle efficiency. |
| **Refrigeration** | Reducing compressor work by minimizing throttling losses (using expander instead of expansion valve). |
| **Chemical Process Industry** | Distillation column design to minimize mixing irreversibility. |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Reducing frictional and heat-transfer losses to push Otto/Diesel cycles closer to ideal efficiency. |
| **Cryogenics** | Minimizing heat leak irreversibility into low-temperature reservoirs. |

---

## 2.5 Block Diagram of an Irreversible Process

```
            ┌──────────────────┐
   Q_in ───▶│   SYSTEM         │─────▶ W_out
   (T_H)    │  (irreversible   │     (actual)
            │   with S_gen)    │
            └──────────────────┘
                  │   │
                  │   └────▶ Q_out (T_L)
                  ▼
            S_gen > 0
```
The block shows the entropy generated *internally* in the system plus the entropy carried in/out with heat transfers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Entropy Generation in Heat Transfer Across Finite $\Delta T$

**Statement:** Show that heat $Q$ transferred from a hot body at $T_H$ to a cold body at $T_L$ (with $T_H > T_L$) generates entropy equal to $Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the two reservoirs:**
- Hot reservoir at constant temperature $T_H$, losing heat $Q$.
- Cold reservoir at constant temperature $T_L$, gaining heat $Q$.

**Step 2 — Entropy change of hot reservoir:**
Because the hot reservoir loses heat, the entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_{hot} = \dfrac{-Q}{T_H}$$

**Step 3 — Entropy change of cold reservoir:**
Because the cold reservoir gains heat, the entropy change is:

$$\Delta S_{cold} = \dfrac{+Q}{T_L}$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of universe (system is the two reservoirs):**
The total entropy change is the sum:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{hot} + \Delta S_{cold}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = -\dfrac{Q}{T_H} + \dfrac{Q}{T_L}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Second Law:**

$$Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right) \ge 0$$

Since $T_L < T_H$, the bracket is positive, and $Q > 0$, confirming $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$, which is **irreversible**.

$$\boxed{S_{gen} = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right) > 0 \quad \text{(irreversible heat transfer)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Entropy Generation in Unrestrained Expansion of an Ideal Gas

**Statement:** Prove that an ideal gas expanding from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$ into a vacuum (adiabatic, $Q = 0$, $W = 0$) is irreversible and find $S_{gen}$.

**Step 1 — System definition:**
An ideal gas (e.g., air, $n$ moles, $C_v$ constant) is separated from an evacuated chamber by a rupture disk. The disk is broken; gas fills the entire volume $V_2 = V_1 + V_{vacuum}$.

**Step 2 — Energy balance (First Law):**
For an adiabatic process with no work done (expansion into vacuum, no piston):

$$Q = 0, \quad W = 0$$

By the First Law, $\Delta U = Q - W = 0$, so the internal energy is constant.

**Step 3 — For an ideal gas, $U = U(T)$:**
Since $\Delta U = 0$ and $U$ depends only on $T$, the temperature is constant:

$$T_2 = T_1 = T$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of the gas (system):**
For an ideal gas at constant $T$:

$$\Delta S_{system} = nR \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Entropy change of surroundings:**
No heat is transferred ($Q = 0$), so:

$$\Delta S_{surroundings} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Total entropy change:**

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings}$$

$$\Delta S_{universe} = nR \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
Since $V_2 > V_1$ (gas expanded), $\ln(V_2/V_1) > 0$, so:

$$\boxed{S_{gen} = nR \ln \left( \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \right) > 0}$$

This process is **irreversible** because $S_{gen} > 0$, even though $\Delta U = 0$ and $Q = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive insight:** The gas has the *same* final temperature and *same* final energy as a reversible isothermal expansion that *did* work. But the gas is unable to do that work back on the surroundings spontaneously. This is the **essence of free expansion irreversibility**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Entropy of Mixing for Two Ideal Gases

**Statement:** Two ideal gases $A$ and $B$, initially at the same $T$ and $P$, occupy separate volumes $V_A$ and $V_B$. On removing the partition, find the entropy of mixing.

**Step 1 — Initial state:**
- Gas $A$: $n_A$ moles at $T$, $P$, in volume $V_A$.
- Gas $B$: $n_B$ moles at $T$, $P$, in volume $V_B$.

**Step 2 — Final state after mixing:**
- Total moles: $n = n_A + n_B$.
- Total volume: $V = V_A + V_B$.
- Each gas now occupies the *entire* volume $V$ at partial pressure $p_i = y_i P$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction.

**Step 3 — Entropy change for each gas (treating as ideal gas expansion at constant $T$):**
For gas $A$ expanding from $V_A$ to $V$ at constant $T$:

$$\Delta S_A = n_A R \ln \left( \dfrac{V}{V_A} \right) = n_A R \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{y_A} \right)$$

Similarly for gas $B$:

$$\Delta S_B = n_B R \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{y_B} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Total entropy of mixing:**

$$\Delta S_{mix} = \Delta S_A + \Delta S_B = n_A R \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{y_A} \right) + n_B R \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{y_B} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of total moles and mole fractions:**

$$\Delta S_{mix} = -n_A R \ln y_A - n_B R \ln y_B$$

Generalizing to $k$ gases:

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{mix} = -n R \sum_{i=1}^{k} y_i \ln y_i \ge 0}$$

Since $0 < y_i < 1$, each $\ln y_i < 0$, so the negative sign makes $\Delta S_{mix} > 0$. The process is **irreversible**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Clausius Inequality from Entropy Principle

**Statement:** Derive the Clausius inequality $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ using the entropy principle.

**Step 1 — Consider a cycle divided into infinitesimal steps:**
For any small step of the cycle, the entropy change of the *system* is, by Clausius equality (reversible reference):

$$dS_{system} = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T_{boundary}}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy balance over the small step:**

$$dS_{system} = \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} + dS_{gen}$$

Rearranging:

$$dS_{gen} = dS_{system} - \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \ge 0$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the entire cycle:**
The system returns to its initial state, so $\oint dS_{system} = 0$:

$$\oint dS_{gen} = -\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \ge 0$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $-1$:**

$$\boxed{\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \le 0}$$

This is the **Clausius inequality**. Equality holds iff $dS_{gen} = 0$ throughout (reversible cycle).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Identification of Irreversibility

| Process Statement | Cause of Irreversibility | $S_{gen}$ Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| Gas flows through a pipe with friction | Friction, viscous dissipation | $S_{gen} > 0$, $\Delta h < v \, dp$ |
| Heat transferred from $500^\circ C$ gases to $200^\circ C$ steam | Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$ | $S_{gen} = Q \left( \frac{1}{473} - \frac{1}{773} \right)$ |
| Sudden opening of a valve into vacuum | Unrestrained expansion | $S_{gen} = nR \ln(V_2/V_1)$ |
| Throttling of refrigerant in expansion valve | Throttling / unresisted flow | $\Delta h = 0$, $\Delta s > 0$ |
| Stirring of viscous oil in a tank | Friction | $W_{in} \rightarrow$ heat, $S_{gen} = W_{stirrer}/T_0$ |
| Spontaneous mixing of $N_2$ and $O_2$ | Mixing of distinct gases | $S_{gen} = -nR \sum y_i \ln y_i$ |
| Current through resistor | Electrical resistance | $S_{gen} = I^2 R t / T$ |
| Combustion of methane | Chemical reaction | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{products} - \Delta S_{reactants} + Q/T$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Irreversibility Causes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IRREVERSIBILITY] --> B[External Irreversibility]
    A --> C[Internal Irreversibility]
    B --> B1[Heat transfer across finite temperature difference]
    B --> B2[Mass transfer across finite pressure or concentration difference]
    C --> C1[Friction in mechanical systems]
    C --> C2[Unrestrained expansion of fluid]
    C --> C3[Mixing of two distinct substances]
    C --> C4[Chemical reaction proceeding spontaneously]
    C --> C5[Electrical resistance heating]
    C --> C6[Inelastic deformation and hysteresis]
    C1 --> C1a[Sliding contact surfaces]
    C1 --> C1b[Fluid viscosity in pipes]
    C2 --> C2a[Free expansion into vacuum]
    C2 --> C2b[Sudden pressure drop across valve]
    C3 --> C3a[Mixing of different gases]
    C3 --> C3b[Mixing of fluids of different composition]
    C4 --> C4a[Combustion in furnace]
    C4 --> C4b[Rusting of metals]
    C5 --> C5a[Resistor in circuit]
    C5 --> C5b[Transmission line losses]
    C6 --> C6a[Plastic bending of metal]
    C6 --> C6b[Magnetic hysteresis in core]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Irreversible Heat Engine vs Reversible Benchmark

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "REVERSIBLE ENGINE Carnot Benchmark"
        R1[Q_in from T_H] --> R2[REVERSIBLE SYSTEM]
        R2 --> R3[Q_out to T_L]
        R2 --> R4[W_max reversible]
    end
    subgraph "ACTUAL IRREVERSIBLE ENGINE"
        I1[Q_in from T_H] --> I2[ACTUAL SYSTEM S_gen positive]
        I2 --> I3[Q_out to T_L greater than ideal]
        I2 --> I5[W_actual less than W_max]
    end
    I2 -. S_gen greater than 0 .-> I2
    I2 -. Irreversibility I equals T_0 times S_gen .-> LOSS[EXERGY DESTROYED]
```

---

## 4.3 Entropy Balance Control Volume (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ENT_IN[Entropy flow in with mass and heat m_in times s_in plus Q_in over T] --> CV[CONTROL VOLUME WITH S_gen]
    ENT_OUT[Entropy flow out with mass and heat m_out times s_out plus Q_out over T] --> CV
    CV --> GEN[S_gen greater than 0 indicates irreversibility]
    CV --> USED[Entropy used to drive useful work output]
    note1[For reversible process S_gen equals 0] -.-> CV
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block: Reversible vs Irreversible Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Type Selection] --> B[Reversible Process]
    A --> C[Irreversible Process]
    B --> B1[No friction or dissipation]
    B --> B2[Quasi equilibrium]
    B --> B3[Heat transfer with infinitesimal dT]
    B --> B4[S_gen equals 0]
    B --> B5[Returns both system and surroundings to original state]
    C --> C1[Friction and unrestrained expansion present]
    C --> C2[Heat transfer across finite dT]
    C --> C3[Mixing of substances]
    C --> C4[S_gen greater than 0]
    C --> C5[Cannot restore both system and surroundings to original state]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Q: Define irreversibility. List any four major causes of irreversibility encountered in engineering systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Irreversibility is the inability of a system and its surroundings to be restored to their original states after a process has occurred. A process accompanied by entropy generation $S_{gen} > 0$ is irreversible.

**Four major causes (1 mark):**
1. Friction in mechanical systems or fluid flow.
2. Heat transfer across a finite temperature difference.
3. Unrestrained (free) expansion of a fluid.
4. Mixing of two different substances.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Q: Explain with a suitable example why heat transfer through a finite temperature difference is an irreversible process.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> When heat $Q$ flows from a hot body at $T_H$ to a cold body at $T_L$ ($T_H > T_L$), the entropy change of the universe is $S_{gen} = Q \left( \frac{1}{T_L} - \frac{1}{T_H} \right) > 0$.

**Example:** In a boiler, hot flue gases at $1200 \, \text{K}$ transfer heat to water/steam at $500 \, \text{K}$. The temperature difference of $700 \, \text{K}$ generates entropy. To reverse the process, we would have to *pump* heat from cold water back to hot gases, which requires external work (refrigeration), and this cannot happen spontaneously. Hence, the process is **irreversible**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *list* causes without explaining *why* they are irreversible. Each cause MUST be tied to either $S_{gen} > 0$, a finite driving potential, or non-equilibrium effects. Marks are awarded for the **physical reasoning**, not the bullet point. Also remember to convert all temperatures to **Kelvin** before substitution — this is the single most common numerical error in entropy problems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the various causes of irreversibility. For each cause, state the physical reason and provide one real-world engineering example.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Introduction (1 mark):** A process is irreversible if it cannot be reversed without leaving permanent changes in the universe. The universal criterion is $S_{gen,universe} \ge 0$.

**Cause 1 — Friction (1 mark):**
Whenever two surfaces in contact slide past each other, the work done against friction is converted into internal energy (heat) at the interface. Heat cannot be fully converted back to work (Second Law). Example: Vehicle disc brakes convert kinetic energy to heat; bearings in turbines dissipate work as heat.

**Cause 2 — Unrestrained Expansion (1 mark):**
When a gas expands into a vacuum or a much lower-pressure region, it does no useful boundary work. The gas passes through non-equilibrium states. Example: Opening a valve from a high-pressure air tank into an evacuated chamber; gas in a ruptured pipe discharging to atmosphere.

**Cause 3 — Heat Transfer Across Finite $\Delta T$ (1 mark):**
Heat conduction/convection/radiation from a hot to a cold body across a finite temperature gradient is driven by the gradient. Spontaneous reversal would violate the Second Law. Example: Combustion gases at $1500 \, \text{K}$ heating boiler tubes with water at $500 \, \text{K}$.

**Cause 4 — Mixing of Different Substances (1 mark):**
Two distinct gases or fluids, when mixed, reach a state of higher entropy. Spontaneous separation is impossible without external work. Example: $N_2$ and $O_2$ mixing after a partition is removed; diesel fuel mixing with air in a combustion chamber before ignition.

**Cause 5 — Chemical Reactions (0.5 mark):**
Spontaneous reactions (combustion, corrosion) proceed in the direction of decreasing Gibbs free energy. The reverse requires substantial energy input. Example: Combustion of methane in a furnace, rusting of iron.

**Cause 6 — Electrical Resistance (0.5 mark):**
Current through a resistor dissipates electrical energy as Joule heat ($I^2 R t$), which cannot be fully recovered. Example: Transmission line losses, heating elements.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Real engineering processes always exhibit one or more of the above. Designers aim to reduce $S_{gen}$ to maximize the useful work output (exergy).

---

**(b) A hot metal block at $200^\circ \text{C}$ is cooled in a room where the surroundings are at $27^\circ \text{C}$. If $100 \, \text{kJ}$ of heat is transferred from the block to the air, calculate the entropy generated during the process.** *(7 Marks)*

**Given Data:**
- Hot body (metal block) temperature: $T_H = 200^\circ \text{C} = 473.15 \, \text{K}$
- Cold body (room air) temperature: $T_L = 27^\circ \text{C} = 300.15 \, \text{K}$
- Heat transferred: $Q = 100 \, \text{kJ} = 100{,}000 \, \text{J}$

**Step 1 — Write entropy generation expression for heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$:**

$$S_{gen} = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$S_{gen} = 100{,}000 \left( \dfrac{1}{300.15} - \dfrac{1}{473.15} \right) \, \text{J/K}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracketed term:**

$$\dfrac{1}{300.15} = 3.3317 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{473.15} = 2.1135 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{300.15} - \dfrac{1}{473.15} = 1.2182 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$S_{gen} = 100{,}000 \times 1.2182 \times 10^{-3} = 121.82 \, \text{J/K}$$

$$\boxed{S_{gen} \approx 121.8 \, \text{J/K} > 0}$$

Since $S_{gen} > 0$, the process is **irreversible**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Define the terms: reversible process, irreversible process, and entropy generation. Discuss why the following processes are irreversible:** *(7 Marks)*
- (i) Friction between two solid surfaces
- (ii) Free expansion of a gas
- (iii) Mixing of two different gases

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversible Process (1 mark):** A process that, after occurring, can be reversed without leaving any net change in the system or surroundings. Both forward and reverse paths follow the same equilibrium states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Irreversible Process (1 mark):** A process that cannot be reversed without permanent changes in the universe. Characterized by $S_{gen} > 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Entropy Generation (0.5 marks):** $S_{gen}$ is the entropy created within a system and its surroundings due to irreversibilities. $S_{gen} \ge 0$ always.

**(i) Friction (1.5 marks):** When two surfaces slide, work is dissipated as heat at the interface. The mechanical work $W_{mech}$ is converted to thermal energy $Q$, and $W_{mech} \to Q$ is a one-way energy conversion per Kelvin-Planck and Clausius statements. Example: brake pads in a vehicle; the heat at the pad cannot reconvert to motion without external energy input.

**(ii) Free Expansion (1.5 marks):** Consider an ideal gas expanding into a vacuum. Initial state $(T, V_1)$ → final state $(T, V_2)$. By First Law, $Q = 0$, $W = 0$, so $\Delta U = 0$, hence $T_2 = T_1$. But entropy change $\Delta S = nR \ln(V_2/V_1) > 0$. Since $S_{gen} = \Delta S > 0$, the process is irreversible. Physically, gas passes through non-uniform, non-equilibrium states, which are undefined by classical thermodynamics.

**(iii) Mixing of Gases (1.5 marks):** For two distinct ideal gases $A$ and $B$ initially separated, on removal of the partition each gas expands into the other's volume. The entropy of mixing is $\Delta S_{mix} = -nR \sum y_i \ln y_i > 0$. Spontaneous unmixing would require work input and never occurs naturally — proof of irreversibility. Example: perfume particles diffusing through still air.

---

**(b) An adiabatic rigid container is divided into two compartments by a thin partition. Compartment 1 contains $2 \, \text{mol}$ of $N_2$ at $300 \, \text{K}$ and $5 \, \text{bar}$ occupying $1 \, \text{m}^3$. Compartment 2 contains $1 \, \text{mol}$ of $He$ at $300 \, \text{K}$ and $5 \, \text{bar}$ occupying $0.5 \, \text{m}^3$. The partition is removed, and the gases mix. Calculate the entropy of mixing.** *(7 Marks)*

**Given Data:**
- $n_{N_2} = 2 \, \text{mol}$, $n_{He} = 1 \, \text{mol}$
- Total moles: $n = 3 \, \text{mol}$
- Temperature: $T = 300 \, \text{K}$ (isothermal, since both compartments at same $T$)
- Initial volume ratio: $V_1 = 1 \, \text{m}^3$, $V_2 = 0.5 \, \text{m}^3$, Total $V = 1.5 \, \text{m}^3$

**Step 1 — Calculate mole fractions:**

$$y_{N_2} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.6667, \quad y_{He} = \dfrac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$

**Step 2 — Apply the entropy-of-mixing formula for ideal gases:**

$$\Delta S_{mix} = -n R \sum_{i} y_i \ln y_i$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$y_{N_2} \ln y_{N_2} = 0.6667 \times \ln(0.6667) = 0.6667 \times (-0.4055) = -0.2703$$

$$y_{He} \ln y_{He} = 0.3333 \times \ln(0.3333) = 0.3333 \times (-1.0986) = -0.3662$$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms:**

$$\sum y_i \ln y_i = -0.2703 + (-0.3662) = -0.6365$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\Delta S_{mix}$:**

$$\Delta S_{mix} = -(3 \, \text{mol}) \times (8.314 \, \text{J/mol}\cdot\text{K}) \times (-0.6365)$$

$$\Delta S_{mix} = 3 \times 8.314 \times 0.6365 \, \text{J/K}$$

**Step 6 — Final evaluation:**

$$\Delta S_{mix} = 15.876 \, \text{J/K}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{mix} \approx 15.88 \, \text{J/K} > 0}$$

Since $T_1 = T_2$ and the container is adiabatic, $\Delta S_{surroundings} = 0$, so $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{mix} > 0$. **Mixing is irreversible**. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the negative sign and write $\Delta S_{mix} = nR \sum y_i \ln y_i$, getting a *negative* answer. Always remember the **minus sign** in front — that's what guarantees the entropy increase. Marks deducted: up to **2 out of 7**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Definition:** Irreversibility = inability to restore system AND surroundings to original state. Quantified by $S_{gen} > 0$.

- **Eight Major Causes:** (1) Friction, (2) Unrestrained expansion, (3) Heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$, (4) Mixing of different substances, (5) Chemical reaction, (6) Electrical resistance (Joule heating), (7) Inelastic deformation/plasticity, (8) Hysteresis.

- **Master Formula:** $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \ge 0$. Equality only for reversible.

- **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \le 0$. Equality reversible; strict inequality irreversible.

- **Heat-transfer irreversibility:** $S_{gen} = Q \left( \dfrac{1}{T_L} - \dfrac{1}{T_H} \right)$. **Always convert to Kelvin** before substitution.

- **Unrestrained expansion:** $S_{gen} = nR \ln(V_2/V_1)$, with $\Delta T = 0$ and $W = 0$.

- **Mixing of ideal gases:** $\Delta S_{mix} = -nR \sum_i y_i \ln y_i$, **negative sign in front is essential**.

- **Friction work dissipation:** $S_{gen} = W_{friction} / T_{boundary}$.

- **Two categories:** *External* (system-surroundings interaction) vs *Internal* (within system).

- **Sign convention:** $Q > 0$ when entering the system, $W > 0$ when done by the system.

- **First Law is silent on direction:** A friction-dissipated process satisfies $\Delta U = Q - W$, but Second Law tells you the *direction* of energy degradation (work $\to$ heat).

- **Reversible limit is unattainable:** It serves as a theoretical upper bound; real processes are always irreversible, but designers *approach* it.

- **Exergy destroyed:** $I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$, where $T_0$ is the **dead-state temperature** (typically **298.15 K** or **300 K** in KTU problems).

- **Engineering mantra:** Minimizing $S_{gen}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ maximizing the useful work output $\Leftrightarrow$ approaching the Carnot efficiency.

- **Quick identification triggers in KTU problems:** Words like "sudden," "throttling," "free," "with friction," "across finite $\Delta T$," "mixing," "combustion," "adiabatic rigid container with partition removed" → all are **red flags for irreversibility**.

> [!TIP]
> **End-of-Module Mantra:** "Reversible processes are ideals. Real processes are irreversible. The closer we engineer to reversible, the more efficient the system. Entropy generation is the price we pay for real-world operation."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMM2
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PMM2

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PMM2 (Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind):** A hypothetical, fictitious device that operates in a cycle and converts heat extracted from a **single thermal reservoir** entirely into an equivalent amount of **net work output**, without any heat rejection to a lower-temperature sink.

Mathematically, a PMM2 violates the **Kelvin–Planck statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics:

$$Q_H = W_{net} \quad \text{with} \quad Q_L = 0$$

For a PMM2 operating between a single reservoir at temperature $T_H$ and producing work $W$:

$$W = Q_H = \oint T \, dS_{surr}$$

$$W = T_H \cdot (S_{gen,universe})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A PMM2 is *impossible* because it would require the entropy of the universe to **decrease**, which is forbidden by the Second Law ($\Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy – The "Magic Ocean Steamship":**
Imagine a ship that sails across the ocean by continuously drawing heat from the seawater itself (the single reservoir), converting that heat entirely into mechanical work (propulsion), and exhausting **nothing** back to the ocean. The ocean would slowly cool, the ship would keep moving forever, and no pollution, no condenser discharge, and no second reservoir would be needed.

| Feature | Magic Ship (PMM2) | Real Ship |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Ocean only | Fuel + Ocean |
| Heat rejected? | **No** | Yes (to ocean/sky) |
| Fuel cost | Zero | Continuous |
| Reality | **Impossible** | Possible |

**Plain-English Explanation:**
- A PMM2 does **not** violate energy conservation (1st Law is satisfied).
- It violates the **direction** of natural processes (2nd Law).
- It is "perfect" in energy terms but **impossible** by nature of entropy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from PMM1:**
> - **PMM1** → Violates the **1st Law** ($W > Q$, creating energy from nothing).
> - **PMM2** → Obeys the **1st Law** but violates the **2nd Law** (no heat sink required).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standards

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Absolute Zero:** $0 \, \text{K} = -273.15^\circ\text{C}$
- **Standard Reference Reservoir (KTU convention):** $T_0 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (or **300 K** for simplified numericals)
- **Sign Convention (KTU standard):** $Q$ positive **into** system; $W$ positive **out of** system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kelvin–Planck Statement (Foundation of PMM2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kelvin–Planck Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a single reservoir and the production of an equivalent amount of work."*

This statement is the **direct prohibition** of PMM2.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown — Why PMM2 Fails

A PMM2 is described by a cyclic heat engine where:

1. **Step 1:** Receives heat $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at $T_H$.
2. **Step 2:** Converts the *entire* $Q_H$ into net work $W_{net}$.
3. **Step 3:** Rejects **zero** heat to any low-temperature sink ($Q_L = 0$).

**The thermodynamic absurdity unfolds in 4 logical steps:**

- **Step A — Energy Balance (1st Law OK):** The First Law is satisfied: $W = Q_H$ (energy is conserved).
- **Step B — Entropy Balance (2nd Law Fails):** Entropy change of reservoir $= -Q_H / T_H$ (reservoir loses entropy).
- **Step C — Universe Entropy Drops:** System is cyclic, so $\Delta S_{sys} = 0$. Therefore $\Delta S_{univ} = -Q_H / T_H < 0$.
- **Step D — Violation Confirmed:** Since $\Delta S_{univ} \geq 0$ is a strict inequality for a real process, PMM2 is impossible.

## 2.3 Equivalence with the Clausius Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements of the Second Law are **logically equivalent** — violating one automatically violates the other.

**Proof Sketch (KTU Hot Topic):**
- Assume a PMM2 exists.
- Couple it with a real refrigerator.
- The combined device **becomes a PMM1** (violates 1st Law).
- Since PMM1 is impossible, PMM2 must also be impossible.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| PMM2 work output | $W = Q_H$ | Full heat converted to work |
| Heat rejected by PMM2 | $Q_L = 0$ | No low-temp sink |
| Thermal efficiency of PMM2 | $\eta = \dfrac{W}{Q_H} = 1$ (or $100\%$) | Apparent "perfect" efficiency |
| Entropy change of universe (PMM2) | $\Delta S_{univ} = -\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} < 0$ | Violates 2nd Law |
| Clausius Inequality (cycle) | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Strict for real cycles |
| PMM2 Clausius check | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} > 0$ | Impossible — violates inequality |
| Real Carnot efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Upper bound, $< 1$ |
| Availability (Exergy) of reservoir | $\Phi = Q_H \left(1 - \dfrac{T_0}{T_H}\right)$ | Maximum convertible to work |

> [!WARNING]
> The **efficiency of a PMM2 = 1 (or 100%)** is a *trap question* in KTU exams. It is the *apparent* efficiency — the device is **impossible**, so the value is **unreachable**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** All real thermal plants must reject heat to a condenser — this is exactly what PMM2 avoids.
- **Refrigeration:** Confirms why refrigerators need both indoor and outdoor coils.
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC):** Real-world attempts to mimic PMM2 (use warm surface + cold deep ocean), but **must** reject heat to the cold sink, so efficiency is bounded by Carnot.
- **Solar Energy:** Sun acts as single reservoir, but real conversion still obeys 2nd Law.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Proof of Impossibility via Entropy Inequality

**Given:** A hypothetical PMM2 operates in a cycle, drawing $Q_H$ from reservoir at $T_H$ and producing $W$.

**Find:** Show that this violates the 2nd Law.

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law (cyclic device):**

For any cyclic device, the net change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} - W_{net} &= \Delta U_{cycle} \\
Q_H - Q_L - W_{net} &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PMM2 definition (no heat rejection):**

By definition of PMM2, $Q_L = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_H - 0 - W_{net} &= 0 \\
\therefore W_{net} &= Q_H
\end{aligned}$$

This satisfies the 1st Law. The 1st Law is **not** violated.

**Step 3 — Apply the Second Law (Clausius Inequality):**

For any real cycle operating between reservoirs, the Clausius inequality states:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

For the PMM2:
