$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - 0 = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

Since $Q_H > 0$ and $T_H > 0$:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} > 0$$

This **violates** the Clausius inequality ($\leq 0$). **Hence, PMM2 is impossible.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PMM2 + Real Refrigerator $\Rightarrow$ PMM1 (Equivalence Proof)

**Given:** Hypothetical PMM2 receiving $Q_2$ at $T_H$ and producing $W$. A real refrigerator operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$ requires work input.

**Step 1 — Work produced by PMM2:**

$$W = Q_2$$

**Step 2 — Refrigerator's heat transfer (real COP):**

A real refrigerator receiving work $W$ at $T_L$ and rejecting $Q_1$ to $T_H$:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 \quad (\text{ideal coupled case})$$

But more rigorously, the refrigerator pumps $Q_2$ from cold to hot using work $W_{in} = W$.

**Step 3 — Net effect of the combined device:**

- PMM2 takes $Q_2$ from hot reservoir, produces $W$.
- Refrigerator uses $W$, takes $Q_2$ from hot reservoir (same one), and rejects $Q_1 + Q_2$ to the same hot reservoir.
- The hot reservoir's net heat: $-Q_2 + (Q_1 + Q_2) = Q_1$.
- Work interaction between combined devices: $+W - W = 0$ (internal).

**Step 4 — Final observation:**

The combined device is effectively a **heat pump** taking $Q_1$ from the **hot reservoir alone** and converting it entirely to work (since no work crosses the outer boundary).

$$W_{net,outer} = Q_1, \quad Q_{L,outer} = 0$$

This is **exactly a PMM1** in terms of net effect.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Since PMM1 is impossible, and a PMM2 + refrigerator = PMM1, then **PMM2 must also be impossible**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PMM1 vs PMM2 vs Real Engine

| **Property** | **PMM1** | **PMM2** | **Real Heat Engine** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Violates 1st Law? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Violates 2nd Law? | Yes (trivially) | **Yes** | No |
| $W$ produced from | Nothing | $Q_H$ only | $Q_H - Q_L$ |
| $Q_L$ rejected | 0 | 0 | $> 0$ |
| Efficiency $\eta$ | $> 1$ (impossible) | $= 1$ (impossible) | $< 1$ (Carnot bound) |
| $\oint \delta Q / T$ | Undefined | $> 0$ | $\leq 0$ |
| $\Delta S_{universe}$ | Indeterminate | $< 0$ (forbidden) | $\geq 0$ |
| Existence | Impossible | Impossible | Exists |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Maximum Work from a Single Reservoir (Availability Limit)

**Given:** A system at $T_H$ in thermal contact with environment at $T_0$.

**Step 1 — Total heat available:** $Q_H$

**Step 2 — Maximum work extractable (Exergy):**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{max} &= Q_H - Q_{rejected} \\
W_{max} &= Q_H - T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{reservoir} \\
W_{max} &= Q_H - T_0 \cdot \left(\frac{Q_H}{T_H}\right) \\
W_{max} &= Q_H \left(1 - \frac{T_0}{T_H}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conclude:**

For $T_0 \to 0$ K, $W_{max} \to Q_H$ (PMM2-like limit), but absolute zero is **unattainable** (3rd Law of Thermodynamics). Thus, PMM2 remains forbidden.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: PMM2 Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reservoirA[Single Thermal Reservoir TH]:::res
    pmm2[PMM2 Hypothetical Cyclic Device]:::dev
    workOut[Net Work Output W]:::out
    sinkA[Low Temperature Sink TL]:::sink
    
    reservoirA -- QH heat input --> pmm2
    pmm2 -- W equals QH --> workOut
    pmm2 -. QL equals ZERO no rejection .-> sinkA
    
    classDef res fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **dashed arrow to the sink** is intentional — it represents the **non-existent** heat rejection. This is the visual signature of a PMM2: only one solid arrow goes in, one solid arrow comes out.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: PMM2 Coupled with Real Refrigerator (Impossibility Proof)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    resHot[Hot Reservoir TH]:::res
    resCold[Cold Reservoir TL]:::sink
    pmm2dev[PMM2 Device Impossible]:::dev
    refDev[Real Refrigerator]:::cool
    workFlow[Work W Internal]:::out
    qReject[Heat Rejected to Hot Reservoir]:::out
    
    resHot -- QH --> pmm2dev
    pmm2dev -- W output --> workFlow
    workFlow -- W input --> refDev
    resCold -- QL absorbed --> refDev
    refDev -- QH plus W --> qReject
    qReject -- returned --> resHot
    
    classDef res fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef cool fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Outcome of coupling:** The two reservoirs exchange net heat, with **zero net work** crossing the outer boundary. This is essentially a **PMM1** — converting heat from a single reservoir into net work without any input — which is impossible.

---

## 4.3 State Flow Diagram: Why PMM2 Fails (Entropy Perspective)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SingleReservoir
    SingleReservoir --> HeatTransfer: QH in from TH
    HeatTransfer --> CyclicProcess: 1st Law OK DeltaU equals 0
    CyclicProcess --> NetWorkOutput: W equals QH
    NetWorkOutput --> EntropyCheck: DeltaS universe equals minus QH over TH
    EntropyCheck --> Forbidden: DeltaS universe less than 0
    Forbidden --> [*]: PMM2 Impossible
    
    note right of Forbidden
        2nd Law violated:
        DeltaS universe must be
        greater than or equal to 0
    end note
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Perpetual Motion Machine of the Second Kind (PMM2). State why it is considered impossible.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **PMM2** is a hypothetical device that operates in a cycle and converts heat extracted from a **single thermal reservoir** entirely into an **equivalent amount of work**, without rejecting any heat to a lower-temperature sink. It is considered impossible because it violates the **Kelvin–Planck statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. For such a device, the entropy change of the universe would be **negative** ($\Delta S_{univ} = -Q_H/T_H < 0$), which is forbidden by the Clausius inequality $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between PMM1 and PMM2 in terms of the laws they violate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PMM1 | PMM2 |
|---|---|---|
| Law violated | **1st Law** (creates energy) | **2nd Law** (no heat sink) |
| Work output | $W > Q$ (impossible) | $W = Q$ (single reservoir) |
| Efficiency | $> 100\%$ | $= 100\%$ (impossible) |
| Heat rejection | Not applicable | $Q_L = 0$ |

PMM1 produces work without any heat input (violates energy conservation), whereas PMM2 obeys energy conservation but produces work from a single reservoir without any heat rejection (violates the Second Law). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** State the **Kelvin–Planck statement** of the Second Law. Explain how it is equivalent to the statement: *"A PMM2 is impossible."* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cyclic heat engine is claimed to produce $W = 500 \, \text{kJ}$ of work by extracting heat from a single reservoir at $T_H = 800 \, \text{K}$, with no heat rejection. Show mathematically why this device cannot be a PMM2, and compute the entropy change of the universe per cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Kelvin–Planck Statement (2 marks):**
"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and performing an equivalent amount of work."

**Step 2 — Logical Equivalence (5 marks):**
- A PMM2, by definition, takes $Q_H$ from a single reservoir at $T_H$ and produces $W = Q_H$ with $Q_L = 0$.
- This is *exactly* the scenario the Kelvin–Planck statement forbids.
- Therefore, the existence of a PMM2 **directly contradicts** Kelvin–Planck.
- Conversely, any device violating Kelvin–Planck (i.e., 100% efficient single-reservoir engine) is, by definition, a PMM2.
- The two statements are **logically identical**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- $W = 500 \, \text{kJ}$
- $Q_H = 500 \, \text{kJ}$ (since claimed PMM2)
- $Q_L = 0$
- $T_H = 800 \, \text{K}$

**Step 1 — Check 1st Law (1 mark):**
$$W = Q_H - Q_L = 500 - 0 = 500 \, \text{kJ} \quad \checkmark$$
1st Law is satisfied — so it's *not* a PMM1.

**Step 2 — Check Clausius Inequality (3 marks):**
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{500}{800} - 0 = 0.625 \, \text{kJ/K}$$

Since $0.625 > 0$, the Clausius inequality $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$ is **violated**.

**Step 3 — Entropy of Universe (2 marks):**
For the cyclic device, $\Delta S_{sys} = 0$. Entropy change of reservoir:
$$\Delta S_{reservoir} = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{500}{800} = -0.625 \, \text{kJ/K}$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0 + (-0.625) = -0.625 \, \text{kJ/K} < 0$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
A negative entropy change for the universe is impossible by the 2nd Law. Hence, this claimed device **cannot** be a PMM2. Such a device is a **violation of the Second Law** and is therefore **forbidden by nature**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** A proposed engine takes heat $Q_2 = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$ from a reservoir at $T_H = 1000 \, \text{K}$ and produces $W = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$ of work, rejecting no heat. Using the **Clausius inequality**, prove that this engine is impossible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same engine is coupled with a reversible Carnot refrigerator operating between $T_H = 1000 \, \text{K}$ and $T_L = 300 \, \text{K}$, show that the combined system would constitute a **PMM1**, hence proving the equivalence of the two Second Law statements. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Given:** $Q_2 = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$, $T_H = 1000 \, \text{K}$, $W = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$, $Q_L = 0$.

**Step 1 — Clausius Integral (3 marks):**
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{1000}{1000} - 0 = 1.0 \, \text{kJ/K}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with 2nd Law (2 marks):**
The Clausius inequality states:
$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$
But we obtained $1.0 > 0$. **Contradiction.**

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation (2 marks):**
This means entropy of the universe decreases by $1.0 \, \text{kJ/K}$ per cycle, which is impossible. Hence, the engine violates the 2nd Law and **cannot exist** as a PMM2. **(Proved)**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Refrigerator's Heat Exchange (3 marks):**
Coupling the proposed engine (which supplies $W = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$) with a Carnot refrigerator between $T_H = 1000 \, \text{K}$ and $T_L = 300 \, \text{K}$:
- Work input to refrigerator: $W = 1000 \, \text{kJ}$
- COP of Carnot refrigerator: $\text{COP} = T_L / (T_H - T_L) = 300 / 700 = 0.4286$
- Heat extracted from cold reservoir: $Q_L = \text{COP} \times W = 0.4286 \times 1000 = 428.6 \, \text{kJ}$
- Heat rejected to hot reservoir: $Q_H = Q_L + W = 428.6 + 1000 = 1428.6 \, \text{kJ}$

**Step 2 — Net Effect of Combined System (2 marks):**
- Hot reservoir: receives $+1428.6 \, \text{kJ}$ from refrigerator, gives $-1000 \, \text{kJ}$ to engine → net gain $= +428.6 \, \text{kJ}$
- Cold reservoir: gives $428.6 \, \text{kJ}$
- Outer boundary: zero net work (internal cancellation)

**Step 3 — Identification as PMM1 (2 marks):**
The combined device takes $Q = 428.6 \, \text{kJ}$ from a single reservoir (cold) and produces **zero net work** at the boundary — but more importantly, in the configuration where work is taken *out*, the system becomes equivalent to a PMM1 extracting heat from a single reservoir to produce net work. **Hence, the assumption of PMM2 leads to PMM1**, which is impossible. **Equivalence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PMM1 with PMM2 (–2 marks):** Always state clearly *which* law is being violated.
> 2. **Forgetting to check Clausius Inequality (–3 marks):** Many students only verify the 1st Law. The 2nd Law check (Clausius integral) is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Heat *into* the system is positive; work *out* is positive (KTU standard).
> 4. **Ignoring Entropy of Universe (–2 marks):** Always calculate $\Delta S_{univ}$ for full marks.
> 5. **Treating PMM2 as 100% efficient without comment (–1 mark):** Mention that the efficiency *appears* 100% but the device is **impossible**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **PMM2** = Single-reservoir cyclic device converting heat entirely to work, with **no heat rejection**.
- [x] PMM2 **obeys the 1st Law** but **violates the 2nd Law** (Kelvin–Planck statement).
- [x] Mathematical signature: $\oint \delta Q / T = Q_H / T_H > 0$, violating the Clausius inequality $\leq 0$.
- [x] Entropy of universe would **decrease** by $Q_H / T_H$ per cycle — forbidden.
- [x] **PMM1 ≠ PMM2**: PMM1 violates energy conservation; PMM2 violates the directionality of natural processes.
- [x] **Equivalence**: Existence of PMM2 $\Rightarrow$ PMM1 when coupled with a real refrigerator.
- [x] **Apparent efficiency of PMM2 = 100%**, but it is **physically unrealizable**.
- [x] **Real engines** must have $Q_L > 0$ and $\eta < \eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$.
- [x] **Standard temperatures in KTU numericals:** $T_0 = 300 \, \text{K}$, $T_H = 800\text{–}1000 \, \text{K}$, $T_L = 300 \, \text{K}$.
- [x] **3rd Law connection:** Even if $T_0 \to 0$ K, absolute zero is unattainable, so PMM2 remains impossible.
- [x] **Mnemonic:** *"PMM2 = Perfect engine = Impossible dream"*.
- [x] **Key equations to memorize:**
  - $W = Q_H$ (PMM2 work)
  - $\eta_{PMM2} = 1$
  - $\oint \delta Q / T \leq 0$ (Clausius)
  - $\Delta S_{univ} = -Q_H / T_H$ (PMM2 violation)
  - $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$ (real limit)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carnot's Theorem and Its Corollaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carnot's Theorem and Its Corollaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 Carnot's Theorem and Its Corollaries — KTU 2024 Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot's Theorem** is a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** that establishes the **maximum theoretical limit of thermal efficiency** for any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. The theorem, originally proposed by **Sadi Carnot (1824)**, declares that the efficiency of all reversible heat engines operating between the same two temperature reservoirs is identical, and no irreversible engine can surpass this universal efficiency bound.

Mathematically, the theorem is expressed as:

$$\eta_{rev} = \eta_{max} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

where $T_H$ and $T_L$ are the absolute (Kelvin) temperatures of the **hot reservoir (source)** and **cold reservoir (sink)**, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Substance Independence:** A pivotal feature of Carnot's Theorem is that the reversible efficiency is **independent of the working fluid** (steam, air, helium, ideal gas, or any other fluid). It depends *exclusively* on the temperature limits of the two thermal reservoirs.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two competitive cyclists racing up a mountain pass:

* **Cyclist A** rides a *perfectly tuned, frictionless bicycle* on a smooth road (representing a **reversible engine**).
* **Cyclist B** rides an ordinary bicycle with friction, wind drag, and rough terrain (representing a **real, irreversible engine**).

Both start at the same base camp ($T_H$) and end at the same summit ($T_L$). No matter how hard Cyclist B pedals or how advanced his gears are, he **can never climb faster (or more efficiently) than Cyclist A's ideal machine**, because A faces *zero losses*. This is precisely Carnot's Theorem: **no real engine can out-perform an ideal reversible engine operating between the same temperature limits.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (KTU context) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot reservoir temperature | $T_H$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 500 K – 1500 K |
| Cold reservoir temperature | $T_L$ | **K (Kelvin)** | 250 K – 400 K |
| Heat absorbed from source | $Q_H$ | **kJ** | varies |
| Heat rejected to sink | $Q_L$ | **kJ** | varies |
| Net work output | $W_{net}$ | **kJ** | varies |
| Thermal efficiency | $\eta$ | dimensionless (%) | 0 < $\eta$ < 1 |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Quick Cue:** Whenever a numerical problem asks "what is the maximum possible efficiency?", you **must** use Carnot's reversible formula. Using any other formula will earn zero marks for that sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Statement of Carnot's Theorem — Two Parts

#### 🔹 Part I — Equality of Reversible Efficiencies

> *"The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs is **independent of the nature of the working substance** and depends **only on the temperatures** of the source and sink."*

In symbolic form, for any two reversible engines R₁ and R₂:

$$\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

#### 🔹 Part II — Reversible Engine is the Most Efficient

> *"No heat engine, whether reversible or irreversible, operating between two given thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$, can have an efficiency greater than that of a **reversible engine** operating between the same two reservoirs."*

$$\eta_{irrev} < \eta_{rev} = \eta_{max}$$

### 2.2 The Corollaries — Logical Consequences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 places heavy weightage on the **Five Corollaries of Carnot's Theorem**. Memorize each one as a stand-alone exam-ready sentence.

**Corollary 1 — Universality of Reversible Efficiency:**
All reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have *exactly the same* efficiency, regardless of working fluid.

**Corollary 2 — Temperature-Only Dependence:**
The reversible efficiency is a *function only* of the two reservoir temperatures, expressible as $\eta_{rev} = f(T_H, T_L)$.

**Corollary 3 — Upper Bound on Efficiency:**
The reversible efficiency represents the **upper theoretical bound** of any heat engine between given temperature limits.

**Corollary 4 — Reversible Efficiency Formula:**
$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
where temperatures **must** be in absolute scale (**Kelvin**).

**Corollary 5 — Irreversibility Penalty:**
$$\eta_{irrev} < 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions and Physical Limits

| Condition | Efficiency Behavior | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $T_L \to 0$ K | $\eta_{rev} \to 100\%$ | Absolute zero → perfect engine (unattainable per 3rd Law) |
| $T_H \to T_L$ | $\eta_{rev} \to 0$ | No work when reservoirs are at same temp |
| $T_L = 0.5 \, T_H$ | $\eta_{rev} = 50\%$ | Half of Carnot efficiency |
| Irreversible engine | $\eta < \eta_{rev}$ | Friction, turbulence, heat leaks |
| Real engine | $\eta \ll \eta_{rev}$ | Typical actual efficiencies: 30–45% |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **exam-day weapon**. Print, memorize, and reproduce accurately.

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency definition | Standard heat engine problems |
| 2 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Efficiency via heat ratio | When $Q_L$ given |
| 3 | $\eta_{rev} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | **Carnot / Reversible efficiency** | **Maximum efficiency problems** |
| 4 | $\eta_{irrev} < 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Inequality for real engines | Prove / compare engines |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Reversible heat ratio | Reversible cycles only |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} > \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Irreversible heat ratio | Real cycle analysis |
| 7 | COP$_{HP,rev} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Heat pump COP (reversible) | Refrigeration modules |
| 8 | COP$_{ref,rev} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Refrigerator COP (reversible) | Refrigeration modules |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ | Kelvin temperature relation | Defining thermodynamic temperature |
| 10 | $\sum \dfrac{Q}{T} \leq 0$ | Clausius inequality (2nd Law) | Proof steps, advanced proofs |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Power Plant Design:** Carnot efficiency sets the design target — modern Rankine and Brayton cycles are benchmarked against $1 - T_L/T_H$.
* **Refrigeration & HVAC:** COP reversal of Carnot cycle defines the **maximum theoretical COP** for any refrigerator or heat pump.
* **Internal Combustion Engines:** Otto, Diesel, and Dual cycles are always compared to the Carnot limit for the same $T_H, T_L$.
* **Cryogenics & Liquefaction:** The unattainability of $\eta = 1$ at finite $T_L$ drives the engineering pursuit of *near-Carnot* cycles.
* **Solar Thermal & Geothermal:** These systems quote "Carnot equivalent" efficiency when marketing thermodynamic performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Mathematical Proof of Carnot's Theorem

#### 📐 Setup of the Proof

Consider two heat engines, **Engine A** (reversible) and **Engine B** (any engine — reversible or irreversible), operating between the **same source** at temperature $T_H$ and the **same sink** at temperature $T_L$.

* Engine A absorbs heat $Q_1$ from the source, produces work $W_A$, and rejects heat $Q_2$ to the sink.
* Engine B absorbs heat $Q_3$ from the source, produces work $W_B$, and rejects heat $Q_4$ to the sink.

**Efficiencies:**

$$\eta_A = \frac{W_A}{Q_1} = 1 - \frac{Q_2}{Q_1}$$

$$\eta_B = \frac{W_B}{Q_3} = 1 - \frac{Q_4}{Q_3}$$

#### 📐 Step 1 — Equalize Work Outputs

We adjust the engine sizes so both produce **identical work output** $W$:

$$W_A = W_B = W$$

#### 📐 Step 2 — Assume the Contradiction (Proof by Contradiction)

Suppose, for contradiction, that Engine B is **more efficient** than Engine A:

$$\eta_B > \eta_A$$

Using the equal-work constraint:

$$\frac{W}{Q_3} > \frac{W}{Q_1} \implies Q_3 < Q_1$$

Thus, Engine B requires **less heat** from the source than Engine A to produce the same work.

#### 📐 Step 3 — Couple the Two Engines

We now run:
* Engine B **forward** (producing work $W$)
* Engine A **in reverse** as a refrigerator (consuming work $W$)

In the reversed mode, Engine A:
* Consumes work $W$ from Engine B
* Extracts heat $Q_2$ from the sink (cold reservoir)
* Delivers heat $Q_1$ to the source (hot reservoir)

#### 📐 Step 4 — Energy Audit of the Combined System

| Reservoir | Heat Given (-) | Heat Received (+) | Net Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source ($T_H$) | $-Q_3$ (lost to B) | $+Q_1$ (received from rev-A) | $+(Q_1 - Q_3)$ |
| Sink ($T_L$) | $-Q_2$ (lost to rev-A) | — | $-Q_2$ |
| Work | $+W$ (from B) | $-W$ (to rev-A) | $0$ |

#### 📐 Step 5 — Apply First Law to Combined System

For the combined system (steady-state, cyclic):

$$Q_3 + Q_2 = W + Q_1$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_2 - (Q_1 - Q_3) = 0 \quad \text{[after substituting } W = Q_1 - Q_2\text{]}$$

$$\text{Net heat to source} = Q_1 - Q_3$$

$$\text{Net heat from sink} = Q_2$$

From the energy balance $W = Q_1 - Q_2 = Q_3 - Q_4$, we deduce:

$$\text{Net heat transferred from sink to source} = Q_1 - Q_3$$

#### 📐 Step 6 — Invoke the Second Law (Clausius Statement)

> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body."*

The combined system does exactly this:
* **No net work** is exchanged with the surroundings.
* **Heat $Q_1 - Q_3$ is transferred from the cold sink ($T_L$) to the hot source ($T_H$)** spontaneously.

This **violates** the Clausius statement. Hence our assumption $\eta_B > \eta_A$ is **false**.

#### 📐 Step 7 — Final Conclusion

$$\boxed{\eta_A \geq \eta_B \quad \text{for any Engine B vs. reversible Engine A}}$$

Reversing the roles of A and B (both reversible), we get $\eta_A = \eta_B$, proving Part I.

For Part II, the strict inequality holds when B is irreversible:

$$\eta_{irrev} < \eta_{rev}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reversible Efficiency Formula $\eta_{rev} = 1 - T_L/T_H$

#### Starting Point — Clausius Equality for Reversible Cycles

For a **reversible cycle**, the Clausius inequality becomes a strict equality:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a Carnot cycle with **isothermal heat transfer** at $T_H$ (heat $Q_H$ absorbed) and $T_L$ (heat $Q_L$ rejected):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{-Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

(The sign convention: heat absorbed is positive, heat rejected is negative.)

Rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

Substituting into the efficiency expression $\eta = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$:

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Proof of Each Corollary

| Corollary | Mathematical Justification | Logical Link |
|---|---|---|
| **C-1** Same efficiency for all reversible engines | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ for any reversible cycle | Clausius equality |
| **C-2** Efficiency depends only on $T_H, T_L$ | $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ contains no other variables | Direct formula |
| **C-3** Reversible = maximum | Proof by contradiction (Steps 1–7 above) | Second Law |
| **C-4** Formula derivation | $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 \implies \eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ | Clausius equality |
| **C-5** Strict inequality for irreversible | $\oint \frac{\delta Q_{irr}}{T} < 0$ | Clausius inequality |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Proof Logic of Carnot's Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Setup: Two engines A rev and B any between same T_H and T_L"] --> B["Adjust both to produce equal work W"]
    B --> C["Assume for contradiction eta B greater than eta A"]
    C --> D["This implies Q3 less than Q1"]
    D --> E["Couple: Engine B forward plus Engine A reversed as refrigerator"]
    E --> F["Compute combined system energy and work balance"]
    F --> G["Result: Zero net work, heat transferred from cold sink to hot source"]
    G --> H["Compare with Clausius statement of 2nd Law"]
    H --> I{"Does this violate Second Law?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Contradiction detected, assumption is false"]
    J --> K["Conclusion: eta A greater than or equal to eta B"]
    K --> L["Reverse roles, conclude eta rev identical for all reversible engines"]
    L --> M["Final: eta irrev less than eta rev equals 1 minus T_L divided by T_H"]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style I fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Five Corollaries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT["Carnot Theorem 1824 by Sadi Carnot"] --> P1["Part 1: All reversible engines have same efficiency"]
    CT --> P2["Part 2: Reversible engine is the most efficient"]
    P1 --> C1["Corollary 1: Universality across working fluids"]
    P1 --> C2["Corollary 2: Efficiency is temperature only function"]
    P2 --> C3["Corollary 3: Reversible sets upper bound"]
    P2 --> C4["Corollary 4: eta rev equals 1 minus T_L divided by T_H"]
    P2 --> C5["Corollary 5: eta irrev is strictly less than eta rev"]
    C4 --> APP1["Power Plant Cycle Design"]
    C4 --> APP2["Refrigeration COP Limits"]
    C5 --> APP3["Real Engine Penalty Analysis"]
    APP1 --> REAL["Modern Rankine and Brayton Cycles"]
    APP2 --> REAL
    APP3 --> REAL

    style CT fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style REAL fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Coupled Engine Configuration (Proof Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Hot Reservoir T_H"] -- "Q_H in B" --> EB["Engine B Forward irrev or rev"]
    EB -- "W produced" --> REV["Reverse A reversible"]
    REV -- "W consumed" --> EB
    REV -- "Q_A delivered back" --> SRC
    REV -- "Q_2 extracted from" --> SNK["Cold Reservoir T_L"]
    EB -- "Q_4 rejected to" --> SNK

    style SRC fill:#ff7043,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style SNK fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style EB fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style REV fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Engine B and reversed Engine A together form a "self-running heat pump" — exactly what the Clausius statement forbids. This contradiction is the heart of the proof.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*State Carnot's theorem. Explain, in brief, the corollary that establishes the upper bound on thermal efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Carnot's Theorem (Part II):** No heat engine, whether reversible or irreversible, operating between two given thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$, can have an efficiency greater than that of a reversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs.
>
> **Upper Bound Corollary:** The efficiency of a reversible engine is given by
>
> $$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
>
> This represents the **maximum theoretical efficiency** attainable between the given temperature limits. Any real (irreversible) engine satisfies
>
> $$\eta_{irrev} < 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
>
> Hence, $1 - T_L/T_H$ is the **absolute upper bound** on thermal efficiency for any cycle operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any three corollaries of Carnot's theorem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. All reversible heat engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the **same efficiency**, irrespective of the working substance. *(1 mark)*
2. The efficiency of a reversible engine is a **function of reservoir temperatures only** and is independent of the working fluid. *(1 mark)*
3. The reversible efficiency $\eta_{rev} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ sets the **maximum possible efficiency** for any engine between $T_H$ and $T_L$. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** *State and prove Carnot's theorem. Show that all reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the same efficiency, regardless of the working substance.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A heat engine operates between a source at 800 K and a sink at 300 K. Determine:*
  *(i) The maximum possible (Carnot) efficiency.*
  *(ii) The actual efficiency if the engine produces 600 kJ of work per 1500 kJ of heat absorbed.*
  *(iii) The heat rejected to the sink.* **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Statement:** Refer to Section 2.1 above. *(2 marks)*

**Proof:** Follow the 7-step derivation in Section 3.1 (Steps 1 to 7). The crucial logical sequence is:

* Assume Engine B (any) has higher efficiency than reversible Engine A.
* Couple them — B forward, A reversed.
* Show the combined system violates Clausius statement.
* Conclude $\eta_{irrev} < \eta_{rev}$ and for two reversible engines $\eta_{R_1} = \eta_{R_2}$. *(5 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
$T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $W = 600$ kJ, $Q_H = 1500$ kJ

**Part (i) — Carnot Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{rev} = 62.5\%} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**Part (ii) — Actual Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{actual} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{600}{1500} = 0.40$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{actual} = 40\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (iii) — Heat Rejected to Sink:**

By First Law: $Q_L = Q_H - W = 1500 - 600 = 900$ kJ

$$\boxed{Q_L = 900 \text{ kJ}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Comparison Check:** $\eta_{actual} = 40\% < \eta_{rev} = 62.5\%$ ✓ — consistent with Corollary 5 (irreversibility penalty). *(implicit verification)*

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *Explain the five corollaries of Carnot's theorem. Derive the expression $\eta_{rev} = 1 - T_L/T_H$ using the Clausius equality.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A reversible Carnot engine operates between 900 K and 300 K. If the engine draws 2000 kJ of heat from the source, calculate:*
  *(i) The efficiency of the cycle.*
  *(ii) The work output per cycle.*
  *(iii) The heat rejected per cycle.*
  *(iv) Compare with an irreversible engine that delivers 800 kJ of work for the same heat input — state which one obeys the Second Law.* **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**Five Corollaries** — listed from Section 2.2 (C1, C2, C3, C4, C5). *(3 marks)*

**Derivation of $\eta_{rev}$:**
For a Carnot cycle, $Q_H$ absorbed at $T_H$ and $Q_L$ rejected at $T_L$.

Applying Clausius equality:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0 \implies \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \quad \text{(4 marks)}$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $T_H = 900$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_H = 2000$ kJ

**Part (i) — Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{rev} = 66.67\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (ii) — Work Output:**

$$W = \eta \cdot Q_H = \frac{2}{3} \times 2000 = 1333.33 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W_{rev} = 1333.33 \text{ kJ}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (iii) — Heat Rejected:**

$$Q_L = Q_H - W = 2000 - 1333.33 = 666.67 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q_L = 666.67 \text{ kJ}} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Part (iv) — Comparison with Irreversible Engine:**

For the irreversible engine:

$$\eta_{irrev} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{800}{2000} = 0.40 = 40\%$$

Compare:

$$\eta_{irrev} = 40\% < \eta_{rev} = 66.67\% \quad ✓$$

**Second Law Check:** The irreversible engine obeys the Second Law because $\eta_{irrev} < \eta_{rev}$. The reversible engine produces *more* work (1333.33 kJ) for the *same* $Q_H$, which is the theoretical maximum. The irreversible engine delivers only 800 kJ due to real-world losses (friction, heat leaks, finite-time processes). *(2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
>
> 1. **Temperature Units:** Always convert °C → **Kelvin (K)** before substituting into $1 - T_L/T_H$. Using °C directly costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Sign Convention on Heat:** Forgetting that $Q_L$ is rejected (negative in Clausius form) is a frequent error. Write $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$, *not* $+$.
> 3. **Reversible vs. Actual Confusion:** Part (b) numericals often ask "maximum efficiency" — students mistakenly use $\eta = W/Q_H$ (actual) instead of $1 - T_L/T_H$ (Carnot). Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Proof by Contradiction Skipping:** The 7-step proof must be reproduced *sequentially*. Skipping directly to "it violates Clausius statement" without showing the coupling logic loses **3 to 4 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting the Conclusion Box:** End the proof with a clearly boxed statement. KTU examiners award 1 extra mark for a clean, boxed final conclusion.
> 6. **Working Substance Disclaimer:** When asked "independent of working substance," state it explicitly — many students omit this crucial phrase.

---

### 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist as your final-minute revision before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

* ⭐ **Carnot's Theorem** has **two parts**: (i) all reversible engines have the same efficiency; (ii) no engine can exceed the reversible efficiency.
* ⭐ The theorem is a **direct consequence of the Second Law** — never the First Law.
* ⭐ The reversible efficiency formula is $\eta_{rev} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — *temperatures must be in Kelvin*.
* ⭐ All **five corollaries** must be memorizable word-for-word — they form a 7-mark answer by themselves.
* ⭐ For any **irreversible** engine: $\eta_{irrev} < 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ (strict inequality).
* ⭐ For any **reversible** engine: $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ (this is the Clausius equality in action).
* ⭐ The **proof by contradiction** uses the Clausius statement: "heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot without external work."
* ⭐ **Boundary limit:** As $T_L \to 0$ K, $\eta_{rev} \to 100\%$ — but the Third Law forbids reaching absolute zero.
* ⭐ **Reversible COP formulas:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ and $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$.
* ⭐ Always check **dimensionless consistency** of efficiency (no units).
* ⭐ **KTU-favourite numerical setup:** $T_H$, $T_L$, $Q_H$ given → ask for $\eta_{rev}$, $W$, $Q_L$.
* ⭐ The theorem is **substance-independent** — steam, air, helium all yield identical $\eta_{rev}$ for the same $T_H, T_L$.
* ⭐ **Real-world penalty:** Actual power-plant efficiency ≈ 30–45%, but Carnot limit ≈ 60–70% for the same $T_H, T_L$ — the gap = irreversibility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale; Clausius Inequality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale; Clausius Inequality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale** (also called the **Kelvin Scale**) is a temperature scale that is defined independently of the properties of any particular thermometric substance. It is based purely on the Second Law of Thermodynamics, using the efficiency of reversible (Carnot) heat engines operating between two thermal reservoirs.

**Lord Kelvin's Original Proposition (1848):**
Kelvin proposed that the efficiency of a reversible Carnot engine depends only on the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs and is independent of the working fluid. This allowed him to define a universal temperature ratio:

$$\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L}$$

where the ratio of absolute temperatures equals the ratio of heat absorbed to heat rejected in a reversible Carnot cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Absolute"?**
> The term *absolute* signifies that this scale is **independent of any working substance** (unlike mercury, alcohol, or gas thermometers). It depends only on fundamental thermodynamic principles and therefore represents a true, universal measure of thermal state.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Universal Yardstick

Imagine two travelers measuring the length of a table. If one uses a rubber band and another uses a steel ruler, they will get different answers because their instruments stretch differently with temperature. The **absolute thermodynamic scale** is like a **steel ruler of the universe** — its "ruler" is the Second Law itself, which is the same everywhere, in every laboratory, in every galaxy. No matter what material you use to measure, if you follow the Second Law, you will get the *exact* same temperature.

### 1.2 Clausius Inequality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The **Clausius Inequality** is a mathematical statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics for any cycle (reversible or irreversible). It states that the cyclic integral of the ratio of heat transfer to absolute temperature is always less than or equal to zero.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

where:
- $\delta Q$ = infinitesimal heat transfer **into** the system
- $T$ = absolute temperature at the boundary where heat is transferred
- The integral is taken over the **entire cycle** (closed loop)
- Equality ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$) holds for **reversible** cycles
- Strict inequality ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$) holds for **irreversible** cycles

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Energy Accountant

Think of $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$ as a "quality-adjusted" energy voucher. Just as a bank keeps track of every transaction, the Clausius Inequality acts as a **ledger of the universe's energy book-keeping**. For any process that returns to its starting point:
- A perfectly efficient (reversible) process has a perfect balance: **sum = 0**.
- A real (irreversible) process always has a "deficit": **sum < 0** — meaning some useful energy potential has been permanently lost as degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Throughout this module, $\delta Q$ is taken as **heat supplied to the system**. If heat is rejected, $\delta Q$ is negative. This convention is critical for correctly applying the Clausius Inequality.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Triple Point of Water:** $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$ (exact, by definition of the Kelvin scale)
- **Absolute Zero:** $T = 0 \text{ K} = -273.15 ^\circ\text{C}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Reference Temperature (Ice Point):** $T_{ice} = 273.15 \text{ K}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why We Need an Absolute Scale

The limitations of empirical thermometric scales (Fahrenheit, Celsius with mercury/alcohol) became apparent in the 19th century:
1. Different thermometric fluids gave **different temperature readings** for the same thermal state.
2. There was no fundamental, reproducible lower bound to temperature from classical theory.
3. The Second Law demanded a temperature scale that is **operationally meaningful** for heat engines.

Kelvin recognized that the **Carnot efficiency** is a property of the cycle, not the substance. Since efficiency can be measured (by measuring $Q_H$ and $Q_L$), and efficiency is a pure number, the **ratio of temperatures** is also a pure, substance-independent number.

### 2.2 The Logical Steps to Define the Absolute Scale

**Step 1: Carnot's Principle (from Sadi Carnot, 1824)**
The efficiency of any reversible heat engine operating between two reservoirs depends only on the reservoir temperatures, not on the working substance.

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

**Step 2: Assigning Arbitrary Reference**
Kelvin set the triple point of water at exactly **273.16 K** so that one Kelvin equals one degree Celsius in magnitude.

**Step 3: Measuring Any Other Temperature**
For a reversible Carnot engine operating between reservoir at unknown temperature $T$ and a reference reservoir at $T_{ref}$:

$$T = T_{ref} \times \frac{Q}{Q_{ref}}$$

where $Q$ is heat extracted from the unknown reservoir and $Q_{ref}$ is heat rejected to the reference.

**Step 4: Universality Verification**
No matter what working fluid is used (ideal gas, steam, any hypothetical substance), the calculated $T$ remains the same — this is the **absolute thermodynamic temperature**.

### 2.3 The Clausius Inequality — Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: Consider a Reversible Cycle**
For a reversible cycle, no entropy is generated. The cyclic integral of $\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ equals zero.

**Step 2: Consider an Irreversible Cycle**
For an irreversible cycle, internal irreversibilities (friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing) generate entropy, making the integral strictly negative.

**Step 3: Combine into a Single Statement**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0 \quad \text{(equality for reversible, inequality for irreversible)}$$

**Step 4: Consequence — Definition of Entropy**
For a reversible process between states 1 and 2:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

Entropy is a **property of state** (point function), while $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$ is a **path function** for irreversible processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Second Law:** The Clausius Inequality is the most general mathematical formulation of the Second Law for closed systems undergoing cycles. It encompasses the Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements as special cases.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Conditions / Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L}$ | Defining ratio of absolute temperatures | Reversible Carnot cycle; $T$ in K, $Q$ in J |
| 2 | $\eta_{rev} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) efficiency | All reversible cycles |
| 3 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | Thermal efficiency (any cycle) | General; $Q$ in J |
| 4 | $\displaystyle T = 273.16 \times \dfrac{Q}{Q_{tp}}$ | Temperature via triple-point reference | Triple point of water as reference |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ | **Clausius Inequality** | All cycles; $\le 0$ general, $= 0$ reversible, $< 0$ irreversible |
| 6 | $\displaystyle dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy differential definition | Reversible process only |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \Delta S_{12} = \int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy change between two states | Path-independent; $S$ in J/K |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \oint dS = 0$ | Entropy cyclic integral | Any cycle, since $S$ is a property |
| 9 | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \ge 0$ | Second Law (entropy form) | $= 0$ reversible, $> 0$ irreversible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Rule for Tables:** When writing absolute value or norm in tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), **never** a vertical pipe `|`, to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** The Clausius Inequality provides the theoretical limit (COP) for any refrigerator or heat pump, dictating minimum work input.
- **Power Plants:** Engineers use the absolute scale to calculate maximum (Carnot) efficiency and benchmark real cycle performance.
- **Cryogenics:** The absolute scale sets the unattainable limit of $0 \text{ K}$, guiding liquefaction of gases (He, H₂, N₂).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Reaction spontaneity, equilibrium constants, and Gibbs free energy all depend on absolute temperature in Kelvin.
- **Heat Exchanger Design:** The logarithmic mean temperature difference (LMTD) and effectiveness-NTU method both rely on the Kelvin scale for accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Ratio

**Starting Point:** Consider two reversible heat engines $E_1$ and $E_2$ operating between the same two reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$ (yet to be defined absolutely).

**Step 1:** By the Second Law, all reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the **same efficiency**, regardless of working substance:

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

This efficiency is a function of the reservoir temperatures alone:

$$\eta_{rev} = f(T_H, T_L)$$

**Step 2:** Combining the two expressions:

$$1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = f(T_H, T_L) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{1}{1 - f(T_H, T_L)}$$

**Step 3:** Define a new function $g$ such that:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{g(T_H)}{g(T_L)}$$

This can always be done by choosing $g$ appropriately. By convention, set $g(T) = T$, giving:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

**Step 4:** Choose the reference point: the triple point of water is set to $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$ (so that $1 \text{ K} = 1 ^\circ \text{C}$ in magnitude).

**Step 5:** For a reversible engine between unknown temperature $T$ and the triple point:

$$T = 273.16 \times \frac{Q}{Q_{tp}}$$

where $Q$ is heat absorbed at $T$ and $Q_{tp}$ is heat rejected at the triple point. **This is the absolute thermodynamic temperature.**

**Step 6:** Verification with Ideal Gas:
For an ideal gas, the absolute temperature satisfies $PV = nRT$. One can prove (textbook exercise) that the ideal-gas temperature scale and the thermodynamic temperature scale are **identical**, validating the construction.

$$\boxed{\,T = 273.16 \lim_{P_{tp} \to 0} \left(\frac{P}{P_{tp}}\right)_{constant\,V}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Clausius Inequality

**Setup:** Consider a closed system undergoing an arbitrary cycle, exchanging heat $\delta Q_i$ with a series of reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$ (where $T_i$ is the reservoir temperature, equal to the system boundary temperature only for reversible heat transfer).

**Step 1:** Introduce a **reference reservoir** at temperature $T_0$ and a set of **reversible Carnot engines** that extract heat $\delta Q_0$ from this reservoir and supply $\delta Q_i$ to the system, mimicking the original heat transfers.

For a reversible Carnot engine between $T_0$ and $T_i$:

$$\frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = \frac{\delta Q_0}{T_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_0 = T_0 \cdot \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate over the entire cycle. Since $T_0$ is constant:

$$Q_0 = \oint \delta Q_0 = T_0 \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

**Step 3:** The composite system (original system + auxiliary reversible engines + reservoir) operates in a cycle. By the Kelvin–Planck statement of the Second Law, it is impossible to extract a net positive amount of work from a single reservoir in a cyclic process. Therefore:

$$Q_0 \le 0$$

with equality if and only if **all processes are reversible**.

**Step 4:** Since $T_0 > 0$ (absolute temperature is positive), we can divide by $T_0$:

$$T_0 \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

**This is the Clausius Inequality.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Equivalence: Reversible Case Defines Entropy

For a **reversible** cycle:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = 0$$

Consider a reversible path $A$ from state 1 to state 2 and a reversible path $B$ from state 2 back to state 1, forming a reversible cycle:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_A + \int_2^1 \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_B = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_A = \int_1^2 \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)_B$$

Since the result is **independent of the path** for reversible processes, we can define a point function:

$$S_2 - S_1 = \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$$

This proves entropy $S$ is a **thermodynamic property of state**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Equality vs. Inequality

| Cycle Type | Clausius Integral | Internal Reversibility | Entropy Generation |
|------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Reversible | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ | All processes reversible | $S_{gen} = 0$ |
| Irreversible | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ | At least one process irreversible | $S_{gen} > 0$ |
| Impossible (violates 2nd Law) | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} > 0$ | Contradicts Second Law | — |

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Example (Conceptual, No Code)

**Problem:** A reversible Carnot engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 600 \text{ K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. It absorbs $Q_H = 1000 \text{ J}$ from the hot reservoir per cycle. Calculate: (i) the Carnot efficiency, (ii) heat rejected, (iii) the cyclic Clausius integral.

**Solution:**

**(i) Carnot efficiency:**

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**(ii) Heat rejected:** Using $\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$:

$$0.50 = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{1000} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_L}{1000} = 0.50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L = 500 \text{ J}$$

**(iii) Clausius integral** (reversible, so must equal zero):

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{-Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{1000}{600} + \frac{-500}{300} = 1.6667 - 1.6667 = 0 \text{ J/K}$$

This confirms the reversible case. $\checkmark$

If the same engine were irreversible (say, $\eta_{actual} = 0.40$ instead of $0.50$), then $Q_L = 600 \text{ J}$ and:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{1000}{600} - \frac{600}{300} = 1.6667 - 2.0000 = -0.333 \text{ J/K} < 0 \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Defining the Absolute Scale

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Two Thermal Reservoirs T-H and T-L"]
    B["Insert Reversible Carnot Engine E"]
    C["Measure Heat Q-H and Q-L"]
    D["Apply Second Law Constraint"]
    E["Compute Temperature Ratio T-H over T-L equals Q-H over Q-L"]
    F["Reference: Triple Point of Water equals 273.16 K"]
    G["Compute Absolute Temperature T"]
    H["Output: Universal Kelvin Temperature"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Clausius Inequality Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Closed System Undergoing Cycle"]
    P2["Identify Heat Interactions dQ-i at Boundary Temperatures T-i"]
    P3["Couple to Auxiliary Reversible Carnot Engines"]
    P4["Reference Reservoir T-zero Greater Than Zero"]
    P5["Compute Equivalent Heat dQ-zero equals T-zero times dQ-i over T-i"]
    P6["Integrate Cyclically Q-zero equals T-zero times Cyclic Integral of dQ over T"]
    P7["Apply Kelvin-Planck Statement Q-zero Less Than or Equal to Zero"]
    P8["Divide by T-zero Greater Than Zero"]
    P9["Output: Clausius Inequality Cyclic Integral dQ over T Less Than or Equal to Zero"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
```

### 4.3 Decision Architecture: Reversible vs. Irreversible Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Evaluate Cyclic Integral of dQ over T"]
    Q2{"Sign of the Integral"}
    Q3["Result: Equal to Zero"]
    Q4["Result: Less Than Zero"]
    Q5["Result: Greater Than Zero"]
    Q6["Conclusion: Reversible Cycle, S-gen equals Zero"]
    Q7["Conclusion: Irreversible Cycle, S-gen Greater Than Zero"]
    Q8["Conclusion: Impossible Cycle, Violates Second Law"]

    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Equals Zero| Q3
    Q2 -->|Less Than Zero| Q4
    Q2 -->|Greater Than Zero| Q5
    Q3 --> Q6
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q5 --> Q8
```

### 4.4 Schematic Mapping: Thermodynamic Scale vs. Ideal Gas Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reversible Carnot Engine Block"] --> B["Compute Q-H over Q-L Ratio"]
    B --> C["Assign T-H over T-L equals Q-H over Q-L"]
    C --> D["Reference Triple Point 273.16 K"]
    D --> E["Kelvin Thermodynamic Scale"]

    F["Ideal Gas Equation PV equals nRT"] --> G["Limit P tends to Zero"]
    G --> H["Measure P and P-triple point at Constant V"]
    H --> I["Ideal Gas Absolute Scale"]

    E --> J["Coincide Identically"]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the Clausius Inequality and explain the significance of the equality and inequality signs.**

**Model Answer:**

The Clausius Inequality is a mathematical statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics for any thermodynamic cycle, expressed as:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

where $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat transferred **to** the system and $T$ is the absolute temperature at the boundary.

**Significance:**
- **Equality sign ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$):** Holds when the cycle is **completely reversible**. There is no entropy generation.
- **Inequality sign ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$):** Holds for **irreversible (real) cycles**, indicating net entropy generation in the universe.
- A value $> 0$ would violate the Second Law (impossible cycle).

It serves as a powerful tool to (i) test cycle feasibility, (ii) prove the existence of entropy as a property, and (iii) establish the direction of natural processes. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is the thermodynamic temperature scale called "absolute"? List two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

The thermodynamic temperature scale is called "absolute" for the following reasons:

1. **Substance Independence:** It is defined using the Second Law and the Carnot cycle alone, without any reference to the physical properties (expansion, resistance, etc.) of any particular thermometric substance. Every substance-based thermometer gives different readings; the thermodynamic scale gives a **unique** value for any thermal state.

2. **True Zero Point:** It has an absolute zero ($0 \text{ K} = -273.15 ^\circ\text{C}$) below which no temperature can exist, derived from the unattainability of converting heat completely into work in a cycle. It is not an arbitrary reference like $0 ^\circ\text{C}$ (ice point) or $0 ^\circ\text{F}$.

Additionally, it coincides with the ideal-gas temperature scale in the limit of zero pressure, providing a rigorous physical basis. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Define the Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale. Starting from the Second Law, derive the relationship $\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L}$ for a reversible Carnot engine. State the chosen reference and its numerical value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reversible Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$. If the engine rejects $600 \text{ J}$ of heat to the cold reservoir per cycle:
   (i) Calculate the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir.
   (ii) Calculate the thermal efficiency.
   (iii) Calculate the cyclic Clausius integral and verify it equals zero. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The Absolute Thermodynamic Temperature Scale is a temperature scale that is **independent of the properties of any thermometric substance**, defined using the Second Law of Thermodynamics and the operation of a reversible (Carnot) heat engine.

**Derivation:**

Consider two reversible heat engines $E_1$ and $E_2$ operating between the same two thermal reservoirs at unknown absolute temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$.

By the Second Law (Carnot's principle), all reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have identical efficiency, **independent of the working fluid**:

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}$$

This efficiency must therefore be a universal function of $T_H$ and $T_L$ only:

$$\eta_{rev} = f(T_H, T_L)$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - f(T_H, T_L)$$

Define a function $g(T)$ such that:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{g(T_H)}{g(T_L)}$$

This is always possible by choosing $g$ appropriately. By the convention of Lord Kelvin, we identify $g(T) \equiv T$ (the absolute thermodynamic temperature):

$$\boxed{\,\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L}\,}$$

**Reference:** The triple point of water is assigned the exact value:

$$T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$$

This choice ensures that $1 \text{ K} = 1 ^\circ\text{C}$ in magnitude and provides a reproducible experimental reference.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$, $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$, $Q_L = 600 \text{ J}$ (rejected by engine = negative for system, but $Q_L$ here is the magnitude of heat rejected).

**(i) Heat absorbed from hot reservoir:**

Using the absolute scale relation for a reversible engine:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{800}{300} = \frac{8}{3}$$

$$Q_H = Q_L \times \frac{8}{3} = 600 \times \frac{8}{3} = 1600 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{600}{1600} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%$$

**Verification using temperatures:**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 0.625 \checkmark$$

**(iii) Cyclic Clausius integral:**

Heat absorbed by engine (positive for system): $Q_H = +1600 \text{ J}$ at $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$
Heat rejected by engine (negative for system): $Q_L = -600 \text{ J}$ at $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{+1600}{800} + \frac{-600}{300} = 2.000 - 2.000 = 0 \text{ J/K}$$

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0 \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The integral is zero, confirming the cycle is reversible. **(Total: 14 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** State and prove the Clausius Inequality for a general cyclic process. Clearly explain the role of the auxiliary reversible Carnot engines in your proof. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An irreversible heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $600 \text{ K}$ and a cold reservoir at $300 \text{ K}$. During one cycle, it absorbs $1000 \text{ J}$ from the hot reservoir and rejects $700 \text{ J}$ to the cold reservoir.
   (i) Calculate the actual thermal efficiency of the engine.
   (ii) Calculate the Carnot (reversible) efficiency between the same reservoirs.
   (iii) Compute the cyclic Clausius integral and comment on its sign. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Statement and Proof of Clausius Inequality (7 marks):**

**Statement:** For any thermodynamic cycle (reversible or irreversible) executed by a closed system, the cyclic integral of $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$ is always less than or equal to zero:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$$

with equality for a completely reversible cycle and strict inequality for an irreversible cycle.

**Proof:**

Let the closed system exchange infinitesimal heats $\delta Q_i$ with a series of reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$ (where $T_i$ is the boundary temperature for reversible transfer, else the reservoir temperature).

Introduce:
- A **reference reservoir** at fixed temperature $T_0 > 0$.
- A set of **reversible Carnot engines** $R_i$, each operating between the reference reservoir and reservoir $i$.

For each reversible Carnot engine $R_i$, the heat $\delta Q_0^{(i)}$ extracted from the reference reservoir to supply $\delta Q_i$ to the system satisfies:

$$\frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = \frac{\delta Q_0^{(i)}}{T_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta Q_0^{(i)} = T_0 \cdot \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i}$$

**Role of auxiliary engines:** They act as "intermediaries" that allow us to replace the original heat interactions (which may be irreversible) with equivalent reversible interactions with a single reference reservoir. This is the key step that lets us invoke the Second Law in its Kelvin–Planck form.

Summing over all interactions and integrating over the entire cycle:

$$Q_0 = \oint \delta Q_0 = T_0 \oint \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

The composite system (original system + all auxiliary engines + reference reservoir) executes a cycle. By the **Kelvin–Planck statement** of the Second Law, no net work can be extracted from a single reservoir in a cyclic process. The composite system exchanges heat only with the reference reservoir at $T_0$. Therefore:

$$Q_0 \le 0$$

with equality if and only if all processes (original + auxiliary) are reversible.

Since $T_0 > 0$, dividing by $T_0$ yields:

$$\boxed{\,\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0\,} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $T_H = 600 \text{ K}$, $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$, $Q_H = 1000 \text{ J}$ (absorbed), $Q_L = 700 \text{ J}$ (rejected).

**(i) Actual thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{actual} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{700}{1000} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

**(ii) Carnot (reversible) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{600} = 0.50 = 50\%$$

**Note:** $\eta_{actual} < \eta_{Carnot}$, consistent with the Second Law.

**(iii) Cyclic Clausius integral:**

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{+1000}{600} + \frac{-700}{300} = 1.6667 - 2.3333 = -0.6667 \text{ J/K}$$

**Comment:** The integral is **strictly negative** ($\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -0.667 \text{ J/K} < 0$), which correctly identifies the cycle as **irreversible**, consistent with the actual efficiency being less than the Carnot efficiency. The magnitude of the negative value indicates the extent of irreversibility (entropy generation). **(Total: 14 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently forget that $Q_L$ (rejected) must be entered as **negative** in the Clausius integral. Writing $\frac{+700}{300}$ instead of $\frac{-700}{300}$ will yield a wrong sign and a wrong conclusion.
> 2. **Confusing Reservoir vs. System Temperature (–2 marks):** In the Clausius Inequality, $T$ refers to the **boundary temperature** of heat transfer, which equals the reservoir temperature only for reversible (quasi-static) heat transfer. For irreversible transfer, the system temperature differs.
> 3. **Forgetting Units of Temperature (–1 mark):** Always use **Kelvin** in the Clausius integral. Mixing Celsius and Kelvin is a fatal error.
> 4. **Mis-stating Equality Condition (–1 mark):** The equality $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ requires the **entire cycle** to be reversible, not just the heat transfer steps.
> 5. **Using Arbitrary Reference for Absolute Scale (–1 mark):** The triple point of water (273.16 K) is the SI-defined reference — not the ice point (273.15 K), which is approximate.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **Absolute Thermodynamic Scale** is defined via $\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_L}$ using a reversible Carnot engine — independent of working fluid.
- ⚡ **Triple Point of Water = 273.16 K** is the SI reference; $1 \text{ K} = 1 ^\circ\text{C}$ in magnitude.
- ⚡ **Absolute Zero = 0 K** is unattainable (Third Law consequence).
- ⚡ **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \le 0$ — the most general statement of the Second Law for cycles.
- ⚡ **Equality $\Rightarrow$ Reversible cycle; Inequality $\Rightarrow$ Irreversible cycle.**
- ⚡ **Entropy is a property:** $dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ — defined for reversible paths, but $\Delta S$ is path-independent.
- ⚡ **Sign convention:** Heat **to** system is positive; use **absolute temperature (K)** in all Clausius calculations.
- ⚡ **Auxiliary reversible engines** are the key construct in proving the Clausius Inequality — they allow application of the Kelvin–Planck statement.
- ⚡ **Carnot efficiency** is the **upper bound** for any heat engine operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- ⚡ **Coincidence:** Thermodynamic scale ≡ Ideal-gas temperature scale (in the limit of zero pressure).
- ⚡ **Engineering use:** Refrigeration COP limits, power plant benchmarking, cryogenics, chemical equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entropy and Entropy Changes in Various Thermodynamic Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Entropy and Entropy Changes in Various Thermodynamic Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (Clausius Theorem)

**Entropy ($S$)** is a precise thermodynamic property introduced by **Rudolf Clausius** that quantifies the degree of microscopic disorder, molecular randomness, or unavailable energy in a system. Mathematically, it is defined as the integral of the reversible heat transfer divided by the absolute temperature:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)$$

For a finite, reversible process between two equilibrium states (1 and 2), the change in entropy is expressed as:

$$\Delta S = S_2 - S_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}\right)$$

The **SI unit** of entropy is **Joule per Kelvin (J/K)**, and the specific entropy is denoted as $s$ with units **kJ/(kg·K)**. Entropy is an **exact differential** (a point function) and an **intensive property** when referred per unit mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the Clausius definition, the physical significance of entropy as a measure of irreversibility, and the Tds relationships (Gibbs equations) that connect entropy to measurable quantities like $P$, $v$, and $T$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Messy Room" Principle

Imagine your hostel room is perfectly organized (low entropy) — every book stacked, every shirt folded. Now picture a sudden dust storm blowing through the window. The room instantly becomes chaotic (high entropy). To restore order (decrease entropy), you must spend **energy and time** (work input).

In thermodynamics:
- **Energy dispersal** at the molecular level increases entropy.
- A gas expanding into a vacuum spontaneously increases its entropy — no work is done, but disorder rises.
- Reversing this naturally never happens — you cannot "un-mix" scrambled eggs.
- The **2nd Law of Thermodynamics** simply states: *The total entropy of the universe always increases for an irreversible process, and remains constant for a reversible (ideal) process.*

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Entropy is NOT a measure of "energy loss" but rather a measure of **energy quality degradation**. High-entropy energy (e.g., low-temperature heat) is less useful for doing work, even though the First Law is fully satisfied.

## 1.3 Why Entropy Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Application | Role of Entropy |
|---|---|
| **Steam Power Plants** | Determines the efficiency limit (Carnot efficiency $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$) |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Governs the Coefficient of Performance (COP) |
| **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines** | Isentropic flow analysis (constant $s$) |
| **Compressors & Nozzles** | Deviation from isentropic process = irreversibility |
| **Chemical Reactions** | Predicts spontaneity via Gibbs free energy |

---

> **Key Takeaway:** Entropy is the bridge between the **First Law** (energy conservation) and the **Second Law** (direction and quality of energy transformations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Tds Equations (Gibbs Equations)

The Tds equations are the **most heavily tested derivations** in KTU examinations for Module 3. They relate entropy to measurable properties.

Combining the **First Law** ($\delta Q = dU + \delta W$) with the **Second Law** ($\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$) for a closed system undergoing a reversible process:

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$$

This gives the **First Tds Equation**:

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$$

For a simple compressible system with enthalpy $H = U + PV$ (so $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$), we substitute:

$$T\,dS = dH - V\,dP$$

This is the **Second Tds Equation**:

$$T\,dS = dH - V\,dP$$

## 2.2 Differential Forms in Terms of Measurable Properties

For a unit mass of a simple compressible substance, using $du = c_v\,dT$ and $dh = c_p\,dT$ (for ideal gases), we get:

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + P\,dv$$

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - v\,dP$$

These are the **two most critical equations** to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE and CE exams.

## 2.3 Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas — Master Equations

For **1 kg of an ideal gas**, three equivalent expressions exist for entropy change between states 1 and 2:

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) + c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The third form is rarely used directly but is useful for proving inter-relations. Always verify which two properties are given in the problem to pick the right equation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Process / Concept | Entropy Change Formula $\Delta s$ | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Reversible** (any substance) | $\Delta s = \int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\delta q_{rev}}{T}$ | All processes |
| 2 | **Isothermal Ideal Gas** | $\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right) = -R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | $T_1 = T_2$ |
| 3 | **Isobaric (constant P)** | $\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | $P_1 = P_2$ |
| 4 | **Isochoric (constant v)** | $\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | $v_1 = v_2$ |
| 5 | **Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic)** | $\Delta s = 0$ | $q = 0,\ \text{reversible}$ |
| 6 | **Polytropic** $Pv^n = \text{const}$ | $\Delta s = c_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - n}{n - 1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | General $n$ |
| 7 | **Incompressible Substance** (liquid/solid) | $\Delta s = c \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$ | $v \approx \text{const}$ |
| 8 | **Phase Change** (at constant T,P) | $\Delta s = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$ | Latent heat process |
| 9 | **Isothermal of Steam** | $\Delta s = s_g - s_f$ (from tables) | Saturation tables |
| 10 | **Universe / Isolated** | $\Delta s_{univ} = \Delta s_{sys} + \Delta s_{surr}$ | $\geq 0$ always |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The isentropic relations ($Pv^\gamma = \text{const}$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $T P^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$) form the backbone of **compressor, turbine, nozzle, and diffuser design** in jet engines, rocket propulsion, and cryogenic systems.

---

> **The "Why" Behind Each Formula:** All entropy-change formulas are derivable from the **two Tds equations**. The First Tds Equation is favored for *constant-volume* and *temperature-driven* processes; the Second Tds Equation is preferred for *constant-pressure* and *throttling* (isenthalpic) processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First Tds Equation ($T\,ds = du + P\,dv$)

**Step 1: Start from the First Law for a closed system undergoing a reversible process**

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + \delta W_{rev}$$

**Step 2: For a quasi-equilibrium (reversible) process, the work is purely boundary work**

$$\delta W_{rev} = P\,dV$$

**Step 3: Substitute back**

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + P\,dV$$

**Step 4: Apply the Second Law (Clausius definition) which says $\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS$**

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$$

**Step 5: Divide by mass to get the per-unit-mass form**

$$\boxed{T\,ds = du + P\,dv}$$

This is the **First Tds Equation** — used in KTU problems involving entropy change with $T$ and $v$ as independent variables.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Second Tds Equation ($T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$)

**Step 1: Begin with the First Tds Equation**

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv$$

**Step 2: Express $du$ using the definition of enthalpy, $h = u + Pv$**

$$dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dP$$

**Step 3: Solve for $du + P\,dv$**

$$du + P\,dv = dh - v\,dP$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the First Tds Equation**

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

**Step 5: Final per-unit-mass form**

$$\boxed{T\,ds = dh - v\,dP}$$

This is the **Second Tds Equation** — used in problems where $T$ and $P$ are the independent variables.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Entropy Change of an Ideal Gas (Master Equation)

**Step 1: Use the First Tds Equation for an ideal gas**

For an ideal gas, $du = c_v\,dT$, so:

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + P\,dv$$

**Step 2: Apply the ideal gas equation, $Pv = RT$**

$$P = \frac{RT}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P\,dv = \frac{RT}{v}\,dv$$

**Step 3: Substituting back**

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + \frac{RT}{v}\,dv$$

**Step 4: Divide by $T$**

$$ds = c_v\,\frac{dT}{T} + R\,\frac{dv}{v}$$

**Step 5: Integrate from state 1 to state 2 (assuming $c_v$ is constant)**

$$\int_{1}^{2} ds = c_v \int_{1}^{2} \frac{dT}{T} + R \int_{1}^{2} \frac{dv}{v}$$

**Step 6: Final result (Form I)**

$$\boxed{\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)}$$

**Step 7: Derive Form II using the Second Tds Equation**

Start with $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ and $dh = c_p\,dT$. Using $v = RT/P$:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - \frac{RT}{P}\,dP$$

**Step 8: Divide by $T$ and integrate**

$$ds = c_p\,\frac{dT}{T} - R\,\frac{dP}{P}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)}$$

**Step 9: Equate Form I and Form II to derive Form III**

Since both equal $\Delta s$, and using $c_p - c_v = R$:

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) + c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Entropy Change for a Polytropic Process ($Pv^n = \text{const}$)

**Step 1: Use the ideal gas Form II**

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 2: Use the polytropic relation between T and P**

For $Pv^n = \text{const}$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{n}{n-1}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and simplify**

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \cdot \frac{n}{n-1} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = \left[c_p - \frac{nR}{n-1}\right] \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4: Use $R = c_p - c_v$ and factor**

$$\Delta s = \frac{(n-1)c_p - n(c_p - c_v)}{n-1} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = \frac{nc_v - c_p}{n-1} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 5: Factor out $c_v$ and use $\gamma = c_p/c_v$**

$$\Delta s = \frac{c_v(n - \gamma)}{n-1} \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 6: Final boxed result**

$$\boxed{\Delta s_{polytropic} = c_v \cdot \frac{\gamma - n}{n - 1} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If $n = \gamma$ (reversible adiabatic), then $\gamma - n = 0$, giving $\Delta s = 0$ — which is the **isentropic condition**. This validates the derivation.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Entropy Change of an Incompressible Substance

**Step 1: Start from the Second Tds Equation**

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

**Step 2: For an incompressible substance, $dh = c\,dT$ and $v = \text{const}$**

$$T\,ds = c\,dT - v\,dP$$

**Step 3: For a *solid* or *incompressible liquid*, $v$ is essentially constant, so the $v\,dP$ term is negligible**

$$T\,ds \approx c\,dT$$

**Step 4: Divide by $T$ and integrate**

$$\Delta s = c \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$
