> **Physical Reasoning:** Pressure changes barely affect the entropy of a liquid or solid because their specific volume is tiny. Only temperature changes matter. This is why heating a copper block from 25 °C to 100 °C only changes its entropy by $c \cdot \ln(373/298)$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** 2 kg of air is compressed in a piston-cylinder device from 100 kPa, 27 °C to 800 kPa, 200 °C. Determine the entropy change.
*(Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K for air.)*

**Solution:**

$$\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{473}{300}\right) - 0.287 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{800}{100}\right)$$

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \cdot (0.4555) - 0.287 \cdot (2.0794)$$

$$\Delta s = 0.4578 - 0.5968 = -0.1390 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Total entropy change:**

$$\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s = 2 \times (-0.1390) = -0.278 \text{ kJ/K}$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates the air's entropy *decreased* (it became more ordered due to compression and heating profile). The surroundings gained more entropy than the system lost, satisfying the Second Law.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Entropy Calculation Methodology

The following block diagram outlines the **decision tree** a KTU student should follow when solving entropy-change problems:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Two States 1 and 2] --> B{What type of system?}
    B --> C[Ideal Gas]
    B --> D[Incompressible Substance]
    B --> E[Pure Substance Phase Change]

    C --> F{Known Properties?}
    F --> G[T and P given]
    F --> H[T and v given]
    F --> I[P and v given]

    G --> J[Use Form II: cp ln T2/T1 minus R ln P2/P1]
    H --> K[Use Form I: cv ln T2/T1 plus R ln v2/v1]
    I --> L[Use Form III: cp ln v2/v1 plus cv ln P2/P1]

    D --> M[Delta s equals c ln T2 over T1]
    E --> N[Delta s equals h_fg divided by T_sat]

    J --> O[Multiply by mass to get Delta S]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P[Check Second Law: Delta S universe greater than or equal to 0]
    P --> Q[End: Final Answer]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Entropy Behavior in Thermodynamic Processes

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Reversible Isothermal Expansion] --> B1[Delta s positive]
    A2[Reversible Adiabatic Process] --> B2[Delta s equals 0 Isentropic]
    A3[Reversible Isobaric Heating] --> B3[Delta s positive]
    A4[Irreversible Free Expansion] --> B4[Delta s positive even though Q equals 0]
    A5[Heat Rejection to Cold Reservoir] --> B5[Delta s positive for surroundings]
```

## 4.3 Process Representation Table (T-s Diagram Mapping)

The **Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram** is the most important graphical tool for entropy analysis. The following table describes how each process appears on a T-s chart:

| Process | Curve on T-s Diagram | Entropy Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Isothermal** | Horizontal line | $s$ increases (expansion) or decreases (compression) |
| **Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic)** | Vertical line | $s = \text{const}$ |
| **Isobaric Heating** | Exponential-like curve | $s$ increases with $T$ |
| **Isochoric Heating** | Steeper exponential curve | $s$ increases; lies above isobaric |
| **Polytropic (general $n$)** | Curve with slope $\left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial s}\right)_P = \dfrac{T}{c_p}$ | $s$ direction depends on $n$ |
| **Carnot Cycle** | Rectangle (2 isotherms + 2 adiabats) | Closed loop: $\oint ds = 0$ |

> **KTU Insight:** On a T-s diagram, the area under any reversible process curve represents the **heat transfer**: $Q_{rev} = \int T\,ds$. This is the foundation of the Carnot efficiency derivation.

---

> **Practical Engineering Application:** In real turbines and compressors, engineers plot the actual process alongside the ideal isentropic process on an **h-s (Mollier) diagram** to calculate isentropic efficiency:
> $$\eta_{isentropic} = \frac{h_1 - h_2^{actual}}{h_1 - h_2^{ideal}}$$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define entropy. State its physical significance and mention its unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Entropy is a thermodynamic property defined via the Clausius theorem as:

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Physical Significance:** Entropy is a measure of the microscopic disorder, molecular randomness, or the unavailability of energy for doing useful work. It is a state function and an exact differential, meaning its value depends only on the state of the system, not the path taken. **(1 Mark)**

**Unit:** In SI, entropy is measured in J/K (specific entropy in kJ/kg·K). **(1 Mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Prove that the entropy change of an ideal gas during an isothermal process is $\Delta s = R \ln(v_2/v_1)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process, $T_1 = T_2$. From the First Tds Equation:

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + P\,dv$$

Since $dT = 0$ for isothermal, $T\,ds = P\,dv$. Using $Pv = RT$:

$$T\,ds = \frac{RT}{v}\,dv \quad \Rightarrow \quad ds = R\,\frac{dv}{v}$$

Integrating from state 1 to state 2:

$$\int_{1}^{2} ds = R \int_{1}^{2} \frac{dv}{v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

---

### **QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **two Tds equations** from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air at 200 kPa and 30 °C is compressed isentropically to a pressure of 800 kPa. Calculate the final temperature and the work done per kg of air. **(7 Marks)**

*(Take $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K)*

---

#### **Solution A(a): Derivation of the Two Tds Equations (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** For a closed system undergoing a quasi-equilibrium (reversible) process, the First Law gives:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = dU + \delta W_{rev} = dU + P\,dV \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2:** From the Second Law (Clausius inequality), for a reversible process:

$$\delta Q_{rev} = T\,dS \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions:

$$T\,dS = dU + P\,dV \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

This is the **First Tds Equation**.

**Step 4:** For a unit mass:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** To obtain the Second Tds Equation, use the enthalpy definition $h = u + Pv$:

$$dh = du + P\,dv + v\,dP$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging: $du + P\,dv = dh - v\,dP$. Substituting into the First Tds Equation:

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \quad \text{(2 Marks — 1 for substitution logic, 1 for final form)}$$

This is the **Second Tds Equation**.

---

#### **Solution A(b): Isentropic Compression of Air (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $T_1 = 303$ K, $P_2 = 800$ kPa, isentropic (reversible adiabatic, so $s_1 = s_2$).

**Step 1:** For an isentropic process of an ideal gas:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\frac{T_2}{303} = \left(\frac{800}{200}\right)^{\frac{1.4 - 1}{1.4}} = (4)^{0.2857}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the exponent:

$$0.2857 = \frac{0.4}{1.4}$$

$$(4)^{0.2857} = 1.4860 \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $T_2$:

$$T_2 = 303 \times 1.4860 = 450.26 \text{ K} = 177.26\,°\text{C} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** Work done in a reversible adiabatic process is given by:

$$w = -\Delta u = -c_v(T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute:

$$w = -0.718 \times (450.26 - 303) = -0.718 \times 147.26$$

**Step 7:** Final answer:

$$w = -105.73 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

> **Sign Convention:** The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the system (compression).

---

### **QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **entropy change of an ideal gas** in terms of (i) temperature and pressure, and (ii) temperature and specific volume. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A piston-cylinder device contains 0.5 kg of nitrogen at 120 kPa, 30 °C. The gas is compressed isothermally to one-third of its initial volume. Determine the entropy change of the nitrogen and the heat transferred. **(7 Marks)**

*(Take $R = 0.2968$ kJ/kg·K for nitrogen, $\ln(1/3) = -1.0986$)*

---

#### **Solution B(a): Entropy Change Derivation (7 Marks)**

**(i) In terms of $T$ and $P$:**

Start with the Second Tds Equation: $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$. For an ideal gas, $dh = c_p\,dT$ and $v = RT/P$:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - \frac{RT}{P}\,dP$$

Divide by $T$: $ds = c_p\,\dfrac{dT}{T} - R\,\dfrac{dP}{P}$

Integrate from state 1 to state 2:

$$\boxed{\Delta s = c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) In terms of $T$ and $v$:**

Start with the First Tds Equation: $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$. For an ideal gas, $du = c_v\,dT$ and $P = RT/v$:

$$T\,ds = c_v\,dT + \frac{RT}{v}\,dv$$

Divide by $T$: $ds = c_v\,\dfrac{dT}{T} + R\,\dfrac{dv}{v}$

Integrate from state 1 to state 2:

$$\boxed{\Delta s = c_v \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)} \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**Concluding remark:** Since $c_p = c_v + R$ and $Pv = RT$, the two forms are equivalent. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Solution B(b): Isothermal Compression of Nitrogen (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $m = 0.5$ kg, $P_1 = 120$ kPa, $T_1 = 303$ K, $v_2 = v_1/3$, isothermal ⇒ $T_1 = T_2 = 303$ K.

**Step 1:** For an isothermal process of an ideal gas:

$$\Delta s = R \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $v_2/v_1 = 1/3$:

$$\Delta s = 0.2968 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = 0.2968 \times (-1.0986)$$

**Step 3:** Specific entropy change:

$$\Delta s = -0.3261 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 4:** Total entropy change of nitrogen:

$$\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s = 0.5 \times (-0.3261) = -0.1631 \text{ kJ/K} \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 5:** For an isothermal process of an ideal gas, $\Delta u = 0$, so by the First Law:

$$Q = W = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right) \quad \text{(1 Mark)}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate $Q$:

$$Q = 0.5 \times 0.2968 \times 303 \times (-1.0986)$$

$$Q = 0.5 \times 0.2968 \times 303 \times (-1.0986) = -49.40 \text{ kJ} \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

> **Sign Interpretation:** The negative $Q$ indicates heat is **rejected** from the system to the surroundings (compression raises the temperature, but isothermal cooling must occur to keep $T$ constant). The compression work done on the gas equals the heat rejected.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by mass:** When asked for total entropy change $\Delta S$ (not specific $\Delta s$), students often forget the $m$ factor. This costs **2–3 marks** in ESE.
> 2. **Sign convention errors:** Heat rejected → negative $Q$; entropy of system decreases during compression → negative $\Delta s$. Mixing signs costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Using $c_p$ instead of $c_v$** in the isochoric formula, or vice versa. Always re-read the constraint (constant $P$ vs constant $v$) before picking the formula.
> 4. **Polytropic derivation:** Students often skip showing the relation $P_2/P_1 = (T_2/T_1)^{n/(n-1)}$. This step is worth **1 full mark** in derivations.
> 5. **Units:** Specific entropy must be in **kJ/(kg·K)**; total entropy in **kJ/K**. Mixing units is a silent killer — KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this.
> 6. **Tds derivation:** Many students write $T\,dS = dU + PdV$ without justifying that the process is **reversible** (so that $\delta W = PdV$ exactly). This missing condition costs **1 mark**.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**

- ⭐ **Clausius definition:** $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$ — entropy is a **state function** and **exact differential**.
- ⭐ **Two Tds Equations** (most tested):
  - $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ (First)
  - $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$ (Second)
- ⭐ **Three Master Equations for Ideal Gas Entropy Change:**
  - $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$
  - $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2/T_1) + R \ln(v_2/v_1)$
  - $\Delta s = c_p \ln(v_2/v_1) + c_v \ln(P_2/P_1)$
- ⭐ **Special Process Results:**
  - Isothermal: $\Delta s = R \ln(v_2/v_1) = -R \ln(P_2/P_1)$
  - Isobaric: $\Delta s = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1)$
  - Isochoric: $\Delta s = c_v \ln(T_2/T_1)$
  - Isentropic (reversible adiabatic): $\Delta s = 0$
  - Polytropic: $\Delta s = c_v \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - n}{n - 1} \cdot \ln(T_1/T_2)$
  - Incompressible: $\Delta s = c \ln(T_2/T_1)$
- ⭐ **Second Law (key exam line):** $\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$
- ⭐ **T-s Diagram is your best friend:** Area under reversible curve = heat transfer; vertical line = isentropic.
- ⭐ **Isentropic relations** ($Pv^\gamma = \text{const}$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $TP^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$) are essential for **turbine, compressor, nozzle, and diffuser** problems.
- ⭐ **Sign conventions are critical:** Expansion → $s$ increases; Compression → $s$ decreases; Isentropic → $s$ constant.
- ⭐ **Phase change entropy:** $\Delta s = h_{fg}/T_{sat}$ — must use **absolute temperature** in Kelvin.
- ⭐ **KTU-Favorite Numerical Setup:** Always check whether the process is reversible or irreversible — for irreversible processes, you must compute entropy change **using a reversible path** between the same two end states.

> **Final Word:** Master the Tds equations, memorize the 10 process-specific formulas, and always draw a T-s diagram before solving. This will fetch you full marks in the KTU 2024 ESE Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Increase of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Increase of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Increase of Entropy (PIE):**
> *For any process occurring in an isolated system, the entropy of the system always **increases** (for an irreversible process) or remains **constant** (for a reversible process). In other words, the entropy of an isolated system never decreases.*

Mathematically, for an isolated system (no heat transfer, no mass transfer with surroundings):

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

The equality sign holds only for a **completely reversible** process, while the strict inequality holds for any **irreversible** (real) process.

## 1.2 The Clausius Inequality as Foundation

The Principle of Increase of Entropy is a direct consequence of the **Clausius Inequality**, which itself is a mathematical expression of the Second Law of Thermodynamics:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **reversible cycle**: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$
>
> For an **irreversible cycle**: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$

Here, $\delta Q$ is the infinitesimal heat transferred at the boundary of the system, and $T$ is the **absolute temperature (in Kelvin)** at the boundary where the heat transfer occurs.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Messy Room"**
>
> Imagine a perfectly tidy, organized room (low entropy). Over time, without anyone cleaning it, the room naturally becomes messy — clothes on the floor, books scattered, dust gathering. This natural progression from order to disorder happens **spontaneously** and never spontaneously reverses.
>
> Similarly, energy in the universe naturally disperses from concentrated, useful forms (like hot steam) to dispersed, less useful forms (like low-temperature ambient heat). This **irreversible dispersion** is precisely what the Principle of Increase of Entropy describes.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C** (the theoretical lower bound of entropy)
- **Entropy Units:** **J/K** (extensive property)
- **Specific Entropy Units:** **J/(kg·K)**

---

## 1.5 Statement of the Principle (KTU Board-Ready Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The total entropy of any isolated thermodynamic system tends to increase over time, approaching a maximum value at thermodynamic equilibrium."**
>
> — Statement attributed to Rudolf Clausius (1865)

This principle is one of the most profound physical laws, second only to the conservation of energy in importance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Defining an Isolated System

An **isolated system** is one that exchanges neither **energy** (heat $Q$ or work $W$) nor **mass** ($m$) with its surroundings.

For such a system: $dU = 0$, $dV = 0$, $dN = 0$

### Step 2: Applying the Clausius Inequality

Starting from Clausius Inequality for any cycle:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$$

Decompose the cycle into two arbitrary processes (1 → 2 via path A and 2 → 1 via path B):

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} + \int_{2B}^{1} \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$$

### Step 3: Reverse One Path and Identify Entropy

The entropy change between two equilibrium states is a **state function** and is defined via a reversible path:

$$dS = \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$

So, reversing the second integral:

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} \leq \int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} = S_2 - S_1$$

Therefore:

$$S_2 - S_1 \geq \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$$

### Step 4: Enforce the Isolation Condition

For an **isolated system**, no heat crosses the boundary: $\delta Q = 0$.

Therefore:

$$S_2 - S_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

This is the **Principle of Increase of Entropy**.

## 2.2 Equality and Inequality Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversible Process:** $\Delta S_{universe} = 0$
>
> **Irreversible Process:** $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$
>
> **Impossible Process (Violates 2nd Law):** $\Delta S_{universe} < 0$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Condition** | **Units** |
|:------------|:----------------|:--------------|:----------|
| $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ | Principle of Increase of Entropy | Isolated system | J/K |
| $\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ | Universal entropy change | Any process | J/K |
| $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ | Clausius Inequality | Any cycle | J/K |
| $dS = \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Entropy definition (state function) | Reversible path | J/K |
| $\Delta S = c_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Ideal gas entropy change | Closed system | J/(kg·K) |
| $\Delta S = c_v \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R\ln\frac{v_2}{v_1}$ | Ideal gas entropy change (v-form) | Closed system | J/(kg·K) |
| $\Delta S_{gen} = \Delta S_{universe} \geq 0$ | Entropy generation | 2nd Law test | J/K |
| $\Delta S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \int\frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | Entropy generation (system view) | Any process | J/K |
| $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{gen}$ | Lost work / Gouy-Stodola theorem | Irreversibility | J |
| $\phi = \text{Exergy} = (u - u_0) - T_0(s - s_0) + P_0(v - v_0)$ | Specific flow exergy | Closed system | J/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The integral $\int\frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ is taken along the **actual (possibly irreversible) process path**, while the entropy change $\Delta S$ is evaluated along any **reversible path** connecting the same two end states.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Principle of Increase of Entropy is foundational to:

- **Heat Engine Design:** Determines maximum theoretical efficiency (Carnot efficiency)
- **Refrigeration & Heat Pumps:** Sets the Coefficient of Performance (COP) limits
- **Power Plant Analysis:** Quantifies irreversibilities (lost work)
- **Chemical Engineering:** Drives reaction spontaneity (Gibbs Free Energy)
- **Cryogenics:** Sets fundamental limits on cooling toward absolute zero
- **Information Theory:** Boltzmann's $S = k_B \ln W$ connects entropy to microscopic disorder

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clausius Inequality → Principle of Increase of Entropy

### Starting Point: The Second Law Statement by Clausius

It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.

### Mathematical Setup

Consider any **thermodynamic cycle** consisting of two arbitrary processes connecting equilibrium states **1** and **2**:

- Process **A**: Path from state 1 → state 2 (any path, possibly irreversible)
- Process **B**: Path from state 2 → state 1 (a reversible path)

Since A + B together form a complete cycle, Clausius Inequality gives:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = \int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} + \int_{2B}^{1} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} \leq 0$$

### Algebraic Manipulation

Reverse the order of integration in the second term. Note that reversing limits changes the sign:

$$\int_{2B}^{1} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} = -\int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b}$$

Substituting:

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} - \int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} \leq 0$$

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} \leq \int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b}$$

### Identifying Entropy Change

Since process **B** is reversible, by the definition of entropy:

$$\int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T} = S_2 - S_1$$

Therefore:

$$S_2 - S_1 \geq \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b}$$

### Special Case: Isolated System

For an isolated system, $\delta Q = 0$ at the boundary, so:

$$S_2 - S_1 \geq 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0}$$

This completes the derivation of the **Principle of Increase of Entropy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Flow Summary:**
> Clausius Statement → Clausius Inequality → Decompose cycle → Reverse integration → Identify entropy → Impose isolation → Obtain PIE

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Entropy Generation for a Closed System

For a closed system undergoing any process, the **entropy balance** is:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = \sum_{in}\frac{\dot{Q}_j}{T_j} + \sum_{in}\dot{m}_i s_i - \sum_{out}\dot{m}_e s_e + \dot{S}_{gen}$$

In differential form for a closed system (no mass flow):

$$dS = \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} + \delta S_{gen}$$

Where $\delta S_{gen} \geq 0$ is the entropy generated due to irreversibilities.

### Interpretation

- $dS$ = **Total** entropy change of the system
- $\frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ = **Entropy transfer** with surroundings
- $\delta S_{gen}$ = **Entropy generated** within the system

Rearranging:

$$\delta S_{gen} = dS - \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \geq 0$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gouy-Stodola Theorem (Lost Work)

The **lost work** $W_{lost}$ is the difference between the work output of a reversible process and the actual (irreversible) process:

$$W_{lost} = W_{rev} - W_{actual}$$

Consider a system interacting with a thermal reservoir at $T_0$. The lost work equals the $T_0$ times the entropy generated:

$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

### Proof Sketch

For a process between states 1 and 2 of a closed system in a thermal environment at $T_0$:

$$\Delta S_{total} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = \Delta S_{sys} + \frac{Q_{out}}{T_0}$$

By the second law: $\Delta S_{total} \geq 0$, so:

$$Q_{out} \geq -T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{sys}$$

Applying the First Law: $Q_{out} = \Delta U + W_{actual}$, therefore:

$$W_{actual} \leq \Delta U + T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{sys} = W_{rev}$$

The difference is the lost work:

$$W_{lost} = W_{rev} - W_{actual} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{total} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

This is the **Gouy-Stodola Theorem**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem [KTU University Exam Pattern]:**
> A perfectly insulated (isolated) rigid tank contains 2 kg of air at 300 K and 150 kPa. A paddle wheel inside the tank does 50 kJ of work on the air. Assuming air behaves as an ideal gas with $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), determine whether the process is reversible, irreversible, or impossible.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $m = 2$ kg, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 150$ kPa
- $W_{in} = 50$ kJ (work done ON the system)
- $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) for air

**Step 1: Apply First Law (Isolated Rigid Tank → Q = 0, ΔV = 0)**

$$\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - (-W_{in}) = W_{in} = 50 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2: Find Final Temperature**

$$\Delta U = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$50 = 2 \times 0.718 \times (T_2 - 300)$$

$$T_2 - 300 = \frac{50}{1.436} = 34.82 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 334.82 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3: Find Final Pressure (Rigid tank → constant volume)**

Using ideal gas law with $V_1 = V_2$:

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \implies P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

$$P_2 = 150 \times \frac{334.82}{300} = 167.41 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Entropy Change of the Air**

For an ideal gas in a closed system:

$$\Delta S = m \left[ c_v \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R \ln\frac{v_2}{v_1} \right]$$

Since $v_2 = v_1$ (rigid tank), the second term vanishes:

$$\Delta S = 2 \times 0.718 \times \ln\left(\frac{334.82}{300}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = 1.436 \times \ln(1.1161) = 1.436 \times 0.1098$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = +0.1577 \text{ kJ/K} = +157.7 \text{ J/K}}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Principle of Increase of Entropy**

The tank is isolated, so $\Delta S_{surr} = 0$:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.1577 + 0 = +0.1577 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Since $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$, the process is **IRREVERSIBLE** and is **allowed by the Second Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The paddle wheel work introduces irreversibility (friction → heat → entropy), causing entropy generation even though no heat leaves the system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Logical Flow of the Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Clausius Statement of 2nd Law"] --> B["Clausius Inequality"]
    B --> C["Cycle Decomposition Path A and B"]
    C --> D["Reverse Integration Limits"]
    D --> E["Identify Entropy as State Function"]
    E --> F["Apply Isolation Condition Q equals 0"]
    F --> G["Principle of Increase of Entropy"]
    G --> H["Reversible Process: Delta S equals 0"]
    G --> I["Irreversible Process: Delta S greater than 0"]
    I --> J["Entropy Generation S_gen positive"]
    J --> K["Lost Work from Gouy Stodola"]
    H --> L["Equilibrium Maximum Entropy State"]
    I --> L
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of Entropy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Closed System Boundary"] --> B["Entropy Transfer Q over T_b"]
    A --> C["Entropy Generation S_gen"]
    B --> D["Total Entropy Change dS"]
    C --> D
    D --> E{"Isolated System?"}
    E -->|Yes| F["dS equals S_gen"]
    E -->|No| G["dS equals Q over T_b plus S_gen"]
    F --> H{"Process Type?"}
    G --> H
    H -->|Reversible| I["S_gen equals 0"]
    H -->|Irreversible| J["S_gen greater than 0"]
    H -->|Impossible| K["S_gen less than 0 violates 2nd Law"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — P–V Diagram Showing Reversible vs Irreversible Paths

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["State 1: P1 V1"] --> B["Path A Irreversible"]
    A --> C["Path B Reversible Isothermal"]
    B --> D["State 2: P2 V2"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["Entropy Change dS same for both paths"]
    E --> F["Path A: Delta S_universe greater than 0"]
    E --> G["Path B: Delta S_universe equals 0"]
    F --> H["Entropy Generated Lost Work Positive"]
    G --> I["No Lost Work Ideal Process"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Key Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Heat Transfer Q at Boundary"] --> B["Entropy Transfer Q over T_b"]
    C["Internal Irreversibilities"] --> D["Entropy Generation S_gen"]
    B --> E["Total Entropy Change dS of System"]
    D --> E
    F["Surroundings"] --> G["Entropy Change of Surroundings"]
    E --> H["Entropy Change of Universe"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Greater than Zero?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Process Allowed by 2nd Law"]
    I -->|Equals Zero| K["Reversible Ideal Process"]
    I -->|Less than Zero| L["Impossible Violates 2nd Law"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The diagrams above map the *functional architecture* and *logical dependencies* of the Principle of Increase of Entropy. The entropy change of a system between two equilibrium states depends **only** on the end states (state function property), but the entropy generated depends on the **path taken** (process-dependent quantity).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the Principle of Increase of Entropy.**

**Model Answer:**

The Principle of Increase of Entropy states that for any process occurring in an isolated system, the entropy of the system either remains constant (reversible process) or increases (irreversible process).

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

**Equality** holds for an internally reversible process; **strict inequality** holds for an irreversible process. This is a direct consequence of the Second Law of Thermodynamics and the Clausius Inequality. It implies that the entropy of the universe is constantly increasing, and equilibrium corresponds to the state of **maximum entropy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Statement (1M) + Equation (1M) + Explanation of equality/inequality (1M)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between entropy change of a system and entropy generation. Under what condition is entropy generation zero?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Entropy Change (ΔS)** | **Entropy Generation (S_gen)** |
|:-----------|:------------------------|:-------------------------------|
| Nature | State function | Path function |
| Cause | Change of state | Irreversibilities |
| Value for reversible | Non-zero possible | Zero |
| Value for isolated | ΔS ≥ 0 | S_gen ≥ 0 |

**Condition for S_gen = 0:** The process must be **internally reversible** (no friction, no unrestrained expansion, no mixing, no chemical reaction, no heat transfer across finite temperature difference).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition of each (1M each) + Condition (1M)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the Clausius Inequality, derive the Principle of Increase of Entropy for an isolated system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at 800 K and a cold reservoir at 300 K, delivering 100 kJ of work per cycle while receiving 400 kJ of heat from the hot reservoir. Determine:
- (i) The entropy change of the hot reservoir, cold reservoir, and the universe per cycle.
- (ii) Whether the engine satisfies the Second Law.
- (iii) The lost work per cycle.

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1: Statement of Clausius Inequality**

For any cyclic process:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$$

**Step 2: Decompose the cycle** into two paths A (1→2) and B (2→1):

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} + \int_{2B}^{1} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} \leq 0$$

**Step 3:** Reverse path B to obtain 1→2 and identify the entropy change:

$$\int_{1A}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b} \leq \int_{1B}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_B}{T_b} = S_2 - S_1$$

$$S_2 - S_1 \geq \int_{1}^{2} \frac{\delta Q_A}{T_b}$$

**Step 4: Apply isolation** ($\delta Q_A = 0$):

$$\boxed{S_2 - S_1 \geq 0}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion** — This is the Principle of Increase of Entropy, stating that the entropy of an isolated system can never decrease.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $T_H = 800$ K, $T_C = 300$ K, $W_{net} = 100$ kJ, $Q_H = 400$ kJ

**(i) Entropy Changes:**

**Hot Reservoir** (loses heat):

$$\Delta S_H = -\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = -\frac{400}{800} = -0.500 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Cold Reservoir** (gains heat — by First Law: $Q_C = Q_H - W = 400 - 100 = 300$ kJ):

$$\Delta S_C = +\frac{Q_C}{T_C} = +\frac{300}{300} = +1.000 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_C = -0.500 + 1.000 = +0.500 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**(ii) Second Law Check:**

Since $\Delta S_{univ} = +0.500$ kJ/K $> 0$, **the engine satisfies the Second Law** (process is allowed).

**(iii) Lost Work (Gouy-Stodola):**

$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{gen} = 300 \times 0.500 = 150 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign error in $Q_C$:** Students often write $Q_C = Q_H + W$ instead of $Q_C = Q_H - W$.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Temperature MUST be in Kelvin, not Celsius. Always convert °C → K before entropy calculations.
> 3. **Lost work formula:** Forgetting to multiply $T_0$ (in K) with $\Delta S_{gen}$ (in kJ/K) gives wrong units.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of entropy generation. How is it related to the Principle of Increase of Entropy? Discuss with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An insulated rigid tank of volume 0.5 m³ initially contains air at 200 kPa and 25°C. A paddle wheel does 90 kJ of work on the air. Determine:
- (i) The final temperature of air.
- (ii) The entropy change of the air.
- (iii) Whether the process is reversible or irreversible.

Use $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K).

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Entropy Generation (S_gen):**

Entropy generation is the entropy **created within a system** due to irreversibilities such as friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing of different gases, chemical reactions, and heat transfer across finite temperature differences.

**Entropy Balance Equation:**

$$dS_{sys} = \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} + \delta S_{gen}$$

**Relationship to PIE:**

For an isolated system ($\delta Q = 0$):

$$dS_{sys} = \delta S_{gen} \geq 0$$

Thus, $\delta S_{gen}$ is the **measure of irreversibility**. The Principle of Increase of Entropy is essentially a statement that $\delta S_{gen} \geq 0$ for **all real processes**.

**Examples:**

- **Friction in bearings:** Mechanical energy → heat → entropy increase
- **Heat transfer across finite ΔT:** Spontaneous heat flow from hot to cold → entropy increase
- **Unrestrained expansion (Joule expansion):** Gas expands into vacuum → entropy increase
- **Mixing of different gases:** Diffusion → entropy increase

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $V = 0.5$ m³, $P_1 = 200$ kPa, $T_1 = 25°C = 298.15$ K, $W_{in} = 90$ kJ (paddle work), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 1: Mass of Air**

$$m = \frac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} = \frac{200 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 298.15}$$

$$m = \frac{100}{85.57} = 1.169 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Final Temperature (Insulated rigid tank → Q = 0)**

First Law: $\Delta U = Q - W = 0 - (-W_{in}) = W_{in}$

$$m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 90 \text{ kJ}$$

$$1.169 \times 0.718 \times (T_2 - 298.15) = 90$$

$$(T_2 - 298.15) = \frac{90}{0.8394} = 107.21 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 405.36 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3: Entropy Change (constant volume process for ideal gas)**

Since the tank is rigid, $v_2 = v_1$:

$$\Delta S = m \cdot c_v \cdot \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 1.169 \times 0.718 \times \ln\left(\frac{405.36}{298.15}\right)$$

$$\Delta S = 0.8394 \times \ln(1.3595) = 0.8394 \times 0.3070$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = +0.2577 \text{ kJ/K} = +257.7 \text{ J/K}}$$

**Step 4: Process Classification**

Since the tank is isolated:

$$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} = 0.2577 + 0 = +0.2577 \text{ kJ/K}$$

Because $\Delta S_{universe} > 0$, the process is **IRREVERSIBLE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong First Law sign convention:** Always state clearly that work done **on** the system is negative (engineering convention: $W > 0$ when done BY system).
> 2. **Using Celsius in logs:** Entropy formulas require absolute temperature. Convert all temperatures to Kelvin **before** applying the formula.
> 3. **Forgetting the mass calculation:** Students often assume $m = 1$ kg, leading to wrong answers. Always compute $m$ using the ideal gas equation first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principle of Increase of Entropy:**

- **Core Statement:** $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ — Entropy of an isolated system never decreases.
- **Equality Condition:** $\Delta S = 0$ only for **reversible** processes (no entropy generation).
- **Inequality Condition:** $\Delta S > 0$ for **all real (irreversible)** processes due to entropy generation $S_{gen} > 0$.
- **Foundation:** Derived from the **Clausius Inequality**: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$.
- **Universe Entropy:** $\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ (always for any process).
- **State vs Path Function:** $\Delta S$ is a **state function** (depends only on end states); $S_{gen}$ is a **path function** (depends on process).
- **Entropy Generation Equation:** $\delta S_{gen} = dS - \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \geq 0$.
- **Gouy-Stodola Theorem:** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ — quantifies irreversibility in terms of lost useful work.
- **Maxwell's Demon Paradox:** Resolved by Landauer's principle — erasing information generates entropy ≥ $k_B \ln 2$ per bit.
- **Thermodynamic Equilibrium:** The state of **maximum entropy** — system reaches this when isolated for sufficient time.
- **Entropy and Disorder:** $S = k_B \ln W$ (Boltzmann) — links macroscopic entropy to microscopic microstates.
- **Sign Convention Reminder:** Temperature in entropy formulas **must** be in Kelvin; always check unit consistency.
- **Heat Transfer Sign:** $Q > 0$ into system → entropy transfer positive into system; $Q < 0$ out of system → entropy transfer negative.
- **Common Irreversibilities:** Friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, finite ΔT heat transfer, chemical reactions, electrical resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** "**E**ntropy **I**ncreases **N**aturally" → **E-I-N** → Always positive for the universe in any real process.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Principle of Increase of Entropy*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Standards | PCMRT502 Thermodynamics*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Entropy Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🟦 MODULE 3 — ENTROPY GENERATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entropy Generation (S_gen)** is a fundamental thermodynamic property representing the **amount of entropy created within a system or control volume due to irreversible processes**. It is the quantitative measure of the *irreversibility* of a real process and is a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

From the KTU syllabus and standard thermodynamics textbooks (Cengel & Boles, Nag), entropy generation is defined as:

> The entropy change of the universe (system + surroundings) that arises *purely because a process is irreversible*. Mathematically, it is the difference between the actual entropy change of a system and the entropy transfer that would have occurred in a perfectly reversible process carrying the same heat interactions.

The governing statement (Clausius Inequality extended):

$$S_{gen} \;\geq\; 0$$

- $S_{gen} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Reversible process**
- $S_{gen} > 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Irreversible process**
- $S_{gen} < 0 \;\Rightarrow\;$ **Impossible (violates the Second Law)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight (KTU Syllabus Marker):**
> Entropy generation is **NOT a property of a system** (unlike entropy itself). It is a *process quantity* — it depends on the path taken between two states, not just the end states.

---

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **mixing a hot cup of coffee with cold milk**:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| **Reversible (theoretical)** | You add heat drop-by-drop through an infinite series of reservoirs at infinitesimally different temperatures. No entropy is created. |
| **Irreversible (real life)** | You just pour the milk in. Heat spontaneously transfers from hot coffee to cold milk. **Entropy is generated**. |

Another analogy: **A bouncing ball on the floor**.
- Each bounce gets lower → kinetic energy degrades to internal energy.
- The total entropy of the universe *increases*.
- The energy is still there (First Law OK), but it is no longer available for useful work (Second Law).
- That "lost availability" is exactly what **entropy generation** quantifies.

> [!TIP]
> **In short:** *Entropy generation = the universe's way of measuring how much "useful order" we destroyed.*

---

### Why Entropy Generation Matters in Engineering

1. **Quantifies losses** in turbines, compressors, nozzles, and heat exchangers.
2. **Drives the design** of efficient power plants, refrigerators, and engines.
3. **Establishes feasibility** — any proposed process must have $S_{gen} \geq 0$.
4. **Foundation for Exergy (Availability) Analysis** in advanced thermodynamics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Concept

#### Step 1 — Starting Point: The Second Law (Clausius Statement)

No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body.

The mathematical expression is the **Clausius Inequality**:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;\leq\; 0$$

where $T_b$ is the **boundary temperature** at the location where heat $\delta Q$ crosses the system boundary.

#### Step 2 — From Inequality to Entropy Generation

For any cycle, we rewrite:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;=\; -\oint \delta S \;\;+\;\; S_{gen,cycle}$$

But since $\oint \delta S = 0$ (entropy is a property), we get:

$$\boxed{\;S_{gen} \;\geq\; 0\;}$$

#### Step 3 — Entropy Balance for a Closed System (Fixed Mass)

For any process (cycle or change of state):

$$\Delta S_{sys} \;=\; \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;+\; S_{gen}$$

Solving for entropy generation:

$$\boxed{\;S_{gen} \;=\; \Delta S_{sys} \;-\; \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}\;}$$

#### Step 4 — Entropy Balance for an Open System (Control Volume)

For a control volume with multiple inlets, outlets, and heat interactions:

$$\frac{dS_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; \sum_k \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k} \;+\; \sum_{in}\dot{m}_i s_i \;-\; \sum_{out}\dot{m}_e s_e \;+\; \dot{S}_{gen}$$

Solving for entropy generation rate:

$$\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; \frac{dS_{cv}}{dt} \;-\; \sum_k \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k} \;-\; \sum_{in}\dot{m}_i s_i \;+\; \sum_{out}\dot{m}_e s_e$$

#### Step 5 — Special Case: Adiabatic Process

If $\dot{Q}_k = 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{S}_{gen,adiabatic} \;=\; \sum_{out}\dot{m}_e s_e \;-\; \sum_{in}\dot{m}_i s_i \;-\; \frac{dS_{cv}}{dt}\;}$$

For a **steady-flow adiabatic** device:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) \;\geq\; 0\;}$$

This means $s_{out} \geq s_{in}$ — entropy can never decrease across an adiabatic device.

#### Step 6 — Reversibility vs Irreversibility — Tabulated

| Process Type | $S_{gen}$ | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| Internally reversible | $0$ | $= 0$ |
| Internally irreversible | $> 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Externally irreversible | $> 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Impossible | $< 0$ | $< 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Any process with friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing of two fluids, heat transfer across finite temperature difference, chemical reaction, or electrical resistance will generate entropy. Even **reversible processes** can be approached only as limits, never truly achieved.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use SI units unless stated. $T$ in **K**, $s$ in **kJ/kg·K**, $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**, $\dot{Q}$ in **kW**.

| # | Formula | Application / Device | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | Closed system | General |
| 2 | $S_{gen} = m(s_2 - s_1) - \int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | Closed system, incompressible / ideal gas | General |
| 3 | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in})$ | Turbine, compressor, nozzle, throttling (steady, adiabatic) | $\dot{Q} = 0$ |
| 4 | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) - \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{T_b}$ | Heat exchanger, condenser, boiler | Steady flow |
| 5 | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}_A s_A + \dot{m}_B s_B - (\dot{m}_A+\dot{m}_B) s_{mix}$ | Mixing chamber / Tee | Steady, adiabatic |
| 6 | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \sum \dfrac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k}$ for two reservoirs | Heat transfer between reservoirs | $T_H > T_C$ |
| 7 | $S_{gen,total} = \dfrac{Q}{T_C} - \dfrac{Q}{T_H}$ | Heat engine / Carnot deviation | $T_H, T_C$ reservoirs |
| 8 | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ | All processes | Second Law |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, you will almost always compute $s_{out} - s_{in}$ using steam tables or ideal-gas relations: $s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln(T_2/T_1) - R \ln(P_2/P_1)$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steam Power Plants:** Quantifies irreversibility in boiler, turbine, condenser.
- **Gas Turbines & Jet Engines:** Evaluates compressor and turbine losses.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Sets the minimum work input via $W_{min} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Drives the design of mixers, separators, reactors.
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar thermal collectors, geothermal cycles — entropy generation minimization improves efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Entropy Generation from the Clausius Inequality

We start with the **Second Law for a cycle** (Clausius):

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;\leq\; 0$$

Add and subtract $\oint \delta S$ on the right-hand side. Since $S$ is a property and the cycle returns to its initial state:

$$\oint \delta S \;=\; 0$$

So:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \;=\; -\oint \delta S \;+\; S_{gen}$$

But the negative of the integral of entropy over a closed cycle is **zero**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;S_{gen,cycle} \;\geq\; 0\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Closed-System Entropy Balance

Consider a closed system undergoing a process from state 1 to state 2.

**Step 1:** The entropy change of the system is purely a function of the end states:

$$\Delta S_{sys} \;=\; S_2 - S_1 \;=\; \int_1^2 \delta S$$

**Step 2:** The entropy *transferred* to the system by heat is:

$$S_{transfer} \;=\; \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}$$

**Step 3:** Because the process may be irreversible, the actual entropy change of the system exceeds the entropy transferred by an amount equal to the **entropy generated internally**:

$$\Delta S_{sys} \;=\; S_{transfer} \;+\; S_{gen}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;S_{gen} \;=\; \Delta S_{sys} \;-\; \int_1^2 \frac{\delta Q}{T_b}\;}$$

**Verification of sign:**
- Reversible: $S_{gen} = 0$, so $\Delta S = \int \delta Q/T$.
- Irreversible: $S_{gen} > 0$, so $\Delta S > \int \delta Q/T$.
- Impossible: $S_{gen} < 0$, contradiction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Open-System (Control Volume) Entropy Balance

Consider a control volume with mass flow, heat transfer, and entropy generation.

**Step 1:** Apply the entropy balance over a finite time interval $\Delta t$:

$$S_{out} - S_{in} + \Delta S_{cv} \;=\; S_{transfer} + S_{gen}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the entropy carried by mass flow:

$$\sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \, s_e \, \Delta t \;-\; \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \, s_i \, \Delta t \;+\; \Delta S_{cv} \;=\; \sum_k \frac{Q_k}{T_k} \;+\; S_{gen}$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $\Delta t$ to obtain rates:

$$\sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \, s_e \;-\; \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \, s_i \;+\; \frac{dS_{cv}}{dt} \;=\; \sum_k \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k} \;+\; \dot{S}_{gen}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\;\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; \frac{dS_{cv}}{dt} \;-\; \sum_k \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k} \;-\; \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \, s_i \;+\; \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \, s_e\;}$$

**Steady-state form** ($\frac{dS_{cv}}{dt} = 0$):

$$\boxed{\;\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; \sum_{out} \dot{m}_e \, s_e \;-\; \sum_{in} \dot{m}_i \, s_i \;-\; \sum_k \frac{\dot{Q}_k}{T_k}\;}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration

> **[Worked Example — KTU Style]**
>
> Air enters a compressor at $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \text{ K}$ and exits at $P_2 = 900 \text{ kPa}$, $T_2 = 560 \text{ K}$. The compressor loses $160 \text{ kW}$ of heat to surroundings at $27^\circ C$. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 5 \text{ kg/s}$. Treat air as ideal gas with $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
>
> **Find:** Entropy generation rate $\dot{S}_{gen}$ in kW/K.

**Step 1 — Entropy change of air (ideal gas):**

$$s_2 - s_1 \;=\; c_p \ln\!\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \;-\; R \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Take $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for air.

$$s_2 - s_1 \;=\; 1.005 \ln\!\left(\frac{560}{300}\right) \;-\; 0.287 \ln\!\left(\frac{900}{100}\right)$$

$$s_2 - s_1 \;=\; 1.005 \times (0.6242) \;-\; 0.287 \times (2.1972)$$

$$s_2 - s_1 \;=\; 0.6273 \;-\; 0.6306 \;=\; -0.0033 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer rate (loss):**

Heat is leaving the air, so $\dot{Q} = -160 \text{ kW}$. Surroundings temperature:

$$T_b \;=\; 27^\circ C \;=\; 300 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-flow entropy balance:**

$$\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; \dot{m}(s_2 - s_1) \;-\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{T_b}$$

$$\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; 5 \times (-0.0033) \;-\; \frac{(-160)}{300}$$

$$\dot{S}_{gen} \;=\; -0.0165 \;+\; 0.5333$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{S}_{gen} \;\approx\; 0.5168 \text{ kW/K}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$\dot{S}_{gen} > 0$ confirms the process is **real and irreversible**, as expected for a real compressor with friction and finite-temperature heat loss. The result is dimensionally consistent and physically realistic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention Reminder:** When heat leaves a system, $\dot{Q}$ is *negative*. Always carry the sign into the entropy balance equation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Entropy Generation in Common Devices

| Device | $\dot{Q}$ | Steady State? | Simplified $\dot{S}_{gen}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turbine** | $\approx 0$ (adiabatic) | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) \geq 0$ |
| **Compressor** | $\approx 0$ | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) \geq 0$ |
| **Nozzle** | $\approx 0$ | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) \geq 0$ |
| **Throttling Valve** | $= 0$ | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \dot{m}(s_{out} - s_{in}) \geq 0$ (since $h_{out}=h_{in}$, use steam tables) |
| **Mixing Chamber** | $\approx 0$ | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} s_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} s_{in}$ |
| **Heat Exchanger** | $\dot{Q} \neq 0$ | Yes | $\dot{S}_{gen} = \sum \dot{m} s_{out} - \sum \dot{m} s_{in} - \sum \dot{Q}/T$ |
| **Closed Reactor** | $\dot{Q} \neq 0$ | No | $S_{gen} = m(s_2 - s_1) - Q/T_b$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Entropy Balance Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System or Control Volume"]:::core
    B["Entropy In via Mass flow (Σ m_in · s_in)"]:::in
    C["Entropy In via Heat transfer (Σ Q_k / T_k)"]:::in
    D["Entropy Out via Mass flow (Σ m_out · s_out)"]:::out
    E["Entropy Out via Heat transfer (Σ Q_k / T_k)"]:::out
    F["Entropy Stored (dS_cv / dt)"]:::store
    G["Entropy Generation (S_gen ≥ 0)"]:::gen
    H["REVERSIBLE if S_gen = 0"]:::judge
    I["IRREVERSIBLE if S_gen > 0"]:::judge
    J["IMPOSSIBLE if S_gen < 0"]:::judge

    B --> A
    C --> A
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J

    classDef core fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef in fill:#10b981,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#ef4444,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef store fill:#a855f7,stroke:#7e22ce,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef gen fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef judge fill:#64748b,stroke:#334155,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Steady-Flow Adiabatic Entropy Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Steady Flow Device (e.g. Turbine / Compressor / Nozzle)"]
        direction LR
        IN1["Inlet: m_dot, h_in, s_in"]:::in
        OUT1["Outlet: m_dot, h_out, s_out"]:::out
        GEN1["S_gen_dot = m_dot × (s_out - s_in) ≥ 0"]:::gen
        IN1 -->|adiabatic, no heat loss| GEN1
        GEN1 --> OUT1
    end

    S1 --> R1["Reversible limit: s_out = s_in"]:::judge
    S1 --> R2["Irreversible real: s_out > s_in"]:::judge
    S1 --> R3["Impossible: s_out < s_in"]:::judge
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — Process Feasibility Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST["Start: Calculate S_gen"]:::core
    CHK{"S_gen value?"}:::judge
    P1["S_gen > 0 → IRREVERSIBLE but POSSIBLE"]:::out
    P2["S_gen = 0 → REVERSIBLE (theoretical limit)"]:::out
    P3["S_gen < 0 → IMPOSSIBLE — violates 2nd Law"]:::out

    CHK -->|positive| P1 --> CON1
    CHK -->|zero| P2 --> CON2
```
