# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an isentropic process. Show that for an ideal gas, $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ during an isentropic process.**

**Model Answer:**

An **isentropic process** is defined as a thermodynamic process in which the **entropy of the system remains constant**, i.e., $ds = 0$ or $s_1 = s_2$. It is simultaneously **adiabatic** ($\delta q = 0$) and **internally reversible**.

**Derivation outline:** Applying the first law to a closed system with $\delta q = 0$:

$$du + \delta w = 0 \Rightarrow mC_v \, dT = -P \, dV$$

Using ideal gas law $P = mRT/V$:

$$C_v \frac{dT}{T} = -R \frac{dV}{V}$$

Integrating: $C_v \ln(T_2/T_1) = R \ln(V_1/V_2)$. Substituting $R/C_v = \gamma - 1$ and exponentiating:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

Substituting $T = PV/(mR)$ yields $\boxed{PV^\gamma = \text{constant}}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between isentropic and adiabatic processes. Why is isentropic efficiency used as a performance metric for turbines?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Adiabatic Process | Isentropic Process |
|:----------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| Heat transfer | $\delta q = 0$ | $\delta q = 0$ |
| Reversibility | May be irreversible | Must be reversible |
| Entropy | $ds \geq 0$ | $ds = 0$ (constant) |
| Is every adiabatic isentropic? | **No** (irreversible cases have $ds > 0$) | — |

**Isentropic efficiency is used** because it provides a **dimensionless, normalized measure** of how close an actual device (turbine, compressor, nozzle) comes to the theoretical ideal of zero-entropy-generation operation. It is defined as:

$$\eta_T = \frac{\text{Actual work output}}{\text{Isentropic work output}} = \frac{h_1 - h_{2a}}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

This ratio always lies between 0 and 1, allowing engineers to compare machines of different sizes on a common performance scale. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B.A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the work done during a reversible isentropic process of an ideal gas in a closed system, starting from the first law of thermodynamics. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Air enters an adiabatic compressor at **100 kPa, 300 K** and is compressed to **1000 kPa**. Calculate:
- (i) The exit temperature of air assuming the process is isentropic.
- (ii) The work of compression per kg of air.

Take $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\gamma = 1.4$. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**Given:** Ideal gas, closed system, reversible isentropic process.

**Step 1:** First law for closed system:

$$\delta q = du + \delta w$$

**Step 2:** For isentropic: $\delta q = 0$:

$$0 = du + \delta w \quad \Rightarrow \quad du = -\delta w$$

**Step 3:** For ideal gas: $du = mC_v dT$; for reversible: $\delta w = P dV$:

$$mC_v dT = -P dV$$

**Step 4:** Using $PV = mRT$ to eliminate $P$, separate variables and integrate:

$$C_v \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T} = -R \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{dV}{V}$$

$$C_v \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} = R \ln\frac{V_1}{V_2}$$

**Step 5:** Compute work by integrating $W = \int P \, dV$ using $P = C V^{-\gamma}$:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} C V^{-\gamma} dV = \frac{C}{1-\gamma}\left[V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $C V^{1-\gamma} = PV$:

$$\boxed{W_{1-2} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 1000$ kPa
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$
- Pressure ratio: $r_p = P_2/P_1 = 10$

**Step 1: Calculate exit temperature using Relation 3:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = (10)^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = (10)^{0.2857}$$

$$T_2 = 300 \times (10)^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.9307 = 579.2 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Calculate work of compression (per kg, steady flow):**

$$w_s = C_p(T_2 - T_1) = 1.005 \times (579.2 - 300)$$

$$w_s = 1.005 \times 279.2 = 280.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Results:**
- Exit temperature: $T_2 = 579.2$ K $= 306.2°C$
- Work of compression: $w_s = 280.6$ kJ/kg (work done **on** the gas; negative if work done **by** the system is taken as positive)

$\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B.B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the isentropic process with the help of **P-V and T-S diagrams**. Derive all four relations for an ideal gas undergoing an isentropic process. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Steam enters a turbine at **3 MPa, 400°C** and expands to **10 kPa**. The isentropic efficiency of the turbine is **85%**. Determine the actual work output per kg of steam and the actual exit enthalpy. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**Isentropic Process on Diagrams:**

- **P-V diagram:** Curve is **steeper** than isothermal. Represented as $PV^\gamma = C$.
- **T-S diagram:** **Vertical straight line** (constant entropy). Area under the curve represents heat transfer, which is **zero** for isentropic.

**Derivation of the four relations:**

Starting from the first law with $\delta q = 0$ and the ideal gas law:

$$C_v \frac{dT}{T} = -R \frac{dV}{V} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dP}{P} + \frac{dV}{V} = \frac{dT}{T}$$

**Relation 1 ($PV^\gamma = C$):**

From the integrated form $C_v \ln(T_2/T_1) = R \ln(V_1/V_2)$, substitute $T = PV/(mR)$ to get:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1^\gamma}{mR} = \frac{P_2 V_2^\gamma}{mR} \Rightarrow \boxed{P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma}$$

**Relation 2 ($TV^{\gamma-1} = C$):**

Direct result of integration: $\boxed{T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1} = T_2 V_2^{\gamma-1}}$

**Relation 3 ($T^\gamma / P^{\gamma-1} = C$):**

Substitute $V = mRT/P$ into Relation 2:

$$T_1 \left(\frac{mR T_1}{P_1}\right)^{\gamma-1} = T_2 \left(\frac{mR T_2}{P_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

$$\frac{T_1^\gamma}{P_1^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{T_2^\gamma}{P_2^{\gamma-1}} \Rightarrow \boxed{\frac{T^\gamma}{P^{\gamma-1}} = \text{constant}}$$

**Relation 4 ($P/T$ relation):**

Rearranging Relation 3:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Given:**
- Inlet: $P_1 = 3$ MPa $= 3000$ kPa, $T_1 = 400°C$
- Exit: $P_2 = 10$ kPa
- Isentropic efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.85$

**Step 1: Read inlet properties from superheated steam tables at 3 MPa, 400°C:**

$$h_1 = 3230.9 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.9212 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2: Read saturation properties at 10 kPa:**

$$s_f = 0.6493 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.5009 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$
$$h_f = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2392.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Determine isentropic exit state:**

Since $s_1 = 6.9212 < s_g = s_f + s_{fg} = 8.1502$ kJ/kg·K, the isentropic exit state lies in the **wet region**.

Dryness fraction:

$$x_{2s} = \frac{s_1 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9212 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.2719}{7.5009} = 0.8362$$

Isentropic exit enthalpy:

$$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8362 \times 2392.1 = 2192.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute isentropic work output:**

$$w_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3230.9 - 2192.2 = 1038.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute actual work output using isentropic efficiency:**

$$w_a = \eta_T \cdot w_s = 0.85 \times 1038.7 = 882.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Compute actual exit enthalpy:**

$$h_{2a} = h_1 - w_a = 3230.9 - 882.9 = 2348.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Results:**
- Actual work output: $w_a = 882.9$ kJ/kg
- Actual exit enthalpy: $h_{2a} = 2348.0$ kJ/kg

$\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of work:** In **compression**, the work done *on* the gas is positive input, but if you use $W = \int P \, dV$ with $V_2 < V_1$, the integral is *negative*. State explicitly whether $W$ is work done *by* or *on* the system to avoid 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Wet vs superheated exit:** In turbine problems, always check whether $s_1 < s_g$ (wet exit) or $s_1 > s_g$ (superheated exit) at the exhaust pressure. KTU students often assume superheated exit and lose 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Numerical $r_p^{(γ-1)/γ}$ calculation:** Carry the calculation to **at least 4 decimal places**. Rounding to 2 decimals early introduces 2–3% error, which examiners dock marks for.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Pressures in steam tables are in **kPa** or **MPa** — convert all $P$ values to a consistent unit *before* reading tables. Mixing kPa and MPa is a top-3 error pattern.
> 5. **Isentropic efficiency formula inversion:** A *very* common mistake is writing $\eta_T = (h_1 - h_{2s})/(h_1 - h_{2a})$ instead of the correct $(h_1 - h_{2a})/(h_1 - h_{2s})$. Always actual/ideal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** Isentropic = constant entropy ($ds = 0$) = reversible adiabatic.
- [x] **Necessary conditions:** Both $\delta q = 0$ (adiabatic) **AND** reversible. Either alone is insufficient.
- [x] **Master relations (ideal gas):** $PV^\gamma = C$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = C$, $T^\gamma / P^{\gamma-1} = C$.
- [x] **Key exponent:** $\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$ appears in nearly every numerical problem; for air ($\gamma = 1.4$), this equals **0.2857**.
- [x] **Work done (closed system):** $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$.
- [x] **Work done (open system):** $w = C_p(T_1 - T_2)$ (turbine) or $w = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ (compressor).
- [x] **T-s diagram signature:** Vertical straight line (no horizontal motion → no heat transfer).
- [x] **P-V diagram signature:** Steeper than isothermal; slope at any point is $-\gamma P/V$.
- [x] **Isentropic efficiency values:** Turbines: 80–95%; Compressors: 75–90%; Nozzles: 90–98%.
- [x] **Real-process deviation:** In real machines, $s_{2a} > s_1$, so $h_{2a} > h_{2s}$ → actual work < isentropic work.
- [x] **Exam focus (KTU 2024):** Numerical problems on adiabatic compressor exit temperature and turbine work output using steam tables with $\eta_T$ — practice these extensively.
- [x] **Memory anchor:** "$P$ falls, $V$ rises, $T$ falls, but $S$ never moves" — captures the entire isentropic process in one line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Third Law of Thermodynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Third Law of Thermodynamics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Third Law of Thermodynamics** is a fundamental postulate concerning the behavior of matter as the temperature approaches **absolute zero (0 K or -273.15 °C)**. In formal KTU/engineering thermodynamics parlance, the law is articulated through two complementary statements: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nernst Heat Theorem (Nernst Statement, 1906):** > *"The entropy change for any isothermal reversible process approaches zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Planck's Statement (1911):** > *"The entropy of a perfectly ordered crystalline substance is zero at the absolute zero of temperature."* Mathematically, both statements can be compactly expressed as: $$ \lim_{T \to 0\,K} \Delta S_{\text{reversible, isothermal}} = 0 $$ $$ \lim_{T \to 0\,K} S_{\text{perfect crystal}} = 0 $$ A **perfect crystal** in this context refers to an ideal lattice in which every atom/molecule occupies a definite, non-degenerate position with no configurational, orientational, or positional disorder. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Quiet Library" Imagine a large, bustling library at noon (high temperature): books are being pulled off shelves, students chatter, pages rustle, and librarians run around — this represents high **entropy** (disorder). Now imagine the library at midnight after everyone has left, every book placed perfectly back on its assigned shelf in exact numerical order, the lights dimming down to complete silence — this represents a state approaching **zero entropy** (perfect order). > [!NOTE] > The Third Law says: *as the "energy" of the room (temperature) is completely drained, the system settles into the most orderly state physically possible — and for a flawless crystal, that final resting entropy value is exactly **0 J/K**.* --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Absolute Zero (Temperature) | $T_0$ | **0 K = -273.15 °C** | | Reference Entropy at 0 K | $S_0$ | **0 J/(mol·K)** (for perfect crystal) | | Boltzmann's Constant | $k_B$ | **1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | | Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | --- ## 1.4 Salient Features of the Third Law - It provides an **absolute reference point** for entropy — unlike internal energy ($U$) and enthalpy ($H$), which can only be measured as *changes*. - It enables calculation of **absolute entropies** of substances at any temperature. - It sets a fundamental **lower bound** to entropy: $S \ge 0$ for any real substance. - It implies that **absolute zero is unattainable** by any finite sequence of thermodynamic processes. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Law The Third Law can be decomposed into three layered concepts, each progressively more powerful: - **Step 1 — Nernst Heat Theorem:** As $T \to 0$, $\Delta S \to 0$ for *any* process. This is the experimental observation Nernst derived from studying galvanic cells at low temperatures. - **Step 2 — Planck's Extension:** Since $\Delta S = S_{\text{final}} - S_{\text{initial}}$, if $\Delta S \to 0$ for *all* transitions at $T = 0$, then *all* states at 0 K must have *the same* entropy value. Planck chose this common value to be **zero** for a perfect crystal. - **Step 3 — Consequence (Unattainability of 0 K):** Cooling a system requires extracting heat. But as $T \to 0$, the heat capacity also approaches zero ($C_p \to 0$), meaning each cooling step extracts vanishingly less heat. An infinite number of steps would be required to reach 0 K — hence, **absolute zero is asymptotically approachable but unreachable**. --- ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step | Concept | Why It Matters | |---|---| | $\Delta S \to 0$ at 0 K | Slopes of Gibbs free energy vs. $T$ curves for *all* substances coincide at 0 K, allowing chemical-affinity calculations at low $T$. | | $S = 0$ at 0 K for perfect crystals | Gives us a **non-arbitrary zero** of the entropy scale, analogous to setting sea level for gravitational PE. | | $C_p \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ | Confirmed experimentally (Debye's $T^3$ law); implies the lattice vibrations freeze out completely. | | Absolute zero unattainable | Acts as a **permanent boundary** — like the speed of light in relativity. | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are standard, board-approved forms. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for any absolute-value / modulus expressions.** | # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\displaystyle\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0$ | Third Law (Planck) | J/(mol·K) | | 2 | $\displaystyle\lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_{\text{rev}} = 0$ | Nernst Theorem | J/(mol·K) | | 3 | $\displaystyle\lim_{T \to 0} \left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial T}\right)_p = \lim_{T \to 0} \frac{C_p}{T} = 0$ | Heat capacity must vanish (since $S$ cannot be negative) | J/(mol·K²) | | 4 | $\displaystyle S^\circ(T) = \int_{0}^{T} \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT + \sum \frac{\Delta H_{\text{trans}}}{T_{\text{trans}}}$ | **Absolute (Third-Law) Entropy** of a substance | J/(mol·K) | | 5 | $\displaystyle S^\circ(T) = \int_{0}^{T_f} \frac{C_{p,s}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{T_f} + \int_{T_f}^{T_b} \frac{C_{p,l}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T_b} + \int_{T_b}^{T} \frac{C_{p,g}}{T}\,dT$ | Full absolute entropy with phase transitions | J/(mol·K) | | 6 | $\displaystyle \Delta S_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum \nu_i S_i^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i S_i^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | Standard entropy of reaction | J/(mol·K) | | 7 | $\displaystyle C_{p,s} = aT^3$ (Debye, low $T$) | Heat capacity of solids at very low temperatures | J/(mol·K) | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cryogenics & Liquefaction of Gases:** Design of helium liquefiers, superconducting magnets (MRI, CERN), and space-propellant storage relies on the unattainability principle. - **Chemical Thermodynamics:** Absolute entropies from the Third Law feed into the **equilibrium constant** expression: $\ln K = -\Delta G^\circ / RT$, which uses $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$. - **Materials Science:** Predicting phase stability of alloys and polymers at cryogenic temperatures. - **Semiconductor Industry:** Understanding thermal behavior of doped silicon and germanium near 0 K is critical for quantum computers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Proof That Heat Capacity Must Vanish at 0 K **Starting point:** The Third Law requires $S \ge 0$ (entropy is non-negative). Now consider an isobaric heating process from 0 K to a small temperature $\delta T$: $$ dS = \frac{C_p\,dT}{T} $$ Integrating between $T = 0$ and $T = \delta T$: $$ S(\delta T) - S(0) = \int_{0}^{\delta T} \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT $$ Because $S(0) = 0$ by Planck's statement and $S(\delta T) \ge 0$, the integral must be non-negative and finite. This places a *constraint* on the behavior of $C_p$ as $T \to 0$: $$ \lim_{T \to 0} \frac{C_p}{T} = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \lim_{T \to 0} C_p = 0 $$ > **Interpretation:** A *finite, non-zero* heat capacity at $T = 0$ would make the integral diverge, violating $S \ge 0$. Therefore, **all substances must have $C_p \to 0$ as $T \to 0$ K**, consistent with experimental data (Debye $T^3$ behavior). --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Absolute Entropy from the Third Law Consider heating 1 mole of a substance from 0 K to a temperature $T$, possibly crossing phase transitions (fusion at $T_f$, vaporization at $T_b$). The total entropy change is: $$ S^\circ(T) = S^\circ(0) + \Delta S_{\text{heating,solid}} + \Delta S_{\text{fusion}} + \Delta S_{\text{heating,liquid}} + \Delta S_{\text{vap}} + \Delta S_{\text{heating,gas}} $$ By the Third Law, $S^\circ(0) = 0$ for a perfect crystal. Each term can be written using $dS = \int C_p dT / T$ for heating, and $\Delta S = \Delta H / T_{\text{trans}}$ for phase changes: $$ \begin{aligned} S^\circ(T) &= \int_{0}^{T_f} \frac{C_{p,s}}{T}\,dT \;+\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{T_f} \;+\; \int_{T_f}^{T_b} \frac{C_{p,l}}{T}\,dT \\[6pt] &\quad+\; \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T_b} \;+\; \int_{T_b}^{T} \frac{C_{p,g}}{T}\,dT \end{aligned} $$ This expression gives the **absolute molar entropy** at any temperature — a uniquely powerful consequence of the Third Law. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Entropy Change from a Reaction (Standard) For a general chemical reaction $\sum \nu_i A_i = 0$ (with stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_i$, positive for products, negative for reactants): $$ \Delta S_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i \, S_i^\circ $$ Equivalently: $$ \Delta S_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum_i \nu_i S_i^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_j \nu_j S_j^\circ(\text{reactants}) $$ This is computable *only* because we have absolute entropy values from the Third Law. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board Standard) **Problem:** Compute the absolute molar entropy of liquid water at 298 K. Given: - $C_{p,\text{ice}} = 38.1 + 0.0145\,T$ J/(mol·K) (valid 0 – 273 K) - $\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = 6008$ J/mol at $T_f = 273.15$ K - $C_{p,\text{water}} = 75.3$ J/(mol·K) - $C_{p,\text{steam}} = 30.0 + 0.0107\,T$ J/(mol·K) - $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 44{,}010$ J/mol at $T_b = 373.15$ K **Solution — Heating solid ice (0 → 273 K):** $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{273.15} \frac{C_{p,s}}{T}\,dT &= \int_{0}^{273.15} \left(\frac{38.1}{T} + 0.0145\right)\,dT \\[4pt] &= 38.1 \ln\!\left(\frac{273.15}{0^+}\right) + 0.0145 \times 273.15 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The integral $\int_0^{273}(38.1/T)\,dT$ diverges at $T=0$ unless $C_{p,s}$ goes to zero fast enough. Using Debye's law near 0 K, the low-$T$ contribution is **finite** and is typically provided in tabulated form as $\int_0^{T_f}(C_{p,s}/T)\,dT \approx 9.0$ J/(mol·K) for ice. So $\Delta S_1 \approx 9.0$ J/(mol·K). **Fusion at 273.15 K:** $$ \Delta S_2 = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{T_f} = \frac{6008}{273.15} = 22.0 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ **Heating liquid water (273 → 298 K):** $$ \Delta S_3 = \int_{273.15}^{298.15} \frac{75.3}{T}\,dT = 75.3 \ln\!\left(\frac{298.15}{273.15}\right) = 75.3 \times 0.0876 = 6.60 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ **Total Absolute Entropy:** $$ S^\circ(298\,\text{K}) = 9.0 + 22.0 + 6.60 = 37.6 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ This agrees excellently with the standard tabulated value of $S^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l, 298\,\text{K}) = 39.3$ J/(mol·K) (the small discrepancy is due to the simplified low-$T$ term). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Position of Third Law in the Thermodynamic Hierarchy The diagram below maps how the Three Laws of Thermodynamics relate to each other and what physical quantity each constrains. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Zeroth Law
Thermal Equilibrium
Defines Temperature T"] node2["First Law
Energy Conservation
Defines Internal Energy U"] node3["Second Law
Entropy Non-Decrease
Defines Entropy S"] node4["Third Law
Absolute Zero Unattainable
Defines S = 0 at 0 K"] node5["State Function: T"] node6["State Function: U"] node7["State Function: S >= 0"] node8["Reference: S(0 K) = 0
C_p -> 0 as T -> 0"] node1 --> node5 node2 --> node6 node3 --> node7 node4 --> node8 node3 --> node9["Enables: Absolute Entropy
S degree T calculation"] node4 --> node9 node9 --> node10["Enables: Equilibrium Constant K
from Delta G degree"] node4 --> node11["Consequence: Absolute Zero
Is Unattainable"] style node1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style node2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style node3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03 style node4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 style node9 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style node10 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style node11 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Flow of Third-Law Entropy Calculation ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA["Start: S degree 0 K = 0
Perfect Crystal"] stepB["Heat Solid: 0 K to T_f
Integrate C_p,s / T"] stepC["Apply Phase Change at T_f
Add Delta H_fus / T_f"] stepD["Heat Liquid: T_f to T_b
Integrate C_p,l / T"] stepE["Apply Phase Change at T_b
Add Delta H_vap / T_b"] stepF["Heat Gas: T_b to T
Integrate C_p,g / T"] stepG["Sum All Contributions
S degree T obtained"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH["Use in Equilibrium:
Delta G degree = Delta H degree - T Delta S degree"] stepG --> stepI["Compute K_eq
ln K = -Delta G / RT"] style stepA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#664d03 style stepG fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style stepH fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style stepI fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Exceptions & Limitations to the Third Law ```mermaid flowchart TD base["Third Law Holds For:
Perfect Crystalline Solids"] exc1["Exception 1: Glasses
Frozen-in disorder
S greater than 0 at 0 K"] exc2["Exception 2: Solid Solutions
Configurational entropy persists"] exc3["Exception 3: Non-equivalent Atoms
CO and N2 type molecules"] exc4["Exception 4: H2 below 6 K
Ortho-Para mixture disorder"] base --> exc1 base --> exc2 base --> exc3 base --> exc4 exc1 --> note1["Reason: Glass is NOT
a true thermodynamic equilibrium state"] exc2 --> note2["Reason: Random mixing
creates residual disorder"] exc3 --> note3["Reason: Residual entropy
from random orientations"] exc4 --> note4["Reason: Symmetry species
do not fully order at 0 K"] style base fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style exc1 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 style exc2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 style exc3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 style exc4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** State the Third Law of Thermodynamics. How does it differ from the Nernst Heat Theorem? **Model Answer (Concise — 3 marks):** The **Third Law of Thermodynamics** (Planck's statement) asserts that the entropy of a perfectly ordered crystalline substance at absolute zero is exactly zero: $$ S_{\text{perfect crystal}}(T = 0\,\text{K}) = 0 $$ The **Nernst Heat Theorem** is the *precursor* statement: it observes that the entropy change $\Delta S$ for any reversible isothermal process vanishes as $T \to 0$: $$ \lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S = 0 $$ **Difference:** Nernst's theorem is *experimental* and applies only to entropy *changes*; Planck's statement is *universal* and assigns an *absolute value* (zero) to entropy itself at 0 K. Planck's law follows directly by integrating Nernst's theorem from 0 K upwards. --- ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Explain why the absolute zero of temperature is unattainable. **Model Answer (Concise — 3 marks):** To cool a substance, heat must be extracted. The entropy change during a small isothermal step at low temperature is: $$ \Delta S = \frac{Q_{\text{rev}}}{T} $$ Using the Third Law, as $T \to 0$, the heat capacity also approaches zero ($C_p \to 0$), so the *minimum amount of heat that must be removed* to cool by an infinitesimal step $dT$ tends to zero. However, the *number of steps* required becomes infinite. Hence, reaching exactly 0 K would require an **infinite sequence of cooling operations**, which is impossible in any finite time — absolute zero is **asymptotically approachable but fundamentally unattainable**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** confuse the Nernst Heat Theorem with the Third Law — they are related but distinct (2-mark deduction if mixed). > - **Do NOT** write "$S = 0$ for all substances at 0 K" — it must be qualified as a *perfectly crystalline* substance. Marks lost for missing "perfect crystal". > - **Always include units** in numerical answers (J/mol·K); failure to do so loses 1 mark. > - When asked to *prove* $C_p \to 0$ at $T = 0$, students often skip the reasoning that $S$ must be non-negative. State it explicitly. --- ### **Question B — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]` **Statement:** *"The Third Law of Thermodynamics enables us to assign absolute values to entropy."* Discuss this statement in detail, including: - **(a)** The Nernst Heat Theorem and Planck's statement, with their mathematical forms. **(7 marks)** - **(b)** Derive the expression for the absolute entropy of a substance, and compute $S^\circ(298\,\text{K})$ for water using the data given below. **(7 marks)** **Data:** - $\int_0^{273.15} (C_{p,s}/T)\,dT = 9.0$ J/(mol·K) - $\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = 6008$ J/mol at $T_f = 273.15$ K - $C_{p,\text{water}} = 75.3$ J/(mol·K) - (For part b, evaluate to liquid water at 298 K — vaporization NOT required) **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Nernst & Planck Statements (7 marks) The **Nernst Heat Theorem (1906)** was developed from low-temperature experimental studies of galvanic cells. It states: $$ \lim_{T \to 0\,\text{K}} \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_p = \lim_{T \to 0} (-\Delta S) = 0 $$ Hence: $$ \lim_{T \to 0} \Delta S_{\text{isothermal, rev}} = 0 $$ **Planck's Extension (1911):** Planck recognized that since $\Delta S \to 0$ for *all* isothermal processes at 0 K, every state at 0 K must share the **same** entropy value. He fixed this constant by convention to **zero for a perfect crystal**: $$ S_{\text{perfect crystal}}(T = 0\,\text{K}) = 0 $$ This is a **postulate** — it cannot be derived from prior laws. It is justified because: 1. A perfect crystal has only one unique microstate ($\Omega = 1$), and by Boltzmann's relation $S = k_B \ln \Omega$, we get $S = 0$. 2. It is consistent with all calorimetric experiments. #### Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical Computation (7 marks) **General expression for absolute entropy** (derivation shown in Section 3.2): $$ S^\circ(T) = \int_{0}^{T_f} \frac{C_{p,s}}{T}\,dT + \frac{\Delta H_{\text{fus}}}{T_f} + \int_{T_f}^{T} \frac{C_{p,l}}{T}\,dT $$ **Numerical substitution:** **Term 1 — Heating ice 0 → 273.15 K:** Given directly as $9.0$ J/(mol·K). **Term 2 — Fusion at 273.15 K:** $$ \Delta S_{\text{fus}} = \frac{6008}{273.15} = 22.00 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ **Term 3 — Heating liquid 273.15 → 298.15 K:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta S_{\text{liq}} &= \int_{273.15}^{298.15} \frac{75.3}{T}\,dT = 75.3 \ln\!\left(\frac{298.15}{273.15}\right) \\[4pt] &= 75.3 \times 0.08761 = 6.60 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \end{aligned} $$ **Total:** $$ S^\circ(298\,\text{K}) = 9.0 + 22.00 + 6.60 = 37.60 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ This matches tabulated data closely and demonstrates the practical use of the Third Law in determining **absolute** entropies — something impossible under the Second Law alone. --- ### **Question B — Choice B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]` **Statement:** Explain the Third Law of Thermodynamics and its consequences. Discuss: - **(a)** The unattainability of absolute zero and the heat-capacity behavior at low temperatures. **(7 marks)** - **(b)** The exceptions to the Third Law with two suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Unattainability & Heat Capacity Behavior (7 marks) **Unattainability Principle (Nernst-Simon, 1927):** *"It is impossible by any procedure, no matter how idealized, to reduce the temperature of any system to absolute zero in a finite number of operations."* **Proof Sketch:** Consider a reversible adiabatic (isentropic) process — by the Second Law, $dS = 0$. But to also satisfy the Third Law, the entropy change during cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$ is: $$ \Delta S = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT $$ For $\Delta S = 0$, either $C_p = 0$ (trivially, no substance present) or $T_1 = T_2$. Therefore, **any finite cooling step leaves a residual temperature** above 0 K. **Heat-Capacity Behavior:** Since $S \ge 0$ and $\lim_{T \to 0} S = 0$, the integral $\int_0^{T}(C_p/T)\,dT$ must converge. This requires $C_p \to 0$ as $T \to 0$. Experimentally, Debye's $T^3$ law confirms: $$ C_{p,s} \approx aT^3 \quad \text{(at very low } T\text{)} $$ This cubic behavior is observed for many crystalline solids (e.g., copper, silver, aluminum) and is a direct empirical confirmation of the Third Law. #### Part (b) — Exceptions to the Third Law (7 marks) The Third Law strictly applies only to **perfect crystals in true thermodynamic equilibrium**. Notable exceptions: **1. Glasses (e.g., SiO₂ glass):** When silica is cooled rapidly, it does not crystallize but forms an amorphous glass. This is a **non-equilibrium frozen state** with disordered molecular arrangement, retaining a **residual entropy** at 0 K greater than zero. **2. CO (carbon monoxide):** In solid CO, the molecules can point in two opposite directions (C–O or O–C). Even at 0 K, this orientational randomness persists, giving a residual entropy: $$ S_{\text{res}} = R \ln 2 = 5.77 \text{ J/(mol·K)} $$ This has been verified experimentally. **3. H₂ below 6 K:** Ortho- and para-hydrogen do not fully interconvert at very low temperatures, leaving a residual disorder. (Optional mention for bonus credit.) In all these cases, the substance fails the "perfect crystal" requirement, so the Third Law does not strictly apply. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The **Third Law** sets entropy of a **perfect crystal** at 0 K equal to **zero** ($S_0 = 0$). - ✅ The **Nernst Heat Theorem** ($\Delta S \to 0$ as $T \to 0$) is a *corollary* — it comes before Planck's postulate but is more limited. - ✅ **Heat capacity must vanish** at 0 K, otherwise $S$ would become negative — a physical impossibility. - ✅ **Absolute zero is unattainable** in a finite number of steps — it's an asymptotic boundary. - ✅ **Absolute entropies** can be calculated using: $$ S^\circ(T) = \int_0^T \frac{C_p}{T}\,dT + \sum \frac{\Delta H_{\text{trans}}}{T_{\text{trans}}} $$ - ✅ **Debye's $T^3$ law** describes low-temperature $C_p$ behavior in solids. - ✅ The law **fails** for glasses, solid solutions, and orientationally disordered crystals (CO, N₂, H₂). - ✅ Applications include **cryogenics**, **chemical equilibrium** calculations via $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$, and **superconductivity studies**. - ⚠️ **Always specify "perfect crystal"** when stating the law — examiners penalize vague wording. - ⚠️ **Units matter**: write J/(mol·K) explicitly in numerical answers.

topics.title: Equation of State of Real Substances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Equation of State of Real Substances # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Equation of State (EoS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive relation that relates the three primary macroscopic properties of a pure substance — **Pressure (P)**, **Specific Volume (v)**, and **Temperature (T)** — in a closed functional form: $$f(P, v, T) = 0$$ For an **Ideal Gas**, this reduces to the classical relation derived from the kinetic theory of gases and Boyle's, Charles', and Avogadro's laws combined: $$Pv = RT \quad \text{(per unit mass)} \quad \text{or} \quad PV = nRT \quad \text{(per mole)}$$ For **Real Substances**, no single universal equation exists because of the complex nature of intermolecular forces (attractive + repulsive), finite molecular volume, quantum effects, and association/dissociation phenomena. Hence, *semi-empirical* and *empirical* equations of state are constructed to match experimental P-v-T data over wide ranges. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *real gas* is a substance whose molecules experience both **attractive forces** (van der Waals, dipole, hydrogen bonding) at long range and **repulsive forces** (electron-cloud overlap) at short range, causing measurable departure from ideal-gas behavior, especially near the saturation dome and in the critical region. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English) Imagine a **party hall**: - **Ideal Gas Model** = Guests spread out evenly in a vast, empty hall. They never bump into each other and never pull on each other. Each guest occupies a *negligible* point, and collisions are perfectly elastic. - **Real Gas Model** = Guests are now **sized marbles** in a finite hall. They: - **Pull each other** slightly (like magnets) when apart — this is the *attractive force* (Van der Waals `a`). - **Cannot occupy the same space** when they collide — this is the *excluded volume* (Van der Waals `b`). > **Why do real gases matter in Kerala's industrial context?** > - LNG terminals at Kochi, ammonia and refrigerant plants, cryogenic oxygen transport, and steam-power cycles all operate at **high pressures and low temperatures** where ideal-gas assumptions break down (error > 5–30 %). > - Accurate P-v-T data is essential for compressor design, liquefaction work, and flow metering. --- ## 1.3 The Need for Real-Gas Equations > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Mantra:** Ideal gas laws are accurate only when: > - Pressure is **low** (typically $P < 5$ bar), > - Temperature is **far above** the critical temperature ($T \gg T_c$), > - Molecules are **non-polar, small, and non-associating** (e.g., He, H₂, N₂ at room temperature). Outside this envelope — for CO₂, NH₃, refrigerants, hydrocarbons, steam near saturation — the ideal gas law can produce **30 % to 200 % error** in specific-volume prediction. The deviation is quantified by the **Compressibility Factor Z**: $$Z \equiv \frac{Pv}{RT} = \frac{PV}{nRT}$$ For ideal gases, **Z = 1**; for real substances, **Z ≠ 1**, and it becomes a function of both T and P. --- ## 1.4 Classification of Real-Gas Equations of State The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus recognizes **four families** of equations: | Family Type | Examples | Nature | |---|---|---| | **Two-Constant Cubic** | Van der Waals, Redlich-Kwong | Analytical, simple, near-critical accuracy moderate | | **Multi-Constant Cubic** | Peng-Robinson, Soave-Redlich-Kwong | Better vapor–liquid equilibrium predictions | | **Virial (Series Expansion)** | Virial EoS | Theoretically grounded, infinite-order | | **Constant-Reference (Generalized)** | Compressibility Charts, Lee-Kesler | Tabulated/correlated experimental data | We will analyze the **Van der Waals equation** in greatest depth because it is the *pedagogical foundation* of all subsequent cubic equations. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Van der Waals Equation (vdW, 1873) The vdW equation modifies the ideal gas law through two physically motivated corrections: $$\boxed{\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b) = RT}$$ where **v** is the molar volume. ### Physical Meaning of Each Term | Term | Physical Meaning | Engineering Interpretation | |---|---|---| | $\dfrac{a}{v^{2}}$ | **Internal pressure correction** for long-range **attractive** intermolecular forces | Lowers the *true* pressure the walls feel because molecules near the boundary are pulled inward by the bulk | | $b$ | **Excluded (covolume) volume** — finite space occupied by the molecules themselves | Reduces the available volume for free translation | | $(v - b)$ | Effective free volume per mole | The "free space" available to molecular motion | | $a, b$ | Substance-specific **vdW constants** | Determined experimentally from $P_c$ and $T_c$ | ### Derivation of vdW Constants from Critical-Point Data The critical point is defined mathematically by two simultaneous conditions: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T=T_c} = 0, \qquad \left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T=T_c} = 0$$ Expanding the vdW equation as $P = \dfrac{RT}{v-b} - \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}$ and applying the above conditions yields three simultaneous relations that solve to give: $$\boxed{\; a = \frac{27\,R^{2} T_{c}^{2}}{64\,P_{c}}, \qquad b = \frac{R T_{c}}{8 P_{c}}, \qquad v_{c} = \frac{3 R T_{c}}{8 P_{c}} = 3b \;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Universal vdW Ratios (Important for Examiners!):** > $$Z_c = \frac{P_c v_c}{R T_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$ > Most real substances exhibit $Z_c$ in the range **0.27 – 0.29**, so vdW over-predicts $Z_c$ by ~30 % — a known limitation. --- ## 2.2 Redlich-Kwong Equation (RK, 1949) $$\boxed{\; P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{T}\,v(v + b)} \;}$$ with $$a = 0.42748\,\frac{R^{2} T_{c}^{2.5}}{P_{c}}, \qquad b = 0.08664\,\frac{R T_{c}}{P_{c}}$$ - Improves vapor-phase predictions over vdW. - Still inadequate for liquid-phase density. - $Z_c \approx 0.333$. --- ## 2.3 Virial Equation of State The most theoretically rigorous form, derived from statistical mechanics (cluster expansion of the partition function): $$Z = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{v} + \frac{C(T)}{v^{2}} + \frac{D(T)}{v^{3}} + \dots$$ Equivalently, in the **pressure-explicit** form: $$Z = 1 + B'(T)P + C'(T)P^{2} + D'(T)P^{3} + \dots$$ | Coefficient | Name | Origin | |---|---|---| | $B(T)$ | 2nd virial coefficient | Pair-wise molecular interactions | | $C(T)$ | 3rd virial coefficient | Three-body interactions | | $D(T), \dots$ | Higher coefficients | Many-body interactions | > The virial equation **converges** for $v > 2v_c$ (gas region). It diverges in the dense-liquid region. --- ## 2.4 Principle of Corresponding States & Reduced Equation **Corresponding States Theorem:** *Substances at the same reduced temperature and reduced pressure exert approximately the same reduced volume, i.e., the same Z-factor.* Reduced (dimensionless) properties: $$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \qquad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \qquad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}$$ ### Reduced Form of the Van der Waals Equation Substituting $P = P_r P_c$, $v = v_r v_c$, $T = T_r T_c$ into vdW: $$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{a}{v_r^{2} v_c^{2}}\right)(v_r v_c - b) = R T_r T_c$$ Using the critical-point relations $P_c = \dfrac{a}{27 b^{2}}$, $v_c = 3b$, $T_c = \dfrac{8 a}{27 R b}$, all substance-specific parameters $a, b, R$ cancel out: $$\boxed{\; \left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3}\, T_r \;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **universal equation** — *no substance-specific constant remains*. Therefore, the same equation describes ALL fluids when expressed in reduced form. This is the foundation of the **Generalized Compressibility Chart** used universally in engineering practice. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering | Application | Why Real-Gas EoS is Essential | |---|---| | **Natural gas pipelines** | Methane at 70 bar needs $Z \approx 0.85$; flow metering requires accurate $v$ | | **Refrigeration cycles** | NH₃, R-134a operate near saturation dome — ideal-gas assumption fails | | **Steam turbines** | Superheated steam at 200 bar deviates significantly from ideality | | **LNG storage** | Methane near $T_c$ — critical phenomena dominate | | **Cryogenic oxygen/hydrogen** | Quantum fluids — modified EoS (Beattie-Bridgeman, BWR) required | | **Chemical reaction equilibria** | $K_p$ depends on fugacity coefficient $\phi(T,P)$ | --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU Module 4. | # | Formula / Relation | Variables / Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Pv = RT$ | $R = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K | Ideal-gas EoS (per mole) | | 2 | $Z = \dfrac{Pv}{RT}$ | dimensionless | Compressibility Factor | | 3 | $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b) = RT$ | $a$ [Pa·m⁶/mol²], $b$ [m³/mol] | Van der Waals EoS | | 4 | $a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_{c}^{2}}{64 P_{c}}$ | derived | vdW constant $a$ | | 5 | $b = \dfrac{R T_{c}}{8 P_{c}}$ | derived | vdW constant $b$ | | 6 | $v_c = \dfrac{3 R T_c}{8 P_c} = 3b$ | derived | vdW critical volume | | 7 | $Z_c = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$ | universal for vdW | Critical compressibility | | 8 | $\left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{8}{3} T_r$ | dimensionless | Reduced vdW EoS | | 9 | $Z = 1 + \dfrac{B(T)}{v} + \dfrac{C(T)}{v^{2}} + \dots$ | virial expansion | Volume-explicit form | | 10 | $Z = 1 + B'(T) P + C'(T) P^{2} + \dots$ | virial expansion | Pressure-explicit form | | 11 | $B(T) = b - \dfrac{a}{RT}$ | for vdW approximation | 2nd virial coefficient from vdW | | 12 | $P_r = P / P_c$, $T_r = T / T_c$ | dimensionless | Reduced properties | | 13 | Boyle Temperature: $T_B = \dfrac{a}{R b}$ | K | Temperature where $Z \approx 1$ over a range of $P$ | --- ## 2.7 Criteria for Choosing an EoS in Engineering Practice | Required Accuracy Region | Recommended EoS | |---|---| | $\; P \ll P_c,\; T \gg T_c\;$ | Ideal gas law | | $P < 10$ bar, $T_r > 0.9$ | Virial (truncated at $B$) | | $0.5 < T_r < 2$, $P_r < 1$ | Redlich-Kwong / SRK | | Near-critical / liquid–vapor coexistence | Peng-Robinson | | All-fluid (chemical engineering) | Lee-Kesler generalized correlation | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Van der Waals Constants from Critical-Point Conditions ### Step 1 — Write vdW Equation in Pressure-Explicit Form Starting from: $$\left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b) = RT$$ Expanding the left-hand side: $$P(v - b) + \frac{a(v - b)}{v^{2}} = RT$$ $$Pv - Pb + \frac{a}{v} - \frac{ab}{v^{2}} = RT$$ Solving for $P$: $$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}} + \frac{ab}{v^{3}}$$ The last term is small; for deriving critical-point relations we use the **standard simplified form**: $$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}} \qquad \text{(2.1)}$$ > **Explanation:** We drop the $\dfrac{ab}{v^3}$ term (third-order correction) because it is two orders of magnitude smaller than the dominant terms in the gas-phase domain. This is the standard engineering form used universally. --- ### Step 2 — Apply First Critical-Point Condition At the critical point, the isotherm has an **inflection point**, so the first partial derivative vanishes: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T = T_c} = 0 \qquad \text{(3.1)}$$ Differentiating equation (2.1) with respect to $v$ at constant $T$: $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial v} = -\frac{RT}{(v - b)^{2}} + \frac{2a}{v^{3}}$$ > **Explanation:** Differentiate each term separately. The derivative of $\dfrac{RT}{v-b}$ with respect to $v$ (chain rule) gives $-\dfrac{RT}{(v-b)^2}$. The derivative of $-\dfrac{a}{v^2}$ gives $+\dfrac{2a}{v^3}$. Setting this to zero at the critical point: $$-\frac{RT_c}{(v_c - b)^{2}} + \frac{2a}{v_c^{3}} = 0$$ $$\frac{RT_c}{(v_c - b)^{2}} = \frac{2a}{v_c^{3}} \qquad \text{(3.2)}$$ --- ### Step 3 — Apply Second Critical-Point Condition The second derivative at the critical point must also vanish (inflection point curvature = 0): $$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T = T_c} = 0 \qquad \text{(3.3)}$$ Differentiating equation from Step 2 once more: $$\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}} = \frac{2RT}{(v - b)^{3}} - \frac{6a}{v^{4}}$$ > **Explanation:** Differentiate $-\dfrac{RT}{(v-b)^2}$ → $+\dfrac{2RT}{(v-b)^3}$ (chain rule, power rule). Differentiate $+\dfrac{2a}{v^3}$ → $-\dfrac{6a}{v^4}$. Setting this to zero at the critical point: $$\frac{2RT_c}{(v_c - b)^{3}} = \frac{6a}{v_c^{4}} \qquad \text{(3.4)}$$ --- ### Step 4 — Divide Equation (3.4) by Equation (3.2) $$\frac{\;\dfrac{2RT_c}{(v_c - b)^{3}}\;}{\;\dfrac{RT_c}{(v_c - b)^{2}}\;} = \frac{\;\dfrac{6a}{v_c^{4}}\;}{\;\dfrac{2a}{v_c^{3}}\;}$$ $$\frac{2}{v_c - b} = \frac{3}{v_c}$$ > **Explanation:** All constants $R$, $T_c$, $a$ cancel out. This gives a direct algebraic relation between $v_c$ and $b$. Cross-multiplying: $$2 v_c = 3(v_c - b)$$ $$2 v_c = 3 v_c - 3b$$ $$v_c = 3b \qquad \text{(3.5)}$$ --- ### Step 5 — Substitute (3.5) into Equation (3.2) Equation (3.2) becomes: $$\frac{RT_c}{(3b - b)^{2}} = \frac{2a}{(3b)^{3}}$$ $$\frac{RT_c}{4b^{2}} = \frac{2a}{27 b^{3}}$$ > **Explanation:** $(3b-b)^2 = (2b)^2 = 4b^2$; $(3b)^3 = 27b^3$. Cross-multiplying: $$27 b^{3} \cdot RT_c = 8 b^{2} \cdot a$$ $$a = \frac{27 R T_c b}{8} \qquad \text{(3.6)}$$ --- ### Step 6 — Find $P_c$ from the vdW Equation at Critical Point Substitute $T = T_c$, $v = v_c = 3b$, $P = P_c$ into the original vdW: $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{v_c - b} - \frac{a}{v_c^{2}}$$ $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{3b - b} - \frac{a}{(3b)^{2}}$$ $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^{2}}$$ > **Explanation:** First term uses $v_c - b = 2b$. Second term uses $v_c^2 = 9b^2$. Substituting (3.6) into this: $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{1}{9b^{2}} \cdot \frac{27 R T_c b}{8}$$ $$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{2b} - \frac{3 R T_c}{8b}$$ > **Explanation:** $\dfrac{27 R T_c b}{8 \cdot 9 b^{2}} = \dfrac{3 R T_c}{8 b}$. Bringing to a common denominator: $$P_c = \frac{4 R T_c}{8b} - \frac{3 R T_c}{8b} = \frac{R T_c}{8b} \qquad \text{(3.7)}$$ Therefore: $$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$$ Substituting back into (3.6): $$a = \frac{27 R T_c}{8} \cdot \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}$$ --- ### Step 7 — Final Compact Result $$\boxed{\; a = \frac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \qquad b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \qquad v_c = 3b = \frac{3 R T_c}{8 P_c} \;}$$ The critical compressibility factor for any vdW fluid: $$Z_c = \frac{P_c v_c}{R T_c} = \frac{P_c \cdot (3R T_c / 8 P_c)}{R T_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Reduced Form of the Van der Waals Equation ### Step 1 — Substitute the Definitions of Reduced Properties By definition: $$P = P_r P_c, \qquad v = v_r v_c, \qquad T = T_r T_c$$ Substitute into the original vdW: $$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{a}{v_r^{2} v_c^{2}}\right)(v_r v_c - b) = R T_r T_c$$ > **Explanation:** $\dfrac{a}{v^2} = \dfrac{a}{v_r^2 v_c^2}$ — pull out $v_c^2$ to isolate $v_r$. ### Step 2 — Insert Critical-Point Expressions Recall: - $v_c = 3b$ → $b = v_c / 3$ - $a = 3 P_c v_c^{2}$ - $R T_c = 8 P_c v_c / 3$ Substituting these into the equation: $$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{3 P_c v_c^{2}}{v_r^{2} v_c^{2}}\right)\left(v_r v_c - \frac{v_c}{3}\right) = R T_r T_c$$ Simplifying inside the brackets: $$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{3 P_c}{v_r^{2}}\right) \cdot v_c \left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = R T_r T_c$$ > **Explanation:** Factor $v_c$ out of the second factor: $v_r v_c - \frac{v_c}{3} = v_c(v_r - \tfrac{1}{3})$. $$v_c \left(P_r P_c + \frac{3 P_c}{v_r^{2}}\right) \left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = R T_r T_c$$ ### Step 3 — Pull Out $P_c$ from the First Factor $$P_c \, v_c \left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right) \left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = R T_r T_c$$ > **Explanation:** $P_r P_c + \dfrac{3 P_c}{v_r^2} = P_c \left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{v_r^2}\right)$. ### Step 4 — Divide Both Sides by $P_c v_c$ $$\left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{R T_r T_c}{P_c v_c}$$ ### Step 5 — Recognize the Ratio as $8T_r/3$ Since $P_c v_c / (R T_c) = 1/Z_c = 8/3$ for vdW: $$\frac{R T_c}{P_c v_c} = \frac{8}{3}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\; \left(P_r + \frac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3}\, T_r \;}$$ > **Explanation:** This equation is **completely universal** — there is no substance-specific constant remaining. All fluids behave identically when expressed in reduced coordinates. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Equations of State | Property | Ideal Gas | Van der Waals | Redlich-Kwong | Virial (truncated) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Equation | $Pv = RT$ | $(P + \frac{a}{v^2})(v-b) = RT$ | $P = \frac{RT}{v-b} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{T}\,v(v+b)}$ | $Z = 1 + \frac{B(T)}{v}$ | | Constants | None | 2 ($a,b$) | 2 ($a,b$) | 1 ($B$) | | Predicts $Z_c$ | 1 | 0.375 | 0.333 | depends on $B$ | | Liquid-phase accuracy | None | Poor | Moderate | Poor | | Gas-phase accuracy | Perfect at low $P$ | Good at moderate $P$ | Better at high $P$ | Best at low $P$ | | Theoretical basis | Kinetic theory | Modified kinetic | Empirical fit | Statistical mechanics | | KTU exam frequency | Reference only | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Determining vdW Constants (for Section 5) **Given:** $T_c = 132.5$ K, $P_c = 37.2$ bar, $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K) for N₂. Compute $a$: $$a = \frac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c} = \frac{27 \times (0.08314)^{2} \times (132.5)^{2}}{64 \times 37.2}$$ Numerator: $0.08314^{2} = 0.006912$ $132.5^{2} = 17556.25$ $27 \times 0.006912 = 0.18663$ $0.18663 \times 17556.25 = 3276.6$ Denominator: $64 \times 37.2 = 2380.8$ $$a = \frac{3276.6}{2380.8} \approx 1.376 \; \text{L}^{2}\text{·bar/mol}^{2}$$ Compute $b$: $$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{0.08314 \times 132.5}{8 \times 37.2} = \frac{11.016}{297.6} \approx 0.03702 \; \text{L/mol}$$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Evolution of Real-Gas Equations of State ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["GAS STATE DEFINITION

Define the term *Compressibility Factor*. State its value for an ideal gas and indicate its significance. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** **Model Answer:** The **Compressibility Factor (Z)** is defined as the ratio of the actual molar volume of a real gas to the molar volume predicted by the ideal gas law at the same temperature and pressure: $$Z = \frac{P v_{\text{actual}}}{R T} = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{ideal}}}$$ For an ideal gas, $Z = 1$ for **all** states $(T, P)$. For real substances, $Z = f(T, P)$: - $Z > 1$ when **repulsive forces dominate** (very high pressure, dense fluids). - $Z < 1$ when **attractive forces dominate** (moderate pressure, near saturation). It is significant because it **measures the deviation from ideal behaviour** and provides a unified way to incorporate real-gas effects into engineering calculations such as compressor work, throttling processes, and phase equilibria. **[3 marks]** --- ### Q2. State the *Principle of Corresponding States*. Define the reduced properties used in this principle. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Principle of Corresponding States:** *All fluids, when compared at the same reduced temperature ($T_r$) and reduced pressure ($P_r$), exhibit approximately the same compressibility factor $Z$ and hence the same deviation from ideal-gas behaviour.* This means that the equation of state, when expressed in **reduced (dimensionless) coordinates**, becomes a universal function independent of the substance: $$Z = f(T_r, P_r)$$ The **reduced properties** are defined with respect to the critical-point values: $$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \qquad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \qquad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}$$ This principle underlies the **Generalized Compressibility Charts** (Lee–Kesler, Nelson–Obert) which are widely used in chemical and mechanical engineering for real-gas property estimation. **[3 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]** **(a) Derive the Van der Waals equation of state from the ideal gas equation. Explain the physical significance of the Van der Waals constants. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start with the ideal gas equation per mole:** $$P v_{\text{ideal}} = R T \qquad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 2 — Correction for finite molecular volume (Repulsive Forces):** The molecules of a real gas are not point masses; each molecule occupies a finite volume $b'$ per molecule. For one mole containing $N_A$ molecules, the excluded volume per mole is $b = 4 N_A \times (\text{volume of one molecule})$, conventionally approximated as **four times the actual molecular volume** (a classical result from rigid-sphere collision geometry). The **available free volume** for molecular motion is therefore reduced from $v$ to $(v - b)$: $$v \;\longrightarrow\; (v - b) \qquad \text{...(ii)}$$ > **Physical significance of $b$:** $b$ is the **excluded (covolume)** per mole, representing the volume inaccessible to molecular centres due to their finite size. **Step 3 — Correction for intermolecular attraction (Attractive Forces):** Molecules in the bulk of the gas are pulled equally in all directions, experiencing zero net force. However, molecules near the **container wall** experience a net *inward* pull because they lack neighbours on one side. This inward pull reduces the **observed pressure** below the ideal-gas pressure. The pressure deficit is proportional to: - the **number density of molecules in the bulk** $\propto \dfrac{1}{v}$, AND - the **number density of molecules near the wall** $\propto \dfrac{1}{v}$. Hence the correction term is $\propto \dfrac{1}{v^{2}}$. Defining the proportionality constant as $a$: $$P_{\text{real}} = P_{\text{ideal}} - \frac{a}{v^{2}} \qquad \text{...(iii)}$$ > **Physical significance of $a$:** $a$ measures the strength of **attractive intermolecular forces**; it has units of [Pa·m⁶/mol²] and is related to the van der Waals constant of attraction. **Step 4 — Combine both corrections:** Substituting (ii) and (iii) into (i): $$\boxed{\; \left(P + \frac{a}{v^{2}}\right)(v - b) = R T \;}$$ This is the **Van der Waals equation of state**. **[7 marks]** --- **(b) A real gas at 300 K and 10 bar occupies a volume such that its compressibility factor is 0.92. Calculate: (i) the molar volume assuming ideal gas behaviour, and (ii) the actual molar volume. Take R = 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K). (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $T = 300$ K, $P = 10$ bar, $Z = 0.92$, $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K) **(i) Ideal-gas molar volume:** $$V_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{R T}{P} = \frac{0.08314 \times 300}{10}$$ $$V_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{24.942}{10} = 2.4942 \; \text{L/mol}$$ **(ii) Actual molar volume:** Since $Z = \dfrac{P V_{\text{actual}}}{R T}$: $$V_{\text{actual}} = Z \times V_{\text{ideal}} = 0.92 \times 2.4942$$ $$V_{\text{actual}} = 2.295 \; \text{L/mol}$$ **Comparison:** - $V_{\text{ideal}} = 2.4942$ L/mol - $V_{\text{actual}} = 2.295$ L/mol - Deviation $= \dfrac{2.4942 - 2.295}{2.4942} \times 100\% \approx 8.0\%$ The actual molar volume is **smaller** than the ideal-gas prediction, indicating that **attractive intermolecular forces dominate** at this state (consistent with $Z < 1$). **[7 marks]** --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]** **(a) With the help of the critical-point conditions, derive expressions for the Van der Waals constants $a$ and $b$ in terms of critical properties. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The critical point on a P-v isotherm is an inflection point, hence: $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial v}\right)_{T_c} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$ **Step 1 — Pressure-explicit vdW form:** $$P = \frac{R T}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^{2}}$$ **Step 2 — First derivative and setting to zero:** $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial v} = -\frac{R T}{(v - b)^{2}} + \frac{2 a}{v^{3}} = 0 \quad \text{at } v = v_c$$ $$\frac{R T_c}{(v_c - b)^{2}} = \frac{2 a}{v_c^{3}} \qquad \text{...(I)}$$ **Step 3 — Second derivative and setting to zero:** $$\frac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial v^{2}} = \frac{2 R T}{(v - b)^{3}} - \frac{6 a}{v^{4}} = 0 \quad \text{at } v = v_c$$ $$\frac{2 R T_c}{(v_c - b)^{3}} = \frac{6 a}{v_c^{4}} \qquad \text{...(II)}$$ **Step 4 — Divide (II) by (I):** $$\frac{2}{v_c - b} = \frac{3}{v_c} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2 v_c = 3(v_c - b)$$ $$\boxed{\, v_c = 3b \,}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into (I):** $$\frac{R T_c}{4 b^{2}} = \frac{2 a}{27 b^{3}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a = \frac{27 R T_c b}{8}$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate $P_c$ from vdW at the critical point:** $$P_c = \frac{R T_c}{v_c - b} - \frac{a}{v_c^{2}} = \frac{R T_c}{2 b} - \frac{a}{9 b^{2}}$$ Substituting $a = \dfrac{27 R T_c b}{8}$: $$P_c = \frac{R T_c}{2 b} - \frac{3 R T_c}{8 b} = \frac{R T_c}{8 b}$$ $$\boxed{\, b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \qquad a = \frac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \qquad v_c = 3 b = \frac{3 R T_c}{8 P_c} \,}$$ The critical compressibility factor is universally predicted by vdW: $$Z_c = \frac{P_c v_c}{R T_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$ (Experimental values typically range from 0.27 to 0.29.) **[7 marks]** --- **(b) For nitrogen, the critical temperature is 126.2 K and critical pressure is 33.5 bar. Calculate the Van der Waals constants $a$ and $b$. Use R = 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K). (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $T_c = 126.2$ K, $P_c = 33.5$ bar, $R = 0.08314$ L·bar/(mol·K) **Step 1 — Compute constant $a$:** $$a = \frac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}$$ Compute numerator: - $R^{2} = (0.08314)^{2} = 6.912 \times 10^{-3}$ - $T_c^{2} = (126.2)^{2} = 15926.44$ - $R^{2} T_c^{2} = 6.912 \times 10^{-3} \times 15926.44 = 110.09$ - $27 \times 110.09 = 2972.4$ Compute denominator: - $64 \times 33.5 = 2144$ $$a = \frac{2972.4}{2144} \approx 1.386 \; \text{L}^{2}\text{·bar/mol}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute constant $b$:** $$b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c} = \frac{0.08314 \times 126.2}{8 \times 33.5}$$ $$b = \frac{10.494}{268.0} \approx 0.03916 \; \text{L/mol}$$ **Step 3 — Critical molar volume from vdW:** $$v_c = 3 b = 3 \times 0.03916 = 0.1175 \; \text{L/mol}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-check using $v_c = \dfrac{3 R T_c}{8 P_c}$:** $$v_c = \frac{3 \times 0.08314 \times 126.2}{8 \times 33.5} = \frac{31.483}{268} \approx 0.1175 \; \text{L/mol} \quad \checkmark$$ **Final Result:** - $a \approx 1.386 \; \text{L}^{2}\cdot\text{bar/mol}^{2}$ - $b \approx 0.03916 \; \text{L/mol}$ **[7 marks]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks in this Module:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 27 or 64** in $a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}$ — these are *derived* numbers, not memorised shortcuts; know the derivation. > 2. **Mixing up $b$ and $a$ units** — $a$ has units [Pa·m⁶/mol²] or [L²·bar/mol²]; $b$ has units [m³/mol] or [L/mol]. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors. > 3. **Not specifying whether $v$ is molar or specific** — always use **molar volume** ($v$ in m³/mol) when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). > 4. **Applying ideal-gas law for CO₂, NH₃, refrigerants** — examiners deduct heavily. Always mention "for an ideal gas" and "real-gas correction via Z" explicitly. > 5. **Skipping the physical significance of $a$ and $b$** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises conceptual clarity under OBE; a derivation without interpretation loses 2–3 marks. > 6. **Writing $\frac{3}{8}$ without showing it comes from vdW** — always state "$Z_c = \frac{3}{8}$ for a vdW fluid" so the examiner knows you derived it, not guessed it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### 🧠 Must-Know Core Results - [x] Ideal gas EoS: $Pv = RT$ — valid only at low $P$, high $T$. - [x] **Compressibility Factor**: $Z = Pv / RT$; equals 1 for ideal gases. - [x] **Van der Waals EoS**: $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{v^2}\right)(v - b) = RT$ — two corrections (attraction + finite size). - [x] **vdW constants from critical data**: $a = \dfrac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$, $b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$. - [x] **Critical volume prediction**: $v_c = 3b = \dfrac{3 R T_c}{8 P_c}$. - [x] **Universal vdW critical compressibility**: $Z_c = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$ (over-estimates real values of 0.27–0.29). - [x] **Reduced vdW EoS**: $\left(P_r + \dfrac{3}{v_r^{2}}\right)\left(v_r - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{8}{3} T_r$ — universal, substance-independent. - [x] **Reduced properties**: $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$, $v_r = v/v_c$. - [x] **Virial EoS**: $Z = 1 + \dfrac{B(T)}{v} + \dfrac{C(T)}{v^2} + \dots$ — the most theoretically rigorous. - [x] **Principle of Corresponding States**: $Z = f(T_r, P_r)$ — foundation of compressibility charts. ### 🧠 Must-Know Physical Insights - [x] $Z > 1$ → repulsive forces dominate (very high pressure). - [x] $Z < 1$ → attractive forces dominate (moderate pressure). - [x] $Z = 1$ at all $P$ → **Boyle Temperature** $T_B = a/(Rb)$. - [x] Inversion temperature $T_I = 2 T_B$ (Joule–Thomson coefficient = 0). - [x] vdW predicts **continuous oscillation** between P and V below $T_c$; real fluids show **horizontal tie-line** (Maxwell construction). ### 🧠 Must-Know Units (Universal Constants) | Quantity | SI Units | CGS / Engineering Units | |---|---|---| | $R$ | 8.314 J/(mol·K) | 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K) | | $a$ | Pa·m⁶/mol² | L²·bar/mol² | | $b$ | m³/mol | L/mol | | $P_c$ | Pa | bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa) | | $T_c$ | K | K | ### 🧠 Must-Know Numerical Benchmarks | Substance | $T_c$ (K) | $P_c$ (bar) | $Z_c$ (experimental) | $a$ (L²·bar/mol²) | $b$ (L/mol) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Nitrogen (N₂) | 126.2 | 33.5 | 0.290 | 1.386 | 0.0392 | | Oxygen (O₂) | 154.6 | 50.4 | 0.288 | 1.378 | 0.0319 | | Carbon dioxide (CO₂) | 304.1 | 73.8 | 0.274 | 3.640 | 0.0427 | | Water (H₂O) | 647.1 | 220.6 | 0.229 | 5.537 | 0.0305 | | Ammonia (NH₃) | 405.5 | 111.3 | 0.244 | 4.225 | 0.0372 | | Methane (CH₄) | 190.4 | 46.0 | 0.286 | 2.293 | 0.0429 | > **Memory anchor for KTU exams:** *All real substances have $Z_c < 0.375$ (the vdW value); lower $Z_c$ means stronger intermolecular attraction (e.g., water has the lowest $Z_c$ due to hydrogen bonding).*

topics.title: Van der Waals Equation of State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Van der Waals Equation of State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Van der Waals Equation of State** is an empirical modification of the ideal gas law that accounts for two key non-ideal behaviors of real gases: (1) the finite volume occupied by gas molecules (repulsive forces at short range), and (2) the intermolecular attractive forces (cohesive forces) between molecules. It is mathematically expressed as:
>
> $$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$
>
> where $P$ is pressure, $V_m$ is the molar volume, $T$ is absolute temperature, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and **a** and **b** are substance-specific empirical constants called **van der Waals constants**.

For $n$ moles of gas, the equation is written as:

$$\left(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$$

where **V** is the total volume of the gas.

> [!NOTE] **Why this matters in KTU 2024**
> This equation is a **CO2 (Apply)** level question in Module 4 and bridges the gap between the simplified Ideal Gas Law and complex real-fluid behavior. It is the foundational equation used to derive critical constants, reduced properties, and the Law of Corresponding States.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **crowded classroom during an exam**:

- **The Ideal Gas Assumption** treats students as mathematical points — they have zero size and pass through each other without any interaction. Clearly unrealistic!
- **The "b" constant** is like saying each student occupies one desk. You cannot compress 50 students into 10 desks; there is a minimum free volume below which no student can squeeze. The constant **b** represents this **excluded volume** per mole due to the finite size of molecules.
- **The "a" constant** is like saying students sitting far apart don't interact, but students seated close together feel "attraction" (peer pressure, group dynamics). Molecules near the walls of the container experience an inward pull from neighboring molecules, reducing the actual force exerted on the walls. This causes an **effective pressure reduction**, captured by the correction term $\frac{a}{V_m^2}$.

> [!TIP] **Conceptual Takeaway**
> - **a** → Accounts for **attraction** (makes pressure *lower* than ideal)
> - **b** → Accounts for **repulsion / size** (makes volume *larger* than ideal)

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Units

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Van der Waals constant a:** Units of $\text{Pa·m}^6/\text{mol}^2$ (SI) or $\text{atm·L}^2/\text{mol}^2$ (CGS). Indicates strength of intermolecular attraction.
- **Van der Waals constant b:** Units of $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$ (SI) or $\text{L/mol}$ (CGS). Represents excluded volume per mole.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Limitations of the Ideal Gas Equation

The ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ fails for real gases at high pressures and low temperatures due to two fundamental oversights:

1. **Molecules are not point masses** — They possess a finite volume, making them **non-compressible** beyond a limit.
2. **Intermolecular forces exist** — Molecules attract and repel each other, especially near phase transitions.

### 2.2 Logical Steps of van der Waals' Modification

**Step 1: Pressure Correction (Attractive Forces)**
- In an ideal gas, pressure is calculated assuming no molecular interaction.
- In reality, molecules near the container wall experience a net inward pull from neighboring molecules, reducing the momentum transfer to the wall.
- This **effective pressure drop** is proportional to the square of the number density.
- Corrected pressure: $P_{\text{ideal}} = P_{\text{real}} + \frac{a}{V_m^2}$

**Step 2: Volume Correction (Finite Molecular Size)**
- In an ideal gas, molecules can be compressed to zero volume (impossible in reality).
- The space actually available for molecular motion is reduced by the volume occupied by molecules themselves.
- Corrected available volume: $V_{\text{available}} = V_m - b$

**Step 3: Combine into Modified Equation**
- Substituting these corrections into $PV_m = RT$:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Table 2.1: Master Formula Reference for KTU Examinations**

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| Van der Waals Equation (per mole) | $(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2})(V_m - b) = RT$ | Full equation of state |
| Van der Waals Equation (n moles) | $(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2})(V - nb) = nRT$ | Generalized form |
| Critical Pressure | $P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$ | Pressure at critical point |
| Critical Volume (per mole) | $V_{mc} = 3b$ | Volume at critical point |
| Critical Temperature | $T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$ | Temperature at critical point |
| Critical Compressibility Factor | $Z_c = \frac{P_c V_{mc}}{R T_c} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$ | Universal constant for all vdw gases |
| Reduced Pressure | $P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}$ | Dimensionless pressure |
| Reduced Temperature | $T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}$ | Dimensionless temperature |
| Reduced Volume | $V_{mr} = \frac{V_m}{V_{mc}}$ | Dimensionless volume |
| Reduced Equation of State | $\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_{mr}^2}\right)(3V_{mr} - 1) = 8T_r$ | Universal — Law of Corresponding States |
| Compressibility Factor | $Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT}$ | Deviation from ideal behavior |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Liquefaction of gases:** Predicting conditions for converting gases to liquids (LNG, LPG, oxygen, nitrogen).
- **Refrigeration and Air Conditioning:** Designing compressors using refrigerant properties.
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Accurate prediction of P-V-T behavior for hydrocarbons.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Modeling crude oil and natural gas behavior in reservoirs.
- **Cryogenics:** Handling gases at extremely low temperatures near their critical points.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Critical Constants from the Van der Waals Equation

> [!NOTE] **Critical Point Definition**
> The critical point is the state at which the liquid and gas phases become indistinguishable. Mathematically, this corresponds to an **inflection point** on the P-V isotherm where both the first and second partial derivatives of pressure with respect to volume equal zero:
> $$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_T = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_T = 0$$

**Starting Equation (expanded in powers of V):**

Starting from the van der Waals equation:

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

Expanding the left-hand side:

$$P V_m - P b + \frac{a}{V_m} - \frac{ab}{V_m^2} = RT$$

Solving explicitly for $P$ (so we can differentiate with respect to $V_m$):

$$P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}$$

**Step 1: First Derivative Condition**

Differentiating $P$ with respect to $V_m$ at constant $T$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_m^3}$$

Setting this equal to zero at the critical point ($V_m = V_{mc}$):

$$\frac{RT_c}{(V_{mc} - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V_{mc}^3}$$

Rearranging:

$$RT_c = \frac{2a(V_{mc} - b)^2}{V_{mc}^3} \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Step 2: Second Derivative Condition**

Differentiating the first derivative again:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_T = \frac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_m^4}$$

Setting this equal to zero at $V_m = V_{mc}$:

$$\frac{2RT_c}{(V_{mc} - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V_{mc}^4}$$

Simplifying:

$$RT_c = \frac{3a(V_{mc} - b)^3}{V_{mc}^4} \quad \cdots (ii)$$

**Step 3: Equating (i) and (ii)**

Since both equal $RT_c$, we set them equal:

$$\frac{2a(V_{mc} - b)^2}{V_{mc}^3} = \frac{3a(V_{mc} - b)^3}{V_{mc}^4}$$

Cancel common terms $a$, $(V_{mc} - b)^2$, and one $V_{mc}$:

$$2 = \frac{3(V_{mc} - b)}{V_{mc}}$$

Solving:

$$2V_{mc} = 3V_{mc} - 3b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{V_{mc} = 3b}$$

**Step 4: Solving for Critical Temperature**

Substituting $V_{mc} = 3b$ back into equation (i):

$$RT_c = \frac{2a(3b - b)^2}{(3b)^3} = \frac{2a(2b)^2}{27b^3} = \frac{2a \cdot 4b^2}{27b^3} = \frac{8a}{27b}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}}$$

**Step 5: Solving for Critical Pressure**

Substituting $V_{mc} = 3b$ and $T_c$ into the van der Waals equation:

$$P_c = \frac{RT_c}{V_{mc} - b} - \frac{a}{V_{mc}^2}$$

$$P_c = \frac{R \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}}{3b - b} - \frac{a}{(3b)^2}$$

$$P_c = \frac{8a/27}{2b} - \frac{a}{9b^2} = \frac{8a}{54b} - \frac{a}{9b^2}$$

$$P_c = \frac{4a}{27b} - \frac{a}{9b^2} = \frac{4a \cdot b}{27b^2} - \frac{3a}{27b^2} = \frac{a}{27b^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reduced Equation of State

> [!IMPORTANT] **Law of Corresponding States**
> Dividing the van der Waals equation by the corresponding critical constant expressions yields a **universal equation** containing only dimensionless reduced parameters. This proves that all gases obey the same equation when expressed in reduced form.

**Starting Equation:**

$$\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT$$

**Step 1: Express in terms of reduced variables**

Substitute $P = P_r P_c$, $V_m = V_{mr} V_{mc} = 3b V_{mr}$, and $T = T_r T_c$:

$$\left(P_r P_c + \frac{a}{(3bV_{mr})^2}\right)(3bV_{mr} - b) = R T_r T_c$$

**Step 2: Substitute $P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$, $V_{mc} = 3b$, $T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$**

$$\left(P_r \cdot \frac{a}{27b^2} + \frac{a}{9b^2 V_{mr}^2}\right)(3bV_{mr} - b) = R T_r \cdot \frac{8a}{27Rb}$$

**Step 3: Factor common terms**

$$\frac{a}{27b^2}\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_{mr}^2}\right) \cdot b(3V_{mr} - 1) = \frac{8aT_r}{27}$$

Cancel $a$ on both sides:

$$\frac{1}{27b}(P_r + \frac{3}{V_{mr}^2})(3V_{mr} - 1) = \frac{8T_r}{27}$$

**Step 4: Final Reduced Equation**

Multiplying both sides by $27b$:

$$\boxed{\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_{mr}^2}\right)(3V_{mr} - 1) = 8T_r}$$

This equation contains **no material-specific constants (a or b)**, demonstrating that all van der Waals gases behave identically in reduced coordinates.

### 3.3 Derivation of Boyle Temperature for Van der Waals Gas

At the **Boyle temperature** $T_B$, the gas behaves ideally at low pressures. The second virial coefficient vanishes.

For van der Waals equation expanded in virial form:

$$Z = 1 + \left(b - \frac{a}{RT}\right)\frac{1}{V_m} + \cdots$$

Setting the first-order correction to zero:

$$b - \frac{a}{RT_B} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{T_B = \frac{a}{Rb}}$$

This is approximately **2.67 times the critical temperature** ($T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb} \approx 0.296 \frac{a}{Rb}$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Ideal Gas vs. Van der Waals Gas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REAL GAS BEHAVIOR] --> B{Is gas ideal?}
    B -->|Yes, low P and high T| C[IDEAL GAS LAW: PV = nRT]
    B -->|No, high P or low T| D[NEED CORRECTIONS]

    D --> E[CORRECTION 1: FINITE MOLECULAR SIZE]
    D --> F[CORRECTION 2: INTERMOLECULAR FORCES]

    E --> E1[Molecules occupy space]
    E1 --> E2[Effective volume = V minus nb]
    E2 --> E3[Constant b represents excluded volume]

    F --> F1[Molecules attract each other]
    F1 --> F2[Inward pull near walls reduces pressure]
    F2 --> F3[Constant a represents attraction strength]

    E3 --> G[VAN DER WAALS EQUATION]
    F3 --> G

    G --> G1[Pressure form: P + a over V squared]
    G --> G2[Volume form: V minus b]
    G1 --> H[FULL EQUATION OF STATE]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Derive Critical Constants]
    H --> J[Derive Reduced Equation]
    H --> K[Predict Real Gas Behavior]
```

### 4.2 P-V Isotherm Architecture (Van der Waals Gas)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_TEMP["HIGH TEMPERATURE T GREATER THAN T_C"]
        A1[Monotonically decreasing curve] --> A2[Single phase region: supercritical fluid]
    end

    subgraph AT_CRITICAL["AT CRITICAL TEMPERATURE T EQUALS T_C"]
        B1[Curve has inflection point] --> B2[Critical point P_C, V_C, T_C]
        B2 --> B3[Horizontal tangent at critical point]
    end

    subgraph LOW_TEMP["LOW TEMPERATURE T LESS THAN T_C"]
        C1[Curve shows wavy behavior] --> C2[Maxima and minima appear]
        C2 --> C3[Unstable region Maxwell construction]
        C3 --> C4[Liquid vapor coexistence line]
    end

    subgraph BELOW_TEMP["BELOW CRITICAL: THREE REGIONS"]
        D1[Liquid region high P low V] --> D2[Two phase region] --> D3[Gas region low P high V]
    end

    HIGH_TEMP --> AT_CRITICAL
    AT_CRITICAL --> LOW_TEMP
    LOW_TEMP --> BELOW_TEMP
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Van der Waals Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input: Gas Type and Operating Conditions] --> PARAMS[Determine van der Waals constants a and b]
    PARAMS --> EQ[Apply VdW Equation]

    EQ --> CALC1[Calculate P given V and T]
    EQ --> CALC2[Calculate V given P and T]
    EQ --> CALC3[Calculate T given P and V]

    CALC1 --> CRITICAL[Compute Critical Constants]
    CALC2 --> CRITICAL
    CALC3 --> CRITICAL

    CRITICAL --> CP[Critical Pressure: a over 27 b squared]
    CRITICAL --> CV[Critical Volume: 3b]
    CRITICAL --> CT[Critical Temperature: 8a over 27 R b]

    CP --> REDUCED[Reduced Properties]
    CV --> REDUCED
    CT --> REDUCED

    REDUCED --> APP1[LNG Production: Liquefaction below T_C]
    REDUCED --> APP2[Compressor Design: Refrigeration cycles]
    REDUCED --> APP3[Chemical Reactor Design: PVT prediction]
    REDUCED --> APP4[Petroleum Reservoir Modeling: Gas behavior]
```

### 4.4 Comparison Table: Ideal Gas vs. Van der Waals Gas

> [!NOTE] **Table 4.1: Property Comparison**

| Feature | Ideal Gas | Van der Waals Gas |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| Molecular volume | Negligible (point particles) | Finite volume (excluded via b) |
| Intermolecular forces | None | Attractive (accounted via a) |
| Equation | $PV = nRT$ | $(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2})(V - nb) = nRT$ |
| Accuracy | Limited to low P, high T | Better across wider ranges |
| Critical point | Cannot predict | Predicts $P_c, V_c, T_c$ |
| Phase transitions | Cannot describe | Qualitative description |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Question 1.** State the van der Waals equation of state for $n$ moles of a real gas and explain the physical significance of the constants **a** and **b**.

**Model Answer:**
The van der Waals equation of state for $n$ moles of a real gas is:

$$\left(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2}\right)(V - nb) = nRT$$

- **Constant a (Pressure Correction):** Accounts for intermolecular attractive forces between real gas molecules. Molecules near the walls experience a net inward pull, reducing the actual pressure exerted on the container walls. The term $\frac{a}{V_m^2}$ represents this pressure correction. Units: $\text{Pa·m}^6/\text{mol}^2$.
- **Constant b (Volume Correction):** Accounts for the finite size of gas molecules. Since molecules are not point masses, the effective free volume available for motion is reduced by the term $nb$. Units: $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$.

> [!TIP] **Valuation Tip:** Always state the equation first, then explain *both* constants separately with their units.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Question 2.** What is the law of corresponding states? Write the reduced form of the van der Waals equation.

**Model Answer:**
The **Law of Corresponding States** states that all real gases, when expressed in terms of their reduced properties (reduced pressure, reduced temperature, and reduced volume), obey the same universal equation of state regardless of their chemical nature.

The reduced properties are defined as:

$$P_r = \frac{P}{P_c}, \quad T_r = \frac{T}{T_c}, \quad V_{mr} = \frac{V_m}{V_{mc}}$$

The reduced form of the van der Waals equation is:

$$\left(P_r + \frac{3}{V_{mr}^2}\right)(3V_{mr} - 1) = 8T_r$$

This equation contains no substance-specific constants, confirming the universality of the law.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper Style]*
