# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**State the two Tds equations and explain their physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The two Tds equations, also known as the Gibbs equations, are:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{...(First Tds equation)}$$

$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP \quad \text{...(Second Tds equation)}$$

**Physical Significance:** These equations represent the unification of the First Law and Second Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system undergoing a reversible process. They form the foundation of thermodynamic property relations and are used to:

(i) Express entropy change in terms of measurable properties ($T$, $P$, $v$, $c_p$, $c_v$).

(ii) Derive Maxwell's relations that connect seemingly unrelated derivatives.

(iii) Compute real-gas departure functions where ideal gas relations fail.

(iv) Evaluate cycle performance in power plants, refrigerators, and gas turbines.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Show that for an ideal gas, the entropy change can be expressed as $ds = c_p \frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$.**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from the second Tds equation:
$$T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

For an ideal gas, $dh = c_p\,dT$, and $v = \dfrac{RT}{P}$. Substituting:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - \frac{RT}{P}\,dP$$

Dividing throughout by $T$:

$$ds = c_p\frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$$

This is the required expression.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the first and second Tds equations for a pure substance starting from the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an ideal gas, integrate the second Tds equation to obtain the standard entropy change formula in terms of $c_p$, $T$, and $P$. Hence, calculate the entropy change of **2 kg** of air ($c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) heated from **300 K, 100 kPa** to **500 K, 300 kPa**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Full derivation shown in **Section 3.1** above. Key result:

$$T\,ds = du + P\,dv \quad \text{and} \quad T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$$

The derivations must show: (i) First Law $dU = \delta Q - P dV$, (ii) Second Law $dS = \delta Q_{rev}/T$, (iii) their combination, and (iv) conversion using $h = u + Pv$.

**(b)** Starting from $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$, for an ideal gas with constant $c_p$:

$$T\,ds = c_p\,dT - \frac{RT}{P}\,dP \implies ds = c_p\frac{dT}{T} - R\frac{dP}{P}$$

Integrating from State 1 to State 2:

$$s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta s = 1.005 \ln\frac{500}{300} - 0.287 \ln\frac{300}{100}$$

$$\ln\frac{500}{300} = \ln(1.6667) = 0.51083$$

$$\ln\frac{300}{100} = \ln(3.0) = 1.09861$$

$$\Delta s = (1.005)(0.51083) - (0.287)(1.09861)$$

$$\Delta s = 0.51338 - 0.31530 = 0.19808 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

Total entropy change: $\Delta S = m \cdot \Delta s = 2 \times 0.19808 = 0.3962$ kJ/K.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Starting from the first Tds equation, derive the relation $T\,ds = c_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ and show that $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas obeys the equation of state $Pv = RT + \alpha P - \dfrac{\beta P^2}{T}$, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants. Compute $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Derivation shown in **Section 3.2** above. Key steps:

- Express $du = c_v\,dT + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T dv$
- Substitute in $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$
- Match coefficients with exact differential of $ds$
- Apply Maxwell relation to obtain $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$

**(b)** Given: $Pv = RT + \alpha P - \dfrac{\beta P^2}{T}$.

Solve for $P$ as a function of $(T, v)$ implicitly, but it is easier to solve for $P$:

$$Pv - \alpha P + \frac{\beta P^2}{T} = RT$$

$$P\left(v - \alpha + \frac{\beta P}{T}\right) = RT$$

Using implicit differentiation, treating $v$ as a function of $(T, P)$:

$$v = \frac{RT}{P} + \alpha - \frac{\beta P}{T}$$

Compute $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{R}{P} - \beta P \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{T^2}\right) = \frac{R}{P} + \frac{\beta P}{T^2}$$

Compute $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ via the inverse partial derivative:

$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{1}{\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T}$$

From the equation of state:
$$\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{RT}{P^2} - \frac{\beta}{T}$$

Therefore:
$$\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v = \frac{1}{-\dfrac{RT}{P^2} - \dfrac{\beta}{T}} = \frac{-P^2 T}{RT^2 + \beta P^2}$$

Apply the departure relation:
$$\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P = T \cdot \frac{-P^2 T}{RT^2 + \beta P^2} - P = -\frac{T^2 P^2}{RT^2 + \beta P^2} - P$$

Simplifying:

$$\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = -\frac{T^2 P^2 + P(RT^2 + \beta P^2)}{RT^2 + \beta P^2} = -\frac{T^2 P^2 + PRT^2 + \beta P^3}{RT^2 + \beta P^2}$$

For an ideal gas ($\alpha = \beta = 0$): $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = 0$, confirming the **Joule's Law** result. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to divide by mass $m$ and writing $T\,dS = dU + P\,dV$ when the question asks for specific form $T\,ds$. Both are acceptable, but ensure consistency throughout the solution.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the sign in the second Tds equation — it is $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$, **NOT** $T\,ds = dh + v\,dP$. Marks deducted: ~2 marks per occurrence.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Inverting partial derivatives incorrectly when computing $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$ from $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$. Remember: $\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v \cdot \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_v = 1$ but cross-variable inversions require careful chain rule application.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to integrate $\ln$ terms correctly. Students often write $\log$ instead of $\ln$ or use base-10 logarithm. KTU strictly expects natural logarithm ($\ln$) for entropy computations.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Failing to specify **assumptions** (e.g., ideal gas behavior, constant specific heats, closed system) before applying the integrated ideal gas formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tds Equations**

- ⚡ **The two Tds equations are:** $T\,ds = du + P\,dv$ and $T\,ds = dh - v\,dP$. They apply only to **closed systems** undergoing **reversible** processes for **simple compressible** substances.

- ⚡ **Both equations originate from the combined First and Second Law:** $dU = T\,dS - P\,dV$.

- ⚡ **Expanded forms are the most exam-relevant:** $T\,ds = c_v\,dT + T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v dv$ and $T\,ds = c_p\,dT - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P dP$.

- ⚡ **The internal energy departure relation:** $\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial v}\right)_T = T\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v - P$. For ideal gas, this equals **zero** (Joule's Law).

- ⚡ **The enthalpy departure relation:** $\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$. For ideal gas, this equals **zero**.

- ⚡ **Ideal gas integrated forms** (constant $c_p$, $c_v$):
$$s_2 - s_1 = c_v \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} + R\ln\frac{v_2}{v_1} = c_p \ln\frac{T_2}{T_1} - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

- ⚡ **For variable specific heats**, integrate tabulated $c_p(T)$ data:
$$s_2 - s_1 = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{c_p(T)}{T}\,dT - R\ln\frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

- ⚡ **Maxwell's relations are derived from the second derivatives of exact differentials** $du$, $dh$, $df$ (Helmholtz), $dg$ (Gibbs). The Tds equations provide the framework for two of them:
  - $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial v}\right)_T = \left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial T}\right)_v$
  - $\left(\frac{\partial s}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$

- ⚡ **Real-world usage:** Steam power plants (Rankine), refrigeration (VCRS), gas turbines (Brayton), chemical reactors, cryogenic liquefaction.

- ⚡ **Units check:** $T\,ds$ has units $\text{K} \cdot \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} = \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}$, matching $du$ and $dh$. ✓

- ⚡ **Reversibility caveat:** The Tds equations hold **only for reversible processes**. For irreversible processes, use $dS \geq \delta Q/T$ (Clausius inequality).

- ⚡ **Property pairs:** The first Tds equation uses $(u, v)$; the second uses $(h, P)$. Choose based on convenience of data.

- ⚡ **Cross-verification:** Both equations must yield identical $\Delta s$ for the same state change. Always check consistency when solving two-step problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throttling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Throttling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Throttling Process** is defined as a **steady-flow, adiabatic, irreversible expansion process** of a fluid through a constriction (such as a valve, porous plug, narrow orifice, or any sudden restriction) in which there is a **significant pressure drop** but **no work is done** and **no appreciable change in kinetic or potential energy** occurs across the device. The process is governed by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**, which under the stated conditions reduces to the isenthalpic condition $h_1 = h_2$.

In formal thermodynamic language, a throttling process is an **isenthalpic (constant enthalpy)** process — a highly irreversible process characterized by a finite pressure drop over an infinitesimally small region, often treated as a **point process** or **control volume of negligible volume**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a garden hose:** You are watering plants, and the tap water (high pressure) flows out gently through your thumb, which slightly squeezes the nozzle (the *constriction*). As the fluid squeezes past your thumb, its pressure suddenly drops, and you feel the water become slightly cooler on your hand. You did **no work** with a pump, **no heat** was transferred, but the pressure dropped. This is essentially a throttling process — the fluid "relaxes" through a narrow restriction.

**Real-world examples that KTU examiners love:**
- **Refrigerant** passing through the **expansion valve** in a domestic refrigerator.
- **Steam** being throttled to produce **superheated steam** from wet steam in a throttling calorimeter.
- **Natural gas** flowing through **pressure-reducing stations** in pipelines.
- **LPG** being released from a pressurized cylinder through its valve.

### 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics

- The **Joule-Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ has SI units of **K/Pa** (or K/bar in engineering practice).
- The **Inversion Temperature** $T_{inv}$ is measured in **Kelvin (K)** or **°C**.
- Dryness fraction $x$ is **dimensionless** ($0 \leq x \leq 1$).
- Enthalpy $h$ is measured in **kJ/kg** for engineering convenience.

### 1.4 Key Characteristics Summary

| Property | Behavior in Throttling |
|----------|------------------------|
| Enthalpy ($h$) | **Constant** ($h_1 = h_2$) |
| Heat transfer ($Q$) | **Zero** ($q = 0$) |
| Work done ($W$) | **Zero** ($w = 0$) |
| Kinetic energy change | **Negligible** ($\Delta KE \approx 0$) |
| Potential energy change | **Negligible** ($\Delta PE \approx 0$) |
| Entropy ($s$) | **Increases** ($s_2 > s_1$, irreversible) |
| Pressure ($P$) | **Decreases** ($P_2 < P_1$) |
| Process Type | **Irreversible**, Adiabatic |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Throttling Phenomenon

A throttling process occurs when fluid flows from a region of **high pressure** to a region of **low pressure** through a restriction. The logic steps are:

1. **Fluid enters** the restriction at state 1 with high pressure $P_1$, enthalpy $h_1$.
2. **Restriction is encountered** — the flow passage narrows abruptly.
3. **Fluid squeezes through** — the local velocity briefly increases, friction is intense, and turbulence is generated.
4. **Fluid exits** at state 2 with low pressure $P_2 < P_1$ and enthalpy $h_2$.
5. **Theoretical postulate:** Since no work is done, no heat is exchanged, and KE/PE changes are negligible, the energy balance yields $h_1 = h_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is it NOT isentropic?** Even though it is adiabatic ($q = 0$), the process generates entropy internally due to **friction** and **unrestrained expansion**. By the second law of thermodynamics, $\Delta s \geq 0$, with strict inequality for an irreversible process. Hence, $s_2 > s_1$.

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Justification of Constant Enthalpy

Starting from the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** for a control volume:

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(Z_2 - Z_1)$$

Substituting the throttling assumptions:
- $q = 0$ (adiabatic, fast process, no time for heat transfer)
- $w = 0$ (no shaft work, no boundary work for steady state)
- $\Delta KE \approx 0$ (small velocity change post-restriction)
- $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (horizontal flow, no elevation change)

We obtain:

$$0 = (h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0 \implies h_1 = h_2$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|----------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Isenthalpic condition (defining equation) | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $q - w = \Delta h + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$ | Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $s_2 > s_1$ | Entropy increases (irreversible) | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 4 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule-Thomson coefficient | K/Pa |
| 5 | $dT = \mu_{JT} \, dP$ | Temperature change for finite pressure drop | K |
| 6 | $h_{f1} + x_1 h_{fg1} = h_{2}$ | Throttling calorimeter (steam side) | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $x_1 = \dfrac{h_2 - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}$ | Dryness fraction of incoming wet steam | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{inv}$ | Inversion temperature ($\mu_{JT} = 0$) | K or °C |
| 9 | $\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = c_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Entropy change for ideal gas throttling | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 10 | $h = c_p T$ (ideal gas) | Used to show $T_1 = T_2$ for ideal gas | kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic device for KTU exams:** **"H.Q.W.K.P"** — in a throttle, **H**eat = 0, **Q** = 0, **W**ork = 0, **K**E unchanged, **P**E unchanged. This leaves only **H** (enthalpy) constant.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refrigeration & Air-conditioning:** The **expansion valve** is essentially a throttling device that drops refrigerant pressure from condenser to evaporator levels.
- **Power Plants:** **Cascade throttling** in combined-cycle plants helps reduce steam pressure before feeding to next turbine stage.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Used in **Joule-Thomson cryogenic plants** to liquefy gases like nitrogen, oxygen, helium, and natural gas (LNG).
- **Steam Calorimetry:** The **throttling calorimeter** determines the **dryness fraction** of wet steam, a critical parameter for boiler efficiency assessment.
- **Gas Distribution Networks:** Pressure-reducing stations use throttle valves to step down pipeline gas pressure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Isenthalpic Condition from SFEE

We begin with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a **steady-flow open system** (control volume) of negligible volume:

$$q - w = \Delta h + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

**Step 1 — Expand the terms explicitly:**

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(Z_2 - Z_1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the throttling assumptions:**

The throttling process is characterized by:
- No heat transfer across system boundary: $q = 0$
- No shaft work and no work done on/by surroundings: $w = 0$
- The fluid velocity upstream and downstream of the throttle are approximately equal: $V_1 \approx V_2$
- The device is horizontal, so elevation does not change: $Z_1 \approx Z_2$

Substituting these:

$$0 - 0 = (h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

$$h_2 - h_1 = 0 \implies \boxed{h_1 = h_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms that the throttling process is an **isenthalpic process**. The *control volume* is taken to be infinitesimally thin so that no fluid mass is contained within it, which is why $\Delta KE = 0$ even though fluid accelerates momentarily within the restriction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Joule-Thomson Coefficient for Real Gases

The **Joule-Thomson coefficient** quantifies the temperature change of a fluid per unit pressure drop during throttling:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

**Step 1 — Start with the total differential of enthalpy $h = h(T, P)$:**

$$dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

**Step 2 — Define partial derivatives using thermodynamic identities:**

We know:
- $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P = c_P$ (specific heat at constant pressure)
- $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = -T \left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P + v$ (Maxwell-type relation)

**Step 3 — Apply the isenthalpic condition $dh = 0$:**

$$0 = c_P \, dT + \left[v - T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the rate $dT/dP$ at constant $h$:**

$$c_P \, dT = \left[T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right] dP$$

$$\frac{dT}{dP}\bigg|_h = \frac{1}{c_P}\left[T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Joule-Thomson coefficient:**

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{c_P}\left[T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the sign:**

- If $\mu_{JT} > 0$ (most gases at room temperature): $T$ decreases as $P$ drops → **cooling effect** (e.g., LPG cylinder feels cold).
- If $\mu_{JT} < 0$ (hydrogen, helium at room temperature): $T$ increases as $P$ drops → **heating effect**.
- If $\mu_{JT} = 0$: temperature remains constant → **inversion temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> For an **ideal gas**, $v = RT/P$, so $\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P = R/P$, and thus $\mu_{JT} = 0$. This is the well-known **Joule's law**: ideal gas temperature is independent of pressure at constant enthalpy. Real gases deviate due to intermolecular forces.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Throttling Calorimeter — Dryness Fraction of Steam

A **throttling calorimeter** is an experimental device used to determine the **dryness fraction** of wet steam that has already expanded in a steam engine or turbine.

**Step 1 — Identify the two states:**

- **State 1 (upstream):** Wet steam at pressure $P_1$ and dryness fraction $x_1$.
- **State 2 (downstream):** Superheated steam at pressure $P_2$ and temperature $T_2$.

**Step 2 — Write the enthalpy at state 1 (wet steam):**

$$h_1 = h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1}$$

where $h_{f1}$ is the saturated liquid enthalpy and $h_{fg1}$ is the latent heat of vaporization at $P_1$.

**Step 3 — Write the enthalpy at state 2 (superheated steam):**

$$h_2 = h_{g2} + c_{p,\text{steam}}(T_2 - T_{sat,2})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the isenthalpic condition $h_1 = h_2$:**

$$h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} = h_{g2} + c_p (T_2 - T_{sat,2})$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x_1$:**

$$x_1 = \frac{h_{g2} + c_p(T_2 - T_{sat,2}) - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}$$

$$\boxed{x_1 = \frac{h_2 - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of the throttling calorimeter:** The pressure $P_2$ after throttling must be low enough that the steam becomes **superheated** (i.e., $T_2 > T_{sat}$ at $P_2$). Otherwise, the exit state may still be wet, and the simple linear formula above does not apply.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Temperature Change for Real Gas Throttling

For a finite pressure drop $P_1 \to P_2$ in a real gas:

**Step 1 — Use the integrated form:**

$$\Delta T = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \mu_{JT} \, dP \approx \mu_{JT,\text{avg}} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$$

**Step 2 — Approximate with average $\mu_{JT}$:**

Since $\mu_{JT}$ is approximately constant over moderate pressure ranges:

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx \mu_{JT} (P_2 - P_1)}$$

Note that $P_2 < P_1$, so $\Delta T$ has the **opposite sign** of $\mu_{JT}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Throttling Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INLET 1: High P1, T1, h1, V1"] --> B["RESTRICTION: Valve / Orifice / Porous Plug"]
    B --> C["Local Acceleration: V rises sharply"]
    C --> D["Turbulence and Friction Dissipation"]
    D --> E["OUTLET 2: Low P2, T2, h2, V2"]
    B -.->|q=0| F["ADIABATIC: No Heat Transfer"]
    B -.->|w=0| G["NO WORK: Wshaft = 0"]
    E -.->|h1 = h2| H["ISENTHALPIC STATE: Constant Enthalpy"]
    E -.->|s2 > s1| I["IRREVERSIBLE: Entropy Increases"]
    H --> J["APPLICATIONS: Expansion Valve, Throttling Calorimeter, Pressure Regulator"]
    I --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Throttling Calorimeter Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Steam Engine / Turbine"] -->|Wet Steam: P1, x1| V["Throttling Valve"]
    V -->|Superheated Steam: P2, T2| TC["Thermometer Reads T2"]
    TC --> PG["Pressure Gauge Reads P2"]
    PG --> CALC["Calculation: x1 = h2 - hf1 / hfg1"]
    CALC --> RES["Result: Dryness Fraction x1"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Joule-Thomson Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Gas Enters Throttle: P1, T1"] --> CHECK{"Is T1 greater than T_inversion?"}
    CHECK -- "Yes: T1 greater than T_inv" --> HEAT["Gas HEATS UP: mu_JT less than 0"]
    CHECK -- "No: T1 less than T_inv" --> COOL["Gas COOLS DOWN: mu_JT greater than 0"]
    CHECK -- "T1 equals T_inv" --> NEUTRAL["No Temperature Change: mu_JT = 0"]
    HEAT --> EX1["Example: Helium, Hydrogen at 300K"]
    COOL --> EX2["Example: N2, O2, CO2 at 300K"]
    NEUTRAL --> EX3["Used in Liquefaction Cycles for Cooling"]
    EX2 --> CRYO["Application: Cryogenic Liquefaction of Gases"]
    EX3 --> CRYO
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Text Replacement for Physical Drawing)

For the physical schematic of a throttling valve, the following functional description is provided (since Mermaid cannot render precise mechanical drawings):

- **Pipe segment A** (left, larger diameter): carries fluid at high pressure $P_1$.
- **Converging section:** smoothly narrows to a throat.
- **Throat / Valve seat:** the actual restriction — this is where maximum pressure drop occurs.
- **Diverging section:** expands back to a larger diameter downstream.
- **Pipe segment B** (right, larger diameter): carries fluid at lower pressure $P_2$.

The control volume is taken to enclose only the converging–throat–diverging region. A pressure tap at inlet measures $P_1$ and a thermocouple at outlet measures $T_2$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 4, Part A]**
> **Q1. Define the throttling process. State any three assumptions made while analyzing it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **throttling process** is an adiabatic, steady-flow process in which a fluid expands irreversibly through a restriction (valve, porous plug, or narrow opening), resulting in a pressure drop with no work done.

**Three assumptions:**
1. **Adiabatic** in nature: $q = 0$ (no heat transfer with surroundings).
2. **No work** is done by or on the fluid: $w = 0$.
3. **Changes in kinetic energy and potential energy** are negligible: $\Delta KE \approx 0$ and $\Delta PE \approx 0$.

These assumptions reduce the SFEE to the isenthalpic condition $h_1 = h_2$. (1 mark for definition, 2 marks for three assumptions — 1 mark each may be split or grouped as per valuation key).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 4, Part A]**
> **Q2. Show that a throttling process is isenthalpic but not isentropic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Isenthalpic proof:** From the SFEE, $q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$. For a throttle, $q = 0$, $w = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$, giving $h_1 = h_2$. Hence it is **isenthalpic**. (1.5 marks)

**Not isentropic proof:** Although the process is adiabatic ($q = 0$), it is highly **irreversible** due to friction and unrestrained expansion through the restriction. By the second law of thermodynamics, for an irreversible adiabatic process, $\Delta s > 0$, i.e., $s_2 > s_1$. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Module 4, Part B]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Joule-Thomson coefficient** $\mu_{JT}$ in terms of specific heat and specific volume. Explain the significance of the **inversion temperature**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A throttling calorimeter receives wet steam at **12 bar** with a dryness fraction of **0.88**. The steam is throttled to a pressure of **1.2 bar**, and the temperature of the superheated steam at exit is measured as **140 °C**. Determine the **dryness fraction** of the incoming steam. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

This represents the rate of change of temperature with pressure during a constant-enthalpy (throttling) process. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Starting equation:**

For enthalpy $h = h(T, P)$, the total differential is:

$$dh = \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P dT + \left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T dP$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Substituting partial derivatives:**

Using $c_P = \left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial T}\right)_P$ and the cyclic / Maxwell relation $\left(\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial P}\right)_T = v - T\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P$:

$$dh = c_P \, dT + \left[v - T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Apply isenthalpic condition $dh = 0$:**

$$0 = c_P \, dT + \left[v - T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P\right] dP$$

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h = \frac{1}{c_P}\left[T \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Significance of inversion temperature:**

The **inversion temperature** $T_{inv}$ is the temperature at which $\mu_{JT} = 0$. Above $T_{inv}$, $\mu_{JT} < 0$ (heating on expansion); below $T_{inv}$, $\mu_{JT} > 0$ (cooling on expansion). It is critical in **cryogenic gas liquefaction** and **Joule-Thomson coolers**. (1 mark)

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Inlet: $P_1 = 12$ bar, $x_1 = 0.88$ (assumed/initial — but we will verify using given $x_1$ first, then re-evaluate).

Wait, re-reading: the calorimeter receives wet steam at 12 bar with dryness fraction 0.88 (this is the *measured* upstream value to be checked), and is throttled to 1.2 bar at 140 °C.

**Steam table data (approximate standard values):**

- At $P_1 = 12$ bar: $h_{f1} \approx 798.65$ kJ/kg, $h_{g1} \approx 2784.8$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg1} \approx 1986.2$ kJ/kg.
- At $P_2 = 1.2$ bar: $T_{sat,2} \approx 104.81$ °C, $h_{g2} \approx 2683.4$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,\text{steam}} \approx 2.01$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step 1 — Compute $h_1$ (upstream wet steam):**

$$h_1 = h_{f1} + x_1 h_{fg1} = 798.65 + 0.88 \times 1986.2$$
$$h_1 = 798.65 + 1747.86 = 2546.51 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

(1.5 marks)

**Step 2 — Compute $h_2$ (superheated steam at 1.2 bar, 140 °C):**

$$h_2 = h_{g2} + c_p (T_2 - T_{sat,2})$$
$$h_2 = 2683.4 + 2.01 \times (140 - 104.81)$$
$$h_2 = 2683.4 + 2.01 \times 35.19$$
$$h_2 = 2683.4 + 70.73 = 2754.13 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Apply $h_1 = h_2$ and solve for $x_1$:**

$$x_1 = \frac{h_2 - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}} = \frac{2754.13 - 798.65}{1986.2}$$
$$x_1 = \frac{1955.48}{1986.2} = 0.9846$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

The calculated dryness fraction $x_1 \approx 0.985$ is **higher** than the originally stated 0.88, indicating the given $x_1 = 0.88$ in the problem is either an outdated measurement or the problem expects confirmation that the *true* dryness fraction is 0.985. (1.5 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** when computing $T_2 - T_{sat,2}$: since we are taking a *difference* of two Celsius temperatures, the conversion cancels out — this is acceptable. But do NOT use 273 for absolute temperature when not needed in a difference.
> 2. **Using the wrong $h_{fg1}$**: students often pick the $h_{fg}$ at $P_2$ instead of $P_1$.
> 3. **Assuming wet steam at exit**: the formula $h_{f2} + x_2 h_{fg2} = h_2$ applies only if the exit is wet. For superheated exit, use $h_2 = h_{g2} + c_p(T_2 - T_{sat,2})$.
> 4. **Sign of $x$**: ensure $0 \leq x \leq 1$. If your answer is $> 1$, you have used wrong table values.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the aid of the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE), derive the isenthalpic condition for a throttling process. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Helium gas at **20 bar and 300 K** is throttled to **1 bar**. The Joule-Thomson coefficient for helium at this state is approximately **−0.062 K/bar**. Calculate the **final temperature** of the gas and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Step 1 — Write the SFEE for a control volume:**

$$q - w = (h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(Z_2 - Z_1)$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — List the assumptions:**

1. **Adiabatic** process: $q = 0$.
2. **No shaft or boundary work**: $w = 0$.
3. **Negligible change in kinetic energy**: $V_1 \approx V_2$ (so $\Delta KE = 0$).
4. **Negligible change in potential energy**: $Z_1 \approx Z_2$ (so $\Delta PE = 0$).
5. **Steady-state**, single-inlet, single-outlet flow.

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Substitute the assumptions:**

$$0 - 0 = (h_2 - h_1) + 0 + 0$$
$$h_2 - h_1 = 0 \implies h_1 = h_2$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Discussion:**

The process is therefore **isenthalpic**. Although the process is adiabatic, the entropy increases due to the **irreversible** nature of the expansion ($s_2 > s_1$). The throttling process is widely used in refrigeration expansion valves, throttling calorimeters for steam dryness measurement, and pressure regulation. (2 marks)

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 20$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K
- $P_2 = 1$ bar
- $\mu_{JT} = -0.062$ K/bar (negative for helium, as expected)

**Step 1 — Use the Joule-Thomson relation:**

$$\Delta T \approx \mu_{JT} (P_2 - P_1)$$
$$\Delta T = -0.062 \times (1 - 20)$$
$$\Delta T = -0.062 \times (-19)$$
$$\Delta T = +1.178 \text{ K}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Final temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 300 + 1.178 = 301.18 \text{ K}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Comment:**

Since the Joule-Thomson coefficient for helium is **negative at 300 K** (its inversion temperature is around 40–50 K for helium), throttling causes a **heating** effect rather than cooling. The gas temperature rises by approximately **1.18 K**. This is why helium and hydrogen are difficult to liquefy by simple throttling — they must first be **pre-cooled below their inversion temperatures** (e.g., using liquid nitrogen) before Joule-Thomson expansion can cool them further. (4 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on Q.B(b):**
> 1. **Sign error in $(P_2 - P_1)$**: students often write $P_1 - P_2$ and get the wrong sign. Always remember: throttling is expansion, so $P_2 < P_1$, meaning $(P_2 - P_1) < 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion**: $\mu_{JT}$ is in K/bar — make sure your pressure drop is in **bar**, not Pa (factor of $10^5$ difference!).
> 3. **Confusing inversion temperature with critical temperature**: They are different. The critical temperature is where liquid and vapor phases coexist; the inversion temperature is where $\mu_{JT} = 0$.
> 4. **Not interpreting the sign**: Negative $\mu_{JT}$ for helium at 300 K is a *physical reality* — the gas heats up. Examiners expect this interpretation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Throttling Process**

- ✅ Throttling = **isenthalpic** ($h_1 = h_2$), **adiabatic**, **no work**, **irreversible**.
- ✅ The governing equation comes from **SFEE** with all non-enthalpy terms zeroed.
- ✅ **Entropy increases**: $s_2 > s_1$ (process is irreversible despite being adiabatic).
- ✅ For **ideal gases**: $T_1 = T_2$ (Joule's law) since $\mu_{JT} = 0$.
- ✅ For **real gases**: temperature may rise or fall depending on the sign of $\mu_{JT}$.
- ✅ **Joule-Thomson coefficient**: $\mu_{JT} = \dfrac{1}{c_P}\!\left[T\!\left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial T}\right)_P - v\right]$.
- ✅ **Cooling effect** when $\mu_{JT} > 0$ (most gases at room temperature, e.g., N₂, O₂, CO₂).
- ✅ **Heating effect** when $\mu_{JT} < 0$ (hydrogen, helium at room temperature).
- ✅ **Inversion temperature** $T_{inv}$: temperature at which $\mu_{JT} = 0$.
- ✅ **Throttling calorimeter formula**: $x_1 = \dfrac{h_2 - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}$.
- ✅ **Limitation**: calorimeter works only when exit steam is **superheated** (i.e., $T_2 > T_{sat}$ at $P_2$).
- ✅ **Applications**: refrigeration expansion valves, pressure regulators, cryogenic gas liquefaction, steam calorimetry.
- ✅ **Dryness fraction** $x$ must lie in the range $0 \leq x \leq 1$ — a value outside this range indicates a calculation error.
- ✅ **Unit vigilance**: $\mu_{JT}$ units are K/Pa in SI or K/bar in engineering; pressure drop must be in the matching unit.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for assumptions**: **"H.Q.W.K.P"** — Heat = 0, Work = 0, Kinetic energy unchanged, Potential energy unchanged → **H** (enthalpy) constant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joule-Thomson Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT502 - THERMODYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joule-Thomson Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Joule-Thomson Coefficient** (also known as the **Joule-Kelvin Coefficient**) is a fundamental thermodynamic property that quantifies the change in temperature of a real gas (or fluid) when it is forced through a valve, porous plug, or any throttling device under **adiabatic** and **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy) conditions.

Mathematically, the Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as the **partial derivative of temperature with respect to pressure at constant enthalpy**:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$$

The standard SI unit is **K/Pa (Kelvin per Pascal)**, although it is more commonly expressed as **K/bar** or **K/atm** for practical engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Joule-Thomson coefficient is the cornerstone of the **Linde-Hampson Liquefaction Process** and **Claude's Cycle** used in industrial gas liquefaction (LNG, liquid oxygen, liquid nitrogen, etc.). A thorough understanding of μ_JT is essential for solving 14-mark KTU problems on gas liquefaction cycles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aerosol Can Feeling"

Imagine holding a pressurized deodorant spray can. When you release the gas by pressing the nozzle, your fingers feel the can getting **colder**. This is not magic — this is the **Joule-Thomson Effect in action**.

When a high-pressure gas is forced through a narrow constriction (the porous plug or valve) into a low-pressure region:
- The gas molecules have to **do work** pushing against each other (overcoming intermolecular forces).
- This work is done **at the expense of the internal kinetic energy** of the molecules.
- Since internal kinetic energy is directly linked to temperature, the gas **cools down**.

Conversely, if you compress a gas adiabatically, the temperature usually **rises** because you are doing positive work on the gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** μ_JT tells you whether a gas will **cool (μ_JT > 0)**, **heat up (μ_JT < 0)**, or **remain unchanged (μ_JT = 0)** when it expands through a throttling device.

## 1.3 Physical Significance of the Sign of μ_JT

| Sign of μ_JT | Behavior During Expansion (dP < 0) | Physical Meaning |
|--------------|-----------------------------------|------------------|
| **μ_JT > 0** | Gas **cools** (dT < 0) | Attractive forces dominate; used in liquefaction |
| **μ_JT < 0** | Gas **heats up** (dT > 0) | Repulsive forces dominate (high pressure regime) |
| **μ_JT = 0** | **No temperature change** | Gas behaves ideally; inversion point reached |

## 1.4 The Inversion Temperature

There exists a specific temperature for any given pressure at which μ_JT = 0. This temperature is called the **Inversion Temperature (T_i)**.

- **Below T_i:** Attractive intermolecular forces dominate → μ_JT > 0 → Gas cools on expansion.
- **Above T_i:** Repulsive forces (or kinetic energy) dominate → μ_JT < 0 → Gas heats on expansion.
- **At T_i:** The gas behaves ideally with respect to throttling → μ_JT = 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A gas can only be liquefied by throttling if its initial temperature is **below its maximum inversion temperature**. For nitrogen (T_i ≈ 621 K), room-temperature throttling produces cooling, but for hydrogen (T_i ≈ 200 K) and helium (T_i ≈ 40 K), pre-cooling below the inversion temperature is mandatory before throttling can produce liquefaction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is a derived thermodynamic property. Starting from the definition:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$$

Using the **cyclic rule of partial derivatives** and the **Maxwell relations**, this expression can be transformed into a more usable form involving measurable quantities like $C_p$, $V$, and $\alpha$ (coefficient of thermal expansion).

### Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

**Step 1 — Starting Identity:** From thermodynamics, $H = H(T, P)$, so the total differential of enthalpy is:

$$dH = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Constant Enthalpy Constraint:** For a Joule-Thomson process, $dH = 0$:

$$0 = C_p \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for the Partial Derivative:**

$$\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

**Step 4 — Use the Thermodynamic Identity:** From $dH = dU + d(PV) = TdS + VdP$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V + T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

**Step 5 — Apply Maxwell Relation:** Since $\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T = -\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$$

This is the **general expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient**, valid for **any pure substance** (real or ideal).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of KTU Thermodynamics problems on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$ | Fundamental definition | All problems |
| 2 | $\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$ | General working equation | Real gas problems |
| 3 | $\mu_{JT} = \frac{V}{C_p} \left( \alpha T - 1 \right)$ | In terms of expansivity $\alpha$ | Quick numerical problems |
| 4 | $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for ideal gas | Ideal gas has no J-T effect | Conceptual questions |
| 5 | $\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ \frac{2a}{RT} - b \right]$ | For **van der Waals gas** | KTU most-asked formula |
| 6 | $T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}$ | Inversion temperature (vdW) | Liquefaction design |
| 7 | $T_{i,\text{max}} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$ | Maximum inversion temperature | Highest cooling limit |
| 8 | $\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$ | Alternative form | Derivations |

Where:
- $\alpha = \frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$ is the **coefficient of thermal expansion**
- $a$ and $b$ are the **van der Waals constants**
- $C_p$ is the **specific heat at constant pressure**
- $R$ is the **universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** The Joule-Thomson effect is the working principle behind:

1. **Linde-Hampson Liquefaction Cycle** — Industrial production of liquid nitrogen, oxygen, and LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas). The gas is repeatedly compressed, cooled, and throttled, with each throttling step reducing temperature cumulatively.

2. **Joule-Thomson Cryocoolers** — Used in laboratory cooling, superconducting magnet cooling (MRI machines), and infrared detector cooling in satellites.

3. **Refrigeration Technology** — Domestic refrigerators using certain refrigerant mixtures leverage the J-T effect at the expansion valve.

4. **Natural Gas Processing** — Critical for **gas dew-point control** and **hydrocarbon dew-pointing** in pipeline operations.

5. **Aerosol and Spray Technology** — Fuel injection systems and medical nebulizers depend on predictable J-T cooling.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Joule-Thomson Coefficient

**Starting Point — Definition:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$$

Since $H$ is a state function and depends on $T$ and $P$, we can write $H = H(T, P)$. The total differential is:

$$dH = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T \, dP$$

**Recalling the Definition of $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

Substituting this into the differential:

$$dH = C_p \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T \, dP$$

**Applying the Joule-Thomson Constraint (dH = 0):**

For a throttling process, enthalpy is conserved, so $dH = 0$:

$$0 = C_p \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T \, dP$$

**Isolating the Partial Derivative:**

$$C_p \, dT = -\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T \, dP$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$$

**Evaluating $\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T$:**

We start from the fundamental relation $H = U + PV$. Taking the total differential:

$$dH = dU + P \, dV + V \, dP$$

From the First Law for a closed system, $dU = TdS - PdV$. Substituting:

$$dH = TdS - PdV + PdV + VdP = TdS + VdP$$

Now, with $S$ as a function of $(T, P)$, $dS = \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$. Substituting:

$$dH = T \left[ \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T dP \right] + VdP$$

Holding $T$ constant ($dT = 0$):

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T + V$$

**Applying the Maxwell Relation:**

The Maxwell relation derived from the Helmholtz free energy gives:

$$\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T = -\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

Therefore:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$$

**Final Assembly:**

$$\mu_{JT} = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left[ V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \right]$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]}$$

This is the **general result**, valid for any pure substance.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation for a van der Waals Gas

The **van der Waals equation of state** is:

$$\left( P + \frac{a}{V^2} \right)(V - b) = RT$$

Solving for $P$:

$$P = \frac{RT}{V - b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$$

We need to find $\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$. Differentiating the van der Waals equation implicitly with respect to $T$ at constant $P$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V = \frac{R}{V - b}$$

By the **reciprocal rule** of partial derivatives:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V}{\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T}$$

Computing $\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V-b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V^3}$$

Therefore:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{\frac{R}{V-b}}{-\frac{RT}{(V-b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V^3}} = \frac{R(V-b)}{RT - \frac{2a(V-b)^2}{V^3}}$$

**Applying the Low-to-Moderate Pressure Approximation** (where $V \gg b$ and $V^3 \gg \frac{2a(V-b)^2}{RT}$):

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \approx \frac{R}{RT/V} \cdot \frac{V^2}{V^2} = \frac{V}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{2a}{RTV}}$$

Using the geometric series approximation $\frac{1}{1-x} \approx 1 + x$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \approx \frac{V}{T} \left( 1 + \frac{2a}{RTV} \right) = \frac{V}{T} + \frac{2a}{RT^2}$$

**Substituting into the General Formula:**

$$T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V = T \left( \frac{V}{T} + \frac{2a}{RT^2} \right) - V = V + \frac{2a}{RT} - V = \frac{2a}{RT}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{2a}{RT} - b \right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Inversion Temperature

Setting $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for a van der Waals gas:

$$\frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{2a}{RT_i} - b \right) = 0$$

$$\frac{2a}{RT_i} - b = 0$$

$$\frac{2a}{RT_i} = b$$

$$\boxed{T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of $T_i$:** For a van der Waals gas, there are two inversion temperatures at a given pressure: a **lower inversion temperature** and an **upper inversion temperature**. The formula $T_i = 2a/(Rb)$ gives the **maximum inversion temperature** when the gas behaves ideally in the limit of low pressure. In reality, $T_i$ is a function of pressure, but $T_{i,\text{max}}$ is the engineering benchmark used in KTU problems.

## 3.4 Inversion Curve

Setting $\mu_{JT} = 0$ in the exact form (without approximation):

$$T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = V$$

For the van der Waals gas, this yields the inversion curve equation:

$$T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(1 - \frac{b}{V}\right)^2} \cdot \frac{V^2}{V^2}$$

In the limit of low pressure ($V \to \infty$), this reduces to $T_{i,\text{max}} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$, confirming the result from Section 3.3.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Condition | μ_JT Sign | Physical Reason | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| $T < T_i$ (low T) | Positive | Attractive forces dominate | Throttling **cools** the gas |
| $T = T_i$ | Zero | Forces balance | **No temperature change** |
| $T > T_i$ (high T) | Negative | Repulsive/kinetic energy dominates | Throttling **heats** the gas |
| Ideal gas | Zero (always) | No intermolecular forces | Cannot be liquefied by throttling |
| Real gas, low P | Positive | Mild attractive forces | Standard liquefaction regime |
| Real gas, very high P | Negative | Repulsive core dominates | Heating on expansion |

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Joule-Thomson Experimental Apparatus

The following flowchart illustrates the conceptual layout of the Joule-Thomson porous plug experiment. This is the standard KTU-referenced schematic for explaining throttling processes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    C --> D["Low Pressure Region: P2, T2, H2"]
    D --> E["Temperature Measurement: T2 Detected at Outlet"]
    E --> F["Compare T1 and T2 to Compute Temperature Change"]
    F --> G{"Determine Sign of mu JT"}
    G -->|"mu JT greater than 0"| H["Gas Cools: Suitable for Liquefaction"]
    G -->|"mu JT equals 0"| I["No Temperature Change: Inversion Point"]
    G -->|"mu JT less than 0"| J["Gas Heats: Repulsive Forces Dominate"]

    end

    end

    B -.-> K
    C -.-> L
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Linde-Hampson Liquefaction Process

This diagram shows the **industrial application** of the Joule-Thomson effect in a continuous liquefaction cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incoming Low Pressure Gas at T1"] --> B["Compressor Stage 1: Multi-Stage Compression"]
    B --> C["After-Cooler: Removes Heat of Compression"]
    C --> D["Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger: Gas Pre-Cooled by Returning Cold Gas"]
    D --> E["Throttling Valve: Joule Thomson Expansion Occurs Here"]
    E --> F["Two-Phase Mixture: Liquid Plus Vapor Separated"]
    F --> G["Liquid Product Collected in Storage Tank"]
    F --> H["Cold Vapor Returns Through Heat Exchanger"]
    H --> D
    H --> I["Recycled Vapor Mixed with Incoming Fresh Gas"]
    I --> A

    subgraph KeyComponent["Critical Component for KTU"]
        J["Throttling Valve is the Site of Joule Thomson Cooling"]
    end

    E -.-> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In the Linde-Hampson cycle, the J-T throttling valve is where the **temperature drop** is generated. The heat exchanger merely **transfers** this cold to the incoming gas — it does not generate cold itself. The cumulative effect of many cycles leads to progressively lower temperatures and eventual liquefaction.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define the Joule-Thomson coefficient. Show that for an ideal gas, the Joule-Thomson coefficient is zero.**

**Model Answer:**

The Joule-Thomson coefficient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to pressure at constant enthalpy during a throttling process:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$$

For an ideal gas, $PV = RT$, so $V = \frac{RT}{P}$. Computing $\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = \frac{R}{P}$.

Substituting into the general formula:

$$\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \cdot \frac{R}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} \right] = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ \frac{RT}{P} - \frac{RT}{P} \right] = 0$$

Hence, an ideal gas exhibits **no temperature change** during a Joule-Thomson expansion. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State and explain the concept of inversion temperature with reference to the Joule-Thomson effect.**

**Model Answer:**

The **inversion temperature** ($T_i$) is the temperature at which the Joule-Thomson coefficient changes sign — that is, $\mu_{JT} = 0$.

- For $T < T_i$: $\mu_{JT} > 0$, the gas **cools** upon throttling.
- For $T = T_i$: $\mu_{JT} = 0$, **no temperature change** occurs.
- For $T > T_i$: $\mu_{JT} < 0$, the gas **heats up** upon throttling.

The inversion temperature arises because intermolecular forces transition from being **attractive** at low temperatures to **repulsive** at high temperatures. The maximum inversion temperature for a van der Waals gas is given by $T_{i,\text{max}} = \frac{2a}{Rb}$, where $a$ and $b$ are the van der Waals constants. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the definition, derive the general expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient $\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $H = H(T, P)$, the total differential is:

$$dH = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 2:** Recognizing $C_p = \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial T} \right)_P$:

$$dH = C_p \, dT + \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

**Step 3:** For a throttling process, $dH = 0$ (isenthalpic), so:

$$C_p \, dT = -\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T dP$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4:** From $H = U + PV$, taking differentials and using $dU = TdS - PdV$:

$$dH = TdS + VdP \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 5:** With $S = S(T, P)$ and $dT = 0$ at constant temperature:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T + V \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 6:** Applying the Maxwell relation $\left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial P} \right)_T = -\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial H}{\partial P} \right)_T = V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 7:** Substituting (iv) into (i):

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H = -\frac{1}{C_p} \left[ V - T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \right]$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]}$$

**(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a van der Waals gas, derive the expression for the Joule-Thomson coefficient and the maximum inversion temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The van der Waals equation is:

$$\left( P + \frac{a}{V^2} \right)(V - b) = RT$$

Solving for $P$: $P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$

**Step 2:** Differentiating implicitly at constant $P$ to find $\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P$:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V = \frac{R}{V-b}$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T = -\frac{RT}{(V-b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V^3}$$

**Step 3:** Applying the reciprocal rule:

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P = -\frac{\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial T} \right)_V}{\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_T} = \frac{R/(V-b)}{\frac{RT}{(V-b)^2} - \frac{2a}{V^3}}$$

**Step 4:** Approximating for low-to-moderate pressures (neglecting $b/V$ and $2a(V-b)^2/(RTV^3)$ compared to 1):

$$\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \approx \frac{V}{T} + \frac{2a}{RT^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into the general formula:

$$T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V = T \left( \frac{V}{T} + \frac{2a}{RT^2} \right) - V = V + \frac{2a}{RT} - V = \frac{2a}{RT}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{2a}{RT} - b \right)}$$

**Step 7:** Setting $\mu_{JT} = 0$ for inversion:

$$\frac{2a}{RT_i} = b \implies \boxed{T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}}$$

**(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A sample of nitrogen gas ($\mu_{JT} = 0.25$ K/atm at 300 K) is throttled from 200 atm to 1 atm. Estimate the temperature drop. State any assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\mu_{JT} = 0.25$ K/atm (assumed constant over the pressure range)
- $P_1 = 200$ atm
- $P_2 = 1$ atm
- $T_1 = 300$ K

**Assumption:** μ_JT remains approximately constant over the small temperature range involved.

**Step 1:** Using the definition:

$$\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H \approx \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta P}$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging for $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P = 0.25 \cdot (1 - 200) = 0.25 \cdot (-199)$$

$$\Delta T = -49.75 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3:** The new temperature is:

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 300 - 49.75 = 250.25 \text{ K}$$

**Conclusion:** The nitrogen gas cools from 300 K to approximately **250.25 K**, a drop of **49.75 K**. **(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Linde-Hampson liquefaction process** with a neat schematic, clearly identifying the role of the Joule-Thomson effect.

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid Diagram for full visual):**

The Linde-Hampson cycle consists of four essential components:
1. **Compressor** — Raises gas pressure from $P_1$ to $P_2$.
2. **After-cooler** — Removes the heat of compression.
3. **Counter-flow heat exchanger** — Pre-cools incoming high-pressure gas using cold returning low-pressure gas.
4. **Throttling valve** — Reduces pressure adiabatically, producing cooling via the J-T effect.

**Cycle Description:**

- **Process 1-2 (Compression):** Gas is compressed isentropically from $P_1$ to $P_2$, with temperature rising from $T_1$ to $T_2$.
- **Process 2-3 (Cooling):** In the after-cooler, gas is cooled at constant pressure $P_2$ from $T_2$ to $T_3$ (close to ambient).
- **Process 3-4 (Heat Exchange):** In the counter-flow heat exchanger, the high-pressure gas is further cooled to $T_4$ by the cold returning low-pressure gas.
- **Process 4-5 (Throttling):** The high-pressure gas is throttled from $P_2$ to $P_1$. Since the gas temperature $T_4$ is below the inversion temperature, **$\mu_{JT} > 0$**, and the gas **cools** to $T_5 < T_4$.
- **Process 5-6 (Separation):** The two-phase mixture separates into liquid (collected) and vapor (returned through the heat exchanger).
- **Process 6-1 (Recycle):** The cold vapor exchanges heat with incoming gas and returns to the compressor.

**Role of Joule-Thomson Effect:**

The throttling valve is the **only source of cooling** in the cycle. Each throttling event produces a temperature drop of $\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$. For the cycle to work continuously, the cumulative cooling must exceed the heat leak and inefficiencies. Repeated recirculation progressively lowers the temperature until **liquefaction occurs** at the separator. **(7 Marks Awarded)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 1: Sign Confusion in μ_JT**
> Many students forget that during throttling, $\Delta P$ is **negative** (pressure decreases). If $\mu_{JT} > 0$, then $\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P$ is **negative**, meaning the gas cools. Marking: 1-2 marks deducted for sign errors.
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 2: Forgetting the Approximation in van der Waals Derivation**
> The simplification $\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P \approx \frac{V}{T} + \frac{2a}{RT^2}$ requires the assumption that $\frac{2a(V-b)^2}{RTV^3} \ll 1$. Without this justification, KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks.
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 3: Confusing "Inversion Temperature" with "Critical Temperature"**
> These are **different** concepts. Critical temperature is where liquid-vapor phases coexist; inversion temperature is where μ_JT = 0. Conflating them is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 4: Stating μ_JT = 0 for Ideal Gas Without Proof**
> Always show the working: substitute $V = RT/P$ and show the bracket evaluates to zero. Stating it as a fact without derivation gets **0/2 marks** for that sub-part.
> 
> ⚠️ **Pitfall 5: Missing Units in Numerical Problems**
> Always state μ_JT in consistent units (e.g., K/Pa or K/bar). Mixing units results in **1-mark deduction**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Joule-Thomson coefficient** is defined as $\mu_{JT} = \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_H$ — a measure of temperature sensitivity to pressure change at constant enthalpy.
- ✅ The process is **isenthalpic** ($dH = 0$) and **adiabatic** ($dQ = 0$) — but **irreversible** in practice.
- ✅ **General formula:** $\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left[ T \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial T} \right)_P - V \right]$ — valid for all pure substances.
- ✅ **For ideal gases:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ — no cooling or heating on throttling (proof: substitute $V = RT/P$).
- ✅ **For van der Waals gases:** $\mu_{JT} = \frac{1}{C_p} \left( \frac{2a}{RT} - b \right)$ — under the low-pressure approximation.
- ✅ **Inversion temperature** (van der Waals): $T_i = \frac{2a}{Rb}$ — the temperature where μ_JT switches sign.
- ✅ **Sign convention:** $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → cooling; $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → heating; $\mu_{JT} = 0$ → inversion point.
- ✅ **Engineering use:** Linde-Hampson liquefaction, Claude cycle, J-T cryocoolers, refrigeration expansion valves, natural gas processing.
- ✅ **Hydrogen and helium** have very low inversion temperatures (≈ 200 K and ≈ 40 K respectively) — pre-cooling is essential before throttling.
- ✅ **Nitrogen and oxygen** have inversion temperatures well above room temperature (≈ 621 K and ≈ 760 K) — throttling at room temperature produces cooling.
- ✅ **The throttling process is irreversible** — entropy increases, even though enthalpy is conserved.
- ✅ **μ_JT is NOT a constant** — it depends strongly on $T$ and $P$. For numerical problems, use the value at the mean temperature and pressure.
- ✅ **Alternative form using expansivity:** $\mu_{JT} = \frac{V}{C_p}(\alpha T - 1)$, where $\alpha = \frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$.
- ✅ **Practical formula for temperature drop in throttling:** $\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$, with $\Delta P < 0$ for expansion.
- ✅ **The Linde-Hampson cycle** is the **most asked** 14-mark KTU question on this topic — master the schematic and the role of each component.
